original_script: अथ प्रथमोऽध्यायः - अर्जुनविषादयोगः
transliteration: Arjuna Viṣhāda Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Arjuna—the warrior; Viṣhāda—dharmic crisis; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Arjuna's Dejection
deep_dive_text: Chapter 1 presents the battlefield of Kurukshetra as the setting for Arjuna's moral crisis. Faced with the prospect of fighting his own teachers, relatives, and friends, he is torn between duty, compassion, and grief. The chapter prepares the ground for the Gita's central teaching by showing the depth of Arjuna's inner conflict.

original_script:
धृतराष्ट्र उवाच

धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः।

मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय।।1.1।।
transliteration:
dhṛitarāśhtra uvācha
dharma-kṣhetre kuru-kṣhetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ
māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāśhchaiva kimakurvata sañjaya
word_meanings: dhṛitarāśhtraḥ uvācha—Dhritarashtra said; dharma-kṣhetre—the land of dharma; kuru-kṣhetre—at Kurukshetra; samavetāḥ—having gathered; yuyutsavaḥ—desiring to fight; māmakāḥ—my sons; pāṇḍavāḥ—the sons of Pandu; cha—and; eva—certainly; kim—what; akurvata—did they do; sañjaya—Sanjay
translation_english: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjaya, after assembling at Kurukshetra, eager for battle, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
deep_dive_text:
Dhritarashtra, the blind king of Hastinapura, opens the Bhagavad Gita by asking Sanjaya what happened when the sons of Pandu and his own sons gathered on the field of Kurukshetra, eager for battle. This is not a casual request for news. Dhritarashtra already knows why the armies are there. Years of jealousy, injustice, insult, and political failure have led to this moment. His question carries anxiety, guilt, and attachment all at once. He wants to know what happened, but he also fears what the answer may reveal.

His physical blindness is an important detail, but readers have long understood that it also reflects moral blindness. Again and again, he failed to restrain Duryodhana's envy and wrongdoing. He loved his son, but that love was mixed with weakness and partiality. Because he could not govern his own attachment, the kingdom itself moved toward ruin. In that sense, the very first voice of the Gita is the voice of human confusion: someone who knows the consequences of past choices, yet still cannot fully rise above personal bias.

Kurukshetra is called the field of dharma, the field of righteousness. That phrase matters. The setting is not only political or military. It is a place where truth, justice, duty, and inner character are tested. The Gita begins here because spiritual teaching does not arise far away from life. It emerges in the middle of crisis, conflict, fear, and moral complexity. Before Krishna gives wisdom, the text first shows why wisdom is necessary.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा।

आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत्।।1.2।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvā tu pāṇḍavānīkaṁ vyūḍhaṁ duryodhanastadā
āchāryamupasaṅgamya rājā vachanamabravīt
word_meanings: sanjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; dṛiṣhṭvā—on observing; tu—but; pāṇḍava-anīkam—the Pandava army; vyūḍham—standing in a military formation; duryodhanaḥ—King Duryodhan; tadā—then; āchāryam—teacher; upasaṅgamya—approached; rājā—the king; vachanam—words; abravīt—spoke
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Seeing the Pandava army arranged for war, King Duryodhana approached teacher Drona and spoke these words.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya begins his narration by describing Duryodhana approaching his teacher Drona after seeing the Pandava army arranged in battle formation. At first this may sound like a simple battlefield movement, but it reveals much about Duryodhana's state of mind. He is a prince and commander, yet instead of responding with calm certainty, he goes quickly to a senior authority figure. He has seen something that unsettles him.

Duryodhana is proud, strategic, and politically sharp. He is not easily shaken outwardly, but he understands power. When he sees the Pandava army standing in disciplined formation, he recognizes that this is not a weak or disorganized opposition. The people across from him are not merely family members who can be intimidated or manipulated. They are supported by capable warriors, experienced allies, and a cause that many consider just. His movement toward Drona shows that beneath confidence there is concern.

This verse also introduces one of the important themes of the chapter: the contrast between outer display and inner reality. Duryodhana often speaks as if he is secure, but his actions show calculation mixed with unease. The battlefield has not yet begun to consume lives, but it has already begun to expose minds. In the Gita, conflict first reveals itself inwardly. Before arrows are released, fear, ambition, loyalty, memory, and conscience all begin to surface. That is why even a small movement like this carries meaning.

original_script:
पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम्।

व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता।।1.3।।
transliteration:
paśhyaitāṁ pāṇḍu-putrāṇām āchārya mahatīṁ chamūm
vyūḍhāṁ drupada-putreṇa tava śhiṣhyeṇa dhīmatā
word_meanings: paśhya—behold; etām—this; pāṇḍu-putrāṇām—of the sons of Pandu; āchārya—respected teacher; mahatīm—mighty; chamūm—army; vyūḍhām—arrayed in a military formation; drupada-putreṇa—son of Drupad, Dhrishtadyumna; tava—by your; śhiṣhyeṇa—disciple; dhī-matā—intelligent
translation_english: O teacher, behold this mighty army of the sons of Pandu, expertly arranged by your intelligent disciple, the son of Drupada.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana points out to Drona the mighty Pandava army and notes that it has been arranged by Dhrishtadyumna, the son of Drupada and a disciple of Drona himself. His words are not neutral. He is choosing his language carefully in order to influence his teacher. By mentioning Dhrishtadyumna in this way, he reminds Drona of an old and painful history.

Drupada and Drona were once connected, but that relationship ended in hostility and humiliation. Dhrishtadyumna was born into that tension, and according to tradition he was destined to play a role against Drona. So when Duryodhana says that this army has been arranged by Drona's own disciple, there is a subtle provocation in the statement. He seems to be saying: look at who stands against us, and remember how personal this war truly is.

This verse shows Duryodhana as psychologically skilled. He does not merely issue commands; he manipulates emotion, pride, and memory. He wants Drona to fight not only from duty, but from personal investment. The battlefield, therefore, is not made of soldiers alone. It is also made of old loyalties, unresolved wounds, and strategic speech.

For a new reader, this verse helps explain why the Mahabharata war is so tragic. Almost everyone standing there is linked by some prior relationship: teacher and student, elder and younger, friend and rival, kin and king. The Gita's setting is painful precisely because duty is tangled with affection, history, and shared past. Duryodhana's sentence brings all of that tension to the surface immediately.

original_script:
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि।

युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथः।।1.4।।
transliteration:
atra śhūrā maheṣhvāsā bhīmārjuna-samā yudhi
yuyudhāno virāṭaśhcha drupadaśhcha mahā-rathaḥ.
word_meanings: atra—here; śhūrāḥ—powerful warriors; mahā-iṣhu-āsāḥ—great bowmen; bhīma-arjuna-samāḥ—equal to Bheem and Arjun; yudhi—in military prowess; yuyudhānaḥ—Yuyudhan; virāṭaḥ—Virat; cha—and; drupadaḥ—Drupad; cha—also; mahā-rathaḥ—a warrior who could single-handedly match the strength of ten thousand ordinary warriors;
translation_english: In this army stand many heroic archers, equal in battle to Bhima and Arjuna.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana begins listing the powerful warriors on the Pandava side, naming figures like Bhima and Arjuna and indicating that others there are equal to them in strength and skill. This is more than a military inventory. By naming these heroes, he is measuring the reality before him and, at the same time, shaping the mood of those around him.

Bhima and Arjuna are not ordinary opponents. Bhima represents tremendous physical force, courage, and intensity. Arjuna represents mastery, discipline, and refined warrior excellence. To acknowledge them is to admit that the coming battle will be severe. Duryodhana's words reveal that he cannot dismiss the Pandavas as weak claimants to the throne. They stand before him as serious and worthy opponents.

This verse also shows how war sharpens perception. When conflict becomes unavoidable, names carry weight. Each warrior is not just a person but a history, a reputation, and a threat. Duryodhana speaks these names because they matter strategically, but their presence also deepens the emotional pressure of the moment. These are not faceless enemies. They are known men with known abilities.

For the reader, the verse builds a sense of scale. The war of Kurukshetra is not a simple clash between good and evil in a cartoonish sense. It is a confrontation between mighty, trained, honorable, and flawed human beings. The tragedy of the Gita is that many great men stand on both sides, yet they are drawn into a conflict that righteousness itself must somehow pass through.

original_script:
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान्।

पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गवः।।1.5।।
transliteration:
dhṛiṣhṭaketuśhchekitānaḥ kāśhirājaśhcha vīryavān
purujit kuntibhojaśhcha śhaibyaśhcha nara-puṅgavaḥ
word_meanings: dhṛiṣhṭaketuḥ—Dhrishtaketu; chekitānaḥ—Chekitan; kāśhirājaḥ—Kashiraj; cha—and; vīrya-vān—heroic; purujit—Purujit; kuntibhojaḥ—Kuntibhoj; cha—and; śhaibyaḥ—Shaibya; cha—and; nara-puṅgavaḥ—best of men
translation_english: There are also great warriors such as Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, the valiant king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana continues by naming Yuyudhana, Virata, and Drupada, all great chariot-warriors supporting the Pandavas. This growing list matters because it shows the breadth of the Pandava alliance. Their strength does not come only from family bonds or from the presence of a few exceptional heroes. It comes from the trust and support of many kingdoms and respected leaders.

Each name adds political and moral weight to the Pandava cause. Virata had sheltered the Pandavas in a time of hardship. Drupada had long-standing reasons to stand against the Kaurava side. Yuyudhana, also known as Satyaki, is associated with loyalty, courage, and connection to Krishna's circle. The Pandava army is therefore not held together by convenience alone. It is a coalition shaped by loyalty, shared history, and a broader recognition that injustice has gone too far.

For Duryodhana, this is troubling. The more names he speaks, the clearer it becomes that the conflict has expanded far beyond a private family quarrel. Other rulers have taken sides because the dispute between Kauravas and Pandavas has become a matter of public righteousness and political order.

For the reader, this verse broadens the world of the Gita. The battlefield is not simply about five brothers against their cousins. It involves the wider moral fabric of society. When leaders fail in justice, the damage spreads outward. Others are drawn in. Alliances form. War becomes larger than the ambitions that first triggered it. This is part of the sobering realism of the Gita's setting.

original_script:
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान्।

सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः।।1.6।।
transliteration:
yudhāmanyuśhcha vikrānta uttamaujāśhcha vīryavān
saubhadro draupadeyāśhcha sarva eva mahā-rathāḥ
word_meanings: yudhāmanyuḥ—Yudhamanyu; vikrāntaḥ—courageous; uttamaujāḥ—Uttamauja; vīrya-vān—gallant; saubhadraḥ—the son of Subhadra; draupadeyāḥ—the sons of Draupadi; sarve—all; eva—indeed; mahā-rathāḥ—mighty chariot-warriors
translation_english: There are Yudhamanyu, Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi, all powerful chariot-warriors.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana adds further names, including Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, the king of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, and Shaibya, each renowned for valor. The list continues to grow, and that growth itself is meaningful. He is not naming random soldiers. He is recognizing that the Pandava side has gathered experienced, distinguished, and committed support from many directions.

The accumulation of names creates a sense of pressure. Every additional warrior is another reminder that the Pandavas are not isolated claimants relying on sentiment. They are backed by respected figures whose presence gives legitimacy to their cause. In epic literature, such lists are also a way of conveying honor. To be named on the battlefield is to be marked as significant, remembered, and dangerous.

At a psychological level, Duryodhana's speech continues to reveal the tension between boldness and insecurity. A truly relaxed commander would not need such extended verbal accounting. But Duryodhana is taking stock of the danger in detail. His mind is active, alert, and measuring.

For a new reader, this verse helps explain why the opening of the Gita spends time on warriors before turning to philosophy. The spiritual teaching that follows does not occur in abstraction. It emerges out of a real world populated by personalities, loyalties, ranks, and consequences. The moral crisis of Arjuna later in the chapter will make more sense because the text first shows us how full this battlefield is with human meaning. These are not symbols alone. They are living people, each with a place in the unfolding tragedy.

original_script:
अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम।

नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते।।1.7।।
transliteration:
asmākaṁ tu viśhiṣhṭā ye tānnibodha dwijottama
nāyakā mama sainyasya sanjñārthaṁ tānbravīmi te
word_meanings: asmākam—ours; tu—but; viśhiṣhṭāḥ—special; ye—who; tān—them; nibodha—be informed; dwija-uttama—best of Brahmins; nāyakāḥ—principal generals; mama—our; sainyasya—of army; sanjñā-artham—for information; tān—them; bravīmi—I recount; te—unto you
translation_english: Now, O best of brahmanas, know also the distinguished leaders of our own army.
deep_dive_text:
After describing the strength of the Pandava side, Duryodhana turns to his own army and tells Drona to hear from him about the distinguished leaders on the Kaurava side. This shift is important. His earlier words acknowledged the enemy's power; now he begins rebuilding confidence by directing attention to the resources available to him.

A leader in crisis often does two things at once: identify danger and stabilize morale. Duryodhana is doing both. He cannot ignore the disciplined power of the Pandava army, but he also cannot allow fear to spread among his own side. By moving to a list of Kaurava heroes, he attempts to reframe the situation. He wants Drona and the others to remember that they too possess formidable strength.

There is also an element of control in the way he speaks. Duryodhana does not simply say, "we have strong warriors." He positions himself as the one naming, arranging, and interpreting the battlefield. His speech performs leadership, even while revealing inner tension.

For the reader, this verse shows that confidence in the Gita's world is often constructed through words. Speech can encourage, manipulate, steady, or inflame. Before battle begins physically, it begins rhetorically. Duryodhana is shaping perception because perception affects courage.

This verse also keeps the chapter balanced. The text is not interested in portraying one side as overwhelmingly weak or strong in a simplistic way. Both armies contain greatness. That is why the coming war is so terrible. Duty will not unfold in an easy landscape. It will unfold in a field crowded with powerful persons and painful loyalties.

original_script:
भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः।

अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च।।1.8।।
transliteration:
bhavānbhīṣhmaśhcha karṇaśhcha kṛipaśhcha samitiñjayaḥ
aśhvatthāmā vikarṇaśhcha saumadattis tathaiva cha
word_meanings: bhavān—yourself; bhīṣhmaḥ—Bheeshma; cha—and; karṇaḥ—Karna; cha—and; kṛipaḥ—Kripa; cha—and; samitim-jayaḥ—victorious in battle; aśhvatthāmā—Ashvatthama; vikarṇaḥ—Vikarna; cha—and; saumadattiḥ—Bhurishrava; tathā—thus; eva—even; cha—also
translation_english: You, Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta are all present.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana names Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and others among the great warriors standing on the Kaurava side. This list carries enormous weight because these are not ordinary fighters. They include elders, teachers, loyal allies, and men of proven reputation. By naming them, Duryodhana calls up the prestige and force that still support his cause.

Bhishma in particular represents the old Kuru order, dignity, vow, and unmatched martial excellence. Karna embodies loyalty to Duryodhana, heroic pride, and tragic intensity. Kripa represents learned discipline and battlefield competence. Ashvatthama carries the legacy of Drona. Even Vikarna is significant because he once showed signs of moral sensitivity in earlier events, yet still stands now with his brothers. The Kaurava side, therefore, is not made of villains alone. It includes men of real greatness and complexity.

This is essential for understanding the emotional depth of the Gita. If Arjuna later breaks down at the thought of battle, it is partly because those facing him include exactly these kinds of men: revered elders, beloved kin, and respected teachers. The battle is not between strangers.

Duryodhana's speech here is meant to strengthen resolve, but it also reveals the moral complexity of the epic. Great people can stand on compromised sides for reasons of duty, loyalty, debt, confusion, or destiny. The Gita does not deny that complexity. It begins inside it. That is one reason its teaching remains powerful: it addresses action in a world where the right path is real, but the people involved are never morally simple.

original_script:
अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविताः।

नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः।।1.9।।
transliteration:
anye cha bahavaḥ śhūrā madarthe tyaktajīvitāḥ
nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ sarve yuddha-viśhāradāḥ
word_meanings: anye—others; cha—also; bahavaḥ—many; śhūrāḥ—heroic warriors; mat-arthe—for my sake; tyakta-jīvitāḥ—prepared to lay down their lives; nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ—equipped with various kinds of weapons; sarve—all; yuddha-viśhāradāḥ—skilled in the art of warfare
translation_english: Many other brave heroes are here as well, ready to lay down their lives for my sake, armed with many weapons and skilled in war.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana declares that many other heroes are also ready to give up their lives for his sake, skilled in various weapons and trained in warfare. On the surface this sounds like confidence, and in one sense it is. He is reminding Drona that the Kaurava army is large, disciplined, and full of determined fighters.

But the wording is revealing. He says these men are ready to give their lives for him. That phrase exposes the personal center of the conflict. The war may be presented politically, but Duryodhana's ambition stands at its heart. Many men are about to die in a struggle ultimately driven by one person's refusal to accept justice and restraint. The verse therefore carries a moral tension: loyalty is admirable, but loyalty given to a disordered cause becomes tragic.

For the reader, this is an important lesson in the ethics of allegiance. Courage by itself is not enough. Skill, sacrifice, and devotion are not automatically righteous. They depend on what they serve. The Gita repeatedly asks not only whether one acts bravely, but whether one acts in harmony with dharma.

Psychologically, Duryodhana is still trying to reinforce certainty. He wants to hear and speak the language of loyalty because it strengthens his sense of command. Yet the very need to assert this reveals insecurity beneath the surface. Truly secure power does not need constant verbal reassurance.

This verse deepens the opening chapter's realism. Armies are made not only of ideals, but of men willing to risk everything under the influence of loyalty, honor, command, and personal obligation. The tragedy is that such noble capacities can be drawn into an unjust cause.

original_script:
अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम्।

पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम्।।1.10।।
transliteration:
aparyāptaṁ tadasmākaṁ balaṁ bhīṣhmābhirakṣhitam
paryāptaṁ tvidameteṣhāṁ balaṁ bhīmābhirakṣhitam
word_meanings: aparyāptam—limitless (or insufficient in fear); tat—that; asmākam—ours; balam—strength; bhīṣhma—by Grandsire Bheeshma; abhirakṣhitam—safely marshalled; paryāptam—limited (or sufficient in fear); tu—but; idam—this; eteṣhām—their; balam—strength; bhīma—Bheem; abhirakṣhitam—carefully marshalled
translation_english: Our strength, protected by Bhishma, seems limitless; their strength, protected by Bhima, seems limited.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana compares the two armies and says that his own force, protected by Bhishma, is unlimited, while the Pandava force, protected by Bhima, is limited. The exact military truth of this statement is less important than its psychological function. He is speaking to encourage his side, strengthen morale, and present confidence where doubt may be rising.

Bhishma and Bhima are not chosen randomly in this comparison. Bhishma represents supreme experience, elder authority, and nearly unmatched battlefield prestige. Bhima represents raw power, ferocity, and the fearsome strength of the Pandava cause. By setting them against one another, Duryodhana frames the battle through emblematic champions.

Yet his language is likely exaggerated. When someone feels pressure, speech often becomes more absolute. To say "our strength is limitless" is not sober military analysis. It is morale-building rhetoric. In that sense, this verse reveals as much about Duryodhana's mind as about the armies themselves. He is fighting fear with confident language.

For a new reader, this verse helps clarify how the Gita portrays human beings under strain. Pride and anxiety can coexist. A person may sound bold and still be inwardly unsettled. This is especially true in moments of high consequence.

The verse also hints at a deeper truth: worldly power often appears immense when measured externally, but that does not decide righteousness. Numbers, prestige, and famous protectors matter, yet they are not the final measure of victory. The Gita will gradually move the reader from outer measures of strength to inner measures such as clarity, surrender, and alignment with dharma.

original_script:
अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिताः।

भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्तः सर्व एव हि।।1.11।।
transliteration:
ayaneṣhu cha sarveṣhu yathā-bhāgamavasthitāḥ
bhīṣhmamevābhirakṣhantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi
word_meanings: ayaneṣhu—at the strategic points; cha—also; sarveṣhu—all; yathā-bhāgam—in respective position; avasthitāḥ—situated; bhīṣhmam—to Grandsire Bheeshma; eva—only; abhirakṣhantu—defend; bhavantaḥ—you; sarve—all; eva hi—even as
translation_english: Therefore, stationed at your strategic points, all of you must support and protect Bhishma.
deep_dive_text:
Duryodhana instructs all the warriors to support Bhishma carefully from their assigned positions. This command shows how central Bhishma is to the Kaurava side. He is not merely one great warrior among many. He is a pillar of morale, legitimacy, and psychological stability for the entire army.

Bhishma's presence matters at several levels. As the grandsire of the Kuru family, he embodies lineage and continuity. As a warrior, he commands fear and respect. As an elder, he gives the Kaurava cause an appearance of dignity, even though the moral truth of the conflict is more complicated. Duryodhana understands that if Bhishma stands firm and remains visibly supported, the army's confidence will remain strong.

This verse also reveals something about leadership under threat. When people feel uncertain, they often concentrate emotional strength around a symbolic figure. Protecting that figure becomes more than a tactical matter; it becomes a way of protecting collective confidence. Duryodhana is therefore thinking strategically, but also psychologically.

For the reader, the verse shows how much human communities depend on visible anchors. Families, organizations, kingdoms, and armies all look for persons who seem to carry order and continuity. But the Gita will eventually show that no external figure, however great, can substitute for inner clarity and divine guidance.

There is also quiet irony here. Bhishma is deeply honorable, yet his honor is serving a damaged political order. The chapter repeatedly places noble persons inside painful moral complexity. That complexity is what makes Arjuna's later crisis understandable. Duty is not unfolding against obvious strangers, but in the shadow of beloved and revered men such as Bhishma.

original_script:
तस्य संजनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्धः पितामहः।

सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चैः शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान्।।1.12।।
transliteration:
tasya sañjanayan harṣhaṁ kuru-vṛiddhaḥ pitāmahaḥ
siṁha-nādaṁ vinadyochchaiḥ śhaṅkhaṁ dadhmau pratāpavān
word_meanings: tasya—his; sañjanayan—causing; harṣham—joy; kuru-vṛiddhaḥ—the grand old man of the Kuru dynasty (Bheeshma); pitāmahaḥ—Grandsire; sinha-nādam—lion’s roar; vinadya—sounding; uchchaiḥ—very loudly; śhaṅkham—conch shell; dadhmau—blew; pratāpa-vān—the glorious
translation_english: Then Bhishma, the grandsire of the Kurus, roared like a lion and blew his conch loudly, delighting Duryodhana.
deep_dive_text:
Bhishma, the mighty grandsire of the Kuru dynasty, responds by blowing his conch with a lion-like roar, filling Duryodhana with joy. This is an important moment because Bhishma's action does more than signal readiness for war. It restores confidence to the Kaurava side. The sound of his conch functions like a public declaration: the elder stands firm, the army is not shaken, and battle will proceed.

Bhishma's stature makes this act especially powerful. He is not only a commander but a moral and ancestral presence. His support lends emotional force to the army. Duryodhana's joy reveals how much he depends on Bhishma's visible commitment. The prince may command outwardly, but his inner confidence is strengthened by the elder's response.

The image of a lion-like roar suggests courage, authority, and commanding vitality. Sound is crucial in epic scenes of battle because it carries energy into the hearts of listeners. Before weapons clash, sound shapes emotion. Bhishma's conch therefore becomes an instrument of morale, unity, and public resolve.

For the reader, this verse shows how leadership is often enacted through symbolic gestures. A single visible act from a respected elder can steady thousands. But it also deepens the tragedy of the scene. Bhishma is noble, disciplined, and worthy of reverence, yet he stands on the side that has tolerated injustice. The Gita repeatedly forces the reader to face this uncomfortable fact: greatness of person does not always mean clarity of moral position.

That is why the coming teaching cannot rest on outer honor alone. It will have to go deeper, into the nature of duty itself.

original_script:
ततः शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखाः।

सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत्।।1.13।।
transliteration:
tataḥ śhaṅkhāśhcha bheryaśhcha paṇavānaka-gomukhāḥ
sahasaivābhyahanyanta sa śhabdastumulo ’bhavat
word_meanings: tataḥ—thereafter; śhaṅkhāḥ—conches; cha—and; bheryaḥ—bugles; cha—and; paṇava-ānaka—drums and kettledrums; go-mukhāḥ—trumpets; sahasā—suddenly; eva—indeed; abhyahanyanta—blared forth; saḥ—that; śhabdaḥ—sound; tumulaḥ—overwhelming; abhavat—was
translation_english: Immediately conches, drums, kettledrums, trumpets, and horns sounded together with a tremendous uproar.
deep_dive_text:
After Bhishma sounds his conch, many other instruments suddenly erupt on the Kaurava side: conches, drums, kettledrums, trumpets, and horns, producing a tremendous noise. The scene is vivid and forceful. War announces itself not first through bloodshed but through rising sound, collective energy, and the stirring of mass emotion.

This verse captures the outer intensity of battle. Noise here is not just volume. It is a gathering of fear, pride, excitement, loyalty, and aggression. Thousands of minds are being drawn into one event. The instruments create a shared field of feeling in which individual hesitation is swallowed by collective momentum.

For a new reader, this helps explain why the Bhagavad Gita begins amid such theatrical force. Spiritual teaching does not descend into a quiet monastery. It arises in the midst of noise, urgency, spectacle, and impending violence. Human beings often seek wisdom only when ordinary emotional momentum can no longer carry them safely.

The verse also invites reflection on how easily collective sound and movement can overwhelm personal conscience. In crowds, armies, and public conflict, people often gain confidence from participation itself. The noise becomes part of the force pushing them forward. Yet inner truth may still remain unaddressed.

This makes the later stillness between Krishna and Arjuna even more striking. The Gita will create a zone of clarity inside this loud world. The outer battlefield roars, but the central teaching will occur through conversation, discrimination, and inward awakening. This contrast between noise outside and insight inside is one of the chapter's most powerful dramatic features.

original_script:
ततः श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ।

माधवः पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतुः।।1.14।।
transliteration:
tataḥ śhvetairhayairyukte mahati syandane sthitau
mādhavaḥ pāṇḍavaśhchaiva divyau śhaṅkhau pradadhmatuḥ
word_meanings: tataḥ—then; śhvetaiḥ—by white; hayaiḥ—horses; yukte—yoked; mahati—glorious; syandane—chariot; sthitau—seated; mādhavaḥ—Shree Krishna; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjun; cha—and; eva—also; divyau—Divine; śhaṅkhau—conch shells; pradadhmatuḥ—blew
translation_english: Then Krishna and Arjuna, seated in their splendid chariot drawn by white horses, blew their divine conches.
deep_dive_text:
Then Krishna and Arjuna, standing in their great chariot drawn by white horses, sound their divine conches. This moment creates a deliberate contrast with the previous uproar. The Kaurava side produced a vast and thunderous collective noise, but now the text narrows our focus to two central figures whose presence will define the entire Gita.

Krishna is still outwardly acting as Arjuna's charioteer, and Arjuna is still outwardly one warrior among many. Yet the scene already signals that something far greater is about to unfold. The white horses suggest purity, clarity, and controlled strength. Their chariot is not merely a vehicle of war; it is the setting for revelation.

The fact that Krishna and Arjuna sound their conches together matters deeply. Their bond is one of companionship, trust, and divine-human partnership. The Gita will show that Arjuna's crisis is not solved by force alone, but by guidance. This moment visually and symbolically places Krishna beside him before the teaching begins.

For the reader, the verse marks a transition from general battlefield description to the inner center of the epic. The armies matter, but now attention begins to gather around the relationship that will carry the whole scripture. Krishna is not a distant God speaking from heaven. He is present in the struggle, near the confused human being, sharing the battlefield.

That nearness is one of the Gita's defining gifts. Divine wisdom does not wait for perfect conditions. It enters directly into the dust, noise, danger, and emotional burden of life.

original_script:
पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनंजयः।

पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः।।1.15।।
transliteration:
pāñchajanyaṁ hṛiṣhīkeśho devadattaṁ dhanañjayaḥ
pauṇḍraṁ dadhmau mahā-śhaṅkhaṁ bhīma-karmā vṛikodaraḥ
word_meanings: pāñchajanyam—the conch named Panchajanya; hṛiṣhīka-īśhaḥ—Shree Krishna; devadattam—the conch named Devadutta; dhanam-jayaḥ—Arjun; pauṇḍram—the conch named Paundra; dadhmau—blew; mahā-śhaṅkham—mighty conch; bhīma-karmā—Bheem; vṛika-udaraḥ—the voracious eater
translation_english: Krishna blew Panchajanya, Arjuna blew Devadatta, and mighty Bhima blew his great conch Paundra.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna blows his conch Panchajanya, Arjuna blows Devadatta, and the mighty Bhima sounds his great conch Paundra. The verse names the conches individually, giving the moment dignity and particularity. These are not anonymous signals. Each sound carries identity, character, and symbolic force.

Krishna's presence sanctifies the scene at a level deeper than battle ritual. When he sounds Panchajanya, the battlefield is no longer merely political ground. Divine participation has become audible. Arjuna's Devadatta expresses readiness, discipline, and heroic purpose. Bhima's mighty conch fits his nature: bold, forceful, and impossible to ignore. Together the three sounds present a striking combination of divine guidance, trained excellence, and raw power.

For a new reader, the naming of these conches may seem like ornament, but it serves an important literary and spiritual purpose. Epic tradition often gives names to important weapons and objects because they carry memory and character. The battlefield is full of persons whose identities matter. Naming intensifies presence.

This verse also strengthens the Pandava side emotionally. Unlike Duryodhana's earlier attempt to build confidence through speech, the Pandava answer comes through the embodied presence of Krishna, Arjuna, and Bhima. Their response does not feel anxious. It feels grounded.

At a deeper level, the verse suggests that righteousness is not weakness. The side of dharma is not timid, passive, or unprepared. It has force, resolve, and divine companionship. The moral seriousness of the Gita never removes the reality of courage. Rather, it seeks to purify courage by aligning it with truth.

original_script:
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिरः।

नकुलः सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ।।1.16।।
transliteration:
anantavijayaṁ rājā kuntī-putro yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ
nakulaḥ sahadevaśhcha sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau
word_meanings: ananta-vijayam—Anantavijay; rājā—king; kuntī-putraḥ—son of Kunti; yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ—Yudhishthir; nakulaḥ—Nakul; sahadevaḥ—Sahadev; cha—and; sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau—the conches Sughosh and Manipushpak
translation_english: King Yudhishthira blew Anantavijaya, while Nakula and Sahadeva blew Sughosha and Manipushpaka.
deep_dive_text:
King Yudhishthira, Nakula, and Sahadeva also blow their conches, adding their voices to the sacred and martial declaration of the Pandava side. This verse is important because it broadens the focus from a few central heroes to the unity of the five brothers. The Pandavas are not simply associated by blood. They stand together in purpose.

Yudhishthira's presence matters especially. He represents lawful rule, restraint, and fidelity to dharma. His participation reminds the reader that the Pandava cause is not built only on martial pride. It is linked to justice, rightful order, and the attempt to restore what was taken through deceit. Nakula and Sahadeva add completeness to the scene. They are sometimes less dramatic than Arjuna or Bhima in the narrative, but their loyalty and presence are essential.

The sounding of their conches expresses solidarity. No one stands alone. Before the moral crisis of Arjuna emerges, the chapter first shows the strength and completeness of the side to which he belongs. This matters because his later collapse is not caused by military fear. He is not shrinking because his side is weak. He is shrinking because he sees too deeply the human cost of what must happen.

For the reader, this verse also highlights a broader truth: righteous action often requires collective steadiness. Dharma is not always carried by isolated heroism. It is often upheld by communities, families, and shared fidelity. The Gita's teaching to Arjuna is intensely personal, but it unfolds within a web of loyal relationships already visible on the field.

original_script:
काश्यश्च परमेष्वासः शिखण्डी च महारथः।

धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजितः।।1.17।।
transliteration:
kāśhyaśhcha parameṣhvāsaḥ śhikhaṇḍī cha mahā-rathaḥ
dhṛiṣhṭadyumno virāṭaśhcha sātyakiśh chāparājitaḥ
word_meanings: kāśhyaḥ—King of Kashi; parama-iṣhu-āsaḥ—excellent archer; śhikhaṇḍī—Shikhandi; mahā-rathaḥ—mighty warrior; dhṛiṣhṭadyumnaḥ—Dhrishtadyumna; virāṭaḥ—Virat; sātyakiḥ—Satyaki; aparājitaḥ—invincible
translation_english: The king of Kashi, the great archer, Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virata, and undefeated Satyaki also blew their conches.
deep_dive_text:
Other great warriors and allied kings on the Pandava side also sound their conches, strengthening the impression of a united and morally serious force. The cumulative effect of these verses is deliberate. The Pandava army is not held together by accident or by one charismatic leader alone. It is supported by many individuals of rank, courage, and conviction.

This collective sound carries a different weight from mere noise. It feels like a declaration of readiness joined to a sense of purpose. The Pandava side is not portrayed as perfect, but it is portrayed as ordered and supported by respected allies who have chosen to stand with it. That choice suggests that the conflict has already been morally judged by many observers beyond the immediate family.

For a first-time reader, this helps explain why Arjuna's coming despair is so significant. He is not someone abandoned by allies or trapped in hopeless weakness. He stands amid support, strength, and legitimacy. His crisis therefore cannot be dismissed as cowardice. It arises from the clash between duty and compassion, justice and affection, war and kinship.

This verse also adds to the sacred atmosphere of the opening scene. The sounding of many conches transforms the battlefield into a space charged with intention. In epic terms, such collective declaration marks the seriousness of the event. In spiritual terms, it underscores that great turning points in life are rarely private. They are surrounded by histories, communities, witnesses, and consequences.

The Gita's teaching will speak to an individual heart, but it will do so in the full presence of a world already gathered around that heart's decision.

original_script:
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वशः पृथिवीपते।

सौभद्रश्च महाबाहुः शङ्खान्दध्मुः पृथक्पृथक्।।1.18।।
transliteration:
drupado draupadeyāśhcha sarvaśhaḥ pṛithivī-pate
saubhadraśhcha mahā-bāhuḥ śhaṅkhāndadhmuḥ pṛithak pṛithak
word_meanings: drupadaḥ—Drupad; draupadeyāḥ—sons of Draupadi; pṛithivī-pate—Ruler of the earth; saubhadraḥ—Abhimanyu; mahā-bāhuḥ—mighty-armed; śhaṅkhān—conch shells; pṛithak pṛithak—individually
translation_english: Drupada, the sons of Draupadi, and the strong-armed son of Subhadra all blew their conches from every side.
deep_dive_text:
The sound of these conches echoes through earth and sky and shakes the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons. This is one of the first clear signs that the Kaurava side, despite its strength, is not inwardly at peace. Sound becomes a mirror of conscience. The Pandava declaration is not merely loud; it is unsettling.

Why are their hearts shaken? At one level, it is the natural fear of war. At another level, the verse suggests something deeper. Those who stand on unstable moral ground may still possess power, but power does not erase inward disturbance. The heart often knows what pride refuses to admit. The Kauravas have force, elders, and numbers, yet they are not free from the tremor that comes when one confronts the consequences of injustice.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it reveals that the battle is already psychological and moral before it becomes physical. The real field of conflict includes the mind. Fear, guilt, confidence, and conscience all shape what follows.

The language of earth and sky also enlarges the scale of the moment. This is not a small family dispute enclosed within palace walls. It is an event whose effects seem to reverberate through the whole order of things. The war matters because dharma itself has been strained.

The verse quietly prepares us for Arjuna's own shaking in the verses ahead. First the Kaurava hearts tremble under the sound of righteous opposition. Soon Arjuna's heart will tremble for another reason entirely: not from guilty insecurity, but from sorrow at the cost of duty. The Gita carefully distinguishes these inner movements.

original_script:
स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत्।

नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलो व्यनुनादयन्।।1.19।।
transliteration:
sa ghoṣho dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇāṁ hṛidayāni vyadārayat
nabhaśhcha pṛithivīṁ chaiva tumulo nunādayan
word_meanings: saḥ—that; ghoṣhaḥ—sound; dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇām—of Dhritarashtra’s sons; hṛidayāni—hearts; vyadārayat—shattered; nabhaḥ—the sky; pṛithivīm—the earth; tumulaḥ—terrific sound; abhyanunādayan—thundering
translation_english: That thunderous sound shook earth and sky and pierced the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons.
deep_dive_text:
The conches of the Pandava side resound with such force that they pierce the hearts of Dhritarashtra's sons. The verse restates and intensifies the emotional effect of the previous moment. The issue is not physical damage, but inner disturbance. Something about the Pandava declaration carries the weight of legitimacy, resolve, and divine support, and that combination is hard for the Kaurava side to hear without inward reaction.

In epic narrative, sound often signals power. Here it also signals truth. The Kauravas are not merely hearing opposing instruments. They are hearing the approach of consequences. Their hearts are struck because war is no longer an abstraction. The moral disorder that has long been building now stands before them in audible form.

This verse is useful for understanding one of the Gita's repeated insights: external situations affect us differently depending on the state of our inner life. The same battlefield sound that strengthens one side disturbs the other. The event is shared, but the experience of it differs according to conscience, attachment, and moral condition.

For the reader, this also deepens the dramatic structure. The chapter is not rushing toward philosophy. It is carefully layering emotional and symbolic conditions so that Arjuna's later collapse will emerge in a fully charged setting. Fear has already touched one side. Soon compassion and confusion will overcome the other.

The battlefield, then, becomes a place where inner truth is exposed. Under pressure, the heart reveals its condition. That is one reason the Gita remains universally relevant. Kurukshetra is not only an ancient field. It is any moment when reality presses so strongly that hidden motives and unresolved truths can no longer remain concealed.

original_script:
अथ व्यवस्थितान् दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान्कपिध्वजः।

प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसंपाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः।।1.20।।
transliteration:
atha vyavasthitān dṛiṣhṭvā dhārtarāṣhṭrān kapi-dhwajaḥ
pravṛitte śhastra-sampāte dhanurudyamya pāṇḍavaḥ
word_meanings: atha—thereupon; vyavasthitān—arrayed; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—Dhritarashtra’s sons; kapi-dwajaḥ—the Monkey Bannered; pravṛitte—about to commence; śhastra-sampāte—to use weapons; dhanuḥ—bow; udyamya—taking up; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjun
translation_english: At that moment, Arjuna, whose banner bore Hanuman, lifted his bow and prepared to speak to Krishna.
deep_dive_text:
As the battle is about to begin, Arjuna, whose banner bears Hanuman, lifts his bow and prepares to act. Yet at that very moment he turns to Krishna and asks that the chariot be placed between the two armies. This is a decisive transition in the chapter. Until now the movement has been outward: armies assembling, warriors named, conches sounding, emotions rising. With Arjuna's request, the narrative begins turning inward.

The detail about Hanuman on his banner is significant. Hanuman symbolizes courage, devotion, strength, and service. Arjuna is not introduced here as a weak or hesitant person. He is a heroic warrior at the height of his ability, supported by sacred association and military readiness. This matters because the crisis that follows is not born of incompetence. It arises in a capable person who is suddenly forced to look deeply at the human meaning of action.

His request to stop between the armies seems simple, but it is spiritually profound. Before acting, he wants to see. He does not want to rush blindly into battle as if duty were purely mechanical. This desire to look directly at reality is noble, yet it will also open the door to intense pain. True moral awareness often begins when one stops acting automatically and allows oneself to face the full weight of the situation.

For a new reader, this verse marks the threshold of the Bhagavad Gita proper. The outward war has already begun in appearance, but now the inward war is about to emerge. Arjuna's question is the first major movement toward the dialogue that will transform the entire battlefield into a place of revelation.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते।

सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत।।1.21।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
senayor ubhayor madhye rathaṁ sthāpaya me ’chyuta
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—both; madhye—in the middle; ratham—chariot; sthāpaya—place; me—my; achyuta—Shree Krishna, the infallible One
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Achyuta, place my chariot between the two armies.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks Krishna to place the chariot between the two armies so that he may look upon those gathered there, eager for war. This request continues the inward turn that began in the previous verse. Outwardly, Arjuna is still acting like a disciplined warrior surveying the field. Inwardly, however, he is moving toward a confrontation not with strategy alone, but with human reality.

The wording matters. He does not simply ask where the weak points in the enemy line are, or which formation should be attacked first. He wants to see the people assembled there. This is the beginning of moral sight. Until now, the battlefield has been described in terms of armies, leaders, formations, sounds, and collective force. Arjuna now narrows the focus from masses to persons.

There is courage in this request. Many people act most easily when they do not look too closely at what their actions will cost. Arjuna does the opposite. He pauses and asks for direct vision. This is spiritually important because the Gita does not begin with blind obedience. It begins with a person who refuses to let action remain superficial.

For a new reader, this verse is the real threshold of Arjuna's crisis. He is not yet broken, but he is stepping toward the experience that will break his old way of seeing. Duty is about to become painful because it will no longer be framed abstractly. He will have to face teachers, elders, cousins, and friends as individuals, not as a military category.

That is often how real ethical struggle begins in life. A principle may seem clear from a distance, but when actual people come into view, the heart is shaken. Arjuna's request places him exactly at that point.

original_script:
यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान्।

कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन्रणसमुद्यमे।।1.22।।
transliteration:
yāvadetān nirīkṣhe ’haṁ yoddhu-kāmān avasthitān
kairmayā saha yoddhavyam asmin raṇa-samudyame
word_meanings: yāvat—as many as; etān—these; nirīkṣhe—look; aham—I; yoddhu-kāmān—for battle; avasthitān—arrayed; kaiḥ—with whom; mayā—by me; saha—together; yoddhavyam—must fight; asmin—in this; raṇa-samudyame—great combat
translation_english: Let me see those who stand here eager to fight and with whom I must battle in this great war.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says he wants to see those who stand here desiring battle, those with whom he must fight in this great undertaking of war. The verse reveals both seriousness and innocence. He still speaks in the language of duty and battle, but he is no longer looking at war only as an event to be managed. He wants to know exactly whom he must face.

The phrase "with whom I must fight" carries weight. It expresses obligation, not enthusiasm. Arjuna is not speaking as someone intoxicated by violence. He recognizes that he stands under the demands of his role as a warrior, but he also senses the gravity of those demands. By asking to see the people involved, he is preparing himself to act with awareness rather than reflex.

This request is also psychologically revealing. Human beings often imagine difficult action in general terms until they are forced to connect it with concrete persons. Only then does the emotional truth appear. Arjuna is now moving from concept to contact. That movement is essential to the drama of the Gita.

For a new reader, this verse helps explain why the coming breakdown is not weakness but depth. Arjuna is not shrinking from danger alone. He is shrinking from the thought of what righteous action may require when righteousness has been violated within one's own family and social world. He is seeing that war, even when unavoidable, does not stop being tragic.

The verse also points to a larger spiritual lesson. It is easier to talk about principle than to live it when human cost becomes visible. Arjuna's honesty in asking to see fully is what opens the door for Krishna's teaching. Without that honesty, the Gita would not happen.

original_script:
योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागताः।

धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षवः।।1.23।।
transliteration:
yotsyamānān avekṣhe ’haṁ ya ete ’tra samāgatāḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrasya durbuddher yuddhe priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ
word_meanings: yotsyamānān—those who come to fight; avekṣhe aham—I desire to see; ye—who; ete—those; atra—here; samāgatāḥ—assembled; dhārtarāṣhṭrasya—of Dhritarashtra’s son; durbuddheḥ—evil-minded; yuddhe—in the fight; priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ—wishing to please
translation_english: I wish to observe those gathered here to please the evil-minded son of Dhritarashtra.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks to behold those who have assembled to please the evil-minded son of Dhritarashtra. This statement shows that he has not forgotten the deeper moral structure of the conflict. He knows that the war has not arisen from a trivial disagreement. It has grown out of Duryodhana's greed, envy, stubbornness, and refusal of rightful settlement.

At the same time, the verse contains a subtle tension. Arjuna recognizes Duryodhana as the driving force of injustice, yet he also knows that many standing with him are not simple embodiments of evil. Some are bound by loyalty, some by debt, some by family duty, some by political circumstance, and some by moral confusion. That tension is crucial. One can condemn the root of a disorder while still grieving the many people entangled in it.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it shows that Arjuna's later sorrow does not arise from moral uncertainty about Duryodhana's conduct. He is not suddenly deciding that the Kaurava cause is righteous. His crisis is more difficult than that. He knows the injustice, yet he is overwhelmed by the human cost of correcting it through war.

This is one of the Gita's deepest insights into ethical life: clarity about wrong does not automatically remove sorrow about the means required to address it. Mature action often involves this painful double awareness. One can know that something must be opposed and still grieve what opposition will destroy.

Arjuna's words therefore prepare the ground for a spiritual problem that remains universal. The challenge is not merely to identify evil, but to know how to stand against it without losing the heart in the process.

original_script:
संजय उवाच

एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत।

सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम्।।1.24।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam ukto hṛiṣhīkeśho guḍākeśhena bhārata
senayor ubhayor madhye sthāpayitvā rathottamam
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam—thus; uktaḥ—addressed; hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ—Shree Krishna; guḍākeśhena—by Arjun; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—the two; madhye—between; sthāpayitvā—having drawn; ratha-uttamam—magnificent chariot
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Addressed by Arjuna, Krishna drove the excellent chariot to the space between both armies.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya says that, having been addressed by Arjuna, Krishna drove the splendid chariot into the space between the two armies. This action appears simple, but it carries deep significance. Krishna does not interrupt Arjuna, dismiss his request, or rush him into battle. He grants exactly what is being asked: a clear view.

This reveals an important trait of Krishna as teacher. He does not begin by giving doctrine before the student is inwardly ready to receive it. First he allows Arjuna to confront reality fully. Spiritual guidance in the Gita is not imposed prematurely. Krishna places Arjuna where truth will become unavoidable.

The phrase describing the chariot as splendid also matters. The vehicle between the two armies becomes the place from which the highest teaching of the Gita will be spoken. In symbolic terms, the chariot stands between action and reflection, force and wisdom, human confusion and divine guidance. It is the meeting point of crisis and revelation.

For a new reader, this verse shows that divine help is not always expressed by immediately removing difficulty. Often it appears by placing us exactly where we must see what we have been unwilling or unable to face. Krishna honors Arjuna enough to let his crisis ripen honestly.

This is a profound spiritual pattern. Clarity often begins not when pain is prevented, but when reality is brought into view in such a way that evasion is no longer possible. Krishna's movement of the chariot is therefore both physical and pedagogical. He is positioning Arjuna for truth, even though truth will first wound before it heals.

original_script:
भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम्।

उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति।।1.25।।
transliteration:
bhīṣhma-droṇa-pramukhataḥ sarveṣhāṁ cha mahī-kṣhitām
uvācha pārtha paśhyaitān samavetān kurūn iti
word_meanings: bhīṣhma—Grandsire Bheeshma; droṇa—Dronacharya; pramukhataḥ—in the presence; sarveṣhām—all; cha—and; mahī-kṣhitām—other kings; uvācha—said; pārtha—Arjun; paśhya—behold; etān—these; samavetān—gathered; kurūn—descendants of Kuru; iti—thus
translation_english: Before Bhishma, Drona, and all the kings, Krishna said: O Partha, behold these Kurus assembled for battle.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna places the chariot in front of Bhishma, Drona, and the other rulers and tells Arjuna to behold the Kurus assembled there. This is a compassionate yet uncompromising act. Krishna does not place the chariot before anonymous soldiers. He places it directly before the very people whose presence will most deeply shake Arjuna: revered elders, beloved teachers, and members of the same family line.

Bhishma and Drona are especially important. Bhishma is the grandsire, a figure of immense dignity, sacrifice, and authority. Drona is Arjuna's own teacher in warfare. By positioning the chariot before them, Krishna ensures that Arjuna will not experience the war as a matter of abstract justice alone. He will experience it as a collision between duty and reverence.

Krishna's instruction, "Behold," is direct. He does not soften the situation. The spiritual path in the Gita begins with looking. Before there can be detachment, there must be honest perception. Before there can be wisdom, there must be the courage to see what one would rather avoid.

For the reader, this verse clarifies why Arjuna's collapse is so devastating. He is not merely facing enemies. He is facing the visible forms of everything he has loved and honored within his own social world. That is what makes his confusion so human and so powerful.

This verse also teaches something enduring: divine guidance is not sentimental. Krishna loves Arjuna, but his love does not protect him from truth. Instead, it leads him into truth so that a deeper freedom can become possible. The healing of the Gita will begin from this uncompromising sight.

original_script:
तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान्पार्थः पितॄनथ पितामहान्।

आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातॄन्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा।।1.26।।
transliteration:
tatrāpaśhyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝīn atha pitāmahān
āchāryān mātulān bhrātṝīn putrān pautrān sakhīṁs tathā
word_meanings: tatra—there; apaśhyat—saw; sthitān—stationed; pārthaḥ—Arjun; pitṝīn—fathers; atha—thereafter; pitāmahān—grandfathers; āchāryān—teachers; mātulān—maternal uncles; bhrātṝīn—brothers; putrān—sons; pautrān—grandsons; sakhīn—friends; tathā—also
translation_english: There Arjuna saw fathers, grandfathers, teachers, uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, and friends on both sides.
deep_dive_text:
There Arjuna sees fathers, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, friends, fathers-in-law, and well-wishers standing in both armies. This is the moment when the battlefield ceases to be merely political and becomes painfully personal. The categories listed here cover almost the entire structure of human relationship. Family, mentorship, friendship, marriage alliance, generational continuity, and social affection are all present before him.

The verse is intentionally crowded with relation. Arjuna does not first see armor, rank, or tactical formations. He sees people through bonds of memory and obligation. That is why his heart begins to fail. War may require seeing an opponent as a target, but compassion sees a person in context, with history, affection, and shared life. Arjuna is suddenly unable to suppress that second kind of sight.

This is essential for understanding the Gita. Arjuna's crisis is not caused by lack of courage. It is caused by the collapse of distance. He cannot reduce those before him to impersonal categories. The people he must fight are woven into his own life.

For a new reader, this verse explains why the opening chapter is called the yoga of Arjuna's despair. His sorrow is not random weakness. It is born from moral sensitivity. He sees that the restoration of justice will demand a terrible rupture in the very network of relationships that shaped him.

This verse also carries a timeless human truth. The hardest decisions are often not those involving strangers, but those involving people we love, respect, or have grown up with. Duty becomes painful when it must move through kinship. Arjuna's vision now becomes the gateway through which the entire Gita must speak.

original_script:
श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि।

तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेयः सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान्।।1.27।।
transliteration:
śhvaśhurān suhṛidaśh chaiva senayor ubhayor api
tān samīkṣhya sa kaunteyaḥ sarvān bandhūn avasthitān
word_meanings: śhvaśhurān—fathers-in-law; suhṛidaḥ—well-wishers; cha—and; eva—indeed; senayoḥ—armies; ubhayoḥ—in both; api—also; tān—these; samīkṣhya—on seeing; saḥ—he; kaunteyaḥ—Arjun; sarvān—all; bandhūn—relatives; avasthitān—present
translation_english: Seeing all his relatives arrayed there, Arjuna was overcome with deep compassion and sorrow.
deep_dive_text:
When Arjuna sees all these relatives and loved ones standing arrayed for battle, he is overcome by deep compassion and sorrow. This is the emotional turning point of the chapter. What was previously tension and seriousness now becomes open inner collapse. The warrior who came prepared for combat now finds himself pierced by tenderness.

The compassion described here is not false or trivial. It is real human feeling in the presence of impending destruction. Arjuna is not thinking first about his own danger, reputation, or victory. He is thinking about the people before him and what war will do to them. That is why his distress carries moral dignity even though Krishna will later challenge its conclusions.

At the same time, the verse marks the beginning of confusion. Compassion by itself is noble, but compassion without clarity can become paralyzing. Arjuna's feeling is true, yet he does not yet know how to integrate that truth with duty, justice, and the deeper structure of the soul. The Gita will not ask him to stop caring. It will ask him to care more deeply and more wisely.

For a new reader, this distinction matters. Krishna does not rebuke Arjuna because compassion is bad. He speaks because compassion alone, when mixed with attachment and grief, may fail to see the whole truth. Spiritual maturity must bring together heart and wisdom.

This verse therefore opens the central human problem of the Gita: how to act rightly without becoming hard, and how to love deeply without becoming incapacitated by sorrow. Arjuna is now caught between those two demands.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

कृपया परयाऽऽविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत्।

दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम्।।1.28।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kṛipayā parayāviṣhṭo viṣhīdann idam abravīt
dṛiṣhṭvemaṁ sva-janaṁ kṛiṣhṇa yuyutsuṁ samupasthitam
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; kṛipayā—by compassion; parayā—great; āviṣhṭaḥ—overwhelmed; viṣhīdan—grieving; idam—this; abravīt—spoke; dṛiṣhṭvā—on seeing; imam—these; sva-janam—kinsmen; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; yuyutsum—eager to fight; samupasthitam—present
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, seeing my own people eager to fight, my limbs fail and my mouth dries up.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says to Krishna that on seeing his own people assembled and eager for battle, his limbs fail and his mouth becomes dry. The crisis now enters his body. What was interior sorrow begins expressing itself physically. This is psychologically precise. In moments of intense moral shock, the body often reacts before thought becomes orderly. Strength drains, breath changes, and composure weakens.

The phrase "my own people" is especially important. Arjuna is not looking across the field and seeing a foreign army. He is seeing his own social world turned against itself. The war is fratricidal in the deepest sense. That is why his body responds so strongly. The field before him is not just dangerous. It is intimate.

For the reader, this verse signals that Arjuna's despair is total. It is not a passing mood that can be dismissed by simple encouragement. His identity as warrior, kinsman, student, and moral being is now colliding all at once. The body becomes the first witness to that collision.

The verse also shows the Gita's realism about emotion. Spiritual texts are sometimes imagined as if they bypass human vulnerability, but the Gita does the opposite. It allows the hero's breakdown to be visible in full detail. Wisdom will not arise by pretending the human instrument is unaffected. It will arise through meeting weakness honestly.

This verse is also deeply relatable. Many people know what it is like for a hard truth to become physically overwhelming. Arjuna's trembling is ancient, but it is also completely familiar. The Gita speaks so powerfully because it does not begin from an unreal image of human control.

original_script:
सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति।

वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते।।1.29।।
transliteration:
sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṁ cha pariśhuṣhyati
vepathuśh cha śharīre me roma-harṣhaśh cha jāyate
word_meanings:
sīdanti—quivering; mama—my; gātrāṇi—limbs; mukham—mouth; cha—and; pariśhuṣhyati—is drying up
vepathuḥ—shuddering; cha—and; śharīre—on the body; me—my; roma-harṣhaḥ—standing of bodily hair on end; cha—also; jāyate—is happening
translation_english: My body trembles, my hair stands on end, and my skin burns.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna continues describing his condition: his body trembles, his hair stands on end, the Gandiva bow slips from his hand, and his skin burns. The great warrior's mastery is visibly unraveling. Each detail makes clear that this is no intellectual debate alone. His entire organism is in revolt against what he is being asked to do.

The mention of Gandiva is especially important. This is not just any bow; it is Arjuna's great weapon, a symbol of his identity, training, and warrior excellence. For it to slip from his hand means that his crisis has reached the very center of who he has believed himself to be. His professional, social, and heroic self-understanding is faltering.

The burning skin and standing hair convey both fear and shock. They show that Arjuna is confronting something that feels greater than ordinary danger. He is facing the collapse of a worldview in which duty once seemed straightforward. Until now, his strength as a warrior had given him direction. Now that same role feels unbearable.

For the reader, this verse shows that genuine moral conflict can destabilize even the most capable person. Skill does not eliminate vulnerability. Sometimes the stronger the role one has inhabited, the more painful it is when its demands become morally unbearable.

This verse also prepares the reader to understand why Krishna's response must go so deep. A few words of reassurance will not be enough. Arjuna is not merely frightened; he is existentially undone. The teaching that follows must reach into body, mind, soul, duty, and the nature of reality itself.

original_script:
गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चैव परिदह्यते।

न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मनः।।1.30।।
transliteration:
gāṇḍīvaṁ sraṁsate hastāt tvak chaiva paridahyate
na cha śhaknomy avasthātuṁ bhramatīva cha me manaḥ
word_meanings: gāṇḍīvam—Arjun’s bow; sraṁsate—is slipping; hastāt—from (my) hand; tvak—skin; cha—and; eva—indeed; paridahyate—is burning all over; na—not; cha—and; śhaknomi—am able; avasthātum—remain steady; bhramati iva—whirling like; cha—and; me—my; manaḥ—mind
translation_english: My bow Gandiva slips from my hand, my mind reels, and I can no longer stand steady.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says he can no longer stand firmly, his mind seems to reel, and he sees only adverse omens, O Krishna. The crisis that began in the body now overtakes perception and judgment. He is losing balance outwardly and inwardly. The world itself seems to him to be turning toward disaster.

When he says he sees adverse omens, he is not merely making a superstitious observation. He is expressing a mind that now interprets everything through foreboding. In moral crisis, perception changes. The person no longer feels anchored in confidence or clarity, and the surrounding world appears charged with threat. Arjuna's inner disorder becomes a lens through which the battlefield is seen.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it shows how quickly grief and confusion can move from emotion into interpretation. Once the mind begins to reel, signs everywhere seem to confirm disaster. This is a deeply human pattern. Under strain, perception is often colored by fear, attachment, and sorrow.

The verse also shows that Arjuna's problem is no longer tactical. He is not talking about whether the formation is favorable or whether victory is likely. He is speaking about his inability to remain inwardly steady. The real battlefield has shifted into consciousness.

This is why Krishna's later teaching on equanimity, vision, and Self-knowledge will be necessary. Arjuna does not merely need motivation. He needs a new ground of being from which he can see clearly again. Until that happens, even the external world appears to him as one continuous omen of ruin.

original_script:
निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव।

न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे।।1.31।।
transliteration:
nimittāni cha paśhyāmi viparītāni keśhava
na cha śhreyo ’nupaśhyāmi hatvā sva-janam āhave
word_meanings: nimittāni—omens; cha—and; paśhyāmi—I see; viparītāni—misfortune; keśhava—Shree Krishna; na—not; cha—also; śhreyaḥ—good; anupaśhyāmi—I foresee; hatvā—from killing; sva-janam—kinsmen; āhave—in battle
translation_english: I see inauspicious omens, O Keshava; I see no good in killing my own people in battle.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says he sees no good in killing his own people in battle. This is the beginning of his explicit argument against war. Until now he has described symptoms, sorrow, and disturbed perception. Now he starts drawing conclusions. His first conclusion is ethical and emotional: how can anything good come from the destruction of one's own kin?

This is a powerful question, and the Gita takes it seriously. Arjuna is not being shallow. He is recognizing that outward success can be hollow when purchased through unbearable loss. Victory, power, and survival do not automatically constitute good. If the means destroy the very human world those goals are supposed to serve, then success itself becomes suspect.

At the same time, the verse also shows the beginning of one-sidedness. Arjuna now focuses entirely on the evil of killing and not yet on the evil of allowing unrighteousness to prevail unchecked. His compassion is genuine, but his vision is narrowing under grief. He sees one side of the moral truth very intensely and the other side dimly.

For a new reader, this verse is crucial because it frames one of the Gita's central tensions: when apparent compassion resists difficult duty, how should one think? The scripture does not answer by dismissing compassion. It answers by asking whether compassion has become entangled with attachment, fear, and aversion to painful responsibility.

Arjuna's words remain deeply human and compelling. Many people know what it means to look at a painful action and ask, "What real good can come from this?" The Gita will honor that question while also pushing beyond its first emotional form.

original_script:
न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च।

किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा।।1.32।।
transliteration:
na kāṅkṣhe vijayaṁ kṛiṣhṇa na cha rājyaṁ sukhāni cha
kiṁ no rājyena govinda kiṁ bhogair jīvitena vā
word_meanings: na—nor; kāṅkṣhe—do I desire; vijayam—victory; kṛiṣhṇa—Krishna; na—nor; cha—as well; rājyam—kingdom; sukhāni—happiness; cha—also; kim—what; naḥ—to us; rājyena—by kingdom; govinda—Krishna; kim—what?; bhogaiḥ—pleasures; jīvitena—life; vā—or
translation_english: I do not desire victory, kingdom, or pleasures, O Krishna.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna tells Krishna that he does not desire victory, kingdom, or pleasures. This is a striking statement because these are precisely the kinds of aims that often drive warfare. Arjuna is saying that even if worldly success lies before him, it no longer appears meaningful in the face of who must be destroyed to obtain it.

This verse exposes the emptiness of ambition when divorced from relationship and moral wholeness. A kingdom is valuable only because of the human and ethical order it is meant to sustain. Pleasure is worth having only if life retains integrity. Victory loses its luster when it must stand on the graves of those whose presence gave life structure, affection, and meaning.

For the reader, this verse can sound spiritually elevated, and in one sense it is. Arjuna's detachment from worldly reward is real. He is not consumed by crude greed. Yet Krishna will later show that renunciation born from sorrow is not the same as renunciation born from wisdom. One may reject pleasure and power not because one has transcended them, but because grief has made them unbearable.

That distinction matters. The Gita is not satisfied with despair-based withdrawal. It seeks luminous understanding. Arjuna's rejection of kingdom and pleasure contains truth, but not yet complete truth.

Still, the verse has great power. It reminds the reader that external reward is never the final measure of meaningful action. When the soul is troubled, worldly gains can lose all taste. This is one reason the Gita remains relevant beyond war. Many people reach moments where success itself feels empty, and they must ask what deeper good is actually worth living for.

original_script:
येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगाः सुखानि च।

त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च।।1.33।।
transliteration:
yeṣhām arthe kāṅkṣhitaṁ no rājyaṁ bhogāḥ sukhāni cha
ta ime ’vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṁs tyaktvā dhanāni cha
word_meanings: yeṣhām—for whose; arthe—sake; kāṅkṣhitam—coveted for; naḥ—by us; rājyam—kingdom; bhogāḥ—pleasures; sukhāni—happiness; cha—also; te—they; ime—these; avasthitāḥ—situated; yuddhe—for battle; prāṇān—lives; tyaktvā—giving up; dhanāni—wealth; cha—also
translation_english: What use is kingdom or enjoyment to us when those for whom we desire them stand here ready to die?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks what use kingdom, enjoyment, or even life itself can be when those for whose sake such things are desired stand here in battle. This verse deepens his argument by moving from personal disgust with power to relational meaning. He is saying, in effect: the value of life is inseparable from the people with whom life is shared.

This is emotionally and philosophically significant. Human beings do not desire wealth, position, or even survival in abstraction. They desire them within a web of belonging. Arjuna sees that if the web is torn apart, the goals themselves are emptied. Kingdom without kin becomes ashes; enjoyment without relationship becomes hollow.

For a new reader, this verse shows the nobility in Arjuna's distress. He is not self-centered. He is measuring value relationally, not selfishly. He is asking whether a life stripped of love, reverence, and family continuity can still be called a desirable life at all.

Yet again, the verse also reveals the narrowing influence of grief. Arjuna is considering what life is for, but he is not yet asking what life is in its deepest spiritual nature. He is measuring value by relational and social continuity, which is deeply human, but Krishna will soon take him beyond that level toward the immortality of the Self and the nature of duty grounded in something more enduring than worldly ties.

This verse therefore stands at a very important point in the chapter. It contains one of the strongest human objections to violent duty: what is the point of achieving anything if the people who make life meaningful are gone? The rest of the Gita will answer that question without dismissing its grief.

original_script:
आचार्याः पितरः पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहाः।

मातुलाः श्चशुराः पौत्राः श्यालाः सम्बन्धिनस्तथा।।1.34।।
transliteration:
āchāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrās tathaiva cha pitāmahāḥ
mātulāḥ śhvaśhurāḥ pautrāḥ śhyālāḥ sambandhinas tathā
word_meanings: āchāryāḥ—teachers; pitaraḥ—fathers; putrāḥ—sons; tathā—as well; eva—indeed; cha—also; pitāmahāḥ—grandfathers; mātulāḥ—maternal uncles; śhvaśhurāḥ—fathers-in-law; pautrāḥ—grandsons; śhyālāḥ—brothers-in-law; sambandhinaḥ—kinsmen
translation_english: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, uncles, in-laws, grandsons, and many relatives stand here in battle.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna names those standing on the battlefield: teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives. The list echoes and intensifies the earlier vision. He is no longer simply describing a crowd of kin. He is specifying the exact relationships that make war intolerable to him.

Each category carries its own emotional and moral weight. Teachers are owed reverence. Fathers and grandfathers represent lineage and authority. Sons and grandsons represent future continuity. In-laws and extended kin reflect the wider social fabric that marriage and family create. Arjuna sees that war will not only kill individuals; it will tear apart the architecture of human society as he has known it.

The repetition of relations serves an important literary purpose. It forces both Arjuna and the reader to dwell on the relational texture of the battlefield. The war is not merely a contest over sovereignty. It is the destruction of a living network of duty, affection, and inherited identity.

For a new reader, this verse explains why the opening chapter feels so heavy. The Gita does not rush to philosophy because it wants the cost to be fully seen first. Wisdom that is worth hearing must answer a sorrow of this scale.

This verse also reflects a universal truth: our deepest dilemmas often arise because different legitimate duties collide. Arjuna owes loyalty to justice, but he also owes loyalty to family and teachers. The Gita will not solve this by pretending one set of obligations never mattered. It must speak into the reality that they all matter, and that is precisely why the crisis is so severe.

original_script:
एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन।

अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतोः किं नु महीकृते।।1.35।।
transliteration:
etān na hantum ichchhāmi ghnato ’pi madhusūdana
api trailokya-rājyasya hetoḥ kiṁ nu mahī-kṛite
word_meanings: etān—these; na—not; hantum—to slay; ichchhāmi—I wish; ghnataḥ—killed; api—even though; madhusūdana—Shree Krishna; api—even though; trai-lokya-rājyasya—dominion over three worlds; hetoḥ—for sake of; mahī-kṛite—for the earth
translation_english: Even for sovereignty over the three worlds, I do not wish to kill them, what to speak of ruling this earth.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that he does not wish to kill them even if he himself were killed, not even for sovereignty over the three worlds, much less for earthly rule. His language becomes absolute. He is no longer weighing strategic or limited concerns. He is declaring that no imaginable worldly gain, even one magnified beyond earthly kingship, could justify the destruction before him.

The reference to the three worlds is significant because it enlarges the scale of his renunciation. Arjuna is not merely saying, "I do not want this particular kingdom." He is saying that power at any level loses meaning if it must come through the slaughter of those to whom he is bound by duty and affection.

This gives the verse a noble force. Arjuna is clearly not a man driven by lust for dominion. Yet the very extremity of his statement also shows how overwhelmed he has become. His thinking is now governed by an all-consuming aversion to the act of killing, without yet discerning whether refusal to fight might itself allow greater harm to continue.

For the reader, this is one of the moments where Arjuna's argument becomes both admirable and incomplete. Admirable, because he rejects the intoxication of power. Incomplete, because he has not yet considered that duty sometimes demands painful action not for private gain, but for restoration of order.

The verse continues to build the emotional credibility of Arjuna's crisis. He is not hesitating because he wants comfort. He is hesitating because he feels the full horror of violence within a broken family order. That seriousness is what makes Krishna's later correction so profound. It must address not selfishness, but anguished moral sincerity.

original_script:
निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्नः का प्रीतिः स्याज्जनार्दन।

पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिनः।।1.36।।
transliteration:
nihatya dhārtarāṣhṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana
pāpam evāśhrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ
word_meanings: nihatya—by killing; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—sons of Dhritarashtra; naḥ—our; kā—what; prītiḥ—pleasure; syāt—will there be; janārdana—Shree Krishna; pāpam—vices; eva—certainly; āśhrayet—must come upon; asmān—us; hatvā—by killing; etān—all these; ātatāyinaḥ—aggressors
translation_english: By killing Dhritarashtra's sons, sin alone will come upon us, even though they are aggressors.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks what joy could come from killing the sons of Dhritarashtra, and he says that sin alone would cling to them if they killed these aggressors. Here he introduces a more explicit moral and religious argument. The issue is no longer only grief or emptiness. He now fears that even justified violence may stain him with sin.
