This verse is important because it reveals how moral horror can reshape judgment. Arjuna knows that the Kauravas have acted unjustly. He even calls them aggressors. Yet he is so distressed by the prospect of killing them that he cannot reconcile action with righteousness. The mere fact that they are wrong no longer feels sufficient to authorize battle in his conscience.

This tension remains deeply relevant. Many people know what it is like to agree that something must be opposed and yet still fear what opposition will do to their own moral being. Arjuna is now asking whether participation in violent duty inevitably corrupts the soul.

The Gita will eventually answer by distinguishing selfish violence from duty performed without hatred and attachment. But Arjuna cannot see that yet. His moral imagination is dominated by the immediate image of killing kin. Therefore he collapses the justice of the cause and the stain of the act into one overwhelming burden.

For the reader, this verse shows that Arjuna's crisis is religious as well as emotional. He is not only afraid of sorrow. He is afraid of being morally ruined. That is why Krishna must teach not only courage, but also the nature of right action, the soul, and detachment from egoic agency.

original_script:
तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान्।

स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिनः स्याम माधव।।1.37।।
transliteration:
tasmān nārhā vayaṁ hantuṁ dhārtarāṣhṭrān sa-bāndhavān 
sva-janaṁ hi kathaṁ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava
word_meanings: tasmāt—hence; na—never; arhāḥ—behoove; vayam—we; hantum—to kill; dhārtarāṣhṭrān—the sons of Dhritarashtra; sva-bāndhavān—along with friends; sva-janam—kinsmen; hi—certainly; katham—how; hatvā—by killing; sukhinaḥ—happy; syāma—will we become; mādhava—Shree Krishna
translation_english: Therefore we should not kill our own kinsmen; how can we be happy after slaying our own people?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that therefore they should not kill the sons of Dhritarashtra, their own relatives. How could they be happy after slaying their own people? His reasoning returns again to kinship and its ethical power. Even though he has named the Kauravas as aggressors, the fact that they remain family still dominates his conscience.

The question about happiness is especially important. Arjuna is measuring action not only by legality or victory, but by the condition of the heart afterward. He senses that there are outcomes the world may call success that the soul cannot peacefully inhabit. This insight is profound. Human beings can win externally and yet become inwardly broken.

At the same time, Arjuna still assumes that refusal to act will preserve happiness or moral wholeness. Krishna will later challenge this assumption. If injustice triumphs because the rightful protectors shrink from difficult duty, can that truly lead to peace? The Gita must answer not only the pain of action, but also the pain of abandoning action.

For a new reader, this verse shows how deeply Arjuna wants a world in which moral goodness and emotional tenderness align without remainder. But the tragedy of Kurukshetra is that such easy alignment is no longer available. He must act in a fractured world where every visible path seems painful.

This is why the Gita remains so powerful in serious moral life. It speaks not to simple choices, but to situations in which every available option wounds something important. Arjuna is still searching for a way to preserve happiness by stepping back. Krishna will teach him that real peace must be grounded deeper than outward avoidance.

original_script:
यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतसः।

कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम्।।1.38।।
transliteration:
yady apy ete na paśhyanti lobhopahata-chetasaḥ
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ mitra-drohe cha pātakam
word_meanings: yadi api—even though; ete—they; na—not; paśhyanti—see; lobha—greed; upahata—overpowered; chetasaḥ—thoughts; kula-kṣhaya-kṛitam—in annihilating relatives; doṣham—fault; mitra-drohe—to wreak treachery upon friends; cha—and; pātakam—sin
translation_english: Though their minds are overpowered by greed and they see no wrong in destroying families, we should know better.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that although these men, their minds overpowered by greed, do not see the evil in destroying a family or betraying friends, why should they not understand it? This is one of the sharpest moments in his argument. He distinguishes between their moral blindness and his own awareness. In effect, he says: even if they are deluded, must we become deluded with them?

This is a strong ethical point. One person's wrongdoing does not automatically authorize another person's moral carelessness. Arjuna does not want to let the corruption of the Kauravas determine the quality of his own action. That impulse is noble and remains permanently relevant. Many moral failures arise when people justify themselves by saying, "The other side is worse." Arjuna resists that logic.

At the same time, the verse also shows his crisis sharpening into selective vision. He sees clearly the Kauravas' greed and blindness, but he still does not ask what responsibility falls on those who do see. If the morally aware withdraw when the morally blind are active, what happens to justice?

For the reader, this verse is valuable because it reveals Arjuna's conscience at its best. He wants to be guided by insight, not imitation. He refuses to let another person's delusion define his own standard. Krishna will not deny this concern. Rather, he will deepen it by teaching that true moral clarity includes the courage to act rightly, not merely the refusal to act wrongly in appearance.

This is a subtle but decisive point. Ethical sensitivity must not become paralysis. The Gita will seek to transform Arjuna's sincerity into wisdom strong enough to bear action.

original_script:
कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभिः पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम्।

कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन।।1.39।।
transliteration:
kathaṁ na jñeyam asmābhiḥ pāpād asmān nivartitum
kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ prapaśhyadbhir janārdana
word_meanings: katham—why; na—not; jñeyam—should be known; asmābhiḥ—we; pāpāt—from sin; asmāt—these; nivartitum—to turn away; kula-kṣhaya—killing kindered; kṛitam—done; doṣham—crime; prapaśhyadbhiḥ—who can see; janārdana—Shree Krishna
translation_english: O Janardana, since we clearly see the evil in family destruction, why should we not turn away from this sin?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that with the destruction of a family, its ancient family dharmas perish, and when dharma is lost, the whole family is overcome by adharma. Here his argument expands from personal grief to social collapse. He is no longer thinking only about individual deaths. He is thinking about the destruction of civilizational continuity.

Family dharma in this context means the inherited practices, duties, values, rituals, and moral structures that hold a lineage together across generations. Arjuna fears that war will not merely remove persons, but also dissolve the disciplined cultural memory that those persons carry. When elders die and families are shattered, the transmission of order becomes fragile.

This is a very important part of his reasoning. The Gita begins not only with private anguish but with concern for the breakdown of social and sacred life. Arjuna sees the household as one of the foundations of civilization. If it collapses, moral confusion spreads outward.

For a new reader, this verse helps explain the larger stakes of the war in Arjuna's mind. He is not simply afraid of grief. He is afraid of cultural ruin. The destruction of family order means the weakening of the structures through which children are raised, duties are taught, and rituals of belonging are sustained.

This concern remains intelligible even today. Whenever social bonds disintegrate, the effects travel beyond immediate loss into long-term moral confusion. Arjuna's fear is therefore not sentimental. It is sociological, ethical, and spiritual all at once. Krishna's later teaching must answer not only individual sorrow, but this fear of civilizational unraveling.

original_script:
कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्माः सनातनाः।

धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत।।1.40।।
transliteration:
kula-kṣhaye praṇaśhyanti kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ
dharme naṣhṭe kulaṁ kṛitsnam adharmo ’bhibhavaty uta
word_meanings: kula-kṣhaye—destruction of a dynasty; praṇaśhyanti—are vanquished; kula-dharmāḥ—family traditions; sanātanāḥ—eternal; dharme—religion; naṣhṭe—is destroyed; kulam—family; kṛitsnam—the whole; adharmaḥ—irreligion; abhibhavati—overcome
translation_english: With the destruction of a family, its eternal traditions perish; when dharma is lost, adharma prevails.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna continues by saying that when adharma prevails in a family, the women of the family are corrupted, and from that corruption arises social disorder. This verse belongs to the social framework of the epic age, and it reflects an ancient understanding of how family continuity and moral order were thought to be preserved across generations.

To read it responsibly, one must see the underlying concern rather than flatten it into a narrow social rule. Arjuna fears that when war destroys protective structures, widens grief, and removes elders and stabilizing relationships, the entire network of household order collapses. In his world, that collapse is expressed through concern for the vulnerability of women and the breakdown of lineage and social responsibility.

The deeper point is that violence does not stop at the battlefield. It travels into homes, marriages, children, inheritance, memory, and identity. War destabilizes the conditions under which moral life is transmitted. Arjuna is imagining the aftermath, not only the clash itself.

For a new reader, this verse shows that Arjuna's mind is moving far beyond his own emotional pain. He is trying to think historically and socially. He fears that one generation's violence can become the next generation's confusion.

The verse also reveals how strongly he believes that duty must be measured by long-range consequence, not just immediate necessity. Whether his conclusion is complete is another matter, and Krishna will challenge him. But his concern is serious: when foundational bonds are destroyed, disorder spreads in ways no battlefield victory can easily repair. That is part of the darkness he sees opening before him.

original_script:
अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रियः।

स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्करः।।1.41।।
transliteration:
adharmābhibhavāt kṛiṣhṇa praduṣhyanti kula-striyaḥ
strīṣhu duṣhṭāsu vārṣhṇeya jāyate varṇa-saṅkaraḥ
word_meanings: adharma—deviation from cosmic order; abhibhavāt—preponderance; kṛiṣhṇa—Shree Krishna; praduṣhyanti—become unmoored from duty; kula-striyaḥ—women of the lineage; strīṣhu—of women; duṣhṭāsu—become unmoored; vārṣhṇeya—descendant of Vrishni; jāyate—are born; varṇa-saṅkaraḥ—breakdown of social structures/Varnashrama
translation_english: When deviation from the cosmic order prevails, the lineage is corrupted, and the breakdown of the foundational social structure (Varnashrama) arises.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that when social disorder prevails, it leads to the mixture of lineages and that those who destroy the family become responsible for that collapse. This verse continues his argument about the long consequences of war. He is no longer focused only on immediate death. He is thinking in terms of inherited identity, ritual continuity, family responsibility, and the moral structure by which a civilization sustains itself.

In the language of the epic, "mixture of lineages" refers to breakdown in the inherited order of family and social obligations. Whatever one makes of the ancient social framework, Arjuna's underlying concern is clear: when a violent rupture destroys stable structures, confusion spreads into the next generation. People lose continuity, duty loses its inherited pathways, and the social body becomes morally disoriented.

For a new reader, this verse is best understood not as a narrow social rule but as a statement about intergenerational damage. Arjuna sees that violence rarely stays confined to the battlefield. It enters family systems, identity, memory, and the moral imagination of children and descendants. One generation's war can become the next generation's confusion.

This concern has real seriousness. Whenever foundational institutions are shattered, the result is not only grief but long-range instability. Arjuna is therefore arguing that the apparent necessity of battle must be weighed against the kind of social disorder it may unleash.

At the same time, his argument still assumes that refusing battle will preserve order. Krishna will later challenge that assumption. If injustice and lawlessness are allowed to stand because the defenders of order withdraw, social ruin may come by another path. Arjuna sees one real danger very clearly, but he has not yet integrated the danger of inaction.

original_script:
सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च।

पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः।।1.42।।
transliteration:
saṅkaro narakāyaiva kula-ghnānāṁ kulasya cha
patanti pitaro hy eṣhāṁ lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ
word_meanings: saṅkaraḥ—unwanted children; narakāya—hellish; eva—indeed; kula-ghnānām—for family destroyers; kulasya—of the family; cha—also; patanti—fall; pitaraḥ—ancestors; hi—verily; eṣhām—their; lupta—deprived; piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ—sacrificial offerings
translation_english: Such disorder leads both the destroyers of the family and the family itself toward downfall, and the ancestors are deprived of offerings.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that through such corruption the ancestral rites cease, and the family and those who have destroyed it fall into hell. Here his argument enters the sacred and ritual dimension of society. In the world of the Mahabharata, families do not merely transmit property and social standing; they also maintain bonds between generations through remembrance, offerings, rites, and inherited duties toward the dead.

What Arjuna fears is therefore not only social instability, but a rupture in the sacred chain connecting past and present. If households are shattered and elders killed, who will preserve the practices through which ancestors are honored and lineage memory is sustained? In his understanding, such neglect is not a small omission. It is a profound disorder in the moral and sacred fabric of life.

For a modern reader, the exact form of the ritual concern may feel distant, but the underlying truth remains understandable. Human communities are shaped by memory, gratitude, continuity, and practices that bind generations together. When those practices collapse, people do not simply lose ritual. They lose orientation, rootedness, and a sense of obligation to something larger than individual desire.

Arjuna's language about hell expresses the severity with which he sees this breakdown. He believes that the destruction of family dharma harms both the living and the dead, the present and the inherited past. His conscience is therefore operating on a scale much larger than personal emotion.

This verse deepens the gravity of his despair. He is not only afraid of guilt for killing. He is afraid of becoming an agent in a rupture that will sever social, ethical, and sacred continuity across generations.

original_script:
दोषैरेतैः कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकैः।

उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वताः।।1.43।।
transliteration:
doṣhair etaiḥ kula-ghnānāṁ varṇa-saṅkara-kārakaiḥ
utsādyante jāti-dharmāḥ kula-dharmāśh cha śhāśhvatāḥ
word_meanings: doṣhaiḥ—through evil deeds; etaiḥ—these; kula-ghnānām—of those who destroy family; varṇa-saṅkara—unwanted progeny; kārakaiḥ—causing; utsādyante—are ruined; jāti-dharmāḥ—social welfare; kula-dharmāḥ—family traditions; śhāśhvatāḥ—eternal
translation_english: Through these faults, eternal family duties and social order are destroyed.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna concludes that by these sins of those who destroy the family, the eternal duties of caste and family are overthrown. This is the culmination of the social argument he has been building from verse 39 onward. He believes that war among kin will not merely produce loss, but will dismantle the inherited structures by which society organizes responsibility and transmits moral order.

In the language of the time, these duties are not arbitrary labels but frameworks of role, obligation, and continuity. Arjuna's fear is that once violent rupture enters the family, the stable channels through which duty is taught and practiced begin to dissolve. What is called eternal here refers not to a rigid social system frozen outside history, but to deeply rooted patterns of responsibility that give a civilization coherence.

For a new reader, this verse should be understood at the level of moral architecture. Every society depends on patterns that outlast individuals: obligations between generations, norms of care, shared rituals, protected institutions, and roles by which people learn what they owe one another. Arjuna sees war as an attack on that architecture.

This is one of the reasons his grief feels so large. He is not only thinking, "People will die." He is thinking, "The form of life we know may be irreparably damaged." That fear is historically realistic. War often destroys not just bodies but the structures by which meaning is passed on.

Still, Krishna will eventually force him to face a harder truth: social order is not preserved merely by avoiding painful action. Sometimes order must be defended against those who are already corrupting it. Arjuna's argument remains serious, but it is still incomplete.

original_script:
उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन।

नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम।।1.44।।
transliteration:
utsanna-kula-dharmāṇāṁ manuṣhyāṇāṁ janārdana
narake ‘niyataṁ vāso bhavatītyanuśhuśhruma
word_meanings: utsanna—destroyed; kula-dharmāṇām—whose traditions; manuṣhyāṇām—of such human beings; janārdana—Shree Krishna; narake—in hell; aniyatam—indefinite; vāsaḥ—dwell; bhavati—is; iti—thus; anuśhuśhruma—I have heard
translation_english: We have heard that those whose family traditions are ruined dwell in hell for a long time.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that he has heard that those whose family dharmas are destroyed must dwell in hell for an indefinite time. This verse appeals not only to his own reasoning but to inherited teaching. He is drawing on what he has been taught by tradition and using that inherited wisdom to interpret the crisis before him.

This appeal matters because it shows that Arjuna's resistance is not impulsive emotionalism alone. He is trying to reason within a moral and religious framework he has received. His sorrow is now supported by remembered doctrine: the destruction of the family and its duties is not merely unfortunate, but spiritually disastrous.

For a new reader, this verse reveals how moral crises often involve conflict between established teachings and immediate responsibilities. Arjuna is a warrior trained to act, yet he is also a member of a sacred culture that condemns the collapse of family and ritual order. He now experiences those two inheritances as though they were pulling in opposite directions.

This is one reason the Gita is so profound. It is not simply a conversation between courage and fear. It is also a conversation between competing visions of righteousness. Arjuna is not rebelling against religion. He is using religion to argue against battle. Krishna's response will therefore have to reinterpret duty at a deeper level, not merely appeal to brute force or honor.

The verse also shows how powerful inherited ideas become in moments of stress. Under pressure, people often return to what they have been taught and ask whether their present action can be reconciled with it. Arjuna is doing exactly that. His crisis is therefore moral, social, emotional, and theological all at once.

original_script:
अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम्।

यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यताः।।1.45।।
transliteration:
aho bata mahat pāpaṁ kartuṁ vyavasitā vayam
yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṁ sva-janam udyatāḥ
word_meanings: aho—alas; bata—how; mahat—great; pāpam—sins; kartum—to perform; vyavasitāḥ—decided; vayam—we; rājya-sukha-lobhena—desire for pleasure; hantum—to kill; sva-janam—kinsmen; udyatāḥ—intending
translation_english: Alas, we are preparing to commit great sin, driven by desire for royal pleasure and power.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna cries out that it is a great sin they are prepared to commit, driven by the desire for royal pleasure, in killing their own people. This verse gathers his entire line of thought into a final exclamation. What began as bodily trembling, then moral hesitation, then social argument, now culminates in a verdict upon the whole enterprise: this is a terrible wrong.

The phrase about desire for royal pleasure is important. Arjuna now frames the war as if it were fundamentally driven by appetite for enjoyment and power. From his current state of mind, all arguments for battle are being absorbed into that interpretation. He can no longer distinguish clearly between fighting for justice and fighting for gain. His grief is collapsing those motives together.

This is psychologically believable. When a person is overwhelmed by the horror of an action, it can become hard to grant that any pure motive remains. Everything begins to look contaminated. Arjuna now sees the war mainly as a sinful pursuit of pleasure disguised as duty.

For the reader, this verse is a crucial sign that his crisis has reached interpretive distortion. Not because his sorrow is false, but because sorrow is now governing the whole moral picture. The Pandava cause is not actually reducible to ambition, yet Arjuna in despair speaks as though it were.

This is why Krishna must respond. If Arjuna's compassion remained perfectly clear, no correction would be needed. But compassion mixed with grief has now begun to blur discernment. The Gita will not dismiss his anguish. It will purify it by separating true renunciation from despair-driven rejection of duty.

original_script:
यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणयः।

धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत्।।1.46।।
transliteration:
yadi mām apratīkāram aśhastraṁ śhastra-pāṇayaḥ
dhārtarāṣhṭrā raṇe hanyus tan me kṣhemataraṁ bhavet
word_meanings: yadi—if; mām—me; apratīkāram—unresisting; aśhastram—unarmed; śhastra-pāṇayaḥ—those with weapons; dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ—sons of Dhritarashtra; raṇe—battlefield; hanyuḥ—kill; kṣhema-taram—better; bhavet—would be
translation_english: It would be better for me if the sons of Dhritarashtra kill me unresisting and unarmed on the battlefield.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that it would be better if the sons of Dhritarashtra, weapons in hand, were to kill him in battle while he remained unresisting and unarmed. This is one of the most dramatic statements in the chapter. He now prefers death to action. The tension of moral conflict has reached such intensity that passive self-sacrifice seems cleaner to him than participation in war.

At one level, this sounds noble. It expresses revulsion toward killing and willingness to suffer personally rather than inflict suffering on kin. Many readers understandably feel the moral beauty in such a thought. Yet the Gita will later show that this apparent nonviolence is not automatically wisdom. One may refuse to act not from transcendence, but from collapse.

That distinction is essential. Arjuna is not resting in serene spiritual insight here. He is overwhelmed, disoriented, and unable to bear the burden of action. His willingness to die is not yet the same as the freedom of a sage. It is the cry of a man who sees no acceptable path and therefore chooses surrender to destruction.

For a new reader, this verse reveals how moral pain can make passivity feel pure. When all action appears stained, doing nothing can seem like the only innocent option. But the Gita will challenge that assumption with great force. In a world where injustice is active, refusal to act may also have consequences, and those consequences may not be innocent.

This verse therefore brings Arjuna to the edge of total renunciation of battle. It sets the stage for Krishna's teaching by showing that the warrior has become, for the moment, inwardly incapable of the role he is called to fulfill.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

एवमुक्त्वाऽर्जुनः संख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत्।

विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानसः।।1.47।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvārjunaḥ saṅkhye rathopastha upāviśhat
visṛijya sa-śharaṁ chāpaṁ śhoka-saṁvigna-mānasaḥ
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam uktvā—speaking thus; arjunaḥ—Arjun; saṅkhye—battlefield; ratha upasthe—on the chariot; upāviśhat—sat; visṛijya—casting aside; sa-śharam—arrows; chāpam—the bow; śhoka—with grief; saṁvigna—distressed; mānasaḥ—mind
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Speaking thus, Arjuna cast aside his bow and arrows and sat down in the chariot, his mind overwhelmed by grief.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya concludes the chapter by saying that Arjuna, his mind overwhelmed by grief, cast aside his bow and arrows and sat down on the chariot. The image is unforgettable. The great warrior who began ready for battle has now become motionless, disarmed by sorrow before the conflict even starts.

The act of setting down the bow is outwardly simple, but spiritually it is immense. Gandiva slipping from Arjuna's hand was already a sign of collapse. Now he relinquishes it completely. This is the visible end of the first chapter's downward movement. Confidence has become trembling, trembling has become argument, and argument has ended in paralysis.

The verse describes his mind as overcome by grief, and that is the proper summary. Arjuna is not sitting in meditation, detachment, or enlightened peace. He is sitting in sorrow. That difference matters greatly. The stillness of despair can look superficially like renunciation, but it is inwardly very different. One is born of wisdom; the other of overwhelm.

For the reader, this ending is perfect preparation for the Gita's central teaching. Krishna does not begin speaking while Arjuna is still posturing as self-sufficient. He begins once Arjuna has reached the limit of his own resources. The collapse becomes the opening through which true instruction can enter.

This final verse of Chapter 1 also carries a universal pattern. Many lives include moments when the familiar instruments of action fail, and a person sits unable to proceed because grief, fear, or confusion has emptied old certainty. The Gita begins there. Its wisdom is not spoken to the invulnerable, but to the person who can no longer continue without a deeper truth.

original_script: अर्जुनविषादयोगः
transliteration: Arjuna Viṣhāda Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of Arjuna's Dejection
deep_dive_text: The narrative begins with a profound crisis of conscience. On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Arjuna is overcome by grief at the thought of fighting his own kin. His hesitation reveals the tension between compassion, family ties, and responsibility, and it opens the way for Krishna's teaching.

original_script: अथ द्वितीयोऽध्यायः - साङ्ख्ययोगः
transliteration: Sāṅkhya Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Sāṅkhya—analytical deduction; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Knowledge (Sankhya Yoga)
deep_dive_text: This chapter serves as the intellectual synthesis of the Samkhya (analytical) and Yoga (practical) traditions. Krishna introduces the indestructible nature of the 'Atman' (Self) versus the 'Deha' (Body), providing a metaphysical framework for action. It establishes 'Samatvam' (equanimity) as the benchmark for wisdom, transitioning Arjuna from a state of paralysis to a state of detached, dharmic engagement.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच

तं तथा कृपयाऽविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम्।

विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः।।2.1।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
taṁ tathā kṛipayāviṣhṭamaśhru pūrṇākulekṣhaṇam
viṣhīdantamidaṁ vākyam uvācha madhusūdanaḥ
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; tam—to him; tathā—thus; kṛipayā—with pity; āviṣhṭam—overwhelmed; aśhru-pūrṇa—full of tears; ākula—distressed; īkṣhaṇam—eyes; viṣhīdantam—grief-stricken; idam—these; vākyam—words; uvācha—said; madhusūdanaḥ—Shree Krishna
translation_english: Sanjaya said: To Arjuna, overwhelmed by pity and sorrow, with tearful eyes and dejected heart, Krishna spoke.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya says that Krishna then addressed Arjuna, who was overwhelmed with pity, his eyes full of tears and his mind deeply troubled. This verse marks the formal beginning of Krishna's spoken teaching, but it begins not with doctrine alone. It begins with a precise picture of Arjuna's condition. He is not thinking clearly, and he is not standing in detached wisdom. He is drowned in emotion.

The description is important because it separates compassion from confusion. Arjuna's tears are real, and his pity is not to be mocked. Yet the verse also says that his mind is afflicted. In other words, his emotional response, though sincere, has become destabilizing. He is no longer simply feeling deeply; he is unable to discern rightly within what he feels. That is the exact point at which true guidance becomes necessary.

For a new reader, this verse clarifies the emotional honesty of the Gita. Krishna does not begin teaching an invulnerable hero. He begins teaching a person whose inner strength has collapsed under grief. This makes the scripture psychologically credible. Wisdom is not offered after the storm has passed, but in the very middle of breakdown.

The verse also reveals Krishna's role. He does not speak while Arjuna is still arguing with confidence. He speaks when Arjuna's sorrow is visible and undeniable. Divine instruction enters not to suppress the human heart, but to heal its confusion and restore proportion. Krishna will not ask Arjuna to stop caring; he will ask him to see more deeply than grief currently allows.

This is why the second chapter matters so much. The outer war remains present, but the real conversation now turns to the deepest human questions: weakness, identity, death, duty, and the nature of wisdom.

original_script:
श्री भगवानुवाच

कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम्।

अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन।।2.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kutastvā kaśhmalamidaṁ viṣhame samupasthitam
anārya-juṣhṭamaswargyam akīrti-karam arjuna
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; kutaḥ—wherefrom; tvā—to you; kaśhmalam—delusion; idam—this; viṣhame—hour of peril; samupasthitam—overcome; anārya—crude person; juṣhṭam—practiced; aswargyam—not leading to higher abodes; akīrti-karam—leading to disgrace; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: From where has this weakness arisen in this critical hour? It is unworthy, dishonorable, and does not lead to higher life.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna asks Arjuna how this weakness has come upon him at such a critical moment. He says it is unworthy of a noble person, does not lead to higher worlds, and brings disgrace. The tone is sharp, and it is meant to be. Krishna does not begin by soothing Arjuna sentimentally. He begins by disrupting the spell of grief.

This verse can sound severe to a first-time reader, but its severity is purposeful. Krishna is not condemning compassion. He is challenging the collapse of clarity disguised as virtue. Arjuna has allowed pity, fear, attachment, and moral confusion to merge into one state of paralysis. Krishna names this not as wisdom, but as weakness at the wrong time.

The phrase about noble persons is especially important. Krishna is appealing to Arjuna's deeper identity, not merely his social pride. Arjuna is capable of steadiness, discernment, and courageous action, yet he is abandoning those capacities under the pressure of sorrow. Krishna's rebuke is therefore restorative. He is calling Arjuna back to his higher nature.

For a new reader, this verse introduces one of the Gita's core lessons: not every emotional impulse that feels compassionate is actually aligned with truth. Feelings must be honored, but they must also be examined. A collapse into inaction can appear morally elevated while actually springing from attachment and confusion.

This verse also shows that divine guidance can come in the form of challenge. Krishna's love is not indulgent. He does not confirm Arjuna in a state that will diminish him. Instead, he confronts him with a higher standard. That confrontation is the beginning of awakening.

original_script:
क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते।

क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप।।2.3।।
transliteration:
klaibyaṁ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayyupapadyate
kṣhudraṁ hṛidaya-daurbalyaṁ tyaktvottiṣhṭha parantapa
word_meanings: klaibyam—unmanliness; mā sma—do not; gamaḥ—yield to; pārtha—Arjun; na—not; etat—this; tvayi—to you; upapadyate—befitting; kṣhudram—petty; hṛidaya—heart; daurbalyam—weakness; tyaktvā—giving up; uttiṣhṭha—arise; param-tapa—conqueror of enemies
translation_english: Do not yield to this unmanliness, O Partha. Cast off this petty weakness of heart and arise, O scorcher of foes.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna tells Arjuna not to yield to impotence, saying it does not befit him. He urges him to cast off this petty weakness of heart and arise, O scorcher of enemies. The verse continues the rebuke, but now it becomes more direct and energizing. Krishna is trying to shake Arjuna out of collapse by reminding him of who he is and what he has the strength to do.

The phrase "weakness of heart" is crucial. Krishna does not say Arjuna lacks intelligence, training, or power. He says that his heart has become small under the pressure of grief. This is an important spiritual diagnosis. Human beings often know what is right and still fail because the inner instrument contracts. Courage, clarity, and devotion are all present in seed, but sorrow narrows the heart until it cannot bear the demand of duty.

At the same time, Krishna's language is not merely motivational in a shallow sense. He is not saying, "Be strong because victory matters." He is saying that Arjuna is acting beneath the truth of his own being. To arise is not merely to move physically; it is to stand again in right relation to oneself, to duty, and to reality.

For a new reader, this verse is deeply relevant. There are moments when people do not need more analysis of their fear. They need a call that addresses the shrinking center of the self and demands a return to steadiness. Krishna provides exactly that.

This verse also prepares the transition from emotional collapse to philosophical instruction. First, Arjuna must be interrupted. Only then can he truly receive the deeper teaching that will explain why rising is possible at all.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच

कथं भीष्ममहं संख्ये द्रोणच मधुसूदन।

इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन।।2.4।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kathaṁ bhīṣhmam ahaṁ saṅkhye droṇaṁ cha madhusūdana
iṣhubhiḥ pratīyotsyāmi pūjārhāv arisūdana
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; katham—how; bhīṣhmam—Bhishma; aham—I; saṅkhye—in battle; droṇam—Drona; cha—and; madhusūdana—Krishna; iṣhubhiḥ—with arrows; pratiyotsyāmi—shall fight; pūjā-arhau—worthy of worship; ari-sūdana—slayer of enemies
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana, how can I fight with arrows in battle against men like Bhishma and Drona, who are worthy of my worship?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna replies by asking how he can fight in battle against Bhishma and Drona, who are worthy of worship. This response is revealing because even after Krishna's rebuke, Arjuna's mind goes immediately back to relationship and reverence. He is not refusing battle because he has forgotten his warrior identity. He is refusing because he cannot reconcile that identity with the sacred bonds he bears toward those before him.

Bhishma and Drona are not random examples. Bhishma is the grandsire, worthy of filial reverence and affection. Drona is the teacher who shaped Arjuna's martial excellence. To raise weapons against them feels, from Arjuna's perspective, like a violation of gratitude itself. This is why the problem is so painful. Duty as warrior appears to demand something that duty as student and kinsman cannot accept.

For a new reader, this verse helps explain why Krishna's earlier rebuke does not instantly solve the issue. Arjuna's objections are not superficial. He is naming a real conflict between forms of obligation. Reverence and justice, gratitude and battle, teacher-love and social duty now stand in collision.

This verse also shows that moral crisis is often relational at its core. Abstract law may say one thing, but a concrete face can change the entire emotional meaning of action. Arjuna is looking at the people who formed him, and the task before him begins to feel like self-betrayal.

Krishna's teaching will eventually have to show that true vision does not deny reverence but places it within a larger understanding of the Self, death, and rightful action. But first Arjuna must articulate the pain honestly, and this verse does exactly that.

original_script:
गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके।
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान्रुधिरप्रदिग्धान्।।2.5।।
transliteration:
gurūn ahatvā hi mahānubhāvān
śhreyo bhoktuṁ bhaikṣhyam apīha loke
hatvārtha-kāmāṁs tu gurūn ihaiva
bhuñjīya bhogān rudhira-pradigdhān
word_meanings: gurūn—teachers; ahatvā—not killing; hi—certainly; mahā-anubhāvān—noble; śhreyaḥ—better; bhoktum—to enjoy; bhaikṣhyam—begging; api—even; iha loke—in this world; hatvā—killing; artha—gain; kāmān—desiring; tu—but; gurūn—teachers; iha—in this world; eva—certainly; bhuñjīya—would enjoy; bhogān—wealth and pleasures; rudhira—with blood; pradigdhān—tainted
translation_english: It would be better to live in this world by begging than to enjoy life by killing these noble teachers. If I kill them, any wealth or pleasure I enjoy will be tainted with their blood.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says it would be better to live in this world by begging than to kill his honored teachers. If he killed them, he says, he would only enjoy blood-stained pleasures. This is one of the clearest expressions of his moral revulsion. He would rather accept humiliation and material loss than build a life on the death of those he reveres.

The image of blood-stained enjoyment is especially powerful. Arjuna is not speaking abstractly about guilt. He is imagining a future in which victory, food, wealth, and royal pleasure are permanently contaminated by the knowledge of what they cost. In his mind, prosperity after such slaughter would not be sweet. It would be stained.

For a new reader, this verse shows again that Arjuna is not motivated by greed or self-protection. He is willing to lose status, comfort, and worldly honor if that is what is required to avoid what he experiences as a morally unbearable act. This gives real dignity to his crisis.

Yet the verse also reveals the narrowing tendency of despair. Arjuna imagines only two possibilities: sinful victory or morally cleaner retreat into hardship. He does not yet see that refusal to fight might also have consequences for justice, order, and the wider world. His imagination is still focused almost entirely on the stain of action.

This verse therefore pushes the Gita toward one of its great questions: how can one act in a broken world without becoming inwardly stained by action? Arjuna assumes that action itself will stain him. Krishna will respond by teaching a radically deeper understanding of agency, motive, and the nature of the Self.

original_script:
न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः।
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषाम-
स्तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः।।2.6।।
transliteration:
na chaitad vidmaḥ kataran no garīyo
yad vā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ
yān eva hatvā na jijīviṣhāmas
te ’vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ
word_meanings: na—not; cha—also; etat—this; vidmaḥ—we know; katarat—which; naḥ—for us; garīyaḥ—better; yat vā—whether; jayema—we might conquer; yadi vā—or if; naḥ—us; jayeyuḥ—they might conquer; yān—whom; eva—certainly; hatvā—by killing; na—not; jijīviṣhāmaḥ—we desire to live; te—they; avasthitāḥ—standing; pramukhe—before us; dhārtarāṣhṭrāḥ—the sons of Dhritarashtra
translation_english: I do not even know which is better—conquering them or being conquered by them. The very people we do not wish to live without are standing before us ready for war.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that he does not know which is better: that they should conquer the Kauravas, or that the Kauravas should conquer them. The very men he would not wish to kill stand before him, sons of Dhritarashtra, ready for battle. This verse makes explicit something that has been building since the first chapter: Arjuna's crisis is not merely emotional, but decisional. He has lost the ability to judge what outcome should even be desired.

This is an important stage in moral collapse. At first one feels pain. Then one argues against action. Finally, one loses the very capacity to tell what would count as good. Arjuna has reached that point. Victory feels poisoned, but defeat does not appear meaningful either. He cannot orient himself toward any result.

For a new reader, this verse shows why the Gita must become more than encouragement. Arjuna no longer needs only courage. He needs discernment. He must be taught how to see the structure of reality, the Self, action, duty, and desire in such a way that a right course can once again become visible.

This verse also reveals how deeply relationship has destabilized judgment. The issue is not battlefield uncertainty in the ordinary sense. Arjuna is not wondering which army is stronger. He is wondering what kind of victory could possibly count as victory if gained against those he loves.

That question is profoundly human. Many people come to crossroads where every visible outcome feels compromised. The Gita begins to answer precisely there. Krishna's wisdom will not consist of a simple strategy choice. It will consist of re-grounding Arjuna in a level of truth deeper than worldly gain and loss.

original_script:
कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः।
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम्।।2.7।।
transliteration:
kārpaṇya-doṣhopahata-svabhāvaḥ
pṛichchhāmi tvāṁ dharma-sammūḍha-chetāḥ
yach chhreyaḥ syān niśhchitaṁ brūhi tan me
śhiṣhyas te ’haṁ śhādhi māṁ tvāṁ prapannam
word_meanings: kārpaṇya—cowardice; doṣha—flaw; upahata—overpowered; sva-bhāvaḥ—nature; pṛichchhāmi—I ask; tvām—you; dharma—duty; sammūḍha—confused; chetāḥ—mind; yat—what; śhreyaḥ—good; syāt—may be; niśhchitam—decisively; brūhi—tell; tat—that; me—to me; śhiṣhyaḥ—disciple; te—your; aham—I am; śhādhi—instruct; mām—me; tvām—to you; prapannam—surrendered
translation_english: I am weighed down by a sense of helpless cowardice; my mind is confused about my duty. I beg you to tell me clearly what is best for me. I am now your disciple; please instruct me, for I have surrendered to you.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna finally says that his nature has been overcome by weakness and confusion regarding dharma. He asks Krishna to tell him clearly what is truly good for him, declaring that he is Krishna's disciple and seeking refuge in his guidance. This verse is one of the decisive turning points in the entire Bhagavad Gita. The real teaching begins here, because Arjuna stops arguing as an equal and starts learning as a disciple.

Several things change at once. First, Arjuna admits confusion. This is an act of strength, not failure. He no longer pretends that his reasoning alone can carry him through. Second, he specifically says he is confused about dharma. The problem is not merely emotion, but right action in relation to truth. Third, he surrenders to instruction. He asks not for comforting agreement, but for clear guidance.

For a new reader, this verse is central because it shows what genuine spiritual inquiry looks like. It begins when self-reliance reaches its limit and a person becomes willing to be taught. Arjuna is still full of grief, but now his grief becomes the doorway to humility rather than just collapse.

The phrase "what is truly good for me" is also important. Arjuna is asking for shreyas, the highest good, not merely what feels pleasant in the moment. This distinction will matter throughout the Gita. What is pleasant under grief may not be what is truly beneficial.

This verse is therefore the birth of discipleship within the Gita. Until now, Krishna has been Arjuna's friend and charioteer. Now he becomes his teacher in the fullest sense. Once Arjuna accepts that role, wisdom can begin to enter.

original_script:
न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्या-
द्यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम्।
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम्।।2.8।।
transliteration:
na hi prapaśhyāmi mamāpanudyād
yach chhokam uchchhoṣhaṇam indriyāṇām
avāpyā bhūmāvasapatnam ṛiddhaṁ
rājyaṁ surāṇām api chādhipatyam
word_meanings: na—not; hi—certainly; prapaśhyāmi—I see; mama—my; apanudyāt—can drive away; yat—which; śhokam—sorrow; uchchhoṣhaṇam—drying up; indriyāṇām—of the senses; avāpya—attaining; bhūmau—on earth; asapatnam—unrivaled; ṛiddham—prosperous; rājyam—kingdom; surāṇām—of the celestial gods; api—even; cha—also; adhipatyam—sovereignty
translation_english: I can find no means to drive away this grief which is drying up my senses. I would not be able to dispel it even if I won a prosperous, unrivaled kingdom on earth or sovereignty like that of the gods.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that he does not see what could remove the grief that is drying up his senses, even if he were to gain an unrivaled kingdom on earth or sovereignty among the gods. This is his final statement before silence gives way to Krishna's sustained teaching. He is declaring total inadequacy of worldly solutions.

The image of grief drying up the senses is psychologically rich. Sorrow has drained taste from life itself. Arjuna is not simply reluctant to fight; he is existentially exhausted. The things human beings usually strive for, power, status, heavenly reward, no longer appear capable of healing what has broken inside him.

For a new reader, this verse is extremely important because it shows that the Gita is not fundamentally about success in the ordinary sense. Arjuna is explicitly saying that no external achievement can cure inner desolation when the soul is disordered. This insight remains timeless. Many people discover that position, wealth, and praise do not answer their deepest anguish.

The reference even to the gods broadens the point. Arjuna is saying that not only earthly reward but celestial reward fails to touch the root of his problem. The sickness is deeper than circumstance. It lies in vision, identity, attachment, and grief.

This is exactly why Krishna's teaching moves immediately toward the immortality of the Self and the nature of true wisdom. Since no external rearrangement can solve Arjuna's state, only a transformation in understanding can do so. The verse therefore closes the old mode of seeking and prepares the opening of a new one.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तप।
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह।।2.9।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvā hṛiṣhīkeśhaṁ guḍākeśhaḥ parantapa
na yotsya iti govindam uktvā tūṣhṇīṁ babhūva ha
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjay said; evam—thus; uktvā—having spoken; hṛiṣhīkeśham—to Krishna; guḍākeśhaḥ—Arjuna, the conqueror of sleep; parantapa—the scorcher of enemies; na yotsye—I shall not fight; iti—thus; govindam—to Krishna; uktvā—having said; tūṣhṇīm—silent; babhūva—became
translation_english: Sanjaya said: Having spoken thus, Arjuna, the chastiser of enemies, told Krishna, 'I shall not fight,' and became silent.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya says that after speaking thus, Arjuna told Krishna, "I shall not fight," and then fell silent. This brief verse carries enormous dramatic force. After all his arguments, symptoms, and appeals, Arjuna finally comes to a decision, or what appears to be a decision: refusal.

Yet the silence that follows is not the silence of peace. It is the silence of exhaustion. Arjuna has reached the end of his own resources. He has reasoned, lamented, protested, and surrendered himself as a disciple, and now there is nothing left for him to say. That silence creates the space into which Krishna's teaching can enter.

For a new reader, this verse marks the end of Arjuna's first inner movement and the beginning of Krishna's response proper. The refusal to fight is important, but perhaps even more important is the silence after it. Spiritual instruction often becomes possible only when the mind has run out of self-generated speeches.

This verse also reminds us that surrender to guidance does not instantly remove resistance. Arjuna has asked to be taught, but he is still clinging emotionally to his present conclusion. This too is realistic. People often seek help while inwardly remaining attached to their despair-based judgments. The teacher must therefore work patiently and deeply.

The scene is now perfectly set: the warrior is silent, the crisis is total, and the disciple has finally admitted he cannot solve himself. It is from this place that the central wisdom of the Gita is spoken.

original_script:
तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः।।2.10।।
transliteration:
tam uvācha hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ prahasann iva bhārata
senayor ubhayor madhye viṣhīdantam idaṁ vachaḥ
word_meanings: tam—to him; uvācha—said; hṛiṣhīkeśhaḥ—Krishna; prahasan—smiling; iva—as if; bhārata—descendant of Bharat; senayoḥ—of the armies; ubhayoḥ—of both; madhye—in the midst; viṣhīdantam—to the grief-stricken one; idam—this; vachaḥ—words
translation_english: O Dhritarashtra, thereafter, in the midst of both armies, Krishna, smiling as it were, spoke these words to the grief-stricken Arjuna.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya says that Krishna, smiling as it were, spoke to the grief-stricken Arjuna standing between the two armies. The mention of Krishna's smile is subtle and profound. It is not a smile of mockery. It is the smile of one who sees more than Arjuna sees, one who understands both the sorrow and the truth beyond it.

This verse creates an extraordinary contrast. Arjuna is overwhelmed, but Krishna is composed. Arjuna sees only ruin, but Krishna stands in serene understanding. That difference in inner state is the very reason Krishna can teach. He is not emotionally swept away by the scene because he sees from the standpoint of reality rather than from the standpoint of attachment alone.

For a new reader, the smile matters because it reveals the tone of divine wisdom. Krishna will speak firmly, even sharply, but not from panic, anger, or despair. He speaks from a centered freedom that Arjuna does not yet possess. His smile suggests compassion joined with clarity.

The verse also reminds us where the teaching occurs: between the two armies. The Gita does not wait for the battlefield to disappear. Wisdom is spoken in the middle of unresolved conflict. This is one of the text's great strengths. It does not depend on perfect outer conditions before truth can be heard.

With this verse, the chapter shifts fully into Krishna's instruction. The time of Arjuna's unchallenged grief has ended. Now begins the great re-education of the human mind, starting from the most fundamental mistake: the confusion of the eternal Self with the changing body and its relationships.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे।
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः।।2.11।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
aśhochyān anvaśhochas tvaṁ prajñā-vādāṁśh cha bhāṣhase
gatāsūn agatāsūṁśh cha nānuśhochanti paṇḍitāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; aśhochyān—not worthy of grief; anvaśhochaḥ—you are mourning; tvam—you; prajñā-vādān—learned words; cha—and; bhāṣhase—speaking; gata—gone; asūn—life; agata—not gone; asūn—life; cha—and; na—never; anuśhochanti—lament; paṇḍitāḥ—the wise
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: While speaking learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of grief. The wise lament neither for the living nor for the dead.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins by saying that Arjuna is grieving for those who should not be grieved for, yet speaking words that sound wise. The truly wise do not grieve for the dead or for the living. This is one of the most famous and difficult opening statements in the Gita. It sounds almost shocking, especially after the emotional weight of Chapter 1. But Krishna is not asking Arjuna to become cold or inhuman. He is correcting the framework from which Arjuna is grieving.

Arjuna's sorrow feels noble because it is tied to love and reverence. Krishna does not deny that. What he denies is the assumption beneath the sorrow: that the deepest truth of a person is exhausted by bodily life and death. If that assumption is false, then grief itself must be re-examined.

The phrase "speaking words of wisdom" is also important. Arjuna has used ethical and social reasoning, and much of it sounded serious. Krishna's point is that language can sound wise while still being grounded in incomplete vision. True wisdom is not measured by emotional seriousness alone, but by knowledge of the real nature of the Self.

For a new reader, this verse introduces the central reorientation of the Gita. Krishna is not beginning with battlefield strategy or even social ethics. He begins with ontology: what are living beings really? Until that question is answered, duty and grief cannot be understood properly.

This verse therefore sets the entire course of the teaching. Arjuna's pain is real, but it rests on mistaken identification. Krishna's task is to uncover that mistake and replace it with a knowledge deep enough to transform how life, death, love, and action are all understood.

original_script:
न त्वेवाहं जातु नासं न त्वं नेमे जनाधिपाः।
न चैव न भविष्यामः सर्वे वयमतः परम्।।2.12।।
transliteration:
na tvevāhaṁ jātu nāsaṁ na tvaṁ neme janādhipāḥ
na chaiva na bhaviṣhyāmaḥ sarve vayam ataḥ param
word_meanings: na—never; tu—but; eva—certainly; aham—I; jātu—at any time; na—not; āsam—existed; na—not; tvam—you; na—not; ime—these; jana-adhipāḥ—kings; na—not; cha—also; eva—certainly; na—not; bhaviṣhyāmaḥ—shall exist; sarve—all; vayam—we; ataḥ param—hereafter
translation_english: Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that never was there a time when he did not exist, nor Arjuna, nor these kings, nor will there be a time when any of them cease to be. This verse is the first explicit statement of the soul's continuity. It does not merely say that life continues in a vague or symbolic sense. It asserts real enduring being beyond bodily change.

This is a decisive answer to Arjuna's grief. The people standing before him are not annihilated by death in the ultimate sense, because their deepest identity does not begin with the body and does not end with it. Krishna includes himself, Arjuna, and the assembled kings in the same statement, thereby establishing continuity as a universal feature of conscious existence.

For a new reader, this verse is foundational. The Gita does not ask us first to suppress grief by force of will. It asks us to reconsider what a person actually is. If the body is only one phase in a longer journey of conscious being, then death, though still painful at the level of relationship, cannot be understood as total destruction.

This verse also implies that individuality is not erased in the Gita's vision. Krishna does not say that all beings simply vanish into abstract sameness. He speaks of enduring existence in a way that preserves meaningful continuity.

That is why this teaching matters so much for duty. If Arjuna sees only bodies, then battle appears as total ruin. If he begins to see embodied persons as eternal selves moving through changing forms, the meaning of death and action becomes more complex. Krishna is laying the metaphysical groundwork for courage without cruelty and detachment without indifference.

original_script:
देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा।
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति।।2.13।।
transliteration:
dehino ’smin yathā dehe kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā
tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati
word_meanings: dehinaḥ—of the embodied soul; asmin—in this; yathā—as; dehe—body; kaumāram—childhood; yauvanam—youth; jarā—old age; tathā—similarly; deha-antara—another body; prāptiḥ—achievement; dhīraḥ—the wise; tatra—thereupon; na—never; muhyati—is deluded
translation_english: Just as the embodied soul passes from childhood to youth and then to old age, so does it pass into another body at death. The wise are not deluded by this.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna explains that just as the embodied soul passes through childhood, youth, and old age in this body, so it passes into another body at death; the wise are not deluded by this. This verse takes the lofty claim of the previous one and makes it concrete. Krishna appeals to something Arjuna already knows: even within one lifetime, the body changes dramatically while a deeper sense of self persists.

A child becomes a youth, a youth becomes an elder, yet throughout these changes a continuity of identity remains. Krishna uses this familiar experience as a bridge to the less familiar idea of rebirth. Just as identity persists through bodily transformation during life, so too it persists through the great transition called death.

For a new reader, this is one of the most accessible and powerful teachings in the Gita. It invites reflection on ordinary experience rather than demanding blind belief. We have already lived through many bodily changes without concluding that the self was destroyed each time. Krishna asks us to extend that insight further.

The phrase about the wise not being deluded is important. Wisdom does not mean refusal to notice change. It means refusal to mistake change for the annihilation of the self. The body moves through stages; the deeper subject of experience remains.

This verse is therefore deeply stabilizing. It reframes death not as an absolute end but as another transition in a larger journey of embodied consciousness. Krishna is slowly loosening Arjuna's identification with bodily form so that grief, duty, and fear can all be seen from a wider horizon.

original_script:
मात्रास्पर्शास्तु कौन्तेय शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः।
आगमापायिनोऽनित्यास्तांस्तितिक्षस्व भारत।।2.14।।
transliteration:
mātrā-sparśhās tu kaunteya śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ
āgamāpāyino ’nityās tāṁs titikṣhasva bhārata
word_meanings: mātrā-sparśhāḥ—contact of the senses with their objects; tu—indeed; kaunteya—son of Kunti (Arjun); śhīta—cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkha—distress; dāḥ—givers; āgama—appearing; apāyinaḥ—disappearing; anityāḥ—non-permanent; tān—them; titikṣhasva—endeavor to tolerate; bhārata—descendant of Bharat
translation_english: O son of Kunti, the contact between the senses and their objects gives rise to feelings of heat and cold, pleasure and pain. These are temporary and fleeting; learn to tolerate them, O descendant of Bharat.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that contacts of the senses with their objects produce cold and heat, pleasure and pain; they come and go and are impermanent, so Arjuna must learn to endure them. Here the teaching shifts from metaphysical truth to practical discipline. Knowing that the self endures is one thing. Learning how to stand steady amid changing experience is another.

Pleasure and pain are described as arising from contact. This means they belong to the field of embodied experience, not to the deepest essence of the self. They are real at the level of life in the body, but they are transient. They come, stay for a while, and pass. Krishna wants Arjuna to stop treating passing states as ultimate realities.

For a new reader, this verse is tremendously practical. Much human suffering is intensified because temporary conditions are treated as if they define the whole of reality. Krishna does not deny discomfort, grief, or distress. He places them in a larger frame. They are part of embodied existence, but they are not the whole truth of what one is.

The call to endure is not a glorification of numbness. It is training in steadiness. If one reacts to every pleasant or painful contact as ultimate, the mind remains unstable and cannot see clearly. Endurance creates space between the passing experience and the deepest self.

This verse is also the beginning of karma-yogic resilience. Before Arjuna can act rightly, he must learn not to be governed by fluctuating emotional weather. Krishna is not removing pain from the world. He is teaching how not to be mastered by it.

original_script:
यं हि न व्यथयन्त्येते पुरुषं पुरुषर्षभ।
समदुःखसुखं धीरं सोऽमृतत्वाय कल्पते।।2.15।।
transliteration:
yaṁ hi na vyathayantyete puruṣhaṁ puruṣharṣhabha
sama-duḥkha-sukhaṁ dhīraṁ so ’mṛitatvāya kalpate
word_meanings: yam—whom; hi—verily; na—not; vyathayanti—disturb; ete—these; puruṣham—a person; puruṣha-ṛiṣhabha—the best of men; sama—equal; duḥkha—distress; sukham—happiness; dhīram—the wise; saḥ—he; amṛitatvāya—for immortality; kalpate—becomes eligible
translation_english: O best of men, that person who is not disturbed by these, and who remains steady in both pain and pleasure, becomes eligible for immortality.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the person whom these experiences do not afflict, who remains steady in pleasure and pain, is fit for immortality. This verse continues the previous teaching but gives it a spiritual destination. Endurance is not merely a coping skill. It is a sign of inner maturity aligned with the eternal.

To remain steady in pleasure and pain does not mean that sensations cease or that the body stops registering experience. It means that the core of identity is no longer thrown into confusion by changing conditions. Such a person is not dragged upward by pleasure or downward by pain because he or she is anchored in something deeper than both.

For a new reader, the phrase "fit for immortality" is very important. Krishna is not talking about mere emotional toughness. He is describing the kind of inward steadiness required to realize the deathless truth of the Self. If the mind is always captured by passing experiences, it cannot recognize what is beyond passing.

This verse also reveals the ethical value of equanimity. A person who is ruled by pleasure and pain cannot act consistently from truth. Such a person will bend duty to comfort and flee from whatever wounds the ego. But a person established in steadiness becomes capable of acting from dharma rather than from mood.

Thus, Krishna is already reshaping Arjuna for action. The path to immortality is not separate from the discipline needed to function rightly in the world. Steadiness before opposites becomes both spiritual practice and moral necessity.

original_script:
नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः।
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः।।2.16।।
transliteration:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ
ubhayor api dṛiṣhṭo ’ntas tv anayos tattva-darśhibhiḥ
word_meanings: na—never; asataḥ—of the unreal; vidyate—is; bhāvaḥ—endurance; na—never; abhāvaḥ—changing; vidyate—is; sataḥ—of the real; ubhayoḥ—of both; api—also; dṛiṣhṭaḥ—observed; antaḥ—conclusion; tu—but; anayoḥ—of them; tattva-darśhibhiḥ—by the seers of the truth
translation_english: The unreal has no permanent existence, and the real never ceases to be. The seers of the truth have concluded this by studying the nature of both.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the unreal never truly comes into being, and the real never ceases to be. The seers of truth have understood the distinction between these two. This verse introduces one of the most philosophically condensed teachings in the Gita. Krishna is distinguishing between what is transient and dependent, and what is abiding and ultimately real.

In context, the unreal does not mean that the world is simply nonexistent like a fantasy. It means that changing forms do not possess absolute, self-sufficient permanence. Bodies arise, alter, and disappear. Emotional states surge and fade. Social roles come and go. These are experientially real, but they are not ultimate. The real, by contrast, is that which does not vanish with change.

For a new reader, this verse is essential because it supplies the metaphysical ground for everything Krishna is saying about grief and death. If Arjuna takes the perishable body to be the full truth of a person, then he confuses the transient with the abiding. Wisdom begins by learning how to distinguish the two without denying the practical world.

The verse also introduces an important discipline of vision. The wise do not simply react to surfaces; they ask what in experience is changing and what is the unchanging basis that makes experience possible. This shift from surface to ground is one of the major movements of spiritual life.

Krishna is therefore giving Arjuna more than comfort. He is giving him a way of seeing reality. Once the distinction between the transient and the abiding becomes clear, fear begins to loosen and action can be rooted in something firmer than bodily change.

original_script:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम्।
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति।।2.17।।
transliteration:
avināśhi tu tadviddhi yena sarvam idaṁ tatam
vināśham avyayasyāsya na kaśhchit kartum arhati
word_meanings: avināśhi—imperishable; tu—but; tat—that; viddhi—know; yena—by whom; sarvam—all; idam—this; tatam—pervaded; vināśham—destruction; avyayasya—of the imperishable; asya—of it; na kaśhchit—no one; kartum—to do; arhati—is able
translation_english: Know that the consciousness which pervades the entire body is indestructible. No one can destroy the imperishable soul.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded. No one can bring about the destruction of that imperishable reality. This verse deepens the previous distinction by pointing directly to the all-pervading nature of the deathless principle.

What is indestructible is not a small inner object hidden inside the body. It is that reality by which all embodied life is pervaded and made possible. Krishna is expanding Arjuna's view beyond individual fear to the presence of the imperishable at the very heart of existence. That which truly is cannot be destroyed because destruction itself belongs only to the field of changing forms.

For a new reader, this verse can be approached in a simple way: there is a level of reality in us and in all beings that is not broken by bodily death. Krishna is asking Arjuna to shift his center of identification from the fragile outer layer of existence to the abiding essence that cannot be cut away by time.

This verse also stabilizes action by changing the scale at which one thinks. If the imperishable is present throughout all beings, then fear of bodily destruction, while still emotionally powerful, no longer has the same absolute claim. The deepest truth of the person stands beyond the reach of violence.

Krishna is not yet denying the pain of battle at the human level. But he is insisting that unless Arjuna knows what cannot be destroyed, he will remain trapped in a shallow understanding of loss. Wisdom begins when the imperishable is recognized as the real center of being.

original_script:
अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः।
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत।।2.18।।
transliteration:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śharīriṇaḥ
anāśhino ’prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata
word_meanings: antavantaḥ—perishable; ime—all these; dehāḥ—bodies; nityasya—of the eternal; uktāḥ—are said; śharīriṇaḥ—of the embodied soul; anāśhinaḥ—of the indestructible; aprameyasya—of the immeasurable; tasmāt—therefore; yudhyasva—fight; bhārata—descendant of Bharat
translation_english: Only the material body is perishable; the soul within is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable. Therefore, fight, O descendant of Bharat.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that these bodies are said to have an end, but the embodied Self is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable; therefore Arjuna should fight. This is one of the most direct places where metaphysics and duty meet. Krishna does not teach the immortality of the Self as an abstract consolation and then leave action untouched. He immediately draws a conclusion for conduct.

The body is finite and perishable. The Self, however, is not subject to the same limit. It cannot be measured, reduced, or destroyed in the way material things can. By calling it immeasurable, Krishna signals that the deepest identity of the person is not fully accessible to outward calculation or bodily categories.

For a new reader, this verse can feel startling because Krishna moves so quickly from spiritual truth to the command to fight. But that movement is exactly the point. If Arjuna truly understands that killing the body does not annihilate the Self, then the meaning of battle changes. The act remains grave, but it no longer appears as absolute destruction.

At the same time, this verse must be held carefully. Krishna is not trivializing violence. He is speaking within a context of dharma-yuddha, a war tied to duty and justice, not private aggression. His teaching is not a blanket license for harm. It is a correction to Arjuna's despair-based confusion about what death ultimately means.

This verse therefore shows the structure of the Gita's reasoning: right action must be grounded in right knowledge. Until Arjuna understands what the person truly is, he cannot understand what battle can and cannot destroy.

original_script:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम्।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते।।2.19।।
transliteration:
ya enaṁ vetti hantāraṁ yaśhchainaṁ manyate hatam
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; enam—this; vetti—thinks; hantāram—the killer; yaḥ—who; cha—and; enam—this; manyate—thinks; hatam—killed; ubhau—both; tau—they; na—not; vijānītaḥ—know; na—never; ayam—this; hanti—kills; na—nor; hanyate—is killed
translation_english: Neither he who thinks the soul a killer, nor he who thinks it killed, knows the truth; for the soul neither kills nor is killed.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one who thinks the Self kills, and one who thinks it is killed, both fail to understand. The Self neither kills nor is killed. This verse pushes even more sharply against ordinary assumptions. From the standpoint of ultimate truth, the deepest self is not the slayer and not the slain.

This does not mean that actions in the world are unreal in every practical sense. Bodies do kill bodies, and moral responsibility remains meaningful at the level of human action. But Krishna is introducing a deeper insight: the eternal Self is not identical with the perishable bodily process. Therefore the absolute language of annihilation does not apply to it.

For a new reader, this verse is crucial because it prevents confusion between the body and the Self. Much of Arjuna's despair comes from identifying the whole person with bodily life. Krishna is dismantling that identification. He is showing that what is most real in a being is not touched by killing in the way Arjuna fears.

At the same time, the verse invites subtle thinking. It should not be used to erase ethics. The Gita never teaches careless violence. Rather, it distinguishes levels of truth so that action may be understood without metaphysical ignorance. One must act responsibly in the world, but one must also know that the eternal self is not reducible to worldly processes.

This distinction between practical reality and ultimate truth is essential to the whole text. Krishna is training Arjuna to hold both together, to act without delusion, fear, or sentimental confusion.

original_script:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचि-
न्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे।।2.20।।
transliteration:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadāchin
nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
ajo nityaḥ śhāśhvato ’yaṁ purāṇo
na hanyate hanyamāne śharīre
word_meanings: na—never; jāyate—is born; mriyate—dies; vā—or; kadāchit—at any time; na—not; ayam—this; bhūtvā—having come into being; bhavitā—will come into being; vā—or; na—not; bhūyaḥ—again; ajaḥ—unborn; nityaḥ—eternal; śhāśhvataḥ—everlasting; ayam—this; purāṇaḥ—primeval; na—not; hanyate—is killed; hanyamāne—when killed; śharīre—the body
translation_english: The soul is neither born, nor does it ever die; nor having once existed, does it ever cease to be. The soul is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and primeval. It is not slain when the body is slain.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the Self is never born, never dies, and once having been, never ceases to be. It is unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient, and not slain when the body is slain. This verse is one of the clearest and most beautiful summaries of the Gita's teaching on the soul. It removes the self from the entire cycle of bodily coming-to-be and passing-away.

To say that the Self is unborn means it does not begin the way bodies do. To say it does not die means it is not ended by bodily destruction. To say it is ancient and everlasting means it does not merely survive as a temporary remainder; it stands outside the processes of decay that define material existence.

For a new reader, this verse is the heart of Krishna's first major argument. If this teaching is grasped even partially, fear of death begins to loosen its absolute grip. One may still mourn separation, but one need not interpret death as total extinction.

This verse also reveals why Krishna can speak with such composure. He is not indifferent to life; he sees it from the standpoint of the eternal. That standpoint changes the meaning of action, sorrow, and obligation. What appears unbearable from bodily identification becomes thinkable, though still grave, from spiritual understanding.

Arjuna's education has now clearly begun. Krishna is taking him out of the narrow field of grief and into the vast horizon of the deathless Self. Everything that follows in the Gita, duty, equanimity, action, devotion, and liberation, will stand on this foundation.

original_script:
वेदाविनाशिनं नित्यं य एनमजमव्ययम्।
कथं स पुरुषः पार्थ कं घातयति हन्ति कम्।।2.21।।
transliteration:
vedāvināśhinaṁ nityaṁ ya enam ajam avyayam
kathaṁ sa puruṣhaḥ pārtha kaṁ ghātayati hanti kam
word_meanings: veda—knows; avināśhinam—indestructible; nityam—always; yaḥ—who; enam—this (soul); ajam—unborn; avyayam—immutable; katham—how; saḥ—that; puruṣhaḥ—person; pārtha—Arjun; kam—whom; ghātayati—causes to be killed; hanti—kills; kam—whom
translation_english: O Partha, how can a person who knows that the soul is indestructible, eternal, unborn, and immutable, kill anyone or cause anyone to be killed?
deep_dive_text:
Krishna asks how one who knows the Self to be indestructible, eternal, unborn, and imperishable could think of killing or causing anyone to be killed. This verse continues the argument from the nature of the soul, but it now frames that truth as a question. The form matters. Krishna is not merely adding information; he is challenging Arjuna to think consistently from what has already been said.

If the deepest identity of a being cannot be destroyed, then the ordinary assumption that bodily death is total annihilation must be abandoned. Krishna is pressing Arjuna to see that his grief is still operating from an unexamined bodily standpoint. He has heard that the soul is deathless, but he has not yet allowed that truth to reshape his emotional logic.

For a new reader, this verse is a good example of how the Gita teaches. It does not only make declarations; it also demands a reorganization of reasoning. Krishna wants Arjuna's mind to become coherent with spiritual truth, not merely acquainted with it.

This verse also helps prevent sentimental misunderstanding of nonviolence. Krishna is not saying that because the soul is eternal, bodily life does not matter at all. Rather, he is insisting that the deepest truth of the person transcends the bodily event of death. Without that distinction, one remains trapped in fear and moral confusion.

The question therefore functions like a lever. Krishna is prying Arjuna loose from his old assumptions. Once the deathless nature of the Self is granted, the meaning of action, killing, and grief must be reconsidered from the ground up.

original_script:
वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय
नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि।
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णा-
न्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही।।2.22।।
transliteration:
vāsāṁsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya
navāni gṛihṇāti naro ’parāṇi
tathā śharīrāni vihāya jīrṇān
yanyāni saṁyāti navāni dehī
word_meanings: vāsāṁsi—garments; jīrṇāni—worn out; yathā—as; vihāya—giving up; navāni—new; gṛihṇāti—takes up; naraḥ—a man; aparāṇi—other; tathā—similarly; śharīrāni—bodies; vihāya—giving up; jīrṇāni—worn out; anyāni—other; saṁyāti—enters; navāni—new; dehī—the embodied soul
translation_english: As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that just as a person casts off worn-out garments and puts on new ones, so the embodied Self casts off old bodies and takes up new ones. This is one of the most famous and vivid metaphors in the Gita. It takes the difficult teaching of rebirth and expresses it through something ordinary and accessible: changing clothes.

The comparison works because garments are associated with us, but they are not our true identity. We wear them, use them, and eventually discard them when they are no longer fit. Krishna is saying that the body stands in a similar relation to the soul. It is not meaningless, but it is not ultimate. It is a covering and instrument, not the deepest self.

For a new reader, this verse is often the point where the teaching becomes more imaginable. The fear of death depends heavily on identifying selfhood with the body. Once the body is seen as a temporary garment, death takes on a different meaning. It becomes a transition, not annihilation.

At the same time, the metaphor should not be made shallow. Changing bodies is not treated as trivial in the Gita. Embodied life remains morally serious because action, karma, and spiritual growth take place through it. But the verse removes the idea that bodily loss is the destruction of the true person.

This image is also psychologically soothing. Krishna knows Arjuna is overwhelmed by the visible destruction of bodies. So he offers an image that helps the imagination loosen its fear. The soul remains, even as forms are changed. That truth does not erase sorrow instantly, but it opens a door beyond despair.

original_script:
नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः।
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः।।2.23।।
transliteration:
nainaṁ chhindanti śhastrāṇi nainaṁ dahati pāvakaḥ
na chainaṁ kledayantyāpo na śhoṣhayati mārutaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; enam—this (soul); chhindanti—shred/cut; śhastrāṇi—weapons; na—never; enam—this; dahati—burns; pāvakaḥ—fire; na—never; cha—also; enam—this; kledayanti—wet; āpaḥ—water; na—never; śhoṣhayati—withers/dries; mārutaḥ—wind
translation_english: Weapons cannot shred the soul, nor can fire burn it. Water cannot wet it, nor can the wind dry it.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that weapons do not cut the Self, fire does not burn it, water does not wet it, and wind does not dry it.
