For a new reader, this verse explains why spiritual struggle can remain intense even after visible habits change. The inner taste, the subtle attraction, the emotional charge, often persists. Krishna does not pretend otherwise.

The transformative element is "seeing the Supreme." Higher vision does what suppression alone cannot do. When consciousness tastes something deeper, fuller, and more real than the old object of craving, the craving itself begins to lose force.

This is a central principle in the Gita: lower attachment is not finally overcome by negation alone, but by a higher fulfillment. Arjuna will not be freed merely by refusing certain actions. He must learn to anchor in something greater than what now binds him. That is why the path is not only restraint, but realization.

original_script:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः।
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनी हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः।।2.60।।
transliteration:
yatato hyapi kaunteya puruṣhasya vipaśhchitaḥ
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṁ manaḥ
word_meanings: yatataḥ—while endeavoring; hi—certainly; api—even; kaunteya—Arjun; puruṣhasya—of a person; vipaśhchitaḥ—learned; indriyāṇi—the senses; pramāthīni—agitated; haranti—carry away; prasabham—forcibly; manaḥ—the mind
translation_english: The senses are so strong and impetuous, O Arjuna, that they forcibly carry away the mind even of a man of discrimination who is endeavoring to control them.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna warns that the turbulent senses can forcibly carry away the mind even of a discerning person striving for mastery. This verse is both realistic and humbling. It prevents spiritual pride. One may understand many truths and still remain vulnerable if the senses are not truly mastered.

The word turbulent is important. The senses are not presented as neutral channels alone, but as active forces with momentum. Their movement can be strong enough to overwhelm even sincere effort. Krishna is therefore warning Arjuna against underestimating the power of embodied habit and attraction.

For a new reader, this verse is often deeply consoling. It explains why sincere spiritual effort can still feel unstable. Difficulty does not necessarily mean hypocrisy; it may simply mean the senses retain great force. The path requires vigilance as well as insight.

This verse also protects against premature confidence. Knowing what is right is not the same as being inwardly secure in it. Discipline must continue until understanding has become embodied enough to withstand pressure.

Krishna's teaching remains compassionate through its realism. He does not say, "If you know the truth, you will never struggle." He says that even the wise-in-training can be swept away if care is lacking. This makes the path serious, but it also makes it honest. Spiritual growth is not magic; it is transformation through disciplined seeing and living.

original_script:
तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः।
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.61।।
transliteration:
tāni sarvāṇi saṁyamya yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ
vaśhe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: tāni—those (senses); sarvāṇi—all; saṁyamya—restraining; yuktaḥ—engaged in Yoga; āsīta—should sit; mat-paraḥ—with the mind in Me; vaśhe—under control; hi—certainly; yasya—whose; indriyāṇi—senses; tasya—his; prajñā—wisdom; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: One who restrains his senses, keeping them under full control, and fixes his consciousness upon Me, is known as a man of steady wisdom.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that having restrained all the senses, one should sit disciplined, intent on him; for the one whose senses are under control, wisdom is firmly established. This verse unites sense-control with devotion. Earlier Krishna spoke of withdrawing the senses and of the difficulty they create. Now he gives the positive center around which restraint becomes stable: orientation toward the Divine.

This matters because self-control without a higher focus often becomes brittle. One may suppress impulses for a while, yet without a deeper center the mind continues to circle around what it is denying. Krishna therefore does not teach mere negation. He says the senses are best mastered when life is gathered around him.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it shows that the Gita does not separate discipline from relationship with the Divine. True steadiness is not only psychological technique. It is supported by devotion, remembrance, and inner alignment with Krishna as the highest reference point.

The phrase about sitting disciplined can be understood literally and inwardly. One must establish oneself, collect oneself, and cease being dragged outward by every impulse. This seatedness becomes possible when the self stops orbiting sense objects and begins resting in something higher.

Thus Krishna closes the discussion of sensory turbulence by giving the remedy: restraint joined to God-centeredness. Wisdom becomes firm not merely when desire is resisted, but when the whole person is reoriented toward the highest reality.

original_script:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते।
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते।।2.62।।
transliteration:
dhyāyato viṣhayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣhūpajāyate
saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho ’bhijāyate
word_meanings: dhyāyataḥ—while contemplating; viṣhayān—objects of the senses; puṁsaḥ—of a person; saṅgaḥ—attachment; teṣhu—in them; upajāyate—develops; saṅgāt—from attachment; sañjāyate—arises; kāmaḥ—desire; kāmāt—from desire; krodhaḥ—anger; abhijāyate—is born
translation_english: While contemplating the objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them, and from such attachment, desire is born; from desire, anger arises.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna explains that when a person dwells on sense objects, attachment to them arises; from attachment comes desire, and from desire comes anger. This is one of the Gita's most psychologically exact descriptions of downfall. It begins not with some dramatic evil, but with attention. What the mind repeatedly dwells on starts to gather emotional weight.

Attachment is the bridge between perception and bondage. One may encounter an object and move on, but if the mind keeps returning, investing, replaying, and enjoying inwardly, attachment forms. From there desire grows, because what is inwardly held becomes something one wants to possess or experience fully.

Anger emerges when desire is blocked, threatened, or frustrated. Krishna is mapping a chain reaction. The process is not random. It has stages, and those stages begin earlier than most people notice.

For a new reader, this verse is extraordinarily practical. It shows that spiritual downfall often starts quietly in thought life. Before the hand acts, the mind has usually been rehearsing attachment. This means freedom also begins early, at the level of what one lets the mind dwell upon.

The verse is not condemning all sensory life. It is diagnosing obsession. Krishna is teaching Arjuna how bondage grows so that it may be interrupted before it matures into inner violence. The battlefield here becomes inward. Victory begins with vigilance over attention.

original_script:
क्रोधाद्भवति सम्मोहः सम्मोहात्स्मृतिविभ्रमः।
स्मृतिभ्रंशाद्बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात्प्रणश्यति।।2.63।।
transliteration:
krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛiti-vibhramaḥ
smṛiti-bhraṁśhād buddhi-nāśho buddhi-nāśhāt praṇaśhyati
word_meanings: krodhāt—from anger; bhavati—comes; sammohaḥ—delusion; sammohāt—from delusion; smṛiti—memory; vibhramaḥ—bewilderment; smṛiti-bhraṁśhāt—from loss of memory; buddhi-nāśhaḥ—destruction of intellect; buddhi-nāśhāt—from destruction of intellect; praṇaśhyati—one perishes
translation_english: Anger leads to clouding of judgment, which results in bewilderment of memory. When memory is bewildered, the intellect is destroyed; and when the intellect is destroyed, one is ruined.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues the chain: from anger comes delusion; from delusion, loss of memory; from loss of memory, destruction of discernment; and when discernment is destroyed, one is ruined. This verse completes one of the most famous psychological sequences in the Gita. It shows how quickly a seemingly small inner movement can spiral into total collapse of judgment.

Delusion here means more than confusion in a general sense. It means distortion of reality under the pressure of emotional agitation. Once anger enters, the mind no longer sees proportion. Then memory is lost, not in the sense of forgetting facts alone, but forgetting what one knows, values, and has learned. One loses living contact with wisdom.

From there discernment, the faculty that separates right from wrong and real from unreal, is damaged. When that inner compass fails, the person may still be intelligent in the ordinary sense, but direction is gone. Ruin follows because action now proceeds without guidance.

For a new reader, this verse is one of the clearest examples of the Gita's realism. Krishna understands that moral collapse is usually a process, not a single event. It starts with repeated dwelling, attachment, and frustrated desire, and only later becomes visible as destruction.

The practical lesson is urgent: one must not wait until ruin is advanced before responding. The chain must be noticed early. Krishna is teaching Arjuna, and the reader, how to guard the mind before agitation grows into full spiritual disintegration.

original_script:
रागद्वेषवियुक्तैस्तु विषयानिन्द्रियैश्चरन्।
आत्मवश्यैर्विधेयात्मा प्रसादमधिगच्छति।।2.64।।
transliteration:
rāga-dveṣha-viyuktais tu viṣhayān indriyaiśh charan
ātma-vaśhyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigachchhati
word_meanings: rāga—attachment; dveṣha—aversion; viyuktaiḥ—free from; tu—but; viṣhayān—objects of the senses; indriyaiḥ—with the senses; charan—acting; ātma-vaśhyaiḥ—under one's own control; vidheya-ātmā—one who has controlled his mind; prasādam—peace/mercy; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: But a person free from all attachment and aversion and able to control his senses through regulative principles of freedom can obtain the complete mercy of the Lord.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one who moves among sense objects with senses under control, free from attraction and aversion, and governed by the self, attains peace. This verse is an important balance to the previous warnings. The Gita is not calling for total abandonment of the world. It is teaching a new way of moving through the world.

The key phrase is freedom from attraction and aversion. Bondage comes not from objects alone, but from the inner reactions that cling to some and hate others. When these compulsive reactions subside, one may still encounter the world without becoming its captive.

For a new reader, this verse is liberating. Spiritual life in the Gita is not a program of fear toward ordinary experience. The senses can function, objects can be encountered, and life can be lived. What must change is the mode of relation. Instead of slavery to liking and disliking, there must be mastery and inward composure.

Peace here is not passivity. It is the calm that comes when the self is no longer endlessly pushed and pulled by appetite. Such peace makes wisdom sustainable because the mind is no longer in constant reaction.

Krishna is showing Arjuna a middle way between indulgence and repression. One need not be dragged outward, nor must one violently reject all contact. The disciplined person can move among things while inwardly remaining free. That freedom is a major mark of steady wisdom.

original_script:
प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते।
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते।।2.65।।
transliteration:
prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hānir asyopajāyate
prasanna-chetaso hy āśhu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣhṭhate
word_meanings: prasāde—on attainment of tranquility; sarva—all; duḥkhānām—of miseries; hāniḥ—destruction; asya—his; upajāyate—takes place; prasanna-chetasaḥ—of the happy-minded; hi—certainly; āśhu—very soon; buddhiḥ—intellect; paryavatiṣhṭhate—becomes established
translation_english: For one thus satisfied [in consciousness], the three-fold miseries of material existence exist no longer; in such satisfied consciousness, one’s intelligence is soon well established.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that in such peace all sorrows come to an end, for the understanding of one whose mind is peaceful soon becomes steady. This verse explains why inner peace is not a luxury but a necessity for wisdom. When the mind is agitated, sorrow multiplies and discernment weakens. When peace is present, the mind can stabilize and see clearly.

The statement that sorrows come to an end should be understood carefully. It does not mean that no painful circumstance ever occurs again. It means that the inner structure that keeps generating and magnifying suffering begins to dissolve. A peaceful mind does not endlessly feed grief, fear, and desire through reactive interpretation.

For a new reader, this verse is deeply practical. Many people treat peace as a side benefit of spiritual life. Krishna treats it as central, because without it the intellect cannot settle. Insight depends on the condition of the mind that receives it.

The verse also explains why Krishna has spent so much time on equanimity, sense-control, and non-attachment. These are not moral decorations. They are the conditions under which understanding becomes firm.

In Arjuna's case, this is especially relevant. His grief has become intellectually destabilizing. Krishna is teaching him not only what to think, but how to recover the interior peace without which right thinking cannot take hold. Peace becomes the soil in which wisdom roots itself.

original_script:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना।
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरजशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम्।।2.66।।
transliteration:
nāsti buddhir ayuktasya na chāyuktasya bhāvanā
na chābhāvayataḥ śhāntir aśhāntasya kutaḥ sukham
word_meanings: na asti—there is no; buddhiḥ—intellect; ayuktasya—for the unsteady; na—not; cha—also; ayuktasya—for the unsteady; bhāvanā—contemplation; na—not; cha—also; abhāvayataḥ—of one who does not meditate; śhāntiḥ—peace; aśhāntasya—for the restless; kutaḥ—wherefrom; sukham—happiness
translation_english: One who is not connected [with the Supreme] can have neither transcendental intelligence nor a steady mind, without which there is no possibility of peace. And how can there be any happiness without peace?
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that for the unintegrated there is no wisdom, no meditation, no peace, and without peace, how can there be happiness? This verse presents a sharp chain in the opposite direction of the earlier downfall sequence. Just as attachment leads downward step by step, so too inner fragmentation blocks the entire ascent toward happiness.

The person who is not integrated or yoked together inwardly cannot sustain higher understanding because the mind is scattered. Without understanding, meditation cannot deepen. Without meditation, peace does not stabilize. Without peace, happiness remains out of reach because the inner life never settles into coherence.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it reveals how interconnected spiritual faculties are. Wisdom, meditation, peace, and happiness are not separate compartments. They support one another. One cannot expect stable happiness while ignoring the disorder of attention, motive, and mind.

This also corrects modern confusion between stimulation and happiness. Many people experience intense activity, distraction, or momentary pleasure and call it happiness. Krishna's standard is different. Real happiness requires peace, and peace requires integration.

The verse therefore deepens the Gita's psychology. Suffering is not only about painful events; it is also about the structure of an ungoverned mind. Krishna is offering not a temporary mood adjustment, but a reordering of the whole inner life from which genuine happiness can emerge.

original_script:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते।
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि।।2.67।।
transliteration:
indriyāṇāṁ hi charatāṁ yan mano ’nuvidhīyate
tad asya harati prajñāṁ vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi
word_meanings: indriyāṇām—of the senses; hi—certainly; charatām—while wandering; yat—which; manaḥ—mind; anuvidhīyate—follows; tat—that; asya—his; harati—carries away; prajñām—wisdom; vāyuḥ—wind; nāvam—boat; iva—as; ambhasi—on the water
translation_english: Just as a strong wind sweeps away a boat on the water, even one of the wandering senses on which the mind focuses can carry away a man's wisdom.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that just as a boat on the water is swept away by the wind, so even one roving sense on which the mind dwells can carry away the understanding. The image is vivid and memorable. A boat may be well made and intended for a clear destination, yet a strong wind can drive it off course. So too the mind, even when it possesses some understanding, can be destabilized by unguarded sensory fixation.

The emphasis on even one sense is important. One does not always fall through total collapse on every front. Sometimes a single unmastered channel becomes enough to drag the whole inner life into confusion. Krishna is warning against selective carelessness.

For a new reader, this verse is again intensely practical. People often assume that because they are strong in many areas, one weakness does not matter. Krishna says it matters greatly. Where the mind habitually roves unchecked, understanding can be carried away.

The verse also helps explain why vigilance is continuous. Spiritual maturity does not create a state where one may abandon watchfulness casually. Wisdom must be protected, not anxiously but steadily.

In context, Krishna is training Arjuna in the seriousness of inner governance. If the mind is to remain established in truth amid action, even small channels of unexamined attachment must be treated carefully. The voyage of the soul requires steering, not drift.

original_script:
तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता।।2.68।।
transliteration:
tasmād yasya mahā-bāho nigṛihītāni sarvaśhaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣhṭhitā
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; yasya—whose; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; nigṛihītāni—so restrained; sarvaśhaḥ—all around; indriyāṇi—the senses; indriya-arthebhyaḥ—from sense objects; tasya—his; prajñā—wisdom; pratiṣhṭhitā—is fixed
translation_english: Therefore, O mighty-armed one, whose senses are restrained from their objects, his intelligence is certainly fixed.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna concludes from the previous teaching that the one whose senses are completely withdrawn from their objects on all sides has firmly established wisdom. This verse restates the central practical criterion after the chain of downfall and the boat metaphor. Wisdom is not measured only by speech about truth, but by whether the senses obey the self rather than rule it.

The phrase "on all sides" suggests completeness of discipline. Krishna is not advocating a partial or occasional self-command that works only under convenient conditions. The sage's wisdom is stable because the sense life has been integrated into a higher order.

For a new reader, this verse reinforces an important lesson: spiritual insight must become embodied. It is not enough to admire steadiness or understand it intellectually. The senses, attention, and habits of life must actually come under governance.

This does not mean that the world must be feared. It means that freedom requires a center strong enough that outward contact no longer dictates inward condition. Wisdom is established where the self governs experience rather than being governed by it.

Krishna is now bringing together the themes of the whole section: attention, desire, anger, sense-control, peace, and understanding. The one who masters the senses is not merely morally impressive. He or she has created the conditions in which true wisdom can remain steady under pressure.

original_script:
या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी।
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः।।2.69।।
transliteration:
yā niśhā sarva-bhūtānāṁ tasyāṁ jāgarti saṁyamī
yasyāṁ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśhā paśhyato muneḥ
word_meanings: yā—what; niśhā—night; sarva-bhūtānām—for all living beings; tasyām—in that; jāgarti—is awake; saṁyamī—the self-controlled; yasyām—in which; jāgrati—are awake; bhūtāni—all beings; sā—that; niśhā—night; paśhyataḥ—who sees; muneḥ—for the sage
translation_english: What is night for all beings is the time of awakening for the self-controlled; and the time of awakening for all beings is night for the introspective sage.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that what is night for all beings is wakefulness for the disciplined one, and what beings are awake to is night for the sage who sees. This verse is poetic and paradoxical. It describes not a difference in clock-time, but a difference in consciousness. The values, concerns, and realities that dominate ordinary life do not hold the same place for the knower of truth.

What the world is awake to, sensory excitement, acquisition, egoic success, restless activity, often appears like darkness to the sage because it does not reveal the deepest reality. What the sage is awake to, the Self, the eternal, inward freedom, appears obscure or invisible to the ordinary mind.

For a new reader, this verse is one of the clearest descriptions of spiritual reversal. Awakening is not simply adding a few holy ideas to an otherwise unchanged consciousness. It is a revaluation of what counts as real, urgent, and meaningful.

This verse also explains why genuine spiritual life can seem strange to the ordinary world. The sage is not merely behaving differently; he is inhabiting a different center of awareness. Therefore what one mind pursues passionately, the other can pass by without agitation.

Krishna is showing Arjuna that wisdom changes not only conduct but perception itself. The realized person does not simply resist the world with effort. He sees from a different light, and therefore the old fascinations lose their authority.

original_script:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत्।
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी।।2.70।।
transliteration:
āpūryamāṇam achala-pratiṣhṭhaṁ
samudram āpaḥ praviśhanti yadvat
tadvat kāmā yaṁ praviśhanti sarve
sa śhāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī
word_meanings: āpūryamāṇam—filled from all sides; achala-pratiṣhṭham—steadily situated; samudram—the ocean; āpaḥ—waters; praviśhanti—enter; yadvat—as; tadvat—similarly; kāmāḥ—desires; yam—whom; praviśhanti—enter; sarve—all; saḥ—that person; śhāntim—peace; āpnoti—attains; na—not; kāma-kāmī—one who desires to fulfill desires
translation_english: As the ocean, though being ever filled by rivers from all sides, remains steady and undisturbed, so does the person into whom all desires enter without causing disturbance attain peace; not the one who strives to satisfy such desires.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that just as the ocean remains full and undisturbed though many rivers enter it, so the person into whom desires enter without causing disturbance attains peace, not the one who longs to satisfy desires. This is one of the chapter's grandest images. The ocean is vast, stable, and not defined by each new influx. Desires may arise, impressions may come, but the sage is not overflowed or agitated by them.

The contrast with the desire-driven person is sharp. One who longs to satisfy desire treats every incoming impulse as a command. Such a person can never rest, because desire multiplies faster than satisfaction can contain it. The sage, by contrast, possesses an inward magnitude that does not become smaller or larger with each new movement of wanting.

For a new reader, this verse gives a beautiful picture of peace. Peace is not the total absence of incoming experience. It is vastness of being. The mind becomes like an ocean rather than a puddle constantly disturbed by every touch.

This image also clarifies why suppression alone is insufficient. The ocean is not tense. It is spacious. Krishna is pointing to a fullness that does not need to clutch at each desire in order to feel complete.

The verse thus returns to one of the chapter's central themes: true peace does not come by feeding endless want. It comes by growing into a consciousness wide and grounded enough that desires lose the power to dictate identity.

original_script:
विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमिगच्छति।।2.71।।
transliteration:
vihāya kāmān yaḥ sarvān pumānśh charati niḥspṛihaḥ
nirmamo nirahankāraḥ sa śhāntim adhigachchhati
word_meanings: vihāya—giving up; kāmān—material desires; yaḥ—who; sarvān—all; pumān—a person; charati—lives; niḥspṛihaḥ—free from cravings; nirmamaḥ—without a sense of 'mine'; nirahankāraḥ—without ego; saḥ—he; śhāntim—peace; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: That person who gives up all material desires and lives free from cravings, without any sense of 'mine' and 'I' (ego), attains true peace.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the person who abandons all desires, moves without craving, free from possessiveness and ego, attains peace. This verse brings the teaching down from image to direct ethical and psychological instruction. Desire, craving, possessiveness, and ego are named as the principal forms of bondage that must be loosened.

To move without craving does not mean to become inert. It means to act without the restless insistence that life must satisfy the ego's demands. To be free from possessiveness means not organizing identity around "mine." To be free from ego means not making the self-image the center of action and interpretation.

For a new reader, this verse is a concise summary of the inner freedom Krishna has been describing from many angles. Peace is not something added from outside. It arises when the compulsions that disturb the mind are allowed to fall away.

This teaching is also deeply social. Possessiveness and ego not only torment the individual; they distort relationships, duty, and action. The person who moves without them becomes capable of participation in the world without constantly converting everything into material for self-expansion.

Thus the verse does not describe extinction of personhood. It describes purification of the person. Action remains, movement remains, life remains, but they are no longer organized around hunger, ownership, and self-importance. That is the peace of the wise.

original_script:
एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति।
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति।।2.72।।
transliteration:
eṣhā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṁ prāpya vimuhyati
sthitvāsyām anta-kāle ’pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛichchhati
word_meanings: eṣhā—this; brāhmī—divine; sthitiḥ—state; pārtha—Arjun; na—never; enam—this; prāpya—attaining; vimuhyati—is deluded; sthitvā—being situated; asyām—in this; anta-kāle—at the end of life; api—even; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation from material existence; ṛichchhati—attains
translation_english: This is the divine state, O Partha. Having attained this, one is never again deluded. Even if one is situated in this state at the final hour of life, one attains liberation from material existence.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by saying that this is the state of Brahman. Having attained it, one is no longer deluded; abiding in it even at the time of death, one attains Brahma-nirvana. This final verse gathers the whole chapter into a single conclusion. The teaching on the Self, equanimity, action, sense-control, and steady wisdom has not been a collection of disconnected insights. It has been a description of a state of being.

To reach this state is to be freed from delusion, from the confusion that identifies the Self with body, desire, fear, and ego. The chapter began with Arjuna overwhelmed by grief and uncertainty. It ends with a vision of unshakable clarity. That contrast defines the whole movement of the teaching.

For a new reader, the mention of abiding in this state even at death is especially important. The chapter has repeatedly returned to mortality, fear, and the meaning of bodily ending. Krishna now says that if one is established in this wisdom, death itself cannot overthrow one's realization. The deepest victory is not external conquest but freedom from delusion at the final transition.

Brahma-nirvana here points to release into the peace of ultimate reality. The chapter closes not on the battlefield as such, but on liberation. That is deliberate. Krishna has taken Arjuna from despair about action to a vision in which action, knowledge, and liberation belong together.

This final verse therefore seals the second chapter as the foundational map of the Gita. It shows where the whole teaching is heading: from grief to wisdom, from bondage to freedom, from restless selfhood to abiding in the Real.

original_script: साङ्ख्ययोगः
transliteration: Sāṅkhya Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of Knowledge (Sankhya Yoga)
deep_dive_text: Krishna begins his teaching by shifting Arjuna's perspective from the temporary body to the eternal Soul (Atman). He explains that the Soul cannot be burned, wet, or killed—it simply changes bodies like a person changes clothes. He introduces the concept of 'Sthitaprajna'—the person of steady wisdom who remains calm in both success and failure.

original_script: अथ तृतीयोऽध्यायः - कर्मयोगः
transliteration: Karma Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Karma—action; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Action
deep_dive_text: Chapter 3 transitions the text from early Vedic ritualism to the internalization of sacrifice. Krishna explains that physical inaction is impossible; thus, the path to liberation lies in 'Karma Yoga'—performing action as 'Yajña' (selfless sacrifice). By acting in alignment with the cosmic cycle (*Chakra*), the individual contributes to the maintenance of the world (*Lokasamgraha*) without incurring personal karmic debt.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन।
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव।।3.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
jyāyasī chet karmaṇas te matā buddhir janārdana
tat kiṁ karmaṇi ghore māṁ niyojayasi keśhava
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjun said; jyāyasī—superior; chet—if; karmaṇaḥ—than action; te—by you; matā—is thought; buddhiḥ—intellect; janārdana—Krishna; tat—then; kim—why; karmaṇi—in action; ghore—terrible; mām—me; niyojayasi—you engage; keśhava—Krishna
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Janardana, O Keshava, if You consider knowledge superior to action, why do You urge me to engage in this terrible war?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks Krishna why, if knowledge is considered superior to action, he is being urged toward what seems like a terrible action. This question arises naturally from everything Krishna has said in Chapter 2. Arjuna has heard about the eternal Self, equanimity, wisdom, and the person established in steady understanding. He now wonders why, if such wisdom is the goal, he should engage in violent duty at all.

This is not a foolish question. It shows that Arjuna is listening carefully and trying to reconcile what he has heard. If inward knowledge is higher than bodily identification, then why not withdraw from action and pursue contemplative stillness? Why fight? Why not renounce? Arjuna senses a tension between wisdom and worldly duty, and he wants Krishna to resolve it plainly.

For a new reader, this verse is crucial because it raises one of the most important issues in the whole Gita: is the spiritual life a path away from action, or a way of acting differently within the world? Many people assume that higher truth must mean withdrawal from difficult responsibility. Arjuna voices exactly that assumption.

The phrase "terrible action" shows that his emotional struggle has not disappeared simply because Krishna has taught metaphysics. Arjuna still sees battle as fearsome and morally costly. Wisdom has begun to challenge his despair, but it has not yet clarified how action and realization fit together.

This verse therefore opens Chapter 3 by forcing Krishna to address the relationship between knowledge and work directly. The answer will become one of the defining contributions of the Gita: liberation is not found by abandoning action alone, but by transforming the consciousness in which action is done.

original_script:
व्यामिश्रेणेव वाक्येन बुद्धिं मोहयसीव मे।
तदेकं वद निश्चित्य येन श्रेयोऽहमाप्नुयाम्।।3.2।।
transliteration:
vyāmiśhreṇeva vākyena buddhiṁ mohayasīva me
tad ekaṁ vada niśhchitya yena śhreyo ’ham āpnuyām
word_meanings: vyāmiśhreṇa—ambiguous; iva—as if; vākyena—by words; buddhim—intellect; mohayasi—you are confusing; iva—as if; me—my; tat—therefore; ekam—one; vada—speak; niśhchitya—decisively; yena—by which; śhreyaḥ—the highest good; aham—I; āpnuyām—may attain
translation_english: My intellect is bewildered by Your seemingly ambiguous advice. Please tell me decisively which path is best for me.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that Krishna's words seem to confuse his understanding, as if they were mixed or ambiguous, and he asks to be told decisively the one path by which he may attain the highest good. This is a very honest verse. Arjuna is not pretending to understand more than he does. He feels that Krishna has praised wisdom while also commanding action, and he experiences those emphases as conflicting.

The request for one clear path is deeply human. When faced with complexity, the mind often longs for a single, simple instruction that removes all tension. Arjuna wants clarity without remainder. He wants to know, plainly and decisively, what leads to the highest good.

For a new reader, this verse explains why the Gita must now become more nuanced. Spiritual life does not always resolve into a simplistic either-or. Krishna will have to show that knowledge and action, far from being enemies, can belong to a single path when rightly understood.

This verse also shows that Arjuna's discipleship is active, not passive. He is not merely receiving words. He is testing them, struggling with them, and asking for integration. This is the mark of serious spiritual inquiry.

The phrase about the highest good remains central. Arjuna is not asking what is easiest or least painful. He wants what truly leads to liberation and right living. Krishna's answer to that desire will define karma yoga as a path capable of uniting wisdom, discipline, and worldly duty without contradiction.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
लोकेऽस्मिन्द्विविधा निष्ठा पुरा प्रोक्ता मयानघ।
ज्ञानयोगेन साङ्ख्यानां कर्मयोगेन योगिनाम्।।3.3।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
loke ’smin dvi-vidhā niṣhṭhā purā proktā mayānagha
jñāna-yogena sāṅkhyānāṁ karma-yogena yoginām
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; loke—in the world; asmin—this; dvi-vidhā—two kinds of; niṣhṭhā—faith/paths; purā—formerly; proktā—were explained; mayā—by Me; anagha—O sinless one; jñāna-yogena—by the path of knowledge; sāṅkhyānām—for those inclined toward contemplation; karma-yogena—by the path of action; yoginām—for those inclined toward action
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: O sinless one, I have already explained that there are two paths in this world: the path of knowledge for those inclined toward contemplation, and the path of action for those inclined toward work.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna replies that in this world a twofold commitment has been taught: the path of knowledge for the contemplative and the path of action for those inclined to yoga through work. This is the first direct resolution of Arjuna's confusion. Krishna acknowledges that there are distinct orientations in spiritual life, but he does not set them against one another as mutually hostile absolutes.

The contemplative path emphasizes discrimination, inward reflection, and direct knowledge of the Self. The path of action emphasizes disciplined engagement in the world without selfish attachment. Krishna is showing that different temperaments and situations require different forms of emphasis. Yet both are ordered toward liberation when rightly followed.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it prevents a false simplification. The Gita does not insist that every person must live in exactly the same way externally. What matters is not uniform outward form, but whether one's path genuinely leads beyond ego and bondage.

At the same time, Krishna is not giving Arjuna permission to choose whichever path feels most comfortable under the pressure of fear. Arjuna's situation, role, and inner condition matter. The path of action is not inferior merely because it is outwardly demanding. For him, it is the necessary path.

This verse thus opens a central Gita principle: the highest truth can be approached through different disciplines, but the right discipline for a given person is determined by reality, temperament, and duty, not by momentary aversion to pain.

original_script:
न कर्मणामनारम्भान्नैष्कर्म्यं पुरुषोऽश्नुते।
न च संन्यसनादेव सिद्धिं समधिगच्छति।।3.4।।
transliteration:
na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣhkarmyaṁ puruṣho ’śhnute
na cha sannyasanād eva siddhiṁ samadhigachchhati
word_meanings: na—not; karmaṇām—of actions; anārambhāt—by non-performance; naiṣhkarmyam—freedom from karma; puruṣhaḥ—a person; aśhnute—attains; na—not; cha—also; sannyasanāt—by renunciation; eva—only; siddhim—perfection; samadhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: One cannot achieve freedom from karmic reactions by merely abstaining from work, nor can one attain perfection by renunciation alone.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that a person does not attain freedom from action merely by refraining from work, nor does one reach perfection simply by external renunciation. This verse strikes directly at Arjuna's temptation. Withdrawal by itself is not liberation. One cannot become free merely by not doing.

The teaching is subtle and essential. Bondage does not reside only in visible action. It resides in ignorance, ego, desire, and attachment. If those remain untouched, then outer inactivity may simply conceal inner turmoil. One may sit still while the mind continues acting feverishly through memory, fantasy, fear, and craving.

For a new reader, this verse is one of the Gita's strongest correctives to superficial spirituality. It is possible to renounce externally while remaining inwardly bound. One can leave duties, roles, or work without actually overcoming the forces that make action selfish and deluded.

Krishna is therefore rescuing Arjuna from a false solution. The desire to avoid battle by outwardly stepping back may look spiritual, but if it arises from grief, fear, and confusion, it is not true renunciation. It is only escape in sacred clothing.

This verse lays the foundation for karma yoga. The real issue is not whether one acts, but how one acts and from what consciousness. Freedom comes not from empty inactivity, but from action purified of egoic motive and grounded in knowledge.

original_script:
न हि कश्चित्क्षणमपि जातु तिष्ठत्यकर्मकृत्।
कार्यते ह्यवशः कर्म सर्वः प्रकृतिजैर्गुणैः।।3.5।।
transliteration:
na hi kaśhchit kṣhaṇam api jātu tiṣhṭhatyakarmakṛit
kāryate hyavaśhaḥ karma sarvaḥ prakṛitijair guṇaiḥ
word_meanings: na—not; hi—certainly; kaśhchit—anyone; kṣhaṇam—a moment; api—even; jātu—at any time; tiṣhṭhati—remains; akarma-kṛit—without performing action; kāryate—is made to do; hi—certainly; avaśhaḥ—helplessly; karma—action; sarvaḥ—everyone; prakṛiti-jaiḥ—born of material nature; guṇaiḥ—by the modes
translation_english: No one can remain without performing action even for a moment. Everyone is driven to act helplessly by the qualities (gunas) born of material nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that no one can remain even for a moment without acting, because everyone is driven to action helplessly by the qualities born of nature. This is a decisive expansion of the teaching. Even if one tries to avoid outward work, life itself is movement. The body breathes, the mind thinks, the senses process, impulses arise, and nature continues its activity.

The verse means that action is woven into embodied existence. One cannot escape it simply by refusing visible duties. The gunas, the forces of nature, continue to operate. Therefore the spiritual question is not whether to act at all, but how to stand in relation to inevitable action.

For a new reader, this verse is deeply practical. Many people imagine that inaction is a real option when faced with difficult duty. Krishna says that such an idea is largely an illusion. Even avoidance is a kind of action, and nature keeps moving through us whether we acknowledge it or not.

This has two important consequences. First, hypocrisy becomes possible if one pretends to be beyond action while still being driven by inner forces. Second, discipline becomes necessary, because if action is unavoidable, it must be directed wisely rather than left to impulse.

Krishna is preparing Arjuna for a radical shift in perspective: action is not the problem. Unconscious, ego-bound action is the problem. Since one cannot stop acting altogether, one must learn how to act without bondage. That is the doorway into karma yoga.

original_script:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन्।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते।।3.6।।
transliteration:
karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya ya āste manasā smaran
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyāchāraḥ sa uchyate
word_meanings: karma-indriyāṇi—the organs of action; saṁyamya—restraining; yaḥ—who; āste—sits; manasā—by the mind; smaran—remembering; indriya-arthān—the objects of the senses; vimūḍha-ātmā—the deluded soul; mithyā-āchāraḥ—a hypocrite; saḥ—he; uchyate—is called
translation_english: One who restrains the organs of action but continues to dwell on sense objects in the mind is a deluded hypocrite.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the person who outwardly restrains the organs of action but continues inwardly dwelling on sense objects is deluded and is called a hypocrite. This verse is a sharp warning against false renunciation. Merely stopping visible activity does not make one free if the mind remains saturated with craving.

The hypocrisy Krishna names is not only social pretense, though it can include that. It is also an inner split. Outward behavior displays restraint, but inwardly the person is still fantasizing, clinging, and revolving around what has supposedly been renounced. The contradiction corrupts spiritual life because it substitutes appearance for transformation.

For a new reader, this verse is crucial because it distinguishes true discipline from moral theater. One can look detached while remaining inwardly enslaved. Krishna is not satisfied with performance. He wants integration between inner state and outer conduct.

This also helps explain why Arjuna's desire to step back from action cannot be accepted uncritically. If he abandons duty while his mind remains tormented by fear, grief, and unresolved desire, the result will not be liberation. It will be a more respectable-looking form of bondage.

The verse therefore deepens the realism of the Gita. Spiritual life is not secured by changing clothing, role, or outward posture alone. The mind must be transformed. Without that, withdrawal becomes only another mask through which ego and desire continue to operate.

original_script:
यस्त्विन्द्रियाणि मनसा नियम्यारभतेऽर्जुन।
कर्मेन्द्रियैः कर्मयोगमसक्तः स विशिष्यते।।3.7।।
transliteration:
yas tvindriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate ’rjuna
karmendriyaiḥ karma-yogam asaktaḥ sa viśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; tu—but; indriyāṇi—the senses; manasā—by the mind; niyamya—restraining; ārabhate—begins; arjuna—Arjun; karma-indriyaiḥ—by the organs of action; karma-yogam—the yoga of action; asaktaḥ—without attachment; saḥ—he; viśhiṣhyate—is superior
translation_english: But he who controls the senses by the mind and engages his active organs in the yoga of action without attachment, O Arjuna, is far superior.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna contrasts the hypocrite with the person who, controlling the senses by the mind, engages the organs of action in karma yoga without attachment. Such a person excels. This verse provides the positive alternative. The true practitioner does not merely suppress action. Rather, he orders the inner and outer life together.

The mind must first take its rightful place as governor rather than servant of the senses. Then the organs of action, speech, hands, movement, responsibility, can be engaged rightly. Action is not the enemy. Action performed without selfish attachment, under inward discipline, becomes the very path of growth.

For a new reader, this verse is central because it shows the Gita's mature ideal: not false stillness, but integrated activity. The person who excels spiritually is not the one who simply disappears from the world, but the one who acts without being inwardly captured by action.

Krishna's use of "without attachment" matters greatly. The difference between bondage and yoga does not lie only in what is done but in how it is done. The same outward action may either tighten ego or become a vehicle of freedom depending on motive and consciousness.

This verse therefore defines karma yoga in a clear way. It is disciplined, responsible engagement in life from a place of inner mastery. For Arjuna, that means battle may become a path of yoga if undertaken from this purified stance rather than from fear, anger, or greed.

original_script:
नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्धयेदकर्मणः।।3.8।।
transliteration:
niyataṁ kuru karma tvaṁ karma jyāyo hyakarmaṇaḥ
śharīra-yātrāpi cha te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ
word_meanings: niyatam—prescribed; kuru—perform; karma—duty; tvam—you; karma—action; jyāyaḥ—superior; hi—certainly; akarmaṇaḥ—than inaction; śharīra—body; yātrā—maintenance; api—even; cha—also; te—your; na prasidhyet—would not be possible; akarmaṇaḥ—without action
translation_english: Perform your prescribed duty, for action is superior to inaction. Even the maintenance of your body would be impossible without work.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna tells Arjuna to perform his prescribed duty, for action is superior to inaction, and even the maintenance of the body would not be possible without action. This verse brings the teaching down to practical necessity. One must live, and life itself requires work. Therefore the issue cannot be settled by fantasy about total withdrawal.

Prescribed duty refers to the responsibilities that arise from one's role, place, and circumstances. For Arjuna, this includes the duty of a warrior bound to the protection of justice. Krishna is reminding him that duty is not an optional ornament to life. It is woven into the very structure of embodied existence.

For a new reader, the mention of bodily maintenance is important because it strips away romanticism. Even the simplest life depends on action. Food must be sought or prepared, relationships maintained, and obligations answered. There is no embodied life untouched by work.

This does not reduce spiritual life to mere functionality. Rather, it shows that the path must be found through life as it actually is, not through imagined escape from all necessity. Krishna consistently teaches from reality, not from fantasy.

The verse therefore reinforces karma yoga's practical footing. Since action is inevitable and necessary, the task is to perform it rightly. Arjuna cannot hide from his human situation by appealing to a spirituality that ignores the demands of living and of duty.

original_script:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर।।3.9।।
transliteration:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo ’nyatra loko ’yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ
tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samāchara
word_meanings: yajña-arthāt—for the sake of sacrifice; karmaṇaḥ—than action; anyatra—otherwise; lokaḥ—world; ayam—this; karma-bandhanaḥ—bondage of action; tat-artham—for that sake; karma—action; kaunteya—son of Kunti; mukta-saṅgaḥ—free from attachment; samāchara—perform
translation_english: Work done as a sacrifice for Vishnu has to be performed, otherwise work causes bondage in this material world. Therefore, O son of Kunti, perform your prescribed duties for His satisfaction, and in that way you will always remain free from bondage.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the world is bound by action unless action is done as sacrifice; therefore one should act for that purpose, free from attachment. This verse introduces a major new concept into Chapter 3: yajna, sacrifice. Action becomes binding when done for ego and gain, but it becomes liberating when offered into a larger sacred order.

Sacrifice here should not be limited to ritual fire offerings alone. Krishna is expanding the idea. To act as sacrifice is to perform action as offering, participation, and service rather than as private possession. The world then ceases to be a marketplace for ego and becomes a field of sacred exchange.

For a new reader, this verse is a breakthrough. It shows how work can be transformed without being abandoned. The same activity that binds when done selfishly can free when done as offering. Intention and orientation change the spiritual character of the deed.

The phrase "the world is bound" also means that this principle is universal. Human beings become entangled precisely because they act as isolated claimants. Sacrifice dissolves that illusion by placing action within a divine and cosmic context.

Krishna is therefore giving Arjuna a new vision of work. Battle, governance, duty, service, labor, all can become bondage or offering. If they are dedicated beyond the ego, they participate in yajna. This is one of the deepest secrets of karma yoga.

original_script:
सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः।
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक्।।3.10।।
transliteration:
saha-yajñāḥ prajāḥ sṛiṣhṭvā purovācha prajāpatiḥ
anena prasaviṣhyadhvam eṣha vo ’stv iṣhṭa-kāma-dhuk
word_meanings: saha—along with; yajñāḥ—sacrifices; prajāḥ—beings; sṛiṣhṭvā—having created; purā—in the beginning; uvācha—said; prajāpatiḥ—the Lord of creatures; anena—by this; prasaviṣhyadhvam—you shall prosper; eṣhaḥ—this; vaḥ—your; astu—let it be; iṣhṭa-kāma-dhuk—bestower of desired objects
translation_english: In the beginning of creation, the Lord of all creatures sent forth generations of men and demigods, along with sacrifices for Vishnu, and blessed them by saying, 'Be thou happy by this yajña [sacrifice] because its performance will bestow upon you everything desirable for living happily and achieving liberation.'
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that in the beginning the Creator brought forth beings together with sacrifice and said, "By this you shall prosper; let this be the cow of plenty that grants your desired ends." This verse places sacrifice at the very foundation of cosmic and social life. Yajna is not an optional religious practice added later; it belongs to the intended structure of existence.

The image of the wish-fulfilling cow suggests abundance generated by right participation in sacred order. Prosperity is meant to come not through isolated grasping, but through alignment with the principle of offering and reciprocity. The world is sustained when beings give as well as take.

For a new reader, this verse broadens the meaning of sacrifice beyond renunciation in the narrow sense. Sacrifice is the pattern by which life flourishes when individuals do not live as closed centers of consumption. It is a vision of reality as relational, ordered, and mutually sustaining.

This also clarifies Krishna's larger point to Arjuna. Duty is not a burden imposed on the self from outside. It belongs to the sacred architecture of existence. To act rightly is to participate in how life was meant to flow.

The verse therefore counters the ego's fantasy of self-sufficient autonomy. We prosper not by severing ourselves from obligation, but by entering rightly into the web of giving, receiving, and offering established in the very beginning. Karma yoga stands within that primordial pattern.

original_script:
देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः।
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ।।3.11।।
transliteration:
devān bhāvayatānena te devā bhāvayantu vaḥ
parasparaṁ bhāvayantaḥ śhreyaḥ param avāpsyatha
word_meanings: devān—celestial gods; bhāvayatā—pleasing; anena—by this; te—those; devāḥ—gods; bhāvayantu—may please; vaḥ—you; parasparam—mutually; bhāvayantaḥ—pleasing one another; śhreyaḥ—the highest good; param—the supreme; avāpsyatha—you shall achieve
translation_english: The demigods, being pleased by sacrifices, will also please you, and thus, by cooperation between men and demigods, prosperity will reign for all.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that by nourishing the gods through sacrifice, the gods will nourish you in return; thus, nourishing one another, you shall attain the highest good. This verse expresses the principle of reciprocal support within the sacred order. Human life does not stand alone. It is sustained through relationships that are cosmic as well as social.

In the traditional language of the text, the gods represent powers that govern and uphold aspects of the world. To "nourish" them means to act in reverence and rightful participation rather than in selfish exploitation. When humans live sacrificially, the sustaining powers of life continue to support them.

For a new reader, the deeper principle is reciprocity. Life thrives where there is mutuality, gratitude, and offering. It decays where there is only taking. Krishna is describing an order in which beings flourish by participating in a pattern larger than themselves.

The highest good is not attained by private accumulation alone. It emerges when one is aligned with this web of sacred exchange. That is why selfish action binds; it interrupts reciprocity and makes the ego imagine itself independent of the whole.

This verse therefore expands karma yoga from individual discipline to a vision of cosmic ethics. Arjuna's action matters not only for himself, but for the larger order to which he belongs. Right action helps sustain the world; disordered action tears at its fabric.

original_script:
इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः।
तैरदत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः।।3.12।।
transliteration:
iṣhṭān bhogān hi vo devā dāsyante yajña-bhāvitāḥ
tair adattān apradāyaibhyo yo bhuṅkte stena eva saḥ
word_meanings: iṣhṭān—desired; bhogān—necessities of life; hi—certainly; vaḥ—unto you; devāḥ—demigods; dāsyante—will give; yajña-bhāvitāḥ—being pleased by sacrifice; taiḥ—by them; adattān—without offering; apradāya—without giving; ebhyāḥ—to them; yaḥ—who; bhuṅkte—enjoys; stenaḥ—thief; eva—certainly; saḥ—he
translation_english: In charge of the various necessities of life, the demigods, being satisfied by the performance of yajña [sacrifice], will supply all necessities to you. But he who enjoys such gifts without offering them to the demigods in return is certainly a thief.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the gods, nourished by sacrifice, will grant the enjoyments desired by people; but one who enjoys what is given without offering in return is a thief. The language is strong because the principle is strong. To receive from the world and its sustaining order without acknowledgment, gratitude, or return is a form of theft.

This verse challenges the ego's assumption that whatever one can take may simply be treated as one's own. In Krishna's vision, life is received. Food, rain, energy, knowledge, and opportunity arise within a network of givenness. To consume without offering back is to deny that dependence.

For a new reader, the verse may be read beyond ritual terms. It speaks to ecology, society, labor, and gratitude. Whenever people live as if they owe nothing, no reverence, no service, no contribution, no care, they become spiritually thieving, even if outwardly respectable.

This is highly relevant to karma yoga. Action becomes sacred when it includes reciprocity and offering. Action becomes binding when it turns the world into raw material for private use. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to see duty as part of giving back into the order that sustains life.

The verse also warns that enjoyment itself is not the problem. The issue is enjoying without relationship, gratitude, or participation in yajna. The same world that becomes holy through offering becomes corrupting when turned into possession alone.

original_script:
यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः।
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात्।।3.13।।
transliteration:
yajña-śhiṣhṭāśhinaḥ santo muchyante sarva-kilbiṣhaiḥ
bhuñjate te tv agham pāpā ye pachanty ātma-kāraṇāt
word_meanings: yajña-śhiṣhṭa—remnants of sacrifice; aśhinaḥ—eaters; santaḥ—the devotees; muchyante—get relief; sarva—from all; kilbiṣhaiḥ—sins; bhuñjate—eat; te—they; tu—but; agham—sin; pāpāḥ—sinners; ye—who; pachanti—cook; ātma-kāraṇāt—for their own sake
translation_english: The devotees of the Lord are released from all kinds of sins because they eat food which is offered first for sacrifice. Others, who prepare food for personal sense enjoyment, verily eat only sin.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the righteous who eat what remains after sacrifice are freed from sin, but those who cook for themselves alone eat sin. The verse uses food as a concrete example because food is among the most basic human acts of reception and consumption. Even eating can either participate in sacred order or reinforce selfish isolation.

What remains after sacrifice signifies life received and enjoyed in gratitude, with the ego no longer acting as absolute owner. Such enjoyment is purified because it has been placed within offering. By contrast, to cook "for oneself alone" symbolizes a life organized solely around private satisfaction.

For a new reader, this verse should not be reduced to a narrow ritual rule. Krishna's larger point is that ordinary activities become spiritually clean or spiritually binding depending on whether they are connected to offering. Even the most common acts of life can either deepen selfishness or sanctify existence.

The phrase "eat sin" is strong because selfish consumption reinforces bondage. When a person lives only to feed the private self, the very act of enjoyment becomes spiritually heavy.

This verse is one of the clearest examples of the Gita's practical spirituality. Nothing is too ordinary to become part of yoga. Eating, working, ruling, serving, all can be transformed by sacrificial consciousness. Arjuna is being taught that the sacred is not separate from life; it is the right way of living life.

original_script:
अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः।
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः।।3.14।।
transliteration:
annād bhavanti bhūtāni parjanyād anna-sambhavaḥ
yajñād bhavati parjanyo yajñaḥ karma-samudbhavaḥ
word_meanings: annāt—from food; bhavanti—subsist; bhūtāni—living beings; parjanyāt—from rains; anna—of food; sambhavaḥ—production; yajñāt—from sacrifice; bhavati—comes; parjanyaḥ—rain; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; karma—from action; samudbhavaḥ—born
translation_english: All living bodies subsist on food grains, which are produced from rains. Rains are produced by performance of yajña [sacrifice], and yajña is born of prescribed duties.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that beings arise from food, food comes from rain, rain comes from sacrifice, and sacrifice arises from action. This verse presents a chain of interdependence linking human life, natural order, sacred offering, and duty. It is one of the most ecological passages in the Gita, showing how deeply material life depends upon moral and sacred alignment.

Food is the basis of embodied existence. Rain makes food possible. Sacrifice sustains the order in which rain and fertility occur. Action generates sacrifice when rightly performed. Thus, action is not merely an individual affair. It is woven into the maintenance of life itself.

For a new reader, this verse expands moral responsibility beyond personal psychology. What we do participates in systems larger than ourselves. Human action touches natural and social life. Krishna sees a world in which inner orientation and outer flourishing are connected.

This also reinforces why inaction or selfish action cannot be treated lightly. If action belongs to a chain that sustains life, then acting wrongly or refusing rightful action has consequences beyond the individual.

The verse thus broadens karma yoga into a cosmological principle. Proper work offered sacrificially helps sustain the world. Arjuna's duty is not an isolated burden. It belongs to the larger circulation by which life, order, and nourishment are maintained.

original_script:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम्।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम्।।3.15।।
transliteration:
karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi brahmākṣhara-samudbhavam
tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣhṭhitam
word_meanings: karma—duty; brahma—from the Vedas; udbhavam—born; viddhi—know; brahma—the Vedas; akṣhara—from the Imperishable; samudbhavam—born; tasmāt—therefore; sarva-gatam—all-pervading; brahma—the Divine; nitya—eternally; yajñe—in sacrifice; pratiṣhṭhitam—situated
translation_english: Regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Consequently the all-pervading Transcendence is eternally situated in acts of sacrifice.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that action arises from the Veda, and the Veda from the Imperishable; therefore the all-pervading Brahman is ever established in sacrifice. This verse completes the chain begun in the previous verse by grounding action and sacrifice in the very structure of sacred reality.

The Veda here represents revealed order, not merely a book of injunctions. Action, when rightly understood, is not arbitrary. It is rooted in a sacred intelligibility that itself comes from the Imperishable. Thus sacrifice is not a human invention alone; it reflects the relation between the world and its divine ground.

For a new reader, the verse shows that karma yoga is not just a psychological method for reducing stress. It stands within metaphysics and revelation. To act sacrificially is to align with Brahman as the sustaining reality underlying existence.

This also explains why selfish action binds so deeply. It breaks from the true order of things by pretending that the ego is the center. Sacrificial action restores action to its rightful place within the all-pervading sacred whole.

Krishna is steadily enlarging Arjuna's vision. Duty is no longer merely personal obligation. It is participation in a cosmic structure rooted in the Imperishable itself. To act rightly, then, is to move in harmony with reality at its deepest level.

original_script:
एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः।
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति।।3.16।।
transliteration:
evaṁ pravartitaṁ chakraṁ nānuvartayatīha yaḥ
aghāyur indriyārāmo moghaṁ pārtha sa jīvati
word_meanings: evam—thus; pravartitam—set into motion; chakram—cycle; na anuvartayati—does not follow; iha—in this life; yaḥ—who; agha-āyuḥ—life of sin; indriya-ārāmaḥ—satisfied in sense objects; mogham—uselessly; pārtha—Arjun; saḥ—he; jīvati—lives
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, one who does not follow in human life the cycle of sacrifice thus established by the Vedas certainly lives a life full of sin. Living only for the satisfaction of the senses, such a person lives in vain.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one who does not follow this wheel thus set in motion, but lives selfishly delighting in the senses, lives in vain. The image of the wheel is important. It conveys motion, continuity, interdependence, and order. Life is meant to move within this sacrificial circulation of receiving, offering, sustaining, and being sustained.

To reject the wheel is to step out of the moral and sacred order of life and attempt to live for private gratification alone. Krishna's judgment is severe because such a life wastes human possibility. One receives from the whole while refusing to participate in its sustaining rhythm.

For a new reader, this verse is a strong warning against purely consumption-centered living. Sensory enjoyment, when made the center of existence, empties life of its higher purpose. The person may be active, successful, and even admired, yet spiritually that life is vain because it refuses its place in the sacred order.

This verse also helps explain why Krishna does not accept Arjuna's desire for passive retreat if that retreat is still self-referential. To live rightly is not merely to avoid obvious wrong; it is to enter the wheel of offering and responsibility.

The word "vain" here means spiritually wasted. Human birth carries the possibility of conscious participation in truth. To live only for the senses is to miss that possibility. Krishna wants Arjuna to choose a life that truly belongs to the larger order, not one reduced to private reaction.

original_script:
यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः।
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते।।3.17।।
transliteration:
yas tvātma-ratir eva syād ātma-tṛiptaśh cha mānavaḥ
ātmanyeva cha santuṣhṭas tasya kāryaṁ na vidyate
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; tu—but; ātma-ratiḥ—rejoicing in the self; eva—certainly; syāt—is; ātma-tṛiptaḥ—satisfied in the self; cha—and; mānavaḥ—a person; ātmani—in the self; eva—only; cha—and; santuṣhṭaḥ—perfectly content; tasya—his; kāryam—duty; na—not; vidyate—exists
translation_english: But for one who rejoices only in the Self, who is satisfied with the Self, and who is fully content in the Self alone, there is no obligatory duty to perform.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the one who delights in the Self, is satisfied in the Self, and content in the Self alone has no obligatory duty. This may seem to contradict the emphasis on action, but it actually refines it. Krishna is describing the rare person who has become inwardly पूर्ण, fulfilled, and no longer depends on action for self-completion.

Such a person does not need to act in order to gain identity, approval, or fulfillment. Duty in the ordinary binding sense falls away because the inner lack that drives self-seeking action has disappeared. This is not laziness. It is the freedom of one who is full within.

For a new reader, this verse is important because it shows the ultimate horizon of karma yoga. Action is necessary for the bound person, and sacrificial action purifies. But the fully realized person no longer acts from deficiency. The Self itself is enough.

At the same time, Krishna is not describing Arjuna's present condition. Arjuna is not established in Self-delight. Therefore this verse does not release him from duty. It explains a state beyond ordinary obligation, not a shortcut into avoidance.

The verse also reveals the Gita's subtlety. Duty belongs to the path of purification, but the goal is a state in which the self is no longer inwardly dependent on action or result. Krishna will next explain how even such a person may still act for the welfare of the world.

original_script:
नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः।।3.18।।
transliteration:
naiva tasya kṛitenārtho nākṛiteneha kaśhchana
na chāsya sarva-bhūteṣhu kaśhchid artha-vyapāśhrayaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; eva—certainly; tasya—his; kṛitena—by performance of duty; arthaḥ—purpose; na—nor; akṛitena—by non-performance; iha—in this world; kaśhchana—any; na—never; cha—also; asya—his; sarva-bhūteṣhu—among all living beings; kaśhchit—any; artha—purpose; vyapāśhrayaḥ—dependence
translation_english: A Self-realized person has no purpose to fulfill in the discharge of his prescribed duties, nor has he any reason not to perform such work. Nor is he dependent on any other living being for any purpose.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that for such a realized person there is no purpose in what is done or what is not done, and no dependence on any being for any end. This verse continues the description of the one satisfied in the Self. Freedom here means radical non-dependence of identity. The realized one does not need action to establish worth, nor inaction to preserve it.

This also means that relationships are no longer instrumentalized. The sage does not lean on others for self-completion in the ordinary egoic way. Dependence in the existential sense falls away because the center of fulfillment is no longer outside.

For a new reader, this verse helps distinguish spiritual fullness from social isolation. Krishna is not praising indifference to others. He is describing freedom from using others as means to private psychic completion. Such freedom actually makes selfless action more possible.

The verse also clarifies why action and inaction lose their old power over the realized person. If the ego is no longer constructing itself through doing or not doing, then both cease to be spiritually loaded in the same way.

Again, Krishna is not excusing Arjuna. He is describing the goal toward which yoga moves. By understanding this state, Arjuna can better see the difference between true freedom from duty and his own current wish to flee duty under the pressure of sorrow.

original_script:
तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः।।3.19।।
transliteration:
tasmād asaktaḥ satataṁ kāryaṁ karma samāchara
asakto hyācharan karma param āpnoti pūruṣhaḥ
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; asaktaḥ—without attachment; satatam—constantly; kāryam—obligatory; karma—work; samāchara—perform; asaktaḥ—unattached; hi—certainly; ācharan—performing; karma—work; param—the Supreme; āpnoti—attains; pūruṣhaḥ—a person
translation_english: Therefore, without being attached to the fruits of activities, one should act as a matter of duty, for by working without attachment one attains the Supreme.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna concludes from all this that one should therefore always perform the work that ought to be done, without attachment; by acting without attachment, a person attains the Supreme. This verse returns from the description of the realized person to the practical instruction for the seeker.

The word "therefore" matters. Because total renunciation belongs only to one already established in Self-sufficiency, the ordinary aspirant must act. But the action must be transformed. It must be done because it ought to be done, not because ego wants reward, praise, or control.

For a new reader, this verse is one of the clearest summaries of karma yoga. Do the necessary work. Do it steadily. Do it without selfish attachment. That path itself becomes a means of liberation.

The promise of attaining the Supreme through detached action is profound. It means the world is not merely an obstacle course to be escaped. Rightly lived, it becomes the field of ascent. Duty becomes a discipline of purification.

This verse therefore resolves the tension between renunciation and work in a practical way. Renunciation of attachment, not abandonment of necessary action, is the path for one still engaged in life. Krishna wants Arjuna to understand that his battlefield duty, done rightly, can itself become a path to the highest.

original_script:
कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिमास्थिता जनकादयः।
लोकसंग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि।।3.20।।
transliteration:
karmaṇaiva hi saṁsiddhim āsthitā janakādayaḥ
loka-saṅgraham evāpi sampaśhyan kartum arhasi
word_meanings: karmaṇā—by performance of duty; eva—only; hi—certainly; saṁsiddhim—perfection; āsthitāḥ—attained; janaka-ādayaḥ—Janaka and others; loka-saṅgraham—welfare of the world; eva api—also; sampaśhyan—considering; kartum—to perform; arhasi—you should
translation_english: Kings such as Janaka attained perfection solely by performance of prescribed duties. Therefore, just for the sake of educating the people in general, you should perform your work.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that even King Janaka and others attained perfection through action alone; therefore Arjuna should act also for the maintenance of the world. This verse adds historical and exemplary authority to the teaching. Krishna does not ask Arjuna to trust an unprecedented ideal. He points to earlier rulers who attained spiritual perfection not by abandoning responsibility, but by fulfilling it rightly.

Janaka is especially significant because he represents a ruler who combined sovereignty with wisdom. His example shows that one can be active, socially placed, and deeply realized. This breaks the assumption that liberation belongs only to outward renunciants.

The phrase "for the maintenance of the world" is central. Action is not only for personal purification. It also serves lokasangraha, the holding together of the world. Human beings, especially those in visible or responsible roles, act in ways that either stabilize or destabilize collective life.

For a new reader, this verse expands karma yoga beyond personal spirituality. The seeker must consider not only private liberation but the welfare and coherence of the world. This makes the path both inwardly liberating and socially responsible.

Krishna is thus giving Arjuna a grander reason to act. He is not only to fight for his own dharma or spiritual progress, but also to serve the sustaining order of society. Duty, liberation, and world-maintenance come together here in one of the most powerful formulations of the chapter.

original_script:
यद्यदाचरति श्रेष्ठस्तत्तदेवेतरो जनः।
स यत्प्रमाणं कुरुते लोकस्तदनुवर्तते।।3.21।।
transliteration:
yad yad ācharati śhreṣhṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ
sa yat pramāṇaṁ kurute lokas tad anuvartate
word_meanings: yat yat—whatever; ācharati—performs; śhreṣhṭhaḥ—a great leader; tat tat—that (and that only); eva—certainly; itaraḥ—common; janaḥ—people; saḥ—he; yat—whatever; pramāṇam—example/standard; kurute—sets; lokaḥ—the world; tat—that; anuvartate—follows
translation_english: Whatever action a great man performs, common men follow. And whatever standards he sets by exemplary acts, all the world pursues.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that whatever a great person does, others follow; whatever standard such a person sets, the world pursues. This verse introduces one of the Gita's most socially important insights: human life is imitative. People learn not only from formal teaching but from the visible conduct of those they admire, fear, trust, or depend on.

This means that action is never merely private for those who hold influence. A leader's choices become models. A parent's habits shape children. A teacher's way of living interprets the teaching more loudly than words. A ruler's conduct sets the tone for a society. Krishna therefore broadens Arjuna's duty beyond his individual conscience. What he does will teach others how to act.

For a new reader, this verse is extremely relevant. Even outside positions of obvious authority, human beings constantly shape one another through example. Conduct carries cultural force. Standards are embodied before they are explained.

The verse also helps explain why Krishna does not accept Arjuna's collapse as a harmless personal decision. If a person of Arjuna's stature abandons duty under pressure, that action will not remain confined to him. It will become a precedent and an interpretation of what courage, dharma, and responsibility mean.

This teaching therefore adds a new layer to karma yoga. One must act not only for personal purification or private liberation, but also with awareness that one's life becomes part of the moral formation of the world. Action educates. Krishna wants Arjuna to see that clearly.

original_script:
न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं वर्त एव च कर्मणि।।3.22।।
transliteration:
na me pārthāsti kartavyaṁ triṣhu lokeṣhu kiñchana
nānavāptam avāptavyaṁ varta eva cha karmaṇi
word_meanings: na—not; me—My; pārtha—Arjun; asti—there is; kartavyam—duty; triṣhu—in the three; lokeṣhu—worlds; kiñchana—any; na—not; anavāptam—wanting; avāptavyam—to be gained; varte—I am engaged; eva—certainly; cha—also; karmaṇi—in duty
translation_english: O son of Pritha, there is no work prescribed for Me within all the three heavenly systems. Nor am I in want of anything, nor have I a need to obtain anything—and yet I am engaged in prescribed duties.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that in all the three worlds there is nothing whatsoever he must do, nothing unattained that he needs to attain, and yet he still engages in action. This is a remarkable statement because it presents Krishna himself as the highest example of action without personal lack.

Ordinary beings act because they want something, fear something, or need something. Krishna says none of this applies to him. He is complete. He lacks nothing. There is no result he needs in order to become fulfilled. Yet action continues. This means action is not necessarily a sign of bondage. It can also flow from fullness.

For a new reader, this verse is crucial because it overturns a common assumption: that only need creates action. Krishna reveals another possibility, action arising from wholeness, responsibility, and sustaining intelligence rather than from craving.

This is directly relevant to Arjuna. Krishna has already described the realized person as inwardly fulfilled. Now he shows that such fulfillment does not demand passivity. Even the one who has nothing to gain may still act.

The verse also prepares the next teaching on world-maintenance. Krishna's own action is not ego-driven, and yet it is real, purposeful, and necessary in a larger sense. This becomes the divine model for karma yoga: not action from deficiency, but action from truth, freedom, and care for the order of things.

original_script:
यदि ह्यहं न वर्तेयं जातु कर्मण्यतन्द्रितः।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः।।3.23।।
transliteration:
yadi hyahaṁ na varteyaṁ jātu karmaṇyatandritaḥ
mama vartmānuvartante manuṣhyāḥ pārtha sarvaśhaḥ
word_meanings: yadi—if; hi—certainly; aham—I; na—not; varteyam—thus engage; jātu—ever; karmaṇi—in duty; atandritaḥ—carefully; mama—My; vartma—path; anuvartante—follow; manuṣhyāḥ—all men; pārtha—Arjun; sarvaśhaḥ—in all respects
translation_english: For if I ever failed to engage in carefully performing prescribed duties, O Partha, certainly all men would follow My path.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that if he were ever to cease acting with full attention, people everywhere would follow his path. This verse applies the principle of exemplarity directly to Krishna himself. Because he stands at the center of order and meaning, his conduct has world-shaping force.

The phrase "with full attention" matters. Krishna is not describing distracted or reluctant action. He acts deliberately and completely. That completeness shows that right action is not incompatible with freedom. The enlightened or divine mode of action is not half-hearted performance. It is wholehearted engagement without selfish bondage.

For a new reader, this verse makes a profound claim: even the highest reality participates actively in sustaining order. The universe is not upheld by indifference. Therefore the spiritual person should not imagine that detachment means careless withdrawal from responsibility.

This also teaches something about leadership. If those most visible or authoritative become negligent, the effect multiplies. Others imitate not only ideals but also indifference. Abandonment by the higher becomes confusion for the lower.

Krishna is therefore showing Arjuna that action matters at every level. Human beings are not the only ones whose conduct participates in order. The divine itself acts as exemplar. This dignifies action rather than reducing it to an unfortunate necessity for the ignorant.

original_script:
उत्सीदेयुरिमे लोका न कुर्यां कर्म चेदहम्।
सङ्करस्य च कर्ता स्यामुपहन्यामिमाः प्रजाः।।3.24।।
