Desire-driven action, by contrast, keeps the person tethered to hope, fear, comparison, and frustration. Such a person remains bound because the heart is invested in what it cannot fully control.

For a new reader, this verse provides one of the most practical summaries of karma yoga. If one wants to know whether an action is spiritually freeing or binding, one may ask: is this being done with grasping attachment to its fruit, or with surrendered dedication?

The verse also clarifies why peace is difficult for many active people. It is not action itself that destroys peace, but anxious and possessive attachment to outcome. The outer schedule may be busy while the inner being remains steady if desire does not rule.

Thus Krishna keeps returning to the same secret from multiple angles. Renunciation of fruit is not a side detail. It is the hinge on which peace and bondage turn. Through that renunciation, work ceases to imprison the soul.

original_script:
सर्वकर्माणि मनसा संन्यस्यास्ते सुखं वशी।
नवद्वारे पुरे देही नैव कुर्वन्न कारयन्।।5.13।।
transliteration:
sarva-karmāṇi manasā sannyasyāste sukhaṁ vaśhī
nava-dvāre pure dehī naiva kurvan na kārayan
word_meanings: sarva—all; karmāṇi—actions; manasā—by the mind; sannyasya—renouncing; āste—remains; sukham—happily; vaśhī—controlled; nava-dvāre—in the nine-gated; pure—in the city; dehī—the 'Dehi' (tenant/citizen soul); na—never; eva—certainly; kurvan—doing; na—nor; kārayan—causing to be done
translation_english: When the Self-controlled tenant (*Dehi*) mentally renounces the fruits of all actions, they reside happily in the city of nine gates (the body), neither acting as the primary agent nor inducing others to act.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the embodied one who has mastered the self dwells happily in the city of nine gates, neither acting nor causing action. The body is here compared to a city with nine openings, a vivid image that helps distinguish the indwelling consciousness from its bodily residence.

To dwell in the city of nine gates means to inhabit the body without being reduced to it. The wise person knows the body as a field or dwelling place, not as the whole identity. Therefore action occurring in the bodily city does not automatically define the deepest Self.

For a new reader, this verse helps make the distinction between Self and body more intuitive. Just as a ruler or resident of a city is not identical with the walls and gates of the city, the conscious self is not identical with the bodily structure through which life is expressed.

The statement that the wise neither acts nor causes action must be read from the level of the deepest Self. At the practical level, bodily processes and social duties continue. But at the spiritual level, the realized one no longer misidentifies the eternal Self as the finite doer.

Thus the verse returns to non-doership while placing it in a contemplative image. The yogin can live in the body, use it, care for it, and act through it, yet remain inwardly free from false identification with its entire machinery.

original_script:
न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः।
न कर्मफलसंयोगं स्वभावस्तु प्रवर्तते।।5.14।।
transliteration:
na kartṛitvaṁ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛijati prabhuḥ
na karma-phala-saṅyogaṁ swabhāvas tu pravartate
word_meanings: na—never; kartṛitvam—authorship/sense of doership; na—never; karmāṇi—actions; lokasya—of the world; sṛijati—creates; prabhuḥ—the Lord of the city (soul); na—never; karma-phala—results of actions; saṅyogam—connection; swabhāvaḥ—nature; tu—but; pravartate—enacts
translation_english: The embodied spirit, master of the city of his body, does not create activities, nor does he induce people to act, nor does he create the fruits of action. All this is enacted by the modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the Lord does not create agency, actions, or the union of actions with their fruits for the world; nature acts instead. This verse deepens the teaching on agency by refusing to place the burden of egoic doership directly upon the Divine.

The world of action, causality, and fruit unfolds through prakriti, nature with its gunas and structures. This means that bondage is not created by a divine whim that arbitrarily traps beings. It arises within the field of nature as beings identify, desire, and act under ignorance.

For a new reader, this verse helps remove a major theological confusion. If suffering and bondage exist, are they simply imposed by God? Krishna says no. The processes of agency and result belong to the realm of nature. The Divine is the ground of reality, but not the petty fabricator of egoic delusion.

This is also important for karma yoga. If action belongs to nature, then liberation requires right knowledge of that fact, not resentment toward God. One must see how nature operates and cease attributing absolute doership to the ego or arbitrary blame to the Divine.

Thus the verse preserves both divine transcendence and the seriousness of natural law. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to understand the machinery of bondage more clearly so that he can stop misplacing blame and start moving toward freedom.

original_script:
नादत्ते कस्यचित्पापं न चैव सुकृतं विभुः।
अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानं तेन मुह्यन्ति जन्तवः।।5.15।।
transliteration:
nādatte kasyachit pāpaṁ na chaiva sukṛitaṁ vibhuḥ
ajñānenāvṛitaṁ jñānaṁ tena muhyanti jantavaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; ādatte—accepts; kasyachit—anyone’s; pāpam—sin; na—never; cha—also; eva—certainly; sukṛitam—pious deeds; vibhuḥ—the Supreme Lord; ajñānena—by ignorance; āvṛitam—covered; jñānam—knowledge; tena—by that; muhyanti—are bewildered; jantavaḥ—living beings
translation_english: The Supreme Lord does not assume anyone’s sinful or pious activities. Embodied beings, however, are bewildered because of the ignorance which covers their real knowledge.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the all-pervading does not take on anyone's sin or merit; knowledge is covered by ignorance, and through that beings are deluded. This verse continues the previous teaching by removing another source of confusion. The Divine does not sit collecting and personally appropriating each person's moral record in a crude sense.

Sin and merit belong to the field of karma under ignorance. Beings become deluded because knowledge is covered. This means the root of bondage is not divine favoritism or hostility, but ignorance obscuring the truth of the Self and the nature of action.

For a new reader, this verse is profoundly important. It shows that liberation depends on illumination rather than appeasing a divine ego. The central human problem is delusion, the covering over of knowledge. Moral consequence remains real, but it unfolds in a lawful spiritual order, not as the product of divine caprice.

This also underscores the urgency of knowledge. If ignorance covers the truth, then purification, yoga, and insight are all aimed at removing that covering. Once knowledge shines, the structure of delusion weakens.

Thus Krishna continues to reframe responsibility. The seeker should not waste energy imagining a God who arbitrarily withholds or assigns moral states. The real battle is against ignorance. When that is dispelled, the whole relation to action, merit, and bondage is transformed.

original_script:
ज्ञानेन तु तदज्ञानं येषां नाशितमात्मनः।
तेषामादित्यवज्ज्ञानं प्रकाशयति तत्परम्।।5.16।।
transliteration:
jñānena tu tad ajñānaṁ yeṣhāṁ nāśhitam ātmanaḥ
teṣhām ādityavaj jñānaṁ prakāśhayati tat param
word_meanings: jñānena—by knowledge; tu—but; tat—that; ajñānam—ignorance; yeṣhām—whose; nāśhitam—is destroyed; ātmanaḥ—of the self; teṣhām—for them; āditya-vat—like the rising sun; jñānam—knowledge; prakāśhayati—reveals; tat param—the Supreme
translation_english: When, however, one is enlightened with the knowledge by which nescience is destroyed, then his knowledge reveals everything, as the sun enlightens everything in the daytime.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that for those whose ignorance is destroyed by knowledge, their knowledge reveals the Supreme like the sun illumining all. This is one of the chapter's strongest images of awakening. Knowledge is not described as a dim lamp or partial guess, but as sunlight, overwhelming, revealing, and comprehensive.

The sun metaphor suggests both certainty and immediacy. When the sun rises, things do not need to be argued into visibility. They become manifest. Similarly, when ignorance falls away, the Supreme is not inferred with anxiety but disclosed with clarity.

For a new reader, this verse is deeply encouraging. The Gita does not present enlightenment as endless fog. It presents true knowledge as illuminating power. The confusion that once made everything uncertain can, in principle, be genuinely overcome.

This also clarifies why Krishna keeps returning to knowledge despite emphasizing action. Action purifies, but knowledge illumines. The path matures into a seeing that is as transformative as sunlight after darkness.

Thus the verse places the seeker's struggle in a hopeful frame. Ignorance may cover the truth, but it is not ultimate. Once the covering is removed, the light was always there. The Supreme is not created by knowledge; it is revealed by it.

original_script:
तद्बुद्धयस्तदात्मानस्तन्निष्ठास्तत्परायणाः।
गच्छन्त्यपुनरावृत्तिं ज्ञाननिर्धूतकल्मषाः।।5.17।।
transliteration:
tad-buddhayas tad-ātmānas tan-niṣhṭhās tat-parāyaṇāḥ
gachchha nty apunar-āvṛittiṁ jñāna-nirdhūta-kalmaṣhāḥ
word_meanings: tat-buddhayaḥ—those whose intelligence is fixed in the Supreme; tat-ātmānaḥ—whose minds are absorbed in the Supreme; tat-niṣhṭhāḥ—whose faith is in the Supreme; tat-parāyaṇāḥ—whose goal is the Supreme; gachchhanti—attain; apunaḥ-āvṛittim—liberation from rebirth; jñāna—by knowledge; nirdhūta—cleansed; kalmaṣhāḥ—of sins
translation_english: When one’s intelligence, mind, faith and refuge are all fixed in the Supreme, then one becomes fully cleansed of misgivings through complete knowledge and thus proceeds straight on the path of liberation.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that those whose intellect is fixed on That, whose Self is That, who are steadfast in That, and who take That as supreme goal, go to the state from which there is no return, their impurities cleansed by knowledge. This verse describes a total orientation of life around the highest reality.

The repetition of "That" is deliberate. It indicates comprehensive spiritual alignment. Intellect, identity, steadfastness, and aspiration all converge upon the one reality. This is not occasional devotion or abstract interest. It is full re-centering of the person.

For a new reader, this verse shows that knowledge in the Gita is not detached philosophical speculation. It is existential alignment. The whole being becomes shaped by the truth it knows. That is why impurities are cleansed, because there is no longer such a large inner gap between knowledge and life.

The state of no return points again to liberation from cyclical bondage. When the being is fully oriented toward the Supreme, the old movement of karmic return is overcome.

Thus the verse shows the mature fruit of the path. Knowledge cleanses, but not as mere concept. It cleanses because it reorders the entire person. The seeker's intelligence, will, identity, and goal all become rooted in the highest truth.

original_script:
विद्याविनयसम्पन्ने ब्राह्मणे गवि हस्तिनि।
शुनि चैव श्वपाके च पण्डिताः समदर्शिनः।।5.18।।
transliteration:
vidyā-vinaya-sampanne brāhmaṇe gavi hastini
śhuni chaiva śhva-pāke cha paṇḍitāḥ sama-darśhinaḥ
word_meanings: vidyā—knowledge; vinaya—humility; sampanne—equipped with; brāhmaṇe—in a priest; gavi—in a cow; hastini—in an elephant; śhuni—in a dog; cha—and; eva—certainly; śhva-pāke—in a dog-eater/outcaste; cha—also; paṇḍitāḥ—the learned; sama-darśhinaḥ—see with equal vision
translation_english: The humble sages, by virtue of true knowledge, see with equal vision a learned and gentle brahmana, a cow, an elephant, a dog and a dog-eater [outcaste].
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the wise see with equal vision a learned and humble brahmana, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and even an outcaste. This is one of the Gita's most radical and beautiful statements of spiritual equality. The sage's vision penetrates beyond outer distinctions of species, status, and social rank to the same underlying reality in all.

This does not deny practical differences. A cow is not handled as an elephant, nor a scholar as an animal. Equal vision does not erase the world of functional distinctions. It means that the same spiritual essence is recognized across those distinctions, preventing arrogance, contempt, and dehumanization.

For a new reader, this verse is tremendously important because it shows the ethical consequence of Self-knowledge. To see the one Self in all is not an abstract metaphysical achievement only. It changes how one regards every being.

This verse also challenges pride rooted in social or intellectual status. Learning, power, species, and social identity do not define ultimate worth. The sage sees deeper.

Thus the chapter's movement toward non-doership and inner freedom does not produce aloof indifference. It produces equal vision. The more one knows the Self, the less one is trapped in ego-driven hierarchies of worth. This is one of the clearest signs that knowledge has become real.

original_script:
इहैव तैर्जितः सर्गो येषां साम्ये स्थितं मनः।
निर्दोषं हि समं ब्रह्म तस्माद्ब्रह्मणि ते स्थिताः।।5.19।।
transliteration:
ihaiva tair jitaḥ sargo yeṣhāṁ sāmye sthitaṁ manaḥ
nirdoṣhaṁ hi samaṁ brahma tasmād brahmaṇi te sthitāḥ
word_meanings: iha—in this life; eva—certainly; taiḥ—by them; jitaḥ—conquered; sargaḥ—birth and death; yeṣhām—whose; sāmye—in equanimity; sthitam—situated; manaḥ—mind; nirdoṣham—flawless; hi—certainly; samam—equal; brahma—the Supreme; tasmāt—therefore; brahmaṇi—in the Supreme; te—they; sthitāḥ—are situated
translation_english: Those whose minds are established in sameness and equanimity have already conquered the conditions of birth and death. They are flawless like Brahman, and thus they are already situated in Brahman.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that even here, in this life, those whose minds rest in equality have conquered creation, for Brahman is flawless and equal; therefore they are established in Brahman. This verse shows that equal vision is not merely an ethical ornament. It is a sign of realized participation in Brahman.

To conquer creation here does not mean to dominate the world externally. It means to transcend bondage to the divisive consciousness through which the world of multiplicity entangles the ego. When equality is established in the mind, the person is no longer ruled by separation, comparison, and reactive bias.

For a new reader, this verse is very important because it says liberation begins now, not only after death. "Even here" matters. The transformed mind already participates in freedom while embodied.

The equality of Brahman grounds the equal vision of the sage. Since ultimate reality is impartial, stainless, and non-divisive, the one established in it begins to mirror that quality.

Thus the verse ties together metaphysics and character. Equal-mindedness is not just moral niceness. It is evidence that the person is beginning to stand where Brahman stands, beyond the fragmenting partialities of ordinary ego-consciousness.

original_script:
न प्रहृष्येत्प्रियं प्राप्य नोद्विजेत्प्राप्य चाप्रियम्।
स्थिरबुद्धिरसम्मूढो ब्रह्मविद्ब्रह्मणि स्थितः।।5.20।।
transliteration:
na prahṛiṣhyet priyaṁ prāpya nodvijet prāpya chāpriyam
sthira-buddhir asammūḍho brahma-vid brahmaṇi sthitaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; prahṛiṣhyet—rejoices; priyam—pleasant; prāpya—obtaining; na—never; udvijet—becomes agitated; prāpya—obtaining; cha—also; apriyam—unpleasant; sthira-buddhiḥ—steady intellect; asammūḍhaḥ—unbewildered; brahma-vit—knower of the Supreme; brahmaṇi—in the Supreme; sthitaḥ—situated
translation_english: A person who neither rejoices upon achieving something pleasant nor laments upon obtaining something unpleasant, who is self-intelligent, who is unbewildered, and who knows the science of God, is already situated in transcendence.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one established in Brahman neither rejoices on obtaining the pleasant nor grieves on obtaining the unpleasant; with steady understanding and freedom from delusion, such a person abides in Brahman. This verse returns to one of the Gita's defining themes: equanimity before opposites.

Pleasant and unpleasant still occur. Brahman-realization does not erase the structure of worldly experience. What changes is the dependence of identity on these fluctuations. The sage does not become emotionally numb, but no longer throws the whole self into excitement or despair according to circumstance.

For a new reader, this verse helps show the emotional shape of spiritual realization. Stable wisdom is not dramatic emotional suppression. It is freedom from the dictatorship of circumstance. The inner center stays grounded.

Freedom from delusion is mentioned again because without it equanimity would be impossible. So long as one mistakes the fleeting for the ultimate, every gain and loss will seem absolute. Once the mind knows what is real, the emotional charge of worldly opposites changes.

Thus the verse closes this batch by linking Brahman-abidance with lived steadiness. The realized person is not defined by intense reaction to circumstance, but by the quiet stability born of truth. This is the emotional fruit of knowledge and the mark of one who dwells in the real.

original_script:
बाह्यस्पर्शेष्वसक्तात्मा विन्दत्यात्मनि यत्सुखम्।
स ब्रह्मयोगयुक्तात्मा सुखमक्षयमश्नुते।।5.21।।
transliteration:
bāhya-sparśheṣhv asaktātmā vindaty ātmani yat sukham
sa brahma-yoga-yuktātmā sukham akṣhayam aśhnute
word_meanings: bāhya-sparśheṣhu—in external sense contacts; asakta-ātmā—unattached soul; vindati—finds; ātmani—in the self; yat—which; sukham—happiness; saḥ—he; brahma-yoga—concentration on the Supreme; yukta-ātmā—self-connected; sukham—happiness; akṣhayam—unlimited/endless; aśhnute—enjoys
translation_english: Such a liberated person is not attracted to material sense pleasure but is always in trance, enjoying the pleasure within. In this way, the self-realized person enjoys unlimited happiness, for he concentrates on the Supreme.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one who is not attached to external contacts, but finds joy within, discovers imperishable happiness through union with Brahman. This verse marks a turning point in the chapter. Up to this point Krishna has been clearing confusion about renunciation and action. Here he shows what true freedom feels like from the inside. It is not merely the absence of duty or the rejection of the world. It is the discovery of a deeper source of satisfaction that does not depend on changing circumstances.

External pleasures come through contact: sights, sounds, praise, possessions, success, comfort, and the many experiences the senses chase. These can stimulate the mind for a while, but they do not settle the heart because they are always dependent on conditions remaining favorable. When conditions shift, the same mind that felt pleasure becomes anxious, empty, or restless. Krishna therefore directs attention inward, not as a denial of life, but as a correction of dependence.

The joy within is the quiet happiness of a self no longer scattered among objects. Inwardly collected, the person becomes capable of tasting a peace that is not borrowed from the world. This is why Krishna calls it imperishable. It is not produced by possession, so it is not destroyed by loss. For a new reader, the lesson is practical: enjoy what comes, but do not ask outward things to carry the weight of your deepest fulfillment. Lasting peace begins when inner life becomes the center of life.

original_script:
ये हि संस्पर्शजा भोगा दुःखयोनय एव ते।
आद्यन्तवन्तः कौन्तेय न तेषु रमते बुधः।।5.22।।
transliteration:
ye hi saṁsparśha-jā bhogā duḥkha-yonaya eva te
ādy-antavantaḥ kaunteya na teṣhu ramate budhaḥ
word_meanings: ye—those; hi—certainly; saṁsparśha-jāḥ—born of contact with the material senses; bhogāḥ—enjoyments; duḥkha—misery; yonayaḥ—sources of; eva—certainly; te—they are; ādi—beginning; anta-vantaḥ—having an end; kaunteya—Arjun; na—never; teṣhu—in those; ramate—takes delight; budhaḥ—the intelligent person
translation_english: An intelligent person does not take part in the sources of misery, which are due to contact with the material senses. O son of Kunti, such pleasures have a beginning and an end, and so the wise man does not delight in them.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna states plainly that pleasures born of contact with the senses are sources of suffering, because they have a beginning and an end. Therefore, the wise do not delight in them as if they were the goal of life. This teaching can sound severe at first, but its purpose is not to condemn every pleasant experience. Krishna is exposing the hidden instability built into pleasure when it is treated as ultimate.

Anything that begins through contact must also end when the contact is broken, weakened, or exhausted. The mind enjoys, then fears losing the object. If the object remains, habit dulls the pleasure and demands more. If the object disappears, dissatisfaction appears. In both cases, dependency creates subtle suffering. The problem is not that the senses exist, but that the mind expects permanent satisfaction from what is temporary by nature.

Krishna calls the wise person one who sees this structure clearly. Such a person does not hate pleasure, nor does he become morally proud by refusing ordinary enjoyments. Rather, he stops worshiping them. He knows that what arises and fades cannot provide final security. This frees him from the endless pattern of chasing, consuming, tiring, and craving again.

For a new reader, the verse offers a mature way to think about desire. It asks: is this experience simply pleasant for the moment, or have I turned it into something I think I cannot live without? Wisdom begins when we stop confusing stimulation with fulfillment. Then pleasure can be kept in its proper place, and the search for deeper peace can begin seriously.

original_script:
शक्नोतीहैव यः सोढुं प्राक्शरीरविमोक्षणात्।
कामक्रोधोद्भवं वेगं स युक्तः स सुखी नरः।।5.23।।
transliteration:
śhaknotīhaiva yaḥ soḍhuṁ prāk śharīra-vimokṣhaṇāt
kāma-krodhodbhavaṁ vegaṁ sa yuktaḥ sa sukhī naraḥ
word_meanings: śhaknoti—is able; iha eva—in this life; yaḥ—who; soḍhum—to tolerate; prāk—before; śharīra—the body; vimokṣhaṇāt—quitting; kāma—desire; krodha—and anger; udbhavam—generated from; vegam—urges; saḥ—he; yuktaḥ—in trance; saḥ—he; sukhī—happy; naraḥ—human being
translation_english: Before giving up this present body, if one is able to tolerate the urges of the material senses and check the force of desire and anger, he is well situated and is happy in this world.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that one who can withstand the force of desire and anger before leaving the body is a disciplined and happy person. This verse brings the teaching down to a very practical battlefield inside the human mind. Desire and anger are not random emotions in the Gita. Desire pulls the mind outward toward possession and experience; anger erupts when desire is frustrated, blocked, or threatened. Together they disturb judgment, weaken self-mastery, and make peace impossible.

The timing in the verse matters. Krishna says this work must be done while living in the body, not postponed to some abstract future. Spiritual life is not a theory reserved for old age or perfect conditions. It is a present discipline. Every moment of irritation, impulse, craving, jealousy, or emotional heat becomes part of the training. The question is whether the person can remain steady instead of being dragged by inner pressure.

To tolerate does not mean to suppress in a dead or unhealthy way. It means to endure the first surge without automatically obeying it. A person learns to let desire arise without surrendering judgment, and to let anger flare without making it the ruler of speech and action. This pause creates freedom. It allows wisdom to enter before reaction becomes karma.

For a new reader, the verse is deeply practical. Happiness does not come merely from having more favorable circumstances. It comes from no longer being overpowered by every inner storm. The person who can remain steady amid desire and anger is already experiencing a freedom that many outward successes cannot give.

original_script:
योऽन्तःसुखोऽन्तरारामस्तथान्तर्ज्योतिरेव यः।
स योगी ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् ब्रह्मभूतोऽधिगच्छति।।5.24।।
transliteration:
yo ’ntaḥ-sukho ’ntar-ārāmas tathāntar-jyotir eva yaḥ
sa yogī brahma-nirvāṇaṁ brahma-bhūto ’dhigachchhati
word_meanings: yaḥ—who; antaḥ-sukhaḥ—happy from within; antar-ārāmaḥ—rejoicing within; tathā—as well as; antar-jyotiḥ—illuminated within; eva—certainly; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; yogī—a yogi; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; brahma-bhūtaḥ—being self-realized; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: One whose happiness is within, who is active within, and who rejoices within and is illuminated within, is actually the perfect mystic. He is liberated in the Supreme, and ultimately he attains the Supreme.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes the true yogi as one whose joy is within, whose rest is within, and whose light is within. Such a person, established in Brahman, attains liberation. This verse continues the inward movement of the chapter, but with a richer picture. The realized person does not simply avoid outer disturbance; he becomes inwardly fulfilled. Krishna uses a series of repeated expressions to show that the center of life has shifted from the changing outer world to the living reality of the Self.

Happiness within means that the person’s contentment no longer depends mainly on events, approval, or possessions. Rest within means the mind has found a place of settlement, rather than constantly running outward in search of the next object. Light within means insight has awakened. The person is no longer living in spiritual darkness, mistaking the temporary for the eternal or the external personality for the whole self.

Krishna calls such a person a brahma-yogi because this inner integration is not self-absorption in a narrow sense. It is participation in a larger reality. The person becomes inwardly whole because he is aligned with truth, not because he has isolated himself from life. This is why liberation is described not as emptiness, but as illuminated fullness.

For a new reader, the verse offers a clear measure of progress. Spiritual growth is not only about ritual, information, or outward identity. It is seen in whether joy becomes less dependent, whether rest becomes more inward, and whether understanding becomes bright enough to guide life from within. That is the direction Krishna calls liberation.

original_script:
लभन्ते ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृषयः क्षीणकल्मषाः।
छिन्नद्वैधा यतात्मानः सर्वभूतहिते रताः।।5.25।।
transliteration:
labhante brahma-nirvāṇam ṛiṣhayaḥ kṣhīṇa-kalmaṣhāḥ
chhinna-dvaidhā yatātmānaḥ sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ
word_meanings: labhante—achieve; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; ṛiṣhayaḥ—those who are active within; kṣhīṇa-kalmaṣhāḥ—who are devoid of all sins; chhinna—having torn away; dvaidhāḥ—duality; yata-ātmānaḥ—self-engaged; sarva-bhūta—for all living entities; hite—in welfare; ratāḥ—engaged
translation_english: Those who are beyond the dualities that arise from doubts, whose minds are engaged within, who are always busy working for the welfare of all living beings, and who are free from all sins achieve liberation in the Supreme.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that those whose impurities have been destroyed, whose doubts are cut, whose minds are disciplined, and who delight in the welfare of all beings attain Brahman-nirvana. This verse is important because it prevents liberation from being imagined as a purely private or self-enclosed state. The realized person is inwardly free, but that freedom expresses itself in a wider concern for life. True spiritual clarity does not harden the heart; it expands it.

When Krishna speaks of impurities being destroyed, he refers to the inner distortions that cloud perception: selfish attachment, pride, envy, possessiveness, and the restless ego-centered habits that keep the mind revolving around itself. Doubts being cut does not mean every intellectual question has vanished, but that the central confusion about identity, purpose, and the path of life has been resolved. The person knows what matters and is no longer divided at the root.

Out of this inner clarity comes universal goodwill. Working for the welfare of all beings is not presented here as separate from realization; it is one of its fruits. The person who sees more deeply cannot remain narrow in sympathy, because the same spiritual vision that loosens ego also awakens kinship with others.

For a new reader, the verse gives an important test of authentic spirituality. If inward practice makes a person proud, cold, or indifferent, something is incomplete. Real progress should reduce inner confusion and widen compassion at the same time. Krishna joins wisdom and benevolence because liberation is not merely escape from suffering; it is transformation of consciousness into a more truthful and generous way of being.

original_script:
कामक्रोधवियुक्तानां यतीनां यतचेतसाम्।
अभितो ब्रह्मनिर्वाणं वर्तते विदितात्मनाम्।।5.26।।
transliteration:
kāma-krodha-viyuktānāṁ yatīnāṁ yata-chetasām
abhito brahma-nirvāṇaṁ vartate viditātmanām
word_meanings: kāma—from desire; krodha—and anger; viyuktānām—of those who are freed; yatīnām—of the saintly persons; yata-chetasām—who have full control over the mind; abhitaḥ—assured in the near future; brahma-nirvāṇam—liberation in the Supreme; vartate—is there; vidita-ātmanām—of those who are self-realized
translation_english: Those who are free from anger and all material desires, who are self-realized, self-disciplined and constantly endeavoring for perfection, are assured of liberation in the Supreme in the very near future.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that for sages who are free from desire and anger, who have mastered themselves and know the Self, the peace of Brahman is near at hand. This verse continues the chapter’s teaching on inner readiness. Liberation is not something arbitrarily granted from outside. It becomes near when the major obstacles to clarity lose their hold. Krishna names desire and anger again because they are among the most powerful forces that keep consciousness outward-bound and agitated.

Freedom from desire does not mean the body becomes lifeless or the personality becomes incapable of preference. It means craving no longer governs the person. Freedom from anger does not mean moral seriousness disappears. It means reaction is no longer violent, ego-driven, or blinding. Self-mastery gives the mind steadiness, and self-knowledge gives it direction. Together they create the conditions in which peace can actually be recognized.

The phrase “near at hand” is especially encouraging. Krishna does not present liberation as impossibly remote. When the mind is purified and disciplined, the highest peace is not far away in time or space. It is close, but usually hidden by turbulence. The work of yoga is therefore less about manufacturing truth than about removing what obscures it.

For a new reader, this verse is both demanding and hopeful. It is demanding because it asks for serious inner discipline. It is hopeful because it says that genuine effort matters immediately. Each step away from anger, compulsion, and self-forgetfulness is already a step toward the peace Krishna describes. The path is gradual, but its destination begins to draw near even now.

original_script:
स्पर्शान्कृत्वा बहिर्बाह्यांश्चक्षुश्चैवान्तरे भ्रुवोः।
प्राणापानौ समौ कृत्वा नासाभ्यन्तरचारिणौ।।5.27।।
transliteration:
sparśhān kṛitvā bahir bāhyānśh chakṣhuśh chaivāntare bhruvoḥ
prāṇāpānau samau kṛitvā nāsābhyantara-chāriṇau
word_meanings: sparśhān—external sense objects; kṛitvā—keeping; bahiḥ—outside; bāhyān—external; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; cha—also; eva—certainly; antare—between; bhruvoḥ—the eyebrows; prāṇa-apānau—upward and downward breaths; samau—equal; kṛitvā—making; nāsa-abhyantara—within the nostrils; chāriṇau—moving
translation_english: Shutting out all external sense objects, keeping the eyes and vision concentrated between the two eyebrows, suspending the inward and outward breaths within the nostrils...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now briefly turns to a more technical description of meditation, saying that the practitioner withdraws attention from external sense objects, directs the gaze between the eyebrows, and regulates the movement of the life-breath within the nostrils. This verse gives a concentrated picture of yogic discipline. The point is not to create fascination with technique for its own sake, but to show how inner steadiness is supported by trained attention, body, and breath.

Withdrawing from external objects means the mind is no longer continually feeding on impressions. Normally the senses pull awareness outward in many directions, and the mind follows them, becoming dispersed. Meditation reverses this movement. The gaze is steadied, not to force mystical drama, but to help collect attention. Breath regulation similarly supports inward balance, since breath and mind are closely linked. When breathing becomes calmer and more ordered, the mind often becomes less scattered.

This verse should be read in the spirit of integration, not performance. Krishna is not offering a mechanical shortcut where posture or breath alone guarantees realization. Rather, he shows that inner life can be trained systematically. The body, breath, and attention can be brought into cooperation with the larger aim of self-mastery.

For a new reader, the lesson is that spiritual life includes method as well as insight. Noble ideas are important, but without some discipline the mind easily falls back into old habits. Meditation begins by reducing outward distraction so that deeper awareness has room to emerge. Technique serves truth when it helps the whole person become inwardly gathered.

original_script:
यतेन्द्रियमनोबुद्धिर्मुनिर्मोक्षपरायणः।
विगतच्छाभयक्रोधो यः सदा मुक्त एव सः।।5.28।।
transliteration:
yatendriya-mano-buddhir munir mokṣha-parāyaṇaḥ
vigatechchhā-bhaya-krodho yaḥ sadā mukta eva saḥ
word_meanings: yata—controlled; indriya—senses; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—intellect; muniḥ—the sage; mokṣha—liberation; parāyaṇaḥ—being dedicated to; vigata—discarded; ichchhā—desire; bhaya—fear; krodhaḥ—anger; yaḥ—who; sadā—always; muktaḥ—liberated; eva—certainly; saḥ—he
translation_english: ...and thus controlling the mind, senses and intelligence, the transcendentalist seeking liberation becomes free from desire, fear and anger. One who is always in this state is certainly liberated.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues the previous verse by describing the sage who has mastered the senses, mind, and intellect, and who is free from desire, fear, and anger. Such a person, intent on liberation, is said to be always liberated. This does not mean the person has ceased to live in the world. It means that bondage has already been broken at its root. The forces that normally enslave the mind no longer dictate identity or action.

Desire, fear, and anger form a powerful chain. Desire chases what it wants. Fear trembles before loss or harm. Anger rises when the ego is obstructed. Together they keep a person inwardly dependent on external conditions. Krishna presents liberation as freedom from domination by these states. The mind may still register impulses or threats, but it is no longer controlled by them. Because the intellect is clear and the senses are disciplined, awareness remains centered rather than captured.

The verse also shows that liberation is not merely a final reward after death. It has a present dimension. One who lives in this steady condition participates already in freedom. In this sense, spiritual realization is not only future attainment but current transformation.

For a new reader, this verse helps clarify what inner freedom looks like in ordinary life. It is not passivity or emotional numbness. It is the ability to act without craving, to face uncertainty without panic, and to respond to difficulty without being ruled by anger. A person who lives like this is already tasting the liberation the Gita teaches.

original_script:
भोक्तारं यज्ञतपसां सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम्।
सुहृदं सर्वभूतानां ज्ञात्वा मां शान्तिमृच्छति।।5.29।।
transliteration:
bhoktāraṁ yajña-tapasāṁ sarva-loka-maheśhvaram
suhṛidaṁ sarva-bhūtānāṁ jñātvā māṁ śhāntim ṛichchhati
word_meanings: bhoktāram—the beneficiary; yajña—of sacrifices; tapasām—and penances; sarva-loka—of all worlds; mahā-īśhvaram—the Supreme Lord; su-hṛidam—the benefactor/friend; sarva-bhūtānām—of all living entities; jñātvā—knowing; mām—Me; śhāntim—peace; ṛichchhati—attains
translation_english: A person in full consciousness of Me, knowing Me to be the ultimate beneficiary of all sacrifices and penances, the Supreme Lord of all planets and demigods, and the benefactor and well-wisher of all living entities, attains peace from the pangs of material miseries.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by declaring that peace comes to the one who knows him as the enjoyer of sacrifices and austerities, the great Lord of all worlds, and the well-wishing friend of all beings. This conclusion gathers the chapter’s teachings into a devotional center. Renunciation, disciplined action, self-mastery, meditation, and Brahman-realization are not left as isolated practices. They reach completion in right understanding of the Divine.

To know Krishna as the enjoyer of sacrifice and austerity means recognizing that spiritual effort is not an offering to the ego. We do not perform discipline in order to become self-important or spiritually superior. All sincere action is fulfilled when offered to the Highest. To know him as the great Lord of all worlds means reality is not ownerless or chaotic. There is an ultimate governing truth beyond the anxious claims of the individual ego. To know him as the friend of all beings is perhaps the most comforting phrase of all. The Divine is not merely powerful, but benevolent. Krishna is not distant from life; he is intimately concerned with the good of all.

This is why peace becomes possible. The mind stops carrying the unbearable burden of private control. Action can be offered, austerity can be purified, and the heart can relax into trust. For a new reader, the verse is a profound ending: the peace Krishna teaches is not only psychological balance. It is the fruit of seeing one’s life, duties, struggles, and aspirations in relation to a Lord who is both supreme and compassionate. That recognition settles the heart.

original_script: कर्मसंन्यासयोगः
transliteration: Karma Sannyāsa Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of Renunciation
deep_dive_text: Chapter 5 resolves the tension between 'doing' and 'being.' Krishna explains that while a monk might renounce the world, a Karma Yogi renounces the 'ego' within the world. This is the art of performing every task as a divine offering, remaining as untouched by sin or attachment as a lotus leaf is by water. The true sage sees with equal vision a learned scholar, a cow, an elephant, and even an outcast, recognizing the same Divine Spark in all. By mastering the senses and centering the intellect, the seeker attains a peace that is independent of external circumstances.

original_script: अथ षष्ठोऽध्यायः - आत्मसंयमयोगः
transliteration: Ātma Saṁyama Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Ātma—self; Saṁyama—control/meditation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Meditation and Self-Discipline
deep_dive_text: This chapter presents the discipline of meditation and inner steadiness. Krishna describes practical foundations such as a clean place, a balanced posture, moderation in living, and the steady training of the mind. He also explains that the mind can become either a friend or an obstacle, and reassures the seeker that sincere spiritual effort is never lost.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः।
स संन्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः।।6.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
anāśhritaḥ karma-phalaṁ kāryaṁ karma karoti yaḥ
sa sannyāsī cha yogī cha na niragnir na chākriyaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; anāśhritaḥ—without taking shelter; karma-phalam—the result of work; kāryam—obligatory; karma—work; karoti—performs; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; sannyāsī—a renunciant; cha—also; yogī—a yogi; cha—also; na—not; niragniḥ—one who maintains no fire; na—not; cha—also; akriyaḥ—without activity
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life, and he is the true mystic, not he who lights no fire and performs no work.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna opens this chapter by redefining who truly deserves to be called a renunciate and a yogi. It is not the person who merely abandons ritual duties, stops tending sacred fires, or gives up visible activity. The real renunciate is the one who performs the work that ought to be done without leaning on the results for identity, pride, or personal gain. This is an important correction, because spiritual life is often misunderstood as external withdrawal alone. Krishna keeps insisting that what matters most is the quality of consciousness behind action.

This verse continues the argument he has been making through earlier chapters. One can remain active and still be inwardly free, or one can become outwardly inactive while remaining full of desire, ego, and expectation. Renunciation, therefore, is not laziness, avoidance, or a dramatic social pose. It is freedom from dependence on outcomes. Yoga is not simply bodily stillness or retreat from the world. It is disciplined union with the highest truth while carrying out one’s rightful responsibilities.

For a new reader, the verse is very practical. Many people assume spirituality begins only when ordinary duties are left behind. Krishna says the opposite can be true. If you do the work that is yours to do, but do not build your worth on success, praise, or reward, you are already walking the path of yoga. This teaching protects spiritual life from hypocrisy. It keeps the focus on sincerity, steadiness, and inward freedom rather than appearance.

original_script:
यं संन्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव।
न ह्यसंन्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन।।6.2।।
transliteration:
yaṁ sannyāsam iti prāhur yogaṁ taṁ viddhi pāṇḍava
na hy asannyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaśhchana
word_meanings: yam—what; sannyāsam—renunciation; iti—thus; prāhuḥ—they say; yogam—Yoga; tam—that; viddhi—know; pāṇḍava—Arjun; na—never; hi—certainly; asannyasta—without renouncing; saṅkalpaḥ—desire for self-satisfaction; yogī—a yogi; bhavati—becomes; kaśhchana—anyone
translation_english: What is called renunciation is the same as Yoga, or linking oneself with the Supreme, O son of Pandu, for no one can become a yogi unless he renounces the desire for self-satisfaction.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna goes further and says that what is called renunciation should also be understood as yoga, because no one becomes a yogi without giving up selfish intention. This verse joins two ideas that people often separate. Renunciation can sound like letting go, while yoga can sound like disciplined union or practice. Krishna shows they are inwardly linked. Real yoga requires renunciation of self-centered craving, and real renunciation is not empty negation but movement toward spiritual integration.

The key phrase is the abandonment of personal desire as the organizing principle of life. Krishna is not saying a person should become incapable of all preference or all initiative. He is saying that the will must stop revolving around private gratification. As long as spiritual practice is secretly driven by ego, ambition, display, or the hunger to possess experience, the mind remains divided. It may look religious, disciplined, or philosophical, but it is not yet truly joined to the Divine.

This verse is important because it prevents yoga from being reduced to technique. Posture, breath, isolation, or concentration can all have value, but if selfish desire still rules the heart, the essential work remains unfinished. Renunciation here means interior simplification. The person stops asking every action, relationship, or discipline to serve the small self.

For a new reader, Krishna’s point is clear: yoga is not only something you do; it is a change in what you live for. The less your life is ruled by private satisfaction alone, the more capable you become of genuine spiritual union.

original_script:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते।।6.3।।
transliteration:
ārurukṣhor muner yogaṁ karma kāraṇam uchyate
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śhamaḥ kāraṇam uchyate
word_meanings: ārurukṣhoḥ—who is just beginning the path; muneḥ—of the sage; yogam—Yoga; karma—action; kāraṇam—the means; uchyate—is said; yoga-ārūḍhasya—of one who has attained the summit; tasya—his; eva—certainly; śhamaḥ—quiescence/stillness; kāraṇam—the means; uchyate—is said
translation_english: For one who is a beginner in the eightfold yoga system, work is said to be the means; and for one who is already elevated in yoga, cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now explains that the path has stages. For one who is beginning in yoga, action is the means. For one who is already established in yoga, stillness and cessation become the means. This verse is important because it introduces spiritual development in a realistic way. Krishna does not prescribe the same discipline in the same form for everyone. What is necessary depends on where the person actually stands.

For the beginner, disciplined action is essential. A restless mind cannot simply be ordered into silence. Work performed in the right spirit helps purify motives, reduce selfishness, and build steadiness. Duties, service, self-restraint, and ethical conduct prepare the inner life for deeper concentration. If a beginner tries to imitate advanced renunciation too early, the result is often fantasy, laziness, or hidden conflict. Action becomes a training ground.

For one already established in yoga, a different emphasis appears. Once the mind has been purified and stabilized, external activity no longer plays the same preparatory role. Then inner stillness, quietude, and withdrawal become more central. Krishna is not contradicting himself; he is describing sequence. First the personality is disciplined through right action. Then the matured seeker can rest more fully in contemplation.

For a new reader, the verse teaches patience and honesty. Spiritual growth has an order. There is no need to imitate the outer form of an advanced sage before the foundation has been laid. The right question is not, “What seems highest?” but “What truly helps me move from where I am toward greater clarity?” Krishna’s answer is practical: begin with disciplined action, and let maturity open the door to deeper stillness.

original_script:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसंन्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते।।6.4।।
transliteration:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣhu na karmasv anuṣhajjate
sarva-saṅkalpa-sannyāsī yogārūḍhas tadochyate
word_meanings: yadā—when; hi—certainly; na—neither; indriya-artheṣhu—in sense objects; na—nor; karmasu—in activities; anuṣhajjate—is attached; sarva-saṅkalpa—of all material desires; sannyāsī—renunciant; yoga-ārūḍhaḥ—elevated in Yoga; tadā—at that time; uchyate—is said
translation_english: A person is said to be elevated in yoga when, having renounced all material desires, he neither acts for sense gratification nor engages in fruitive activities.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says a person is said to be established in yoga when he has given up material desires and no longer acts for sense gratification or merely fruitive aims. This verse describes the transition from outer discipline to inner stabilization. The yogi is not defined here by costume, isolation, or reputation, but by what no longer rules his choices. Desire has lost its commanding place, and action is no longer organized around acquisition, excitement, or reward.

This does not mean such a person becomes inactive or emotionally dead. Rather, the center of motivation changes. Most people act because they want pleasure, recognition, security, or possession. Even noble-seeming action can be mixed with the hope of gain. Krishna describes yoga as the loosening of that inner dependence. The mind becomes less transactional. It is no longer constantly asking, “What will I get?” or “How will this enhance me?”

This verse also helps explain why freedom cannot be judged from outside alone. A person may still be working, speaking, teaching, or serving, yet inwardly remain detached from selfish aims. Another person may appear quiet or detached while still burning with hidden desire. Krishna keeps forcing attention back to the inner structure of consciousness.

For a new reader, the verse offers a useful test. When you act, are you driven mainly by appetite, ego, or the expectation of results, or is there growing freedom in the mind? Yoga begins to mature when action is no longer chained to self-gratification. The verse invites the seeker to measure progress not by display, but by reduced slavery to desire.

original_script:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत्।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः।।6.5।।
transliteration:
uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet
ātmāiva hyātmano bandhur ātmāiva ripur ātmanaḥ
word_meanings: uddharet—one must deliver; ātmanā—by the mind; ātmānam—the self; na—never; ātmānam—the self; avasādayet—degrade; ātmā—mind; eva—certainly; hi—indeed; ātmanaḥ—of the self; bandhuḥ—friend; ātmā—mind; eva—certainly; ripuḥ—enemy; ātmanaḥ—of the self
translation_english: One must deliver himself with the help of his mind, and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns inward with one of the chapter’s most powerful psychological teachings: a person must lift himself by means of the mind and not let himself fall, for the mind can be both friend and enemy. This verse is striking because it places enormous importance on inner agency. Human life is not described as helplessly controlled from outside. The mind plays a decisive role in whether a person rises toward clarity or sinks into confusion.

To “lift oneself” does not mean the ego can manufacture enlightenment by force. It means one must participate in one’s own transformation. The mind can be educated, disciplined, directed, and purified. If neglected, it pulls downward through habit, fear, resentment, indulgence, and fantasy. If trained, it becomes a support for wisdom. Krishna therefore presents the mind as a field of responsibility. Spiritual life is not only belief; it is the daily work of ordering one’s interior world.

This verse is also compassionate in an exacting way. It does not encourage self-hatred or despair. Krishna does not say, “You are your own enemy by nature.” He says the mind becomes an enemy when left unconquered. The same mind can become an ally. This makes change possible.

For a new reader, the lesson is immediate. You may not control every event, but you must take seriously how you train attention, speech, desire, and thought. Every repeated pattern strengthens either bondage or freedom. The mind is not a neutral background. It is an instrument that can carry you upward if rightly guided, or drag you downward if simply obeyed.

original_script:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत्।।6.6।।
transliteration:
bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmāivātmanā jitaḥ
anātmanas tu śhatrutve vartetātmāiva śhatru-vat
word_meanings: bandhuḥ—friend; ātmā—the mind; ātmanaḥ—of the self; tasya—of him; yena—by whom; ātmā—the mind; eva—certainly; ātmā—by the self; jitaḥ—is conquered; anātmanaḥ—of one who has failed to control the mind; tu—but; śhatrutve—for an enemy; varteta—remains; ātmā eva—the mind itself; śhatru-vat—like an enemy
translation_english: For him who has conquered the mind, the mind is the best of friends; but for one who has failed to do so, his mind will remain the greatest enemy.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna develops the previous verse by stating that for one who has conquered the mind, the mind becomes the best of friends, but for one who has failed to do so, it remains the greatest enemy. This is a remarkably realistic description of human experience. The mind is not automatically trustworthy simply because it is our own. It can support truth, or it can distort reality so thoroughly that we begin to cooperate with our own bondage.

To conquer the mind does not mean to destroy it. Krishna is not advocating numbness or violence toward inner life. Conquest here means mastery. The mind no longer runs wherever habit, craving, or fear sends it. It learns to obey deeper intelligence. Once disciplined, the mind becomes immensely helpful. It can hold attention steady, return to what matters, endure difficulty, and reinforce wise action. In that condition it truly becomes a friend.

An undisciplined mind does the opposite. It magnifies insult, fuels fantasy, feeds addiction, and continually interprets life through ego. It can make temporary things seem ultimate and trivial reactions seem unavoidable. In this sense it becomes an enemy not because it wants harm in a personal way, but because its disorder repeatedly leads the person away from peace.

For a new reader, the verse provides a crucial reorientation. The question is not simply whether your thoughts are intense, clever, or emotionally convincing. The question is whether your mind has been trained to serve truth. If it has, it becomes one of your greatest assets. If not, the battle for spiritual life will be fought and often lost from within.

original_script:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः।।6.7।।
transliteration:
jitātmanaḥ praśhāntasya paramātmā samāhitaḥ
śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣhu tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
word_meanings: jita-ātmanaḥ—of one who has conquered the mind; praśhāntasya—who has attained tranquility; parama-ātmā—the Supreme Self; samāhitaḥ—perfectly situated; śhīta—cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkheṣhu—in distress; tathā—also; māna—honor; apamānayoḥ—in dishonor
translation_english: For one who has conquered the mind, the Supersoul is already reached, for he has attained tranquility. To such a man happiness and distress, heat and cold, honor and dishonor are all the same.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that for one who has mastered the mind, the Supersoul is already attained, because such a person has become peaceful. Heat and cold, pleasure and pain, honor and dishonor are then regarded alike. This verse connects inner discipline with spiritual presence. Mastery of the mind is not presented as a merely mental achievement. It opens the person to communion with the deeper divine reality already present within.

The term tranquility is central here. The person who has conquered the mind is not tossed about by every outer change. Life continues to bring opposites: comfort and discomfort, praise and blame, success and disappointment. Krishna does not pretend these disappear. What changes is the way consciousness relates to them. They no longer have the power to overthrow one’s center. Peace becomes more fundamental than fluctuation.

This does not mean indifference in a cold sense. The yogi still experiences the world, but does not surrender identity to passing conditions. Because the mind is steady, the divine presence is no longer obscured by constant agitation. Krishna thus suggests that spiritual realization is not merely gained through extraordinary experiences; it also appears when disturbance no longer dominates perception.

For a new reader, the verse explains why emotional steadiness matters so much in the Gita. If every external opposite controls the mind, inner clarity cannot last. The more one learns to remain balanced amid changing conditions, the more accessible peace becomes. This is not emotional suppression, but spiritual grounding. A stable mind becomes a place where higher truth can actually be known.

original_script:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः।।6.8।।
transliteration:
jñāna-vijñāna-tṛiptātmā kūṭa-stho vijitendriyaḥ
yukta ity uchyate yogī sama-loṣhṭāśhma-kāñchanaḥ
word_meanings: jñāna—knowledge; vijñāna—realized knowledge; tṛipta—satisfied; ātmā—soul; kūṭa-sthaḥ—unchanging; vijita-indriyaḥ—having conquered the senses; yuktaḥ—united; iti—thus; uchyate—is said; yogī—a yogi; sama—equal; loṣhṭa—pebble; aśhma—stone; kāñchanaḥ—gold
translation_english: A person is said to be established in self-realization and is called a yogi when he is fully satisfied by virtue of acquired knowledge and realization. Such a person is situated in transcendence and is self-controlled. He sees everything—whether it be pebbles, stones or gold—as the same.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes the realized yogi as one satisfied by knowledge and direct realization, steady and self-controlled, seeing clod, stone, and gold alike. The verse does not teach that all objects are identical in practical use. Gold can buy what a clod cannot. The point is deeper: the yogi is no longer inwardly overpowered by socially assigned value. Possessions do not dictate worth, and glitter does not command devotion.

Krishna distinguishes between knowledge and realization because information alone does not transform consciousness. A person may know teachings intellectually yet still be emotionally enslaved by status, wealth, and comparison. Realization means truth has moved from concept into perception. The person has seen enough of the Self and of reality to stop exaggerating the power of material distinctions.

This satisfaction is crucial. The yogi is not pretending that riches are meaningless while secretly longing for them. He is content through deeper understanding. Because he has touched something more enduring than possession, his relation to objects changes naturally. Self-control follows from fulfilled intelligence. When the heart no longer expects salvation from what is external, grasping relaxes.

For a new reader, the verse speaks directly to modern life. Much suffering comes from assigning absolute value to what is only relative: money, status, display, and comparison. Krishna does not deny that these have a place in worldly life. He says they must be seen in perspective. The person established in spiritual knowledge is no longer inwardly impoverished by lacking what society glorifies. That is one mark of freedom.

original_script:
सुहृन्मित्रार्युदासीनमध्यस्थद्वेष्यबन्धुषु।
साधुष्वपि च पापेषु समबुद्धिर्विशिष्यते।।6.9।।
transliteration:
suhṛin-mitrāry-udāsīna-madhyastha-dveṣhya-bandhuṣhu
sādhuṣhv api cha pāpeṣhu sama-buddhir viśhiṣhyate
word_meanings: su-hṛit—well-wishers; mitra—friends; ari—enemies; udāsīna—the indifferent; madhyastha—mediators; dveṣhya—the envious; bandhuṣhu—relatives; sādhuṣhu—the pious; api—even; cha—also; pāpeṣhu—the sinners; sama-buddhiḥ—having equal vision; viśhiṣhyate—is considered even higher
translation_english: A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well-wishers, affectionate friends, enemies, the indifferent, mediators, the envious, relatives, the pious and the sinners all with an equal mind.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says the advanced yogi maintains an equal mind toward friends, well-wishers, enemies, neutrals, mediators, the hateful, relatives, the righteous, and even the unrighteous. This is one of the chapter’s most demanding teachings because it confronts the mind’s habit of dividing the world into categories that immediately determine emotional response. Krishna does not deny that people behave differently or that moral discernment is necessary. He is speaking about equality of inner regard, not blindness to conduct.

An equal mind means the yogi does not lose spiritual balance because of social labels. Affection, hostility, usefulness, kinship, and moral evaluation do not erase the deeper truth that all beings participate in the same underlying reality. The ego prefers selective humanity: generosity toward allies, contempt toward opponents, and indifference toward strangers. Krishna calls the yogi beyond that narrowness.

This teaching also protects the seeker from reaction-based living. If the mind becomes noble only toward those who please it, then its virtue is still dependent on preference. Equality means a stable vision that does not collapse under emotional provocation. One may still oppose wrongdoing, maintain boundaries, or choose wise company, but without hatred becoming the organizing principle of consciousness.

For a new reader, this verse is a serious spiritual challenge. It asks whether you can see more than roles, wounds, and loyalties. Equal-mindedness does not erase judgment; it purifies it. The aim is not confusion about good and evil, but freedom from egoic partiality so that compassion, justice, and discernment can arise from a steadier source.

original_script:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः।।6.10।।
transliteration:
yogī yuñjīta satatam ātmānaṁ rahasi sthitaḥ
ekākī yata-chittātmā nirāśhīr aparigrahaḥ
word_meanings: yogī—a yogi; yuñjīta—should concentrate; satatam—constantly; ātmānam—himself (the mind); rahasi—in a secluded place; sthitaḥ—situated; ekākī—alone; yata-chitta-ātmā—having controlled mind and self; nirāśhīḥ—free from desires; aparigrahaḥ—free from possessiveness
translation_english: A yogi should constantly engage his mind in meditation on the Supreme Self; he should live alone in a secluded place and should always carefully control his mind. He should be free from desires and feelings of possessiveness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns to the actual discipline of meditation. The yogi, he says, should regularly gather the mind in meditation, living in seclusion, self-controlled, free from expectation, and without possessiveness. This verse marks a shift from general principles to concrete practice. Up to now Krishna has clarified who the yogi is inwardly. Now he begins to describe the conditions that help such inward steadiness become real.

Seclusion here does not mean permanent social rejection or hostility toward the world. It means creating conditions where distraction is reduced and the mind can stop feeding constantly on impressions, conversations, ambitions, and possessions. Most minds are scattered not because they are incapable of concentration, but because they are rarely withdrawn from stimulation long enough to become collected. Solitude becomes a training ground for clarity.

Freedom from expectation and possessiveness is equally important. Meditation is undermined when the mind arrives full of acquisition, fantasy, and subtle bargaining. If the practitioner sits only to gain power, experiences, prestige, or control, the heart remains outward-facing. Krishna therefore links inner practice to simplicity of motive and reduced attachment.

For a new reader, the verse teaches that meditation is not just closing the eyes for a few minutes. It is part of a broader simplification of life. Space, restraint, regularity, and non-possessiveness all support the inward turn. Even if one does not literally live alone, the principle remains: the mind needs protected intervals of quiet and a reduction of grasping if it is to deepen beyond surface restlessness.

original_script:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम्।।6.11।।
transliteration:
śhuchau deśhe pratiṣhṭhāpya sthiram āsanam ātmanaḥ
nāty-uchchhritaṁ nāti-nīchaṁ chailājina-kuśhottaram
word_meanings: śhuchau—in a clean; deśhe—place; pratiṣhṭhāpya—having established; sthiram—steady; āsanam—seat; ātmanaḥ—his own; na—not; ati-uchchhritam—too high; na—not; ati-nīcham—too low; chaila-ajina-kuśha-uttaram—covered with cloth, deerskin, and kusha grass
translation_english: To practice yoga, one should go to a secluded place and should lay kusha grass on the ground and then cover it with a deerskin and a soft cloth. The seat should be neither too high nor too low and should be situated in a sacred place.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna specifies that the yogi should establish a proper seat in a clean and secluded place, neither too high nor too low, traditionally prepared with kusa grass, skin, and cloth. The detail may seem merely ritualistic, but it serves an important purpose. Meditation is not treated as a vague mood. It is given form, order, and physical seriousness. The body and environment matter because they influence the mind.

The instruction that the seat should be clean and balanced suggests moderation and stability. Too much discomfort distracts; too much luxury softens effort. The setting should support wakefulness without agitation. In the ancient context, the listed materials reflected available practice conditions. The deeper principle is that the place of meditation should be intentionally prepared. Sacred work is helped by physical order.

This verse also teaches that spiritual practice includes embodiment. One cannot always leap directly into pure contemplation while ignoring posture, place, habit, and atmosphere. Krishna recognizes that human consciousness is shaped by environment. A stable seat supports a stable practice, and a stable practice supports a stable mind.

For a new reader, the lesson is simple and useful. If meditation is always attempted casually, in clutter, noise, or physical imbalance, the mind receives mixed signals. Creating a consistent and respectful setting is not superstition; it is intelligent preparation. The external arrangement does not produce realization by itself, but it can remove unnecessary obstacles and remind the practitioner that inner life deserves deliberate space.

original_script:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये।।6.12।।
transliteration:
tatraikāgraṁ manaḥ kṛitvā yata-chittendriya-kriyaḥ
upaviśhyāsane yuñjyād yogam ātma-viśhuddhaye
word_meanings: tatra—there; eka-agram—one-pointed; manaḥ—mind; kṛitvā—making; yata-chitta-indriya-kriyaḥ—controlling the mind, senses, and activities; upaviśhya—sitting; āsane—on the seat; yuñjyāt—should practice; yogam—Yoga; ātma-viśhuddhaye—for self-purification
translation_english: The yogi should then sit on it and practice yoga by controlling the mind, senses and activities and fixing the mind on one point to purify the self.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by saying that seated there, the yogi should practice yoga for purification by bringing mind, senses, and activity under control and fixing attention in one direction. Meditation is therefore not escape into vagueness. It is a disciplined gathering of scattered powers. The mind, senses, and actions normally move in many directions, each pulling consciousness outward. Yoga begins when this dispersion is reduced and inner energy becomes unified.

The purpose Krishna gives is purification. This is a significant word. The mind is not purified merely by accumulating information or by performing external acts alone. It is purified when its compulsions, distractions, and mixed motives begin to settle. Concentration reveals how restless and fragmented the personality often is. It also becomes a means of healing that fragmentation by training the person to remain present, steady, and inwardly ordered.

Fixing the mind on one point does not mean shrinking consciousness into rigidity. Rather, it means overcoming its habit of continual wandering. Without some form of gathered attention, deeper insight cannot stabilize. The senses keep reporting the world, memories keep surfacing, desires keep intruding, and the mind keeps following them. Yoga interrupts that chain.

For a new reader, the verse clarifies that meditation is an active discipline even when outwardly still. Sitting quietly is not enough by itself. The real work is to collect what has become scattered. Over time, this purification makes the mind clearer, the will stronger, and the inner life less dominated by impulse. Krishna presents concentration not as a narrow trick, but as a doorway to a more unified self.

original_script:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्।।6.13।।
transliteration:
samaṁ kāya-śhiro-grīvaṁ dhārayann achalaṁ sthiraḥ
samprekṣhya nāsikāgraṁ svaṁ diśhaśh chānavalokayan
word_meanings: samam—straight; kāya—body; śhiraḥ—head; grīvam—neck; dhārayan—holding; achalam—unmoving; sthiraḥ—steady; samprekṣhya—gazing; nāsika-agram—at the tip of the nose; swam—own; diśhaḥ—all directions; cha—also; anavalokayan—not looking
translation_english: One should hold one’s body, neck and head erect in a straight line and stare steadily at the tip of the nose. Thus, with an unagitated, subdued mind, devoid of fear, completely free from sex life, one should meditate upon Me within the heart and make Me the ultimate goal of life.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna instructs the yogi to keep the body, head, and neck upright and steady, and to direct the gaze in a restrained way. Though brief, this verse shows how closely spiritual practice involves physical composure. The body is not dismissed as irrelevant. It can either support concentration or constantly interrupt it. Postural steadiness helps the mind become less erratic, just as a restless body often reflects and reinforces a restless mind.

The instruction about the gaze serves the same purpose. The eyes are major channels of outward movement. When they are undisciplined, attention easily scatters. Krishna therefore recommends a restrained visual focus, not as an end in itself, but as an aid to collected awareness. The point is not strain or artificial intensity. It is balanced attentiveness.

The verse also implies dignity in practice. Meditation is neither collapse nor theatrical display. The posture is alert but not tense, composed but not rigid. This physical balance mirrors the mental state yoga seeks: wakeful calm. A person who sits in this way signals inwardly that the practice is serious, present, and disciplined.

For a new reader, the teaching is practical. Many people think meditation begins only in the mind, but physical disarray can keep the mind unsettled. An upright posture supports clarity, steadiness, and self-respect in practice. Krishna’s instruction reminds us that inner life is not floating abstraction. The way one sits, breathes, and directs attention participates in the work of making consciousness more stable and available to truth.

original_script:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तॊ युक्त आसीत मत्परः।।6.14।।
transliteration:
praśhāntātmā vigata-bhīr brahmachāri-vrate sthitah
manaḥ saṁyamya mach-chitto yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ
word_meanings: praśhānta-ātmā—serene soul; vigata-bhīḥ—fearless; brahmachāri-vrate—in the vow of celibacy; sthitaḥ—situated; manaḥ—mind; saṁyamya—subduing; mat-chittaḥ—thinking of Me; yuktaḥ—united; āsīta—should sit; mat-paraḥ—devoted to Me
translation_english: With a serene and fearless mind, firm in the vow of celibacy, the yogi should subdue the mind, fix the heart on Me, and make Me the ultimate goal.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna adds that the meditator should be serene, fearless, disciplined, and inwardly devoted, with the mind controlled and directed toward him as the highest goal. This verse makes clear that meditation is not just posture and breath. It is also moral and devotional orientation. Serenity, fearlessness, self-restraint, and God-centered intention belong to the structure of yoga itself.

Fearlessness matters because a deeply quiet mind often begins to encounter what it usually avoids: insecurity, loneliness, memory, mortality, and the loss of control. Without courage, meditation can become shallow or evasive. Serenity matters because agitation keeps consciousness tied to reaction. Discipline protects practice from being ruled by impulse. Krishna’s mention of celibate restraint in the traditional sense points more broadly to conservation of vital energy and the refusal to live as a servant of appetite.

Most importantly, the mind is to be fixed on Krishna. This gives the practice a devotional center. The goal is not merely self-calming or private inwardness. Meditation is ordered toward relationship with the Divine. That orientation keeps yoga from becoming self-enclosed. The heart is not asked simply to become empty, but to become rightly directed.

For a new reader, the verse shows that true meditation involves the whole person. One cannot expect deep peace while living carelessly, feeding fear, and treating spiritual practice as a technique for comfort alone. Krishna asks for inward purity, courage, restraint, and loving focus. When these begin to converge, meditation becomes more than relaxation. It becomes a path of transformation.

original_script:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति।।6.15।।
transliteration:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ
śhāntiṁ nirvāṇa-paramāṁ mat-saństhām adhigachchhati
word_meanings: yuñjan—practicing; evam—thus; sadā—constantly; ātmānam—the self (mind); yogī—the yogi; niyata-mānasaḥ—with a disciplined mind; śhāntim—peace; nirvāṇa-paramām—culminating in liberation; mat-saństhām—existing in Me; adhigachchhati—attains
translation_english: Thus practicing constant control of the body, mind and activities, the mystic transcendentalist, his mind regulated, attains to the kingdom of God [or the abode of Krishna] by cessation of material existence.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that by practicing in this way with disciplined mind and controlled activity, the yogi attains peace and reaches the highest state in him. This verse gives the fruit of the meditation discipline just described. The result is not merely temporary calm or improved concentration. Krishna speaks of profound peace and of a state that transcends ordinary material existence. The practice is therefore serious in aim: it leads beyond agitation toward spiritual fulfillment.

The phrase regulated mind is important. Peace is not treated as an accidental mood. It emerges when consciousness is steadily ordered through practice. A mind constantly driven by desire, fear, comparison, and sensory excess cannot settle deeply. Through repeated discipline, however, the movements that obscure inner clarity begin to lose force. What remains is a quieter, more transparent consciousness capable of receiving higher reality.

Krishna also keeps the goal personal and devotional. The culmination of meditation is not a sterile void but resting in the Divine. This protects the teaching from being reduced to psychological technique. Even when meditation produces tranquility, Krishna points beyond tranquility itself to spiritual union and God-centered peace.

For a new reader, the verse offers both motivation and perspective. Meditation is not only about feeling better after a stressful day, though it may help with that. In the Gita it is part of a larger path whose end is deep inner peace rooted in the Divine. The more sincerely the practice is undertaken, the more it becomes a way of leaving behind the tyranny of restless material consciousness.

original_script:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः।
न चाति स्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन।।6.16।।
transliteration:
nāty-aśhnatas tu yogo ’sti na chaikāntam anaśhnataḥ
na chāti svapna-śhīlasya jāgrato naiva chārjuna
word_meanings: na—not; ati—too much; aśhnataḥ—of one who eats; tu—but; yogaḥ—Yoga; asti—there is; na—not; cha—also; ekāntam—at all; anaśhnataḥ—of one who does not eat; na—not; cha—also; ati—too much; svapna-śhīlasya—of one who sleeps; jāgrataḥ—of one who keeps awake; na eva—not at all; cha—and; arjuna—Arjun
translation_english: There is no possibility of one’s becoming a yogi, O Arjuna, if one eats too much or eats too little, sleeps too much or does not sleep enough.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now warns against extremes. One cannot become a yogi by overeating or by starving, by oversleeping or by refusing rest altogether. This verse is simple, but extremely important. Spiritual life is often distorted by excess, either through indulgence or through harsh self-denial. Krishna rejects both. Yoga requires balance because the body and mind are instruments of practice, and damaged instruments do not serve well.

Overindulgence makes the mind dull, heavy, and dependent. Severe deprivation can make it agitated, weak, and self-absorbed. In both cases, attention becomes trapped by bodily imbalance. The seeker may imagine such extremes are signs of seriousness, but Krishna exposes them as obstacles. Spiritual maturity is not measured by how dramatically one mistreats the body. Discipline must be intelligent, not theatrical.

This verse also reflects a broader Gita principle: the path is neither worldliness nor self-torture. The aim is freedom, and freedom grows best under conditions of proportion. Balance allows energy to be available for concentration, insight, and steadiness. Extremes often feed the ego by making the practitioner feel exceptional, while quietly undermining actual progress.

For a new reader, the lesson is straightforward and liberating. You do not need to choose between comfort-worship and punishing austerity. Krishna recommends regulated living. Eat in a way that supports clarity. Rest in a way that supports wakefulness. The middle way here is not mediocrity. It is disciplined wisdom, shaped by the recognition that body and mind work together in the path of yoga.

original_script:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा।।6.17।।
transliteration:
yuktāhāra-vihārasya yukta-cheṣhṭasya karmasu
yukta-svapnāvabodhasya yogo bhavati duḥkha-hā
word_meanings: yukta—temperate; āhāra—eating; vihārasya—recreation; yukta—temperate; cheṣhṭasya—of activities; karmasu—in performing duties; yukta—temperate; svapna-avabodhasya—sleep and wakefulness; yogaḥ—Yoga; bhavati—becomes; duḥkha-hā—the destroyer of sorrows
translation_english: He who is temperate in his habits of eating, sleeping, working and recreation can mitigate all material pains by practicing the yoga system.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues the theme of moderation by saying that one who is regulated in eating, recreation, work, sleep, and wakefulness can destroy suffering through yoga. This verse broadens the previous teaching. Balance is not only about food and sleep; it extends to the whole rhythm of life. Yoga does not flourish in chaos. The ordinary structure of one’s days matters deeply because repeated habits either support clarity or constantly sabotage it.

Regulation does not mean mechanical rigidity. Krishna is not asking the seeker to become lifeless or obsessive. He is describing harmony. Eating is measured, recreation is wholesome, work is appropriate, and rest is sufficient. Such balance protects the mind from both exhaustion and excess. When life has proportion, inward practice has a better foundation.

The verse is striking in its realism. Krishna does not separate spirituality from daily routine as if higher truth belonged only to special hours of formal practice. The way one lives from morning to night becomes part of yoga.
