This verse also shows that true knowledge of God does not lead to boredom. Finite things tire the soul when consumed repeatedly. The Divine, by contrast, can be heard of again and again because the reality exceeds exhaustion. Repetition becomes deepening, not depletion.

For a new reader, the verse provides a test of spiritual appetite. When the heart begins to awaken, hearing of God no longer feels like obligation alone. It becomes nourishment. Arjuna's unsatisfied listening is not greed for novelty, but love for truth. That disposition prepares him to receive the long cascade of divine glories that follows.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
हन्त ते कथयिष्यामि दिव्या ह्यात्मविभूतयः।
प्राधान्यतः कुरुश्रेष्ठ नास्त्यन्तो विस्तरस्य मे।।10.19।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
hanta te kathayiṣhyāmi divyā hy ātma-vibhūtayaḥ
prādhānyataḥ kuru-śhreṣhṭha nāsty anto vistarasya me
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; hanta—yes; te—unto you; kathayiṣhyāmi—I shall speak; divyāḥ—divine; hi—certainly; ātma-vibhūtayaḥ—personal opulences; prādhānyataḥ—which are principal; kuru-śhreṣhṭha—O best of the Kurus; na asti—there is not; antaḥ—limit; vistarasya—to the extent; me—My
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Yes, I will tell you of My splendorous manifestations, but only of those which are prominent, O best of the Kurus, for My opulence is limitless.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna agrees to speak of his glorious manifestations, but he adds an important qualification: he will mention only the chief ones, because his divine opulence is endless. This verse protects the whole chapter from a misunderstanding. The coming list is not exhaustive. It is selective, illustrative, and pedagogical. Krishna is not cataloging all of himself. He is giving Arjuna enough points of vision to train contemplative awareness.

The endlessness of divine opulence matters spiritually. If God's manifestations are limitless, then no list, doctrine, or image can capture him fully. Every example given in the chapter is therefore a sign, not a prison. It reveals without enclosing.

This also means the chapter is not inviting superstition about isolated sacred objects. Krishna is teaching a pattern of perception. He points to what is most luminous, powerful, beautiful, central, or excellent so that the mind learns to move from manifestation to source.

For a new reader, the verse is a needed interpretive key. The point is not to memorize a closed inventory and stop there. The point is to let the examples educate the imagination. Once the heart understands the principle, the world begins to appear differently. Divine glory can be recognized wherever created excellence silently points beyond itself.

original_script:
अहमात्मा गुडाकेश सर्वभूताशयस्थितः।
अहमादिश्च मध्यं च भूतानामन्त एव च।।10.20।।
transliteration:
aham ātmā guḍākeśha sarva-bhūtāśhaya-sthitaḥ
aham ādiśh cha madhyaṁ cha bhūtānām anta eva cha
word_meanings: aham—I; ātmā—the soul; guḍākeśha—O Arjuna (conqueror of sleep); sarva-bhūta—of all living entities; āśhaya-sthitaḥ—situated within the heart; aham—I; ādiḥ—the origin; cha—also; madhyam—middle; cha—also; bhūtānām—of all living entities; antaḥ—end; eva—certainly; cha—and
translation_english: I am the Supersoul, O Arjuna, seated in the hearts of all living entities. I am the beginning, the middle and the end of all beings.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins the actual list by saying that he is the Self seated in the hearts of all beings and that he is the beginning, middle, and end of all creatures. This is a fitting starting point. Before naming external wonders, Krishna points inward and fundamental. The first place to seek him is not in rare objects alone, but in the very principle of life and consciousness.

To call himself the Self in the hearts of all beings reveals an intimate universality. Krishna is not merely an external ruler above life. He is present at the core of living existence. Then, by saying he is the beginning, middle, and end of beings, he frames the whole arc of creaturely life within himself. Origin, duration, and completion all depend on him.

This verse prevents the vibhutis from becoming a scattered list of marvels. Krishna first establishes the most basic truth: he is already present at the center of every life and every process of becoming. The particular glories that follow will elaborate this, not replace it.

For a new reader, the verse is a powerful anchor. If God is the inner Self and the beginning, middle, and end of beings, then divine remembrance need not wait for extraordinary sights. Krishna is already nearer than our nearest inwardness and broader than the whole span of our existence. The chapter's many examples flow outward from this inward center.

original_script:
आदित्यानामहं विष्णुर्ज्योतिषां रविरंशुमान्।
मरीचिर्मरुतामस्मि नक्षत्राणामहं शशी।।10.21।।
transliteration:
ādityānām ahaṁ viṣhṇur jyotiṣhāṁ ravir aṁśhumān
marīchir marutām asmi nakṣhatrāṇām ahaṁ śhaśhī
word_meanings: ādityānām—of the Adityas; aham—I; viṣhṇuḥ—the Supreme Lord; jyotiṣhām—of all luminaries; raviḥ—the sun; aṁśhu-mān—radiant; marīchiḥ—Marichi; marutām—of the Marutis; asmi—I am; nakṣhatrāṇām—of the stars; aham—I; śhaśhī—the moon
translation_english: Of the Adityas I am Vishnu, of lights I am the radiant sun, of the Maruts I am Marichi, and among the stars I am the moon.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the Adityas he is Vishnu, among lights the radiant sun, among the Maruts he is Marichi, and among the stars he is the moon. The pattern is now clear: Krishna identifies himself with what is most luminous, central, life-giving, or eminent within each class. He is training Arjuna to see the Divine through preeminence.

Light is an especially fitting starting field for this contemplative exercise. The sun governs visibility, growth, and rhythm for life on earth. The moon orders time, tides, and the night sky's gentler illumination. Vishnu among the Adityas represents sustaining divine majesty. Marichi among the storm deities suggests ordered brilliance within dynamic cosmic power.

The point is not that these beings or lights are identical to Krishna in exhaustive fullness. Rather, their excellence becomes a sign. When Arjuna encounters what is radiant, sustaining, or centrally luminous, he is meant to remember the higher source from which that glory flows.

For a new reader, this verse shows how spiritual perception can begin with things as immediate as sunlight and moonlight. Krishna is not absent from the world's beauty and order. He points to the brightest and most governing realities in the visible heavens so that the mind learns to move from splendor to source. The world becomes contemplatively transparent when excellence is read as borrowed glory.

original_script:
वेदानां सामवेदोऽस्मि देवानामस्मि वासवः।
इन्द्रियाणां मनश्चास्मि भूतानामस्मि चेतना।।10.22।।
transliteration:
vedānāṁ sāma-vedo ’smi devānām asmi vāsavaḥ
indriyāṇāṁ manaśh chāsmi bhūtānām asmi chetanā
word_meanings: vedānām—of all the Vedas; sāma-vedaḥ—the Sama Veda; asmi—I am; devānām—of all the demigods; asmi—I am; vāsavaḥ—the heavenly king, Indra; indriyāṇām—of all the senses; manaḥ—the mind; cha—also; asmi—I am; bhūtānām—in all living beings; asmi—I am; chetanā—the living force (consciousness)
translation_english: Of the Vedas I am the Sama Veda; of the demigods I am Indra, the king of heaven; of the senses I am the mind; and in living beings I am the living force [consciousness].
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues: among the Vedas he is the Sama Veda, among the gods he is Indra, among the senses he is the mind, and in living beings he is consciousness itself. Again the choices reveal a principle. Krishna identifies with what is most harmonizing, sovereign, central, and animating within each domain.

The Sama Veda is traditionally associated with chant and musical exaltation. Indra represents rulership and force among the gods. The mind stands as the coordinating faculty among the senses, gathering what the senses bring. Consciousness is even deeper: it is that by which life is actually experienced and known. Without consciousness, the rest of the apparatus is inert.

This progression is spiritually elegant. Krishna moves from sacred sound to divine rule, from bodily coordination to the sheer fact of awareness. He is teaching Arjuna to see not only external majesty but also the hidden center of experience. The most immediate clue to the Divine may be the very consciousness by which all things are known.

For a new reader, this verse is especially powerful because it pulls the contemplative exercise inward. One does not have to look only to cosmic or mythic grandeur. The mind that unifies sensation and the consciousness that makes experience possible are already signs of a deeper source. Krishna is using both the outer hierarchy of the cosmos and the inner hierarchy of human experience to guide remembrance.

original_script:
रुद्राणां शङ्करश्चास्मि वित्तेशो यक्षरक्षसाम्।
वसूनां पावकश्चास्मि मेरुः शिखरिणामहम्।।10.23।।
transliteration:
rudrāṇāṁ śhaṅkaraśh chāsmi vitteśho yakṣha-rakṣhasām
vasūnāṁ pāvakaśh chāsmi meruḥ śhikhariṇām aham
word_meanings: rudrāṇām—of all the Rudras; śhaṅkaraḥ—Lord Shiva; cha—also; asmi—I am; vitta-īśhaḥ—the lord of wealth (Kuvera); yakṣha-rakṣhasām—of the Yakshas and Rakshasas; vasūnām—of the Vasus; pāvakaḥ—fire; cha—also; asmi—I am; meruḥ—Meru; śhikhariṇām—of all mountains; aham—I
translation_english: Of all the Rudras I am Lord Shiva, of the Yakshas and Rakshasas I am the Lord of wealth [Kuvera], of the Vasus I am fire [Agni], and of mountains I am Meru.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the Rudras he is Shiva, among Yakshas and Rakshasas he is the lord of wealth, among the Vasus he is fire, and among mountains he is Meru. These examples move through power, treasure, energy, and stability. Krishna points to what is greatest, most commanding, or most symbolically charged within each category.

Shiva among the Rudras represents fierce divine potency. Kuvera, the lord of wealth, suggests abundance and guardianship of treasure. Fire stands out among the Vasus because of its transformative energy, sacrificial importance, and visible force. Meru, the cosmic mountain, signifies loftiness, centeredness, and enduring height.

The diversity of the images matters. Krishna's glories are not one-note. They appear in austerity, wealth, energy, and immovable grandeur. This keeps Arjuna from narrowing the Divine to a single mood. God can be recognized in what is intense, what is sustaining, what transforms, and what towers above lesser things in majesty.

For a new reader, the verse teaches a practical contemplative rule: when you encounter unusual concentration of power, brilliance, or magnitude, let it remind you of the One from whom all such excellence is derived. Krishna is not reducing himself to fire or mountain. He is using fire and mountain as windows. The heart learns to reverence the highest in created things without stopping there.

original_script:
पुरोधसां च मुख्यं मां विद्धि पार्थ बृहस्पतिम्।
सेनानीनामहं स्कन्दः सरसामस्मि सागरः।।10.24।।
transliteration:
purodhasāṁ cha mukhyaṁ māṁ viddhi pārtha bṛihaspatim
senānīnām ahaṁ skandaḥ sarasām asmi sāgaraḥ
word_meanings: purodhasām—of all priests; cha—also; mukhyam—the chief; mām—Me; viddhi—understand; pārtha—O son of Pritha; bṛihaspatim—Brihaspati; senānīnām—of all commanders; aham—I; skandaḥ—Kartikeya; sarasām—of all reservoirs of water; asmi—I am; sāgaraḥ—the ocean
translation_english: Of priests, O Arjuna, know Me to be the chief, Brihaspati. Of generals I am Kartikeya, and of bodies of water I am the ocean.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among priests he is Brihaspati, among military leaders he is Kartikeya, and among bodies of water he is the ocean. The chosen examples bring together wisdom, command, and vastness. Krishna is not limiting his manifestations to one social function. He appears through the highest forms of counsel, leadership, and natural immensity.

Brihaspati represents priestly intelligence and sacred guidance. Kartikeya embodies martial command and victorious organization. The ocean stands for depth, breadth, and the power to contain innumerable rivers without ceasing to be itself. Together these images move from human order to natural grandeur.

This verse also shows that divine glory is not opposed to structured roles in the world. Sacred instruction, strategic leadership, and the majesty of creation can all become sites of remembrance when seen rightly. Krishna is present not only in private inwardness but in the highest expression of public and cosmic order.

For a new reader, the ocean may be the easiest doorway in this verse. Its vastness naturally humbles the ego. Krishna invites Arjuna to carry that same sense of reverence into domains such as wisdom and leadership. The highest instances of intelligence, command, and magnitude are not ends in themselves. They are reflections of a greater sovereignty and depth grounded in God.

original_script:
महर्षीणां भृगुरहं गिरामस्म्येकमक्षरम्।
यज्ञानां जपयज्ञोऽस्मि स्थावराणां हिमालयः।।10.25।।
transliteration:
maharṣhīṇāṁ bhṛigur ahaṁ girām asmy ekam akṣharam
yajñānāṁ japa-yajño ’smi sthāvarāṇāṁ himālayaḥ
word_meanings: mahā-ṛiṣhīṇām—among the great sages; bhṛiguḥ—Bhrigu; aham—I; girām—of vibrations; asmi—I am; ekam akṣharam—the syllable Om; yajñānām—of sacrifices; japa-yajñaḥ—chanting; asmi—I am; sthāvarāṇām—of immovable things; himālayaḥ—the Himalayan mountains
translation_english: Of the great sages I am Bhrigu; of vibrations I am the transcendental Om. Of sacrifices I am the chanting of the holy names [japa], and of immovable things I am the Himalayas.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among great sages he is Bhrigu, among uttered sounds he is Om, among sacrifices he is the sacrifice of repetition, and among immovable things he is the Himalayas. The examples combine spiritual discernment, sacred sound, inward worship, and majestic stillness. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to perceive holiness not only in visible splendor but also in contemplative depth.

Om represents primordial sacred vibration, the concentrated sign of transcendence. Japa, the repeated sacred utterance, stands out among sacrifices because it turns ritual inward and continual. The Himalayas signify purity, elevation, and unshakable grandeur. Bhrigu represents sage-like insight and spiritual authority.

The movement of the verse is beautiful. It travels from the sage, to the sacred syllable, to the sacrifice of repeated remembrance, to the great mountains. The thread running through them is concentration. Each image points to what gathers, elevates, and steadies the mind toward the highest.

For a new reader, this verse suggests that some of the clearest signs of the Divine are not noisy or dramatic. A sacred sound, a repeated prayer, a mountain's stillness, the authority of deep wisdom - these too are vibhutis. Krishna is enlarging the field of remembrance so that both silence and majesty, both repetition and height, become reminders of him.

original_script:
अश्वत्थः सर्ववृक्षाणां देवर्षीणां च नारदः।
गन्धर्वाणां चित्ररथः सिद्धानां कपिलो मुनिः।।10.26।।
transliteration:
aśhvatthaḥ sarva-vṛikṣhāṇāṁ devarṣhīṇāṁ cha nāradaḥ
gandharvāṇāṁ chitrarathaḥ siddhānāṁ kapilo muniḥ
word_meanings: aśhvatthaḥ—the banyan tree; sarva-vṛikṣhāṇām—of all trees; deva-ṛiṣhīṇām—of all the sages among the demigods; cha—and; nāradaḥ—Narada; gandharvāṇām—of the citizens of the Gandharva planet; chitrarathaḥ—Chitraratha; siddhānām—of all those who are perfected; kapilaḥ muniḥ—Kapila Muni
translation_english: Of all trees I am the banyan tree, and of the sages among the demigods I am Narada. Of the Gandharvas I am Chitraratha, and among perfected beings I am the sage Kapila.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among trees he is the banyan, among divine sages he is Narada, among Gandharvas he is Chitraratha, and among perfected beings he is Kapila. The verse brings together rooted longevity, sacred communication, beauty, and realized wisdom. Each chosen figure embodies a kind of excellence that can redirect the mind toward its source.

The banyan tree is expansive, sheltering, and enduring. Narada represents inspired devotion and the transmission of divine knowledge through song and speech. Chitraratha evokes beauty, refinement, and celestial artistry. Kapila represents penetrating insight and perfected understanding. Krishna is showing that the Divine can be remembered through life, music, beauty, and wisdom all at once.

This range matters because it prevents a narrow spiritual imagination. God's glories are not confined to austere or purely abstract symbols. They also appear in beauty, communication, artistic splendor, and the generative life of nature. The world of form becomes less spiritually flat.

For a new reader, this verse is a reminder that what is most enduring, most beautiful, and most illuminating in the world need not be spiritually distracting. It can become spiritually instructive. Krishna is teaching Arjuna how to let excellence in nature, devotion, art, and philosophy become a bridge rather than a barrier to remembrance.

original_script:
उच्चैःश्रवसमश्वानां विद्धि माममृतोद्भवम्।
ऐरावतं गजेन्द्राणां नराणां च नराधिपम्।।10.27।।
transliteration:
uchchaiḥśhravasam aśhvānāṁ viddhi mām amṛitodbhavam
airāvataṁ gajendrāṇāṁ narāṇāṁ cha narādhipam
word_meanings: uchchaiḥśhravasam—Uchchaihshrava; aśhvānām—among horses; viddhi—know; mām—Me; amṛita-udbhavam—produced from the churning of the ocean; airāvatam—Airavata; gaja-indrāṇām—of lordly elephants; narāṇām—among human beings; cha—and; nara-adhipam—the king
translation_english: Of horses know Me to be Uchchaihshrava, produced during the churning of the ocean for nectar. Of lordly elephants I am Airavata, and among men I am the monarch.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among horses he is Uchchaihshravas, among elephants Airavata, and among human beings the king. The pattern here emphasizes nobility, royal stature, and commanding excellence. Krishna selects creatures and roles that traditionally signify majesty, dignity, and visible superiority.

Uchchaihshravas and Airavata both carry mythic associations of extraordinary origin and splendor. The king among men represents governing authority at its highest legitimate form. Krishna is not endorsing every historical ruler. He is identifying with the principle of eminent order, leadership, and nobility when it appears in its fullest representative form.

This verse is helpful because it shows that preeminence itself can be contemplated theologically. When greatness is encountered, the right response is not idolatry of the creature nor resentful dismissal of greatness. It is recognition that whatever genuinely stands out in majesty does so as a fragmentary reflection of divine grandeur.

For a new reader, the verse invites a mature relation to excellence. Human beings often either worship superiority blindly or attack it from envy. Krishna offers another path: let true nobility turn your thought toward God. The highest rank or splendor in creation is still derivative. Its value is greatest when it becomes a sign rather than a rival to the Lord.

original_script:
आयुधानामहं वज्रं धेनूनामस्मि कामधुक्।
प्रजनश्चास्मि कन्दर्पः सर्पाणामस्मि वासुकिः।।10.28।।
transliteration:
āyudhānām ahaṁ vajraṁ dhenūnām asmi kāmadhuk
prajanaśh chāsmi kandarpaḥ sarpāṇām asmi vāsukiḥ
word_meanings: āyudhānām—of all weapons; aham—I; vajram—the thunderbolt; dhenūnām—of cows; asmi—I am; kāma-dhuk—the surabhi cow; prajanaḥ—the cause for begetting children; cha—and; asmi—I am; kandarpaḥ—Cupid; sarpāṇām—of serpents; asmi—I am; vāsukiḥ—Vasuki
translation_english: Of weapons I am the thunderbolt; among cows I am the surabhi. Of causes for procreation I am Kandarpa, the god of love, and of serpents I am Vasuki.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among weapons he is the thunderbolt, among cows the wish-fulfilling Surabhi, among causes of procreation he is Kandarpa, and among serpents he is Vasuki. These examples span force, nourishment, generative attraction, and latent power. Krishna is showing that divine excellence can be recognized in what strikes, sustains, creates, and coils with concentrated potency.

The thunderbolt represents irresistible force. Surabhi represents abundance and beneficent provision. Kandarpa, understood here as love or desire aligned with generative purpose, points to the creative impulse by which life continues. Vasuki represents serpent power in a mythic and concentrated form. Together they show that divine glory is not limited to calm gentleness alone.

This verse also requires discernment. Krishna is not identifying himself with distorted violence or uncontrolled lust. He selects what is archetypally powerful in a rightly understood sense. The vibhutis reveal excellences, not corruptions.

For a new reader, the verse broadens contemplative perception into areas often either feared or trivialized. Force, fertility, provision, and hidden power all belong to life's structure. Krishna teaches Arjuna to see their highest form as dependent on the Divine. The result is not confusion of God with raw impulse, but recognition that even intense forms of created power point beyond themselves to the Lord.

original_script:
अनन्तश्चास्मि नागानां वरुणो यादसामहम्।
पितॄणामर्यमा चास्मि यमः संयमतामहम्।।10.29।।
transliteration:
anantaśh chāsmi nāgānāṁ varuṇo yādasām aham
pitṝīṇām aryamā chāsmi yamaḥ sanyamatām aham
word_meanings: anantaḥ—Ananta; cha—also; asmi—I am; nāgānām—of the many-hooded serpents; varuṇaḥ—the demigod controlling the water; yādasām—of all aquatics; aham—I; pitṝīṇām—of the ancestors; aryamā—Aryama; cha—also; asmi—I am; yamaḥ—the controller of death; sanyamatām—of all regulators; aham—I
translation_english: Of the many-hooded Nagas I am Ananta, and among the aquatics I am the demigod Varuna. Of departed ancestors I am Aryama, and among the dispensers of law I am Yama, the lord of death.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the many-hooded nagas he is Ananta, among aquatics he is Varuna, among ancestors Aryama, and among dispensers of law he is Yama. The verse moves through depth, sovereignty, ancestry, and justice. Krishna's manifestations are not only spectacular; they also touch the hidden structures of continuity, order, and moral consequence.

Ananta represents endless support and cosmic depth. Varuna evokes dominion over the waters and the mysterious order associated with them. Aryama points to ancestral dignity and continuity across generations. Yama represents judgment, law, and the unavoidable seriousness of death and moral order. These are realities before which human beings instinctively become sober.

This verse is important because it keeps the chapter from becoming merely celebratory. Divine glory also appears in what establishes seriousness, accountability, and depth. God is reflected not only in beauty and brilliance but in the structures that remind creatures they are not sovereign.

For a new reader, the verse can deepen reverence. The world contains mysteries of inheritance, law, mortality, and unfathomable depth that resist trivial treatment. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to see even these sobering realities as bearing witness to him. The sacred is present not only in what pleases us, but also in what humbles and judges us.

original_script:
प्रह्लादश्चास्मि दैत्यानां कालः कलयतामहम्।
मृगाणां च मृगेन्द्रोऽहं वैनतेयश्च पक्षिणाम्।।10.30।।
transliteration:
prahlādaśh chāsmi daityānāṁ kālaḥ kalayatām aham
mṛigāṇāṁ cha mṛigendro ’haṁ vainateyaśh cha pakṣhiṇām
word_meanings: prahlādaḥ—Prahlada; cha—also; asmi—I am; daityānām—of the demons; kālaḥ—time; kalayatām—of subduers; aham—I; mṛigāṇām—of animals; cha—and; mṛiga-indraḥ—the lion; aham—I; vainateyaḥ—Garuda; cha—also; pakṣhiṇām—of birds
translation_english: Among the Daitya demons I am the devoted Prahlada, among subduers I am time, among beasts I am the lion, and among birds I am Garuda.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the Daityas he is Prahlada, among subduers he is time, among beasts the lion, and among birds Garuda. This is a vivid verse because it moves from devotion in hostile surroundings to unstoppable power, regal animal strength, and majestic flight. Krishna is revealing his glory in courage, inevitability, kingship, and transcendence.

Prahlada is especially striking. Among beings born in a demonic lineage, Krishna identifies with the devotee. This shows that divine excellence can appear even where one would not expect it socially or genealogically. Time among subduers is equally searching: nothing overcomes creatures more universally than time. The lion among beasts and Garuda among birds signify unsurpassed natural majesty.

The combination is spiritually rich. Krishna is present in the saint amid corruption, in the force no creature can finally resist, and in the nobility that rules its realm by sheer excellence. The verse therefore joins devotion, inevitability, and majesty.

For a new reader, this verse shows that divine signs are found in both moral and natural orders. Prahlada teaches steadfast devotion where it seems least likely. Time humbles every ego. The lion and Garuda display forms of magnificence that instinctively command awe. Krishna wants Arjuna to see all of these as reminders that the Lord's greatness exceeds easy categories and appears across the full drama of existence.

original_script:
पवनः पवतामस्मि रामः शस्त्रभृतामहम्।
झषाणां मकरश्चास्मि स्रोतसामस्मि जाह्नवी।।10.31।।
transliteration:
pavanaḥ pavatām asmi rāmaḥ śhastra-bhṛitām aham
jhaṣhāṇāṁ makaraśh chāsmi srotasām asmi jāhnavī
word_meanings: pavanaḥ—the wind; pavatām—of all that purifies; asmi—I am; rāmaḥ—Rama; śhastra-bhṛitām—of the wielders of weapons; aham—I; jhaṣhāṇām—of all fishes; makaraḥ—the shark; cha—also; asmi—I am; srotasām—of flowing rivers; asmi—I am; jāhnavī—the River Ganges
translation_english: Of purifiers I am the wind, of the wielders of weapons I am Rama, of fishes I am the shark, and of flowing rivers I am the Ganges.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among purifiers he is the wind, among wielders of weapons he is Rama, among fishes he is the shark, and among flowing rivers he is the Ganges. The verse moves through purification, heroic righteousness, commanding natural power, and sacred flow. Krishna continues to identify himself with what is most eminent, cleansing, or sovereign within each field.

Wind purifies by movement and clearing. Rama represents ideal kingly strength joined with dharma. The shark among aquatic creatures signifies dominance and fearsome distinction. The Ganges stands for sanctity, flow, and life-giving sacred presence. Krishna is therefore using elements of nature, epic memory, and symbolic geography to teach remembrance.

These examples again show that divine glory appears through different modes: cleansing power, righteous force, natural supremacy, and holy life-giving current. The world is not spiritually mute. Its most striking forms can become reminders when read rightly.

For a new reader, the mention of the Ganges is especially helpful. A river can become sacred not only because of religious tradition, but because it symbolizes origin, movement, nourishment, and purification. Krishna invites Arjuna to see all such eminent realities - whether heroic, natural, or sacred - as reflections of a greater source. Remembrance grows when excellence becomes transparent to God.

original_script:
सर्गाणामादिरन्तश्च मध्यं चैवाहमर्जुन।
अध्यात्मविद्या विद्यानां वादः प्रवदतामहम्।।10.32।।
transliteration:
sargāṇām ādir antaśh cha madhyaṁ chaivāham arjuna
adhyātma-vidyā vidyānāṁ vādaḥ pravadatām aham
word_meanings: sargāṇām—of all creations; ādiḥ—the beginning; antaḥ—end; cha—and; madhyam—middle; cha—also; eva—certainly; aham—I am; arjuna—O Arjuna; adhyātma-vidyā—spiritual knowledge; vidyānām—of all education; vādaḥ—the natural conclusion; pravadatām—of arguments; aham—I am
translation_english: Of all creations I am the beginning and the end and also the middle, O Arjuna. Of all sciences I am the spiritual science of the self, and among logicians I am the conclusive truth.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among creations he is the beginning, the end, and the middle; among sciences he is the science of the Self; and among logicians he is the decisive truth. The verse gathers temporal totality, inward wisdom, and intellectual clarity. Krishna is not only found in powerful objects. He is also present in the most fundamental structures of order and understanding.

To be the beginning, middle, and end of creation means that the whole arc of becoming is contained within him. The science of the Self is singled out because among disciplines it addresses the deepest question: who or what the person truly is. Among arguments and reasoning, Krishna identifies with the conclusion that settles truth rather than endless verbal combat.

This verse is important because it prevents the vibhutis from remaining only outward spectacle. The highest divine manifestation among sciences is not technical skill or dominance over the external world, but knowledge of the Self. The highest manifestation among debates is not cleverness, but truth that resolves and illumines.

For a new reader, the verse helps clarify intellectual priorities. Not all knowledge carries the same weight. The most useful science is the one that reveals the self, and the most valuable reasoning is reasoning that arrives at truth rather than feeding pride. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to look for the Divine not only in greatness of power, but in depth of insight and finality of wisdom.

original_script:
अक्षराणामकारोऽस्मि द्वन्द्वः सामासिकस्य च।
अहमेवाक्षयः कालो धाताहं विश्वतोमुखः।।10.33।।
transliteration:
akṣharāṇām akāro ’smi dvandvaḥ sāmāsikasya cha
aham evākṣhayaḥ kālo dhātāhaṁ viśhvato-mukhaḥ
word_meanings: akṣharāṇām—of letters; akāraḥ—the first letter; asmi—I am; dvandvaḥ—the dual; sāmāsikasya—of compounds; cha—and; aham—I am; eva—certainly; akṣhayaḥ—inexhaustible; kālaḥ—time; dhātā—the creator; aham—I am; viśhvataḥ-mukhaḥ—Brahma
translation_english: Of letters I am the letter A, and among compound words I am the dual compound. I am also inexhaustible time, and of creators I am Brahma.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among letters he is the letter A, among compound words the dual compound, and that he is inexhaustible time and the all-faced creator. The examples may seem technical at first, but they reveal Krishna's presence in the elementary structures through which language, thought, and temporal process unfold.

The letter A stands at the beginning of sound and speech, suggesting origin and foundational simplicity. The dual compound represents a grammatical form that holds plurality in relation. Inexhaustible time points to the ceaseless, devouring, and measuring flow within which created life moves. The creator with faces on every side suggests all-encompassing generative intelligence.

This verse is subtle because it shows that God's glories are found not only in grand images like mountains and suns, but in the hidden architecture of speech, time, and conceptual relation. What feels abstract is not spiritually empty. The most basic conditions of expression and duration also bear witness to the Divine.

For a new reader, the verse offers a contemplative challenge: learn to see God not only in what overwhelms the senses, but in what silently structures experience. The alphabet, the relations of language, the movement of time, and the all-reaching power of creation are easy to take for granted. Krishna turns them into reminders. The Divine is present even in the foundations that make thinking and speaking possible.

original_script:
मृत्युः सर्वहरश्चाहमुद्भवश्च भविष्यताम्।
कीर्तिः श्रीर्वाक्च नारीणां स्मृतिर्मेधा धृतिः क्षमा।।10.34।।
transliteration:
mṛityuḥ sarva-haraśh chāham udbhavaśh cha bhaviṣhyatām
kīrtiḥ śhrīr vāk cha nārīṇāṁ smṛitir medhā dhṛitiḥ kṣhamā
word_meanings: mṛityuḥ—death; sarva-haraḥ—all-devouring; cha—and; aham—I am; udbhavaḥ—generating principle; cha—also; bhaviṣhyatām—of future manifestations; kīrtiḥ—fame; śhrīḥ—opulence or beauty; vāk—fine speech; cha—and; nārīṇām—of women; smṛitiḥ—memory; medhā—intelligence; dhṛitiḥ—firmness; kṣhamā—patience
translation_english: I am all-devouring death, and I am the generating principle of all that is yet to be. Among women I am fame, fortune, fine speech, memory, intelligence, steadfastness and patience.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna declares that he is all-devouring death and also the generating principle of all that is yet to be. Then he names among feminine excellences fame, fortune, speech, memory, intelligence, steadfastness, and patience. The verse joins endings and beginnings, dissolution and generative grace. Divine glory appears not only in power that ends life, but also in the qualities that sustain culture, relation, and moral endurance.

Death here is not sentimentalized. Krishna claims the force before which all embodied beings finally bow. Yet he also claims the principle of future arising. This guards against a one-sided spirituality. The Divine is Lord over cessation and emergence alike. Nothing begins or ends outside him.

The feminine excellences named here are not trivial ornaments. Speech, memory, intelligence, steadfastness, patience, and renown are civilizational and moral powers. They sustain transmission, dignity, endurance, and wise action. Krishna includes them to show that subtle excellences are as revelatory as dramatic force.

For a new reader, the verse invites a deeper reverence for both mortality and virtue. Death sobers the ego, while patience, memory, intelligence, and faithful speech sustain human life meaningfully in the face of that sobriety. Krishna is present in both the limit that humbles us and the excellences that let us live nobly within that limit. Divine glory is therefore not narrow. It spans the whole drama of arising, enduring, and passing.

original_script:
बृहत्साम तथा साम्नां गायत्री छन्दसामहम्।
मासानां मार्गशीर्षोऽहमृतूनां कुसुमाकरः।।10.35।।
transliteration:
bṛihat-sāma tathā sāmnāṁ gāyatrī chhandasām aham
māsānāṁ mārga-śhīrṣho ’ham ṛitūnāṁ kusumākaraḥ
word_meanings: bṛihat-sāma—the Brihat-sama; tathā—also; sāmnām—of the Sama Veda songs; gāyatrī—the Gayatri hymns; chhandasām—of all poetry; aham—I am; māsānām—of months; mārga-śhīrṣhaḥ—the month of November-December; aham—I am; ṛitūnām—of all seasons; kusuma-ākaraḥ—spring
translation_english: Of the hymns in the Sama Veda I am the Brihat-sama, and of poetry I am the Gayatri. Of months I am Margashirsha [November-December], and of seasons I am flower-bearing spring.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the hymns of the Sama Veda he is the Brihat-sama, among poetic meters the Gayatri, among months Margashirsha, and among seasons the flower-bearing spring. Here the divine glories appear through beauty, rhythm, auspiciousness, and renewal. Krishna points to what is especially harmonious, fertile, and delight-giving within the order of time and sacred expression.

The Brihat-sama and Gayatri evoke musical and metrical excellence in sacred sound. Margashirsha traditionally carries a sense of richness and auspicious timing. Spring represents blossoming, abundance, and the renewal of life after barrenness. The thread connecting these examples is fullness in form and joyful emergence.

This verse reminds the reader that divine manifestation is not confined to austerity or force. It also shines in beauty rightly ordered, in seasons of flourishing, and in the cadence of sacred language. The world becomes contemplatively richer when one learns to see beauty as revelatory rather than merely consumable.

For a new reader, the verse can reshape the experience of delight. Beauty, rhythm, and seasonal renewal are not spiritually irrelevant pleasures. At their highest, they can become reminders of the One from whom all harmonious fullness comes. Krishna is teaching Arjuna to let excellence in art, time, and natural blossoming turn the mind upward in gratitude and remembrance.

original_script:
द्यूतं छलयतामस्मि तेजस्तेजस्विनामहम्।
जयोऽस्मि व्यवसायोऽस्मि सत्त्वं सत्त्ववतामहम्।।10.36।।
transliteration:
dyūtaṁ chhalayatām asmi tejas tejasvinām aham
jayo ’smi vyavasāyo ’smi sattvaṁ sattvavatām aham
word_meanings: dyūtam—gambling; chhalayatām—of all cheats; asmi—I am; tejaḥ—the splendor; tejasvinām—of everything splendid; aham—I am; jayaḥ—victory; asmi—I am; vyavasāyaḥ—enterprise or effort; asmi—I am; sattvam—the strength; sattva-vatām—of the strong; aham—I am
translation_english: I am also the gambling of cheats, and of the splendid I am the splendor. I am victory, I am adventure, and I am the strength of the strong.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among deceivers he is gambling, among the splendid he is splendor, among the victorious he is victory, among the adventurous he is resolve, and among the strong he is strength. This verse can unsettle readers, especially the mention of gambling. But the principle remains the same: Krishna points to the most intense, effective, or striking force within a category, not to moral endorsement of every use of that force.

Gambling among cheats signifies sheer power of fascination, strategy, and unsettling unpredictability. Splendor among the splendid, victory among victors, resolve among the resolute, and strength among the strong all point to concentrated excellence. Krishna is identifying the potency that makes these realities effective, radiant, or compelling.

This verse therefore requires discernment. Divine manifestation in a category does not mean all expressions of that category are righteous. Strength can be corrupted, victory abused, and cunning turned toward destruction. Krishna is claiming the raw excellence or operative force, not blessing distortion.

For a new reader, the verse is a warning against simplistic spirituality. God is not revealed only through what feels gentle or obviously pious. Even formidable powers in morally dangerous fields bear witness, in their highest energetic essence, to a source beyond themselves. The right response is not to sanctify corruption, but to recognize that every real potency is borrowed and therefore accountable to the Lord.

original_script:
वृष्णीनां वासुदेवोऽस्मि पाण्डवानां धनञ्जयः।
मुनीनामप्यहं व्यासः कवीनामुशना कविः।।10.37।।
transliteration:
vṛiṣhṇīnāṁ vāsudevo ’smi pāṇḍavānāṁ dhanañjayaḥ
munīnām apy ahaṁ vyāsaḥ kavīnām uśhanā kaviḥ
word_meanings: vṛiṣhṇīnām—of the descendants of Vrishni; vāsudevaḥ—Krishna in Dwarka; asmi—I am; pāṇḍavānām—of the Pandavas; dhanañjayaḥ—Arjuna; munīnām—of the sages; api—also; aham—I am; vyāsaḥ—Vyasa, the compiler of all Vedic literature; kavīnām—of all great thinkers; uśhanā—Ushana; kaviḥ—the thinker
translation_english: Of the descendants of Vrishni I am Vasudeva, and of the Pandavas I am Arjuna. Of the sages I am Vyasa, and among great thinkers I am Ushana.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among the descendants of Vrishni he is Vasudeva, among the Pandavas he is Arjuna, among sages he is Vyasa, and among thinkers he is Ushana. The verse now becomes wonderfully personal. Krishna includes Arjuna himself as one of the manifestations of excellence, which gives the chapter an intimate turn. The hearer is not outside the field of divine reflection.

Vasudeva represents Krishna's own royal and divine standing in the Yadava line. Vyasa evokes wisdom, authorship, and spiritual authority. Ushana represents penetrating counsel and insight. Arjuna among the Pandavas signifies a distinguished human form of devotion, skill, and noble responsiveness. Krishna is showing that even the person receiving the teaching can be understood as bearing a reflection of divine glory.

This is not flattery. It is vocation clarified. Arjuna's excellence is real, but it is derivative. To see himself as a vibhuti is not to become proud. It is to understand his gifts as belonging within a larger divine order.

For a new reader, the verse can be deeply encouraging. The point of Chapter 10 is not only to find God in remote cosmic symbols. It is also to see that human excellence, when genuine, is borrowed light. Your gifts are not grounds for egoic ownership. They are invitations to gratitude, service, and remembrance. Krishna even lets Arjuna hear his own life reflected back as a sign.

original_script:
दण्डो दमयतामस्मि नीतिरस्मि जिगीषताम्।
मौनं चैवास्मि गुह्यानां ज्ञानं ज्ञानवतामहम्।।10.38।।
transliteration:
daṇḍo damayatām asmi nītir asmi jigīṣhatām
maunaṁ chaivāsmi guhyānāṁ jñānaṁ jñānavatām aham
word_meanings: daṇḍaḥ—punishment; damayatām—of all means of suppression; asmi—I am; nītiḥ—morality; asmi—I am; jigīṣhatām—of those who seek victory; maunam—silence; cha—and; eva—also; asmi—I am; guhyānām—of secrets; jñānam—knowledge; jñāna-vatām—of the wise; aham—I am
translation_english: Among all means of suppressing lawlessness I am punishment, and of those who seek victory I am morality. Of secret things I am silence, and of the wise I am the wisdom.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that among forces of restraint he is punishment, among seekers of victory he is morality or wise policy, among secrets he is silence, and among the wise he is wisdom. This verse draws attention to order, restraint, and inward depth. Divine glory is found not only in what shines outwardly, but also in what governs, limits, and brings things to rightful form.

Punishment here represents the principle by which lawlessness is checked. Morality or policy among those seeking victory means that true success is not mere conquest, but victory guided by right order. Silence among secrets suggests that what is deepest is not always disclosed through more speech. Wisdom among the wise identifies the living center of discernment itself.

The verse is especially rich because it honors hidden power. Silence can preserve what talk trivializes. Wisdom can guide where force alone cannot. Restraint can protect what indulgence destroys. Krishna is training Arjuna to see divine manifestation in moral and inward forms of strength.

For a new reader, this verse is a corrective to a culture that often prizes display over depth. The Divine is revealed not only in spectacle, but in the power that restrains evil, the integrity that guides action, the silence that keeps truth from being cheapened, and the wisdom that sees rightly. Chapter 10 is teaching reverence for both visible greatness and invisible mastery.

original_script:
यच्चापि सर्वभूतानां बीजं तदहमर्जुन।
न तदस्ति विना यत्स्यान्मया भूतं चराचरम्।।10.39।।
transliteration:
yach chāpi sarva-bhūtānāṁ bījaṁ tad aham arjuna
na tad asti vinā yat syān mayā bhūtaṁ charācharam
word_meanings: yat—whatever; cha—also; api—may be; sarva-bhūtānām—of all creations; bījam—seed; tat—that; aham—I am; arjuna—O Arjuna; na—not; tat—that; asti—there is; vinā—without; yat—which; syāt—exists; mayā—Me; bhūtam—created being; chara-acharam—moving and nonmoving
translation_english: Furthermore, O Arjuna, I am the generating seed of all existences. There is no being—moving or nonmoving—that can exist without Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that he is the generating seed of all existences and that nothing moving or unmoving can exist without him. This verse gathers the chapter's many examples into a single universal claim. All the particular glories listed so far are expressions of one deeper truth: everything that exists depends on Krishna at its root.

Seed signifies origin, hidden potential, and formative principle. To call Krishna the seed of all beings is to say that no creature, force, object, or order stands by itself. Movement and stillness alike rest on a prior dependence. The entire field of existence is derivative.

This verse is important because it prevents the vibhutis from being misread as disconnected sacred islands. Krishna is not saying he is present only in the greatest examples while absent from the rest. Rather, the greatest examples are pedagogical highlights within a universe that as a whole depends on him.

For a new reader, this verse provides a simple and comprehensive takeaway. Whenever anything exists at all, it does so through a reality it did not create. Krishna is that originating and sustaining principle. The soul's task is to learn to move from wonder at particular excellences to awareness of universal dependence. The seed is one, though the forms are many.

original_script:
नान्तोऽस्ति मम दिव्यानां विभूतीनां परन्तप।
एष तूद्देशतः प्रोक्तो विभूतेर्विस्तरो मया।।10.40।।
transliteration:
nānto ’sti mama divyānāṁ vibhūtīnāṁ parantapa
eṣha tūddeśhataḥ prokto vibhūter vistaro mayā
word_meanings: na—not; antaḥ—limit; asti—there is; mama—My; divyānām—of divine; vibhūtīnām—opulences; parantapa—O conqueror of enemies; eṣhaḥ—this; tu—but; uddeśhataḥ—briefly; proktaḥ—spoken; vibhūteḥ—of opulences; vistaraḥ—the extent; mayā—by Me
translation_english: O mighty conqueror of enemies, there is no end to My divine manifestations. What I have spoken to you is but a mere brief indication of My infinite opulences.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now says plainly that there is no end to his divine manifestations and that what he has spoken is only a brief indication. This verse closes the catalog by reopening infinity. No matter how long the list becomes, God is not exhausted by it. The chapter therefore ends the enumeration by preserving transcendence once again.

This matters spiritually because human beings quickly turn examples into limits. Krishna refuses that. The vibhutis are sparks, not totality. They educate perception without pretending to contain the whole. Divine glory remains inexhaustible.

The verse also reinforces the pedagogical nature of the chapter. Krishna has not been offering a complete map of all sacred symbols. He has been giving Arjuna a sufficient pattern. Once the principle is learned, the mind can continue recognizing borrowed glory beyond the list.

For a new reader, the verse is freeing. One need not anxiously memorize every example as if forgetting a name meant losing the teaching. The point is to understand how the examples function. Krishna's greatness exceeds the list, and the list exists to awaken vision. Once that is grasped, the whole world becomes a possible field of remembrance.

original_script:
यद्यद्विभूतिमत्सत्त्वं श्रीमदूर्जितमेव वा।
तत्तदेवावगच्छ त्वं मम तेजोंऽशसम्भवम्।।10.41।।
transliteration:
yad yad vibhūtimat sattvaṁ śhrīmad ūrjitam eva vā
tat tad evāvagachchha tvaṁ mama tejo ’nśha-sambhavam
word_meanings: yat yat—whatever; vibhūti-mat—opulent; sattvam—being; śhrī-mat—splendid; ūrjitam—powerful; eva—certainly; vā—or; tat tat—all that; eva—certainly; avagachchha—know; tvam—you; mama—My; tejaḥ—effulgence; anśha—fragment; sambhavam—born of
translation_english: Understand that every opulent, splendid, or powerful being originates from but a fragment of My effulgence.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna gives the principle in its most concise form: whatever is glorious, beautiful, or powerful should be understood as arising from a fragment of his splendor. This is one of the chapter's most practical summary verses. It teaches a way of reading the world. Whenever something genuinely excels, instead of stopping at admiration or envy, the soul can trace that excellence back toward God.

The word fragment is crucial. Created greatness is real, but it is not self-sufficient and not total. No creaturely excellence, however dazzling, equals the fullness of the Lord. It is a ray, not the sun.

This verse also purifies the experience of admiration. Human beings are often trapped between worshiping worldly greatness and resenting it. Krishna offers a third response: recognize greatness as borrowed. That allows reverence without idolatry and appreciation without envy.

For a new reader, this verse can transform ordinary perception. Beauty, genius, courage, strength, brilliance, and moral grandeur need not distract the mind from God. They can become occasions of remembrance. The right question becomes: what does this excellence reveal about the source from which it shines? Chapter 10 reaches its contemplative climax here.

original_script:
अथवा बहुनैतेन किं ज्ञातेन तवार्जुन।
विष्टभ्याहमिदं कृत्स्नमेकांशेन स्थितो जगत्।।10.42।।
transliteration:
athavā bahunaitena kiṁ jñātena tavārjuna
viṣhṭabhyāham idaṁ kṛitsnam ekānśhena sthito jagat
word_meanings: athavā—or; bahunā—many; etena—by this; kim—what; jñātena—knowing; tava—your; arjuna—O Arjuna; viṣhṭabhya—pervading; aham—I; idam—this; kṛitsnam—entire; eka—by one; anśhena—part; sthitaḥ—am situated; jagat—universe
translation_english: But what need is there, Arjuna, for all this detailed knowledge? With a single fragment of Myself I pervade and support this entire universe.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by asking what need there is for further detail, since with only a single fragment of himself he pervades and sustains the entire universe. This ending is breathtaking in its compression. After many verses of examples, Krishna suddenly relativizes them all. The whole cosmos, with all its splendor and complexity, is upheld by only a tiny expression of his being.

The purpose is not to diminish creation into worthlessness, but to magnify the Lord beyond all created scale. If the universe itself rests on a fragment, then God's fullness exceeds even the totality of all worlds beyond measure. Arjuna is meant to be left in awe.

This verse also prepares for the next chapter's vision of the universal form. The contemplative list of glories has stretched Arjuna's imagination. Now Krishna points beyond even that stretched imagination. The vibhutis were stepping stones. The sustaining reality behind them is immeasurably greater.

For a new reader, this is a fitting conclusion to Chapter 10. Learn to see God through the greatest things. Then do not stop there. Even the whole universe is not an adequate measure of the Divine. Krishna teaches remembrance through fragments so that the soul may be led into wonder before the One whom no fragment can contain. That wonder is the fruit of vibhuti-yoga.

original_script: विभूतियोगः
transliteration: Vibhūti Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of Divine Glories
deep_dive_text: In Chapter 10, Krishna changes the way Arjuna sees the world. Instead of seeing a collection of random objects, Arjuna learns to see the universe as an exhibition of Divine opulence. Krishna reveals that He is the seed of all existence, the intelligence of the wise, and the strength of the strong. He concludes by stating that all beautiful and glorious things spring from just a single spark of His infinite splendor, shifting Arjuna from doubt into profound devotion.

original_script: अथ एकादशोऽध्यायः - विश्वरूपदर्शनयोगः
transliteration: Viśhvarūpa Darśhana Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Viśhvarūpa—Universal Form; Darśhana—Vision/Revelation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form
deep_dive_text: Chapter 11 brings Arjuna to a direct vision of Krishna's universal form. In this revelation, he beholds the vastness of the cosmos, the movement of time, and the inevitability of birth and destruction within the Divine order. The vision deepens Arjuna's understanding of Krishna's supreme nature and transforms his perspective.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
मदनुग्रहाय परमं गुह्यमध्यात्मसंज्ञितम्।
यत्त्वयोक्तं वचस्तेन मोहोऽयं विगतो मम।।11.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
mad-anugrahāya paramaṁ guhyam adhyātma-sañjñitam
yat tvayoktaṁ vachas tena moho ’yaṁ vigato mama
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; mat-anugrahāya—out of compassion for me; paramam—supreme; guhyam—secret; adhyātma—spiritual; sañjñitam—subject matter; yat—which; tvayā—by You; uktam—spoken; vachaḥ—words; tena—by that; mohaḥ—illusion; ayam—this; vigataḥ—is dispelled; mama—my
translation_english: Arjuna said: By my hearing the profoundly secret spiritual subjects You have mercifully explained to me, my illusion has now been dispelled.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna opens the chapter by saying that the confusion clouding his judgment has been dispelled by Krishna's compassionate teaching. This matters because the vision that follows does not arise from idle curiosity. It grows out of instruction already received. The earlier chapters have begun to reorder Arjuna's understanding, and he now stands at the point where teaching is ripening into direct encounter.

At the same time, this verse shows that clarity of mind is not the end of the path. Arjuna is no longer trapped in the same moral paralysis, yet he still longs to see more deeply. Spiritual life often unfolds in this order. First, error is loosened. Then, a more intense hunger for truth appears.

There is also gratitude in Arjuna's words. He recognizes that Krishna's teaching has been an act of mercy, not a display of cleverness. The "secret" subject is spiritual because it concerns the deepest reality of self, duty, and God. Arjuna is beginning to understand that Krishna has not merely advised him about war; he has opened a doorway into the nature of existence.

For a reader, the verse offers an important test. Real understanding does not make a person self-satisfied. When delusion begins to clear, one becomes humbler, more grateful, and more ready to receive what is still beyond ordinary sight.

original_script:
भवाप्ययौ हि भूतानां श्रुतौ विस्तरशो मया।
त्वत्तः कमलपत्राक्ष माहात्म्यमपि चाव्ययम्।।11.2।।
transliteration:
bhavāpyayau hi bhūtānāṁ śhrutau vistaraśho mayā
tvattaḥ kamala-patrākṣha māhātmyam api chāvyayam
word_meanings: bhava—appearance; apyayau—and disappearance; hi—certainly; bhūtānām—of all living beings; śhrutau—have been heard; vistaraśhaḥ—in detail; mayā—by me; tvattaḥ—from You; kamala-patra-akṣha—O lotus-eyed one; māhātmyam—glories; api—also; cha—and; avyayam—inexhaustible
translation_english: O lotus-eyed one, I have heard from You in detail about the appearance and disappearance of every living entity and have realized Your inexhaustible glories.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna next says that he has heard in detail from Krishna about the arising and passing away of beings, and about Krishna's inexhaustible greatness. This is his acknowledgment that the teaching has been comprehensive. Krishna has spoken about creation, dissolution, the field of action, the Self, devotion, and the divine glories that uphold the world. Arjuna is not asking to see the universal form because Krishna has said too little, but because Krishna has said so much.

The verse marks an important movement from hearing to assimilation. Arjuna is not repeating information mechanically. He says he has understood enough to recognize Krishna's greatness as inexhaustible. That word matters. Divine reality is not exhausted by concepts, definitions, or a single angle of vision. The more the mind learns, the more it senses the abundance still beyond its grasp.

There is also a subtle contrast between the appearance and disappearance of beings and the inexhaustibility of Krishna. Created things emerge and pass. Empires rise and fall. Bodies are born and die. Krishna alone remains the unfailing ground in relation to which all change is understood. Arjuna has heard this, and now he wishes to see how that unchanging Lord contains the changing universe.

For a new reader, the verse teaches that spiritual listening should enlarge wonder, not close it down. The aim of teaching is not merely to store conclusions. It is to prepare the heart to recognize the limitless depth of what it has heard.

original_script:
एवमेतद्यथात्थ त्वमात्मानं परमेश्वर।
द्रष्टुमिच्छामि ते रूपमैश्वरं पुरुषोत्तम।।11.3।।
transliteration:
evam etad yathāttha tvam ātmānaṁ parameśhvara
draṣhṭum ichchhāmi te rūpam aiśhvaraṁ puruṣhottama
word_meanings: evam—thus; etat—this; yathā—as; āttha—have spoken; tvam—You; ātmānam—Yourself; parama-īśhvara—O Supreme Lord; draṣhṭum—to see; ichchhāmi—I wish; te—Your; rūpam—form; aiśhvaram—divine; puruṣha-uttama—O Supreme Personality
translation_english: O greatest of all personalities, O supreme form, though I see You here before me in Your actual position, as You have described Yourself, I wish to see how You have entered into this cosmic manifestation. I want to see that of Your divine form.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now speaks the desire that has been building within him: he wants to see the divine form as Krishna has described it. This request reveals the natural next step after theological understanding. It is one thing to accept that the Lord pervades and sustains the universe. It is another to behold that truth in a single overwhelming disclosure.

The verse also shows that Arjuna wants to understand the relation between Krishna's familiar presence and cosmic reality. Krishna stands before him as charioteer and friend, yet has declared himself the source, support, and inner Self of all that exists. Arjuna asks, in effect, to see how the personal Lord standing beside him is also present within the totality of manifestation. He wants sight to catch up with revelation.

This request is deeply human. We often understand a truth in outline before we can feel its force. Arjuna has heard enough to know that Krishna is no ordinary being, but the mind still seeks a form adequate to such claims. The universal vision will not replace Krishna's personal reality. It will temporarily uncover its vastness.

For a reader, the verse reminds us that true inquiry does not stop with slogans. Arjuna does not settle for a pious phrase about divine omnipresence. He asks to see what such a truth really means. The chapter opens because he is willing to press beyond partial comprehension.

original_script:
मन्यसे यदि तच्छक्यं मया द्रष्टुमिति प्रभो।
योगेश्वर ततो मे त्वं दर्शयात्मानमव्ययम्।।11.4।।
transliteration:
manyase yadi tach chhakyaṁ mayā draṣhṭum iti prabho
yogeśhvara tato me tvaṁ darśhayātmānam avyayam
word_meanings: manyase—You think; yadi—if; tat—that; śhakyam—is able; mayā—by me; draṣhṭum—to be seen; iti—thus; prabho—O Lord; yoga-īśhvara—O master of all mystic power; tataḥ—then; me—unto me; tvam—You; darśhaya—show; ātmānam—Yourself; avyayam—eternal
translation_english: If You think that I am able to behold Your cosmic form, O my Lord, O master of all mystic power, then kindly show me that unlimited universal Self.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna's request becomes more careful in this verse. He does not demand the vision as though entitled to it. Instead, he places the matter before Krishna: if Krishna judges him capable, then let the universal form be shown. This humility is not ornamental. It reflects an essential spiritual principle. The highest realities are received by grace, not seized by force.

Arjuna also addresses Krishna as the master of mystic power. He understands that such a vision is not produced by ordinary perception or imagination. It requires divine initiative. Even the desire to see is not enough by itself. Readiness matters, and readiness is measured not by self-assertion but by surrender.

The verse therefore balances longing with restraint. Arjuna deeply wants the vision, yet he allows Krishna to decide whether he can bear it. That is wise, because spiritual revelation is not always gentle. To see more than the ego can contain is as dangerous as it is glorious. Arjuna's humility becomes part of his protection.

For readers, this verse offers a corrective to spiritual ambition. It is good to long for deeper truth, but not good to presume mastery over it. Arjuna asks boldly, yet leaves the outcome in Krishna's hands. That mixture of earnestness and reverence is one of the healthiest postures on the path.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
पश्य मे पार्थ रूपाणि शतशोऽथ सहस्रशः।
नानाविधानि दिव्यानि नानावर्णाकृतीनि च।।11.5।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
paśhya me pārtha rūpāṇi śhataśho ’tha sahasraśhaḥ
nānā-vidhāni divyāni nānā-varṇākṛitīni cha
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Blessed Lord said; paśhya—behold; me—My; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rūpāṇi—forms; śhataśhaḥ—hundreds; atha—also; sahasraśhaḥ—thousands; nānā-vidhāni—various kinds; divyāni—divine; nānā—many; varṇa—colors; ākṛitīni—shapes; cha—and
translation_english: The Blessed Lord said: My dear Arjuna, O son of Pritha, see now My opulences, hundreds of thousands of varied divine and multicolored forms.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins to answer by inviting Arjuna to behold his divine forms in their countless variety. The language is intentionally abundant: hundreds, thousands, many colors, many shapes. The universal form is not a single rigid image. It is inexhaustible expression, the one Divine appearing as the source of endless manifestation without losing unity.

This verse also makes clear that God's fullness is not monochrome. Spiritual imagination sometimes reduces transcendence to blankness or abstraction, but Krishna reveals a richness that includes distinction, pattern, splendor, and multiplicity. Diversity is not outside the Divine. In the right sense, it flows from him. What Arjuna is about to see is not chaos but ordered abundance.

Krishna's invitation also carries authority. The vision is not self-generated by Arjuna's meditation. It comes because Krishna says, "See." Revelation begins with divine permission and divine self-disclosure. The Lord chooses to become visible in a way no human effort could compel.

For a reader, the verse prepares the mind not to mistake spiritual truth for narrowness. If all forms arise from the Divine, then the world's legitimate variety can become a field of contemplation rather than a threat. Krishna's greatness is shown not by excluding difference, but by containing immeasurable difference within one sovereign reality.

original_script:
पश्यादित्यान्वसून्रुद्रानश्विनौ मरुतस्तथा।
बहून्यदृष्टपूर्वाणि पश्याश्चर्याणि भारत।।11.6।।
transliteration:
paśhyādityān vasūn rudrān aśhvinau marutas tathā
bahūny adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvāṇi paśhyāśhcharyāṇi bhārata
word_meanings: paśhya—behold; ādityān—the twelve Adityas; vasūn—the eight Vasus; rudrān—the eleven Rudras; aśhvinau—the twin Ashvinis; marutaḥ—the forty-nine Maruts; tathā—also; bahūni—many; adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvāṇi—never seen before; paśhya—behold; āśhcharyāṇi—marvels; bhārata—O best of the Bharatas
translation_english: O best of the Bharatas, see here the different manifestations of Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Ashwini-kumaras and all the other demigods. Behold the many wonderful things which no one has ever seen or heard of before.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next names whole classes of divine beings and cosmic powers, telling Arjuna to behold them all within him. The point is not merely to impress him with a long list of mythic figures. It is to gather the entire sacred universe into a single center. Powers that tradition may contemplate separately are shown as expressions within the one Lord.

This is an important theological moment. The Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Ashvins, and others represent different functions, energies, and orders within the cosmos. By placing them all within himself, Krishna reveals that none of these realities stand independently over against him. They are not rival sources of authority. Their reality is upheld in him and derived from him.

Krishna also says that Arjuna will see wonders never seen or heard before. That line suggests the limits of inherited religious imagination. Sacred tradition can prepare the mind, but the fullness of divine reality always exceeds established categories. Revelation confirms what is true in prior understanding, yet it also overflows it.

For the reader, the verse teaches that spiritual maturity unifies what lesser vision keeps fragmented. Instead of being scattered among many powers, fears, and loyalties, one begins to see that all legitimate greatness and all cosmic order have their root in a single divine source.

original_script:
इहैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं पश्याद्य सचराचरम्।
मम देहे गुडाकेश यच्चान्यद्द्रष्टुमिच्छसि।।11.7।।
transliteration:
ihaika-sthaṁ jagat kṛitsnaṁ paśhyādya sa-charācharam
mama dehe guḍākeśha yach chānyad draṣhṭum ichchhasi
word_meanings: iha—here; eka-stham—situated in one place; jagat—the universe; kṛitsnam—entire; paśhya—behold; adya—now; sa—with; chara—the moving; acharam—and not moving; mama—in My; dehe—body; guḍākeśha—O Arjuna; yat—whatever; cha—also; anyat—else; draṣhṭum—to see; ichchhasi—you wish
translation_english: O Arjuna, whatever you wish to see, behold at once in this body of Mine! This universal form can show you whatever you now desire to see and whatever you may want to see in the future. Everything—moving and nonmoving—is here completely, in one place.
deep_dive_text:
Here Krishna declares that in his one body Arjuna may behold the entire universe: whatever he wishes to see, whatever moves or does not move, and even what pertains to the future. This is one of the chapter's most startling claims. The universal form is not simply very large. It is a mode of divine disclosure in which totality becomes present in one place without ceasing to be itself.

The verse therefore collapses ordinary assumptions about separation. We normally experience reality as broken into countless pieces across space and time. Krishna reveals a higher standpoint in which the many are held together at once. Past, present, and future do not confront him as they confront us. The moving and the unmoving, the distant and the near, are all contained within his awareness and power.

This also explains why the vision will be overwhelming. To see the universe as a whole is not merely to see more objects. It is to see their hidden unity, their interdependence, and their simultaneity in God. The battlefield is no longer an isolated crisis. It appears within a vast divine horizon that includes everything.

For a reader, this verse can loosen the tyranny of partial perspective. We suffer partly because we mistake our fragment for the whole. Krishna's universal form reminds us that reality is held together by a wisdom larger than our immediate sight.

original_script:
न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुमनेनैव स्वचक्षुषा।
दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम्।।11.8।।
transliteration:
na tu māṁ śhakyase draṣhṭum anenaiva sva-chakṣhuṣhā
divyaṁ dadāmi te chakṣhuḥ paśhya me yogam aiśhvaram
word_meanings: na—never; tu—but; mām—Me; śhakyase—are you able; draṣhṭum—to see; anena—with these; eva—certainly; sva-chakṣhuṣhā—your own eyes; divyam—divine; dadāmi—I give; te—to you; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; paśhya—behold; me—My; yogam aiśhvaram—inconceivable mystic power
translation_english: But you cannot see Me with your present eyes. Therefore I give you divine eyes. Behold My mystic opulence!
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now states the condition for the vision: Arjuna cannot see it with his ordinary eyes, so Krishna grants him divine sight. This is one of the most important interpretive verses in the chapter. It prevents the universal form from being treated as a strange object available to common perception. The limitation lies not in the form but in the instrument of seeing.

Ordinary sight is fitted for ordinary distinctions. It can perceive surfaces, shapes, and physical relations, but not the divine totality in which all things are grounded. To behold that, consciousness itself must be elevated. Krishna does not merely point Arjuna toward a hidden spectacle; he transforms Arjuna's capacity to receive it.

This gift of divine vision also underscores grace. The highest realities are not reached by sharper senses alone. A new mode of seeing must be given. In spiritual life, this principle appears again and again. We do not merely need more information. We need purification, illumination, and a form of perception shaped by God.

For a new reader, the verse offers a practical lesson. Many spiritual frustrations come from trying to grasp transcendent truth with an unchanged mind. Krishna first grants vision, then reveals form. The order matters. Transformation of sight is itself part of revelation.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एवमुक्त्वा ततो राजन्महायोगेश्वरो हरिः।
दर्शयामास पार्थाय परमं रूपमैश्वरम्।।11.9।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
evam uktvā tato rājan mahā-yogeśhvaro hariḥ
darśhayām āsa pārthāya paramaṁ rūpam aiśhvaram
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; evam—thus; uktvā—having spoken; tataḥ—thereafter; rājan—O King; mahā-yoga-īśhvaraḥ—the great Lord of mystic power; hariḥ—the Supreme Personality of Godhead; darśhayām āsa—showed; pārthāya—unto Arjuna; paramam—the divine; rūpam aiśhvaram—universal form
translation_english: Sanjaya said: O King, having spoken thus, the Supreme Lord of all mystic power, the Personality of Godhead, displayed His universal form to Arjuna.
deep_dive_text:
At this point the narration shifts to Sanjaya, who describes Krishna as the Lord of mystic power revealing the universal form to Arjuna. The change of speaker is not incidental. It gives the event a witnessing frame. The vision is not presented as a private fantasy or inner mood alone, but as an event solemn enough to be reported before a king and preserved in sacred memory.

Sanjaya's description also emphasizes Krishna's sovereignty. The universal form is displayed, not stumbled upon. Krishna remains the agent. Even at the moment of greatest disclosure, he is not dissolved into an impersonal force. He reveals the cosmic totality because he governs it.

There is another subtle effect in this verse. By pausing to let Sanjaya speak, the text creates distance just before the vision's intensity rises. The reader is prepared to enter something vast. A narrator can sometimes steady us where a direct plunge would overwhelm. In that sense, Sanjaya performs for the hearer what Krishna has already done for Arjuna: he mediates access to a difficult reality.

For readers, the verse is a reminder that revelation is often transmitted through testimony. Not everyone sees what Arjuna sees, but all may still be instructed by the reliable witness who tells what was shown and how it transformed the one who received it.

original_script:
अनेकवक्त्रनयनमनेकाद्भुतदर्शनम्।
अनेकदिव्याभरणं दिव्यानेकोद्यतायुधम्।।11.10।।
transliteration:
aneka-vaktra-nayanam anekādbhuta-darśhanam
aneka-divyābharaṇaṁ divyānekodyatāyudham
word_meanings: aneka—many; vaktra—faces; nayanam—and eyes; aneka—many; adbhuta—wonderful; darśhanam—sights; aneka—many; divya—divine; ābharaṇam—ornaments; divya—divine; aneka—many; udyata—uplifted; āyudham—weapons
translation_english: Arjuna saw in that universal form unlimited mouths, unlimited eyes, unlimited wonderful visions. The form was decorated with many celestial ornaments and bore many divine upraised weapons.
deep_dive_text:
The first description of the universal form presents an overwhelming multiplicity: countless mouths, eyes, wondrous sights, ornaments, and uplifted weapons. Every element suggests inexhaustibility. Arjuna is not facing an enlarged human figure. He is beholding a form in which categories multiply beyond measure because the Lord's relation to all things is being made visible at once.

The details matter. Mouths and eyes suggest knowledge, expression, and power directed everywhere. Ornaments suggest majesty and beauty. Weapons suggest authority, protection, and judgment. The universal form is not soft abstraction. It includes the splendor that attracts, the vigilance that sees, and the force that governs. Divine reality is beautiful, but it is also active and sovereign.

There is also a challenge here to the human desire for manageable images of God. We often prefer a form simple enough to fit our emotional comfort. Krishna instead unveils a form whose very abundance resists control. The mind cannot easily reduce it to a single mood. It is wondrous and unsettling at once.

For a reader, this verse teaches that God exceeds every one-sided expectation. If one seeks only consolation, the weapons disturb. If one seeks only power, the ornaments and wonder complicate the picture. The Lord contains more than any narrow religious preference can hold.

original_script:
दिव्यमाल्याम्बरधरं दिव्यगन्धानुलेपनम्।
सर्वाश्चर्यमयं देवमनन्तं विश्वतोमुखम्।।11.11।।
transliteration:
divya-mālyāmbara-dharaṁ divya-gandhānulepanam
sarvāśhcharyamayaṁ devam anantaṁ viśhvato-mukham
word_meanings: divya—celestial; mālya—garlands; ambara—and garments; dharam—wearing; divya—celestial; gandha—fragrances; anulepanam—smeared with; sarva—all; āśhcharya-mayam—wonderful; devam—shining; anantam—unlimited; viśhvataḥ-mukham—all-pervading
translation_english: He wore celestial garlands and garments, and many divine scents were smeared over His body. All was wondrous, brilliant, boundless, all-expanding.
deep_dive_text:
The description deepens with celestial garlands, divine garments, sacred fragrances, and a form that is wondrous, radiant, boundless, and expanding everywhere. These details show that the universal form is not only terrifying power. It is also sacred beauty. Krishna's totality includes liturgical splendor, fragrance, adornment, and majesty.

This matters because the divine is not revealed merely as raw force. The universe rests in a reality that is also worthy of worship. The ornaments and scents signal sanctity, not decoration for its own sake. They communicate that what Arjuna sees is holy. Even in a form too vast for ordinary containment, the marks of divinity remain.

The phrase "all-expanding" adds another dimension. The vision is not static. It gives the impression of inexhaustible unfolding. However much Arjuna sees, there is always more. The boundlessness is not only spatial but qualitative. Krishna's form surpasses the viewer's capacity to complete the act of seeing.

For the reader, this verse prevents a false opposition between transcendence and devotion. The highest reality is not less beautiful because it is more immense. Instead, beauty itself is gathered up and intensified in God. Sacred attraction and cosmic magnitude belong together.

original_script:
दिवि सूर्यसहस्रस्य भवेद्युगपदुत्थिता।
यदि भाः सदृशी सा स्याद्भासस्तस्य महात्मनः।।11.12।।
transliteration:
divi sūrya-sahasrasya bhaved yugapad utthitā
yadi bhāḥ sadṛiśhī sā syād bhāsas tasya mahātmanaḥ
word_meanings: divi—in the sky; sūrya—of suns; sahasrasya—of thousands; bhavet—there were; yugapat—simultaneously; utthitā—present; yadi—if; bhāḥ—light; sadṛiśhī—like that; sā—that; syāt—might be; bhāsaḥ—effulgence; tasya—of Him; mahā-ātmanaḥ—the great Lord
translation_english: If hundreds of thousands of suns were to rise at once into the sky, their radiance might resemble the effulgence of the Supreme Person in that universal form.
deep_dive_text:
To suggest the radiance of the universal form, the text compares it to the simultaneous rising of hundreds of thousands of suns. Even this, however, is only an approximation. The comparison stretches language to its limit. Human speech must borrow from the brightest thing it knows and then multiply it beyond ordinary imagination.

The verse therefore teaches something about revelation as well as about light. Divine reality does not fit easily within literal description. It can be indicated, approached, and symbolized, but not neatly contained. When the text turns to such an image, it shows that the experience exceeds common measures. The universal form is not merely visible; it is superabundantly luminous.

This light also has a spiritual meaning. Krishna's radiance is not just physical brilliance. It signifies truth unveiled, ignorance dispelled, and all partial lights gathered into their source. Every lesser illumination, whether intellectual, moral, or cosmic, is derived from the one supreme light.

For readers, the verse is a reminder that God is not one more bright object within the universe. He is the source from which the very possibility of brightness, intelligibility, and manifestation arises. The image of many suns shocks the imagination so that it may become ready for reverence.

original_script:
तत्रैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं प्रविभक्तमनेकधा।
अपश्यद्देवदेवस्य शरीरे पाण्डवस्तदा।।11.13।।
transliteration:
tatraika-sthaṁ jagat kṛitsnaṁ pravibhaktam anekadhā
apaśhyad deva-devasya śharīre pāṇḍavas tadā
word_meanings: tatra—there; eka-stham—in one place; jagat—the universe; kṛitsnam—complete; pravibhaktam—divided; anekadhā—into many; apaśhyat—could see; deva-devasya—of the Supreme Personality of Godhead; śharīre—in the universal body; pāṇḍavaḥ—Arjuna; tadā—at that time
translation_english: At that time Arjuna could see in the universal form of the Lord the unlimited expansions of the universe situated in one place although divided into many, many thousands.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now sees the whole universe, with all its many divisions, gathered in one place within the Lord's body. This is the verse of unity-in-diversity. The world is not denied in its variety. It remains manifold, differentiated, and immense. Yet all that multiplicity is seen together within one divine reality.

The philosophical force of this vision is profound. We ordinarily know only fragments: one region, one life, one crisis, one point in time. Krishna discloses a standpoint from which the scattered many are embraced in a single whole. The divisions of the universe are real at their own level, but they do not possess final independence. They are held together within a higher unity.

This has immediate relevance to Arjuna's situation. The battlefield seems like a private catastrophe, but in the universal form it becomes one moment inside an immeasurably larger order. To see the whole in God is not to become indifferent to events. It is to stop treating one's isolated angle as the measure of reality.

For a reader, this verse invites a discipline of perspective. Much suffering is sharpened by fragmentation. Krishna's universal form reveals that the many do not float without relation. They belong to a deeper coherence, even when our ordinary vision cannot yet see it.

original_script:
ततः स विस्मयाविष्टो हृष्टरोमा धनञ्जयः।
प्रणम्य शिरसा देवं कृताञ्जलिरभाषत।।11.14।।
transliteration:
tataḥ sa vismayāviṣhṭo hṛiṣhṭa-romā dhanañjayaḥ
praṇamya śhirasā devaṁ kṛitāñjalir abhāṣhata
word_meanings: tataḥ—then; saḥ—he; vismaya-āviṣhṭaḥ—overwhelmed with wonder; hṛiṣhṭa-romā—with his bodily hairs standing on end; dhanañjayaḥ—Arjuna; praṇamya—bowing down; śhirasā—with his head; devam—to the Supreme Lord; kṛita-añjaliḥ—with folded hands; abhāṣhata—spoke
translation_english: Then, bewildered and astonished, his hair standing on end, Arjuna bowed his head to offer obeisances and with folded hands began to pray to the Supreme Lord.
deep_dive_text:
After beholding this astonishing form, Arjuna is overwhelmed: his hair stands on end, he bows, and with folded hands begins to pray. The verse records not just what he thinks, but how his whole being responds. Divine disclosure affects body, speech, and emotion together. True encounter is not merely intellectual.

Arjuna's astonishment is matched by reverence. Wonder alone can remain passive, but wonder joined to bowing becomes worship. The vision has moved beyond spectacle. It has forced Arjuna into recognition. He is no longer speaking to Krishna merely as companion or counselor. He is standing before the unveiled majesty of the Lord.

The bodily signs matter. Hair standing on end, folded hands, and bowed head show that spiritual truth is not always received calmly. The human frame trembles under greatness. In many traditions, fear and adoration meet at the threshold of the holy. This verse captures that meeting point with unusual vividness.

For a reader, the lesson is clear. When truth is seen more fully, the proper response is not casual analysis alone. Reverence becomes fitting. The verse teaches that prayer may begin where control ends, when the soul recognizes a reality before which it can only bow and speak from awe.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
पश्यामि देवांस्तव देव देहे
