सर्वांस्तथा भूतविशेषसङ्घान्।
ब्रह्माणमीशं कमलासनस्थ-
मृषींश्च सर्वानुरगांश्च दिव्यान्।।11.15।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
paśhyāmi devāns tava deva dehe
sarvāns tathā bhūta-viśheṣha-saṅghān
brahmāṇam īśhaṁ kamalāsana-stham
ṛiṣhīṅśh cha sarvān uragāṅśh cha divyān
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; paśhyāmi—I see; devān—all the demigods; tava—Your; deva—O Lord; dehe—in the body; sarvān—all; tathā—also; bhūta—living entities; viśheṣha-saṅghān—specifically assembled; brahmāṇam—Lord Brahma; īśham—Lord Shiva; kamala-āsana-stham—sitting on the lotus flower; ṛiṣhīn—great sages; cha—also; sarvān—all; uragān—serpents; cha—also; divyān—divine
translation_english: Arjuna said: My dear Lord Krishna, I see assembled in Your body all the demigods and various other living entities. I see Brahma sitting on the lotus flower, as well as Lord Shiva and all the sages and divine serpents.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna begins his prayer by naming what he sees within Krishna's body: all the gods, all kinds of beings, Brahma, Shiva, sages, and divine serpents. This is a comprehensive act of recognition. Figures that religious imagination often contemplates separately now appear together within the one universal form. Krishna is revealed as the encompassing ground of every sacred order.

The verse is especially important because it refuses rivalry at the highest level. Brahma the creator, Shiva the great lord, sages, serpents, celestial beings, and ordinary creatures all stand within Krishna's body. Whatever their dignity, none exist independently of the supreme reality now unveiled. The universal form does not abolish their identities, but it places them in true relation.

Arjuna's seeing is also becoming more articulate. At first he is stunned. Now he begins to interpret the vision by naming its contents. Prayer often develops this way. Awe gives way to praise, and praise gives way to theological clarity. Arjuna is learning, in real time, what Krishna's cosmic lordship means.

For readers, the verse teaches that spiritual maturity unifies what superficial religion divides. Instead of scattering devotion among disconnected powers, the heart begins to see that all real greatness, all sacred function, and all living order have their root and coherence in the one Lord.

original_script:
अनेकबाहूदरवक्त्रनेत्रं
पश्यामि त्वां सर्वतोऽनन्तरूपम्।
नान्तं न मध्यं न पुनस्तवादिं
पश्यामि विश्वेश्वर विश्वरूप।।11.16।।
transliteration:
aneka-bāhūdara-vaktra-netraṁ
paśhyāmi tvāṁ sarvato ’nanta-rūpam
nāntaṁ na madhyaṁ na punas tavādiṁ
paśhyāmi viśhveśhvara viśhva-rūpa
word_meanings: aneka—many; bāhu—arms; udara—bellies; vaktra—mouths; netram—eyes; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; sarvataḥ—on all sides; ananta-rūpam—unlimited form; na antam—no end; na madhyam—no middle; na punaḥ—nor again; tava—Your; ādim—beginning; paśhyāmi—I see; viśhva-īśhvara—O Lord of the universe; viśhva-rūpa—O universal form
translation_english: O Lord of the universe, O universal form, I see in Your body many, many arms, bellies, mouths and eyes, expanded everywhere, without limit. I see in You no end, no middle and no beginning.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now describes the Lord as having endless arms, bellies, mouths, and eyes, present everywhere, with no beginning, middle, or end. The key point is not simply numerical excess. It is the collapse of ordinary limits. Human thought understands a thing by tracing its outline, identifying its center, and locating its boundaries. The universal form defeats that method completely.

To say there is no beginning, middle, or end is to say that Krishna cannot be grasped as one object among others. Even when he becomes visible, he remains beyond containment. Infinity here is not an abstract idea but a direct challenge to finite perception. Arjuna sees a form that can be witnessed, yet never measured.

This vision also reveals why divine totality is both attractive and frightening. The ego prefers a world it can map. But Krishna's universal form has no edge at which control can resume. Arjuna is learning that the supreme reality is not merely greater than creatures in degree. It is categorically beyond creaturely boundedness.

For a reader, the verse asks for intellectual honesty. Much of religion goes wrong when we imagine that God has been adequately enclosed by our concepts. Arjuna's testimony breaks that illusion. However clearly God is revealed, he always exceeds the frame within which the mind tries to settle him.

original_script:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रिणं च
तेजोराशिं सर्वतो दीप्तिमन्तम्।
पश्यामि त्वां दुर्निरीक्ष्यं समन्ताद्
दीप्तानलार्कद्युतिमप्रमेयम्।।11.17।।
transliteration:
kirīṭinaṁ gadinaṁ chakriṇaṁ cha
tejo-rāśhiṁ sarvato dīptimantam
paśhyāmi tvāṁ durnirīkṣhyaṁ samantād
dīptānalārka-dyutim aprameyam
word_meanings: kirīṭinam—with helmets; gadinam—with maces; chakriṇam—with discs; cha—and; tejaḥ-rāśhim—effulgence; sarvataḥ—on all sides; dīpti-mantam—luminous; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; durnirīkṣhyam—difficult to see; samantāt—everywhere; dīpta-anala—blazing fire; arka—of the sun; dyutim—the sunshine; aprameyam—immeasurable
translation_english: Your form is difficult to see because of its glaring effulgence, spreading on all sides, like blazing fire or the immeasurable glare of the sun. Yet I see this glowing form everywhere, adorned with various crowns, clubs and discs.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna continues by describing the universal form as blazing like fire and sun, adorned with crowns, clubs, and discs. This verse is significant because it combines overwhelming radiance with recognizable signs of divine rulership. Even within the cosmic immensity, Krishna is not reduced to an anonymous force. Marks of kingship and sovereignty remain visible.

The blazing light shows transcendence. It is difficult to see because the Lord's glory is too intense for ordinary vision to bear easily. But the crowns, mace, and discus show that the One revealed here is still the same personal Lord who governs, protects, and judges. Majesty is not lost in infinity. Rather, infinity deepens majesty.

There is a spiritual lesson in that conjunction. Human beings sometimes divide the Divine into two separate ideas: impersonal immensity on one side and personal deity on the other. This verse refuses that split. Krishna appears as immeasurable radiance while still bearing the emblems of purposeful lordship.

For a reader, the verse teaches that transcendence need not cancel relationship. The Lord is beyond all finite limits, yet he is not less personal for being more glorious. His power does not erase identity; it reveals its fullness.

original_script:
त्वमक्षरं परमं वेदितव्यं
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।
त्वमव्ययः शाश्वतधर्मगोप्ता
सनातनस्त्वं पुरुषो मतो मे।।11.18।।
transliteration:
tvam akṣharaṁ paramaṁ veditavyaṁ
tvam asya viśhvasya paraṁ nidhānam
tvam avyayaḥ śhāśhvata-dharma-goptā
sanātanas tvaṁ puruṣho mato me
word_meanings: tvam—You; akṣharam—the inexhaustible; paramam—supreme; veditavyam—to be understood; tvam—You; asya—of this; viśhvasya—universe; param—supreme; nidhānam—basis; tvam—You; avyayaḥ—inexhaustible; śhāśhvata-dharma-goptā—maintainer of the eternal religion; sanātanaḥ—eternal; tvam—You; puruṣhaḥ—the Supreme Personality; mataḥ me—is my opinion
translation_english: You are the supreme primal objective. You are the ultimate resting place of all this universe. You are inexhaustible, and You are the oldest. You are the maintainer of the eternal religion, the Personality of Godhead. This is my opinion.
deep_dive_text:
Here Arjuna begins to interpret the vision in theological language. Krishna is the supreme object to be known, the final resting place of the universe, the inexhaustible one, the oldest, and the guardian of eternal dharma. This is not mere emotional praise. Arjuna is drawing doctrinal conclusions from what he has seen. The vision is producing understanding.

The phrase "ultimate resting place" is especially rich. All beings move, strive, rise, and fall, but their deepest ground and final stability lie in Krishna alone. Likewise, to call him the maintainer of eternal dharma means that moral and spiritual order are not inventions of society. They endure because they are rooted in the nature and will of the Lord.

Arjuna also says, "This is my opinion," which gives the verse a thoughtful tone. He is not parroting a formula. He is speaking from insight born of encounter. Revelation does not bypass intelligence; it illumines it. Arjuna's confession is therefore personal, rational, and devotional at once.

For readers, the verse offers a model of mature faith. One does not merely feel awe before God. One learns to recognize what God must be if the vision is true: the source of meaning, the basis of order, and the unfailing refuge of the world.

original_script:
अनादिमध्यान्तमनन्तवीर्य-
मनन्तबाहुं शशिसूर्यनेत्रम्।
पश्यामि त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्र-
स्वतेजसा विश्वमिदं तपन्तम्।।11.19।।
transliteration:
anādi-madhyāntam ananta-vīryam
ananta-bāhuṁ śhaśhi-sūrya-netram
paśhyāmi tvāṁ dīpta-hutāśha-vaktraṁ
sva-tejasā viśhvam idaṁ tapantam
word_meanings: anādi—without beginning; madhya—middle; antam—or end; ananta—unlimited; vīryam—glories; ananta—unlimited; bāhum—arms; śhaśhi—the moon; sūrya—and sun; netram—eyes; paśhyāmi—I see; tvām—You; dīpta—blazing; hutāśha-vaktram—fire coming out of Your mouth; sva-tejasā—by Your radiance; viśhvam—universe; idam—this; tapantam—heating
translation_english: You are without origin, middle or end. Your glory is unlimited. You have numberless arms, and the sun and moon are Your eyes. I see You with blazing fire coming forth from Your mouth, burning this entire universe by Your own radiance.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna next speaks of Krishna as without beginning, middle, or end, with countless arms, with the sun and moon as eyes, and with blazing fire issuing from his mouth. The imagery intensifies the sense that the entire cosmos is gathered into the Lord's living form. Luminaries that govern ordinary time and vision are now revealed as aspects within him.

The sun and moon as eyes suggest that nothing is hidden from divine awareness. Day and night, illumination and measure, all belong to Krishna's seeing. The fire issuing from his mouths adds a more fearsome dimension. The Lord is not only the source of life and order; he is also the power before which all formed things can be consumed. Creation and dissolution meet in him.

This verse begins to move Arjuna from admiration into dread. The universal form is not merely vast and beautiful. It includes the force by which the universe is judged, transformed, and burned away. That is why the vision cannot be reduced to mystical comfort. It reveals God as absolute reality, not as an enlarged version of our preferences.

For readers, the verse invites sobriety. We often welcome divine nearness while resisting divine supremacy. Arjuna is shown that the Lord who sustains life is also the Lord before whom every life must finally stand. Such knowledge is frightening, but it is also purifying.

original_script:
द्यावापृथिव्योरिदमन्तरं हि
व्याप्तं त्वयैकेन दिशश्च सर्वाः।
दृष्ट्वाद्भुतं रूपमुग्रं तवेदं
लोकत्रयं प्रव्यथितं महात्मन्।।11.20।।
transliteration:
dyāv ā pṛithivyor idam antaraṁ hi
vyāptaṁ tvayaikena diśhaśh cha sarvāḥ
dṛiṣhṭvādbhutaṁ rūpam ugraṁ tavedaṁ
loka-trayaṁ pravyathitaṁ mahātman
word_meanings: dyau—from heaven; ā pṛithivyoḥ—to the earth; idam—this; antaram—space between; hi—certainly; vyāptam—pervaded; tvayā—by You; ekena—alone; diśhaḥ—directions; cha—and; sarvāḥ—all; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; adbhutam—wonderful; rūpam—form; ugram—terrible; tava—Your; idam—this; loka-trayam—all the planetary systems; pravyathitam—perturbed; mahā-ātman—O great one
translation_english: Although You are one, You spread throughout the sky and the planets and all space between. O great one, seeing this wondrous and terrible form, all the planetary systems are perturbed.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that Krishna, though one, fills the whole expanse between heaven and earth and all directions, and that the worlds are shaken by this terrible wonder. The verse joins unity and total pervasion. Krishna is not one being among many spread through space. He is the one reality whose presence saturates the whole field in which beings appear.

The result is not sentimental calm but cosmic disturbance. The worlds are perturbed because this revelation strips away the illusion of independent stability. Every realm, however exalted, must confront the fact that it rests under a power greater than itself. The universal form is wondrous, but its wonder is immense enough to unsettle all created orders.

This is a crucial lesson for Arjuna. The battlefield has made him fear social collapse, dishonor, and loss. Krishna now reveals a scale at which all worlds are already dependent and vulnerable before the Divine. What seems ultimate from a human viewpoint is shown to be secondary within the larger reality of God's presence.

For a reader, the verse warns against treating spiritual truth as a decorative addition to ordinary life. If God truly pervades all things, then his reality does not leave our settled worlds untouched. Revelation first disturbs false security so that trust can be rebuilt on firmer ground.

original_script:
अमी हि त्वां सुरसङ्घा विशन्ति
केचिद्भीताः प्राञ्जलयो गृणन्ति।
स्वस्तीत्युक्त्वा महर्षिसिद्धसङ्घाः
स्तुवन्ति त्वां स्तुतिभिः पुष्कलाभिः।।11.21।।
transliteration:
amī hi tvāṁ sura-saṅghā viśhanti
kechid bhītāḥ prāñjalayo gṛiṇanti
svastīty uktvā maharṣhi-siddha-saṅghāḥ
stuvanti tvāṁ stutibhiḥ puṣhkalābhiḥ
word_meanings: amī—all those; hi—certainly; tvām—You; sura-saṅghāḥ—groups of demigods; viśhanti—are entering; kechit—some of them; bhītāḥ—out of fear; prāñjalayaḥ—with folded hands; gṛiṇanti—are offering prayers; svasti—all peace; iti—thus; uktvā—speaking; mahā-ṛiṣhi—great sages; siddha-saṅghāḥ—perfect beings; stuvanti—are singing hymns; tvām—unto You; stutibhiḥ—with prayers; puṣhkalābhiḥ—Vedic hymns
translation_english: All the hosts of demigods are surrendering before You and entering into You. Some of them, very much afraid, are offering prayers with folded hands. Hosts of great sages and perfected beings, crying 'All peace!' are praying to You by singing the Vedic hymns.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now sees hosts of gods entering Krishna, some with folded hands in fear and reverence, while great sages and perfected beings sing peace and praise. The universal form draws out different responses, yet all of them acknowledge divine superiority. No celestial power meets this revelation as an equal.

The verse is striking because fear and worship appear together. Some beings tremble, others praise, and others cry out for peace. This does not indicate contradiction. Rather, it shows that holiness exposes each being according to its own condition. What is aligned with the Divine rejoices to praise. What feels the weight of judgment trembles. Both reactions testify to the seriousness of the encounter.

The sages' hymns also matter. In the midst of cosmic upheaval, sacred speech continues. Worship does not cease when the vision becomes overwhelming. On the contrary, praise becomes the proper way to remain rightly oriented before a reality too vast for analysis alone.

For the reader, the verse suggests that spiritual maturity is not the absence of trembling. It is the capacity to turn trembling into surrender, prayer, and peace-seeking. The holy does not always feel comfortable, but it always calls for a response ordered toward reverence.

original_script:
रुद्रादित्या वसवो ये च साध्या
विश्वेऽश्विनौ मरुतश्चोष्मपाश्च।
गन्धर्वयक्षासुरसिद्धसङ्घा
वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताश्चैव सर्वे।।11.22।।
transliteration:
rudrādityā vasavo ye cha sādhyā
viśhve ’śhvinau marutaśh choṣhmapāśh cha
gandharva-yakṣhāsura-siddha-saṅghā
vīkṣhante tvāṁ vismitāśh chaiva sarve
word_meanings: rudra—manifestations of Lord Shiva; ādityāḥ—the Adityas; vasavaḥ—the Vasus; ye—all those; cha—and; sādhyāḥ—the Sadhyas; viśhve—the Vishvedevas; aśhvinau—the Ashwini-kumaras; marutaḥ—the Maruts; cha—and; uṣhma-pāḥ—the forefathers; cha—and; gandharva—of the Gandharvas; yakṣha—the Yakshas; asura—the demons; siddha—and the perfected demigods; saṅghāḥ—the assemblies; vīkṣhante—are beholding; tvām—You; vismitāḥ—in wonder; cha—also; eva—certainly; sarve—all
translation_english: All the various manifestations of Lord Shiva, the Adityas, the Vasus, the Sadhyas, the Vishvedevas, the two Ashvis, the Maruts, the forefathers, the Gandharvas, the Yakshas, the Asuras and the perfected demigods are beholding You in wonder.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna broadens the list even further, naming many classes of divine and semi-divine beings who all gaze upon Krishna in amazement. The point of the catalog is cumulative. It is meant to exhaust the imagination of cosmic rank and then show that every rank stands before the same supreme reality. No order of being escapes inclusion.

This is important because spiritual pride can attach itself even to exalted levels of existence. Yet here the Adityas, Vasus, Sadhyas, Maruts, ancestors, Gandharvas, Yakshas, Asuras, and perfected beings are all spectators. Whatever powers they possess, they do not own the mystery now revealed. They too are learners before Krishna's boundlessness.

The verse also shows that wonder is universalized. Not only Arjuna but the whole hierarchy of beings is stunned. This enlarges the significance of the vision. It is not merely a private religious experience given special intensity. It is a disclosure of the Lord in the face of whom all finite greatness becomes receptive rather than self-sufficient.

For readers, the lesson is cleansing. We tend to rank strength, status, intelligence, and spiritual attainment. Krishna's universal form relativizes every ladder. Before the Supreme, even the mighty are astonished. Wonder becomes the great equalizer.

original_script:
रूपं महत्ते बहुवक्त्रनेत्रं
महाबाहो बहुबाहूरुपादम्।
बहूदरं बहुदंष्ट्राकरालं
दृष्ट्वा लोकाः प्रव्यथितास्तथाहम्।।11.23।।
transliteration:
rūpaṁ mahat te bahu-vaktra-netraṁ
mahā-bāho bahu-bāhūru-pādam
bahūdaraṁ bahu-danṣhṭrā-karālaṁ
dṛiṣhṭvā lokāḥ pravyathitās tathāham
word_meanings: rūpam—form; mahat—very great; te—Your; bahu—many; vaktra—faces; netram—and eyes; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; bahu—many; bāhu—arms; ūru—thighs; pādam—and legs; bahu-udaram—many bellies; bahu-danṣhṭrā—many teeth; karālam—terrible; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; lokāḥ—all the planets; pravyathitāḥ—perturbed; tathā—similarly; aham—I
translation_english: O mighty-armed one, all the planets with their demigods are disturbed at seeing Your great form, with its many faces, eyes, arms, thighs, legs, and bellies and Your many terrible teeth; and as they are disturbed, so am I.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that all the worlds with their gods are disturbed by Krishna's great form, with its many faces, eyes, arms, thighs, legs, bellies, and terrible teeth, and that he himself is likewise shaken. This confession matters. Arjuna does not hide behind brave language. The more honestly he sees, the more honestly he admits his fear.

The details show why the form is so unsettling. It is not merely immense. It is embodied in a way that suggests total agency and total capacity. Many arms signify action everywhere. Many eyes signify perception everywhere. The terrible teeth introduce the certainty that what the Lord encompasses he can also devour. The vision presses beyond majesty into judgment.

Arjuna's fear is therefore not weakness but realism. A finite creature encountering unshielded divine totality cannot remain casually composed. The verse legitimizes trembling before truths that exceed our emotional readiness. Spiritual seriousness often begins when we stop pretending that ultimate reality can be handled without disturbance.

For readers, this verse offers permission to be truthful in prayer. Reverence need not disguise distress. Arjuna teaches that one may stand before God in awe and alarm at once, naming the impact without dishonesty or rebellion.

original_script:
नभःस्पृशं दीप्तमनेकवर्णं
व्यात्ताननं दीप्तविशालनेत्रम्।
दृष्ट्वा हि त्वां प्रव्यथितान्तरात्मा
धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो।।11.24।।
transliteration:
nabhaḥ-spṛiśhaṁ dīptam aneka-varṇaṁ
vyāttānanaṁ dīpta-viśhāla-netram
dṛiṣhṭvā hi tvāṁ pravyathitāntar-ātmā
dhṛitiṁ na vindāmi śhamaṁ cha viṣhṇo
word_meanings: nabhaḥ-spṛiśham—touching the sky; dīptam—glowing; aneka—many; varṇam—colors; vyātta—open; ānanam—mouths; dīpta—glowing; viśhāla—very great; netram—eyes; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; hi—certainly; tvām—You; pravyathita—perturbed; antaḥ-ātmā—within the soul; dhṛitim—steadiness; na—not; vindāmi—I find; śhamam—mental tranquility; cha—and; viṣhṇo—O Lord Vishnu
translation_english: O all-pervading Vishnu, seeing You with Your many radiant colors touching the sky, Your gaping mouths, and Your great glowing eyes, my mind is perturbed by fear. I can no longer maintain my steadiness or equilibrium of mind.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now speaks more personally. Seeing the sky-touching, many-colored form with gaping mouths and blazing eyes, he says his mind is shaken and he can no longer maintain steadiness. The universal vision has passed beyond wonder into disorientation. The Lord's greatness is not simply admired; it is endured.

This verse exposes the limits of human equilibrium. We often imagine spiritual attainment as increasing inner control, but there are moments when genuine revelation interrupts control altogether. Arjuna's instability is not a failure of faith. It is the natural result of beholding a reality too immense for ordinary consciousness to organize.

The blazing colors and open mouths also carry symbolic force. Beauty and terror are interwoven. Krishna's form is radiant, but that radiance is not sentimental. It burns through the arrangements by which the mind keeps itself comfortable. Arjuna's inner balance collapses because his familiar scale of reality has collapsed.

For the reader, the verse is a caution against romanticizing mystical vision. To see more of truth can be consoling, but it can also be destabilizing. One reason divine grace matters so much is that human beings need help not only to receive revelation, but also to survive it rightly.

original_script:
दंष्ट्राकरालानि च ते मुखानि
दृष्ट्वैव कालानलसन्निभानि।
दिशो न जाने न लभे च शर्म
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.25।।
transliteration:
danṣhṭrā-karālāni cha te mukhāni
dṛiṣhṭvaiva kālānala-sannibhāni
diśho na jāne na labhe cha śharma
prasīda deveśha jagan-nivāsa
word_meanings: danṣhṭrā—teeth; karālāni—terrible; cha—also; te—Your; mukhāni—faces; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; eva—thus; kāla-anala—the fire of death; sannibhāni—as if; diśhaḥ—the directions; na—not; jāne—I know; na—not; labhe—I obtain; cha—and; śharma—grace; prasīda—be pleased; deva-īśha—O Lord of all the demigods; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe
translation_english: O Lord of lords, O refuge of the worlds, please be gracious to me. I cannot keep my balance seeing thus Your blazing deathlike faces and awful teeth. In all directions I am bewildered.
deep_dive_text:
Overwhelmed, Arjuna cries out to the Lord of lords, the refuge of the worlds, asking for mercy. He says he cannot find direction or composure while seeing those blazing, deathlike faces and terrible teeth. This is an important turning point. The vision has driven him away from observation and into petition.

Arjuna's instinct is correct. When divine reality becomes unbearable, relationship becomes the only refuge. He does not try to master the experience. He turns to Krishna as shelter. The universal form by itself is too vast and fierce to be navigated by the isolated self. Only the Lord who reveals it can guide the one who sees it.

The verse also shows that terror can purify prayer. Arjuna's language is stripped down. He does not speak with philosophical distance here. He asks for grace because he knows he needs it. In that sense, the frightening aspect of revelation performs a merciful function: it exposes dependency.

For a reader, the verse teaches that spiritual life does not require emotional invulnerability. There are moments when the wisest action is simply to ask for mercy. Arjuna's prayer is not less devout because it rises from fear. It is devout precisely because fear has driven him toward the true refuge.

original_script:
अमी च त्वां धृतराष्ट्रस्य पुत्राः
सर्वे सहैवावनिपालसङ्घैः।
भीष्मो द्रोणः सूतपुत्रस्तथासौ
सहास्मदीयैरपि योधमुख्यैः।।11.26।।
वक्त्राणि ते त्वरमाणा विशन्ति
दंष्ट्राकरालानि भयानकानि।
केचिद्विलग्ना दशनान्तरेषु
सन्दृश्यन्ते चूर्णितैरुत्तमाङ्गैः।।11.27।।
transliteration:
amī cha tvāṁ dhṛitarāṣhṭrasya putrāḥ
sarve sahaivāvani-pāla-saṅghaiḥ
bhīṣhmo droṇaḥ sūta-putras tathāsau
sahāsmadīyair api yodha-mukhyaiḥ
vaktrāṇi te tvaramāṇā viśhanti
danṣhṭrā-karālāni bhayānakāni
kechid vilagnā daśhanāntareṣhu
sandṛiṣhyante chūrṇitair uttamāṅgaiḥ
word_meanings: amī—these; cha—and; tvām—You; dhṛitarāṣhṭrasya—of Dhritarashtra; putrāḥ—the sons; sarve—all; saha—with; eva—indeed; avani-pāla—of warrior kings; saṅghaiḥ—the groups; bhīṣhmaḥ—Bhishma; droṇaḥ—Drona; sūta-putraḥ—Karna; tathā—also; asau—that; saha—with; asmadīyaiḥ—our; api—also; yodha-mukhyaiḥ—chief soldiers; vaktrāṇi—mouths; te—Your; tvaramāṇāḥ—rushing; viśhanti—are entering; danṣhṭrā-karālāni—terrible with teeth; bhayānakāni—very fearful; kechit—some of them; vilagnāḥ—caught; daśhana-antareṣhu—between the teeth; sandṛiṣhyante—are seen; chūrṇitaiḥ—smashed; uttama-aṅgaiḥ—with their heads
translation_english: All the sons of Dhritarashtra, along with their allied kings, and Bhishma, Drona, Karna—and our chief soldiers also—are rushing into Your fearful mouths. And some I see trapped with heads smashed between Your teeth.
deep_dive_text:
The vision now becomes painfully concrete. Arjuna sees the sons of Dhritarashtra, allied kings, Bhishma, Drona, Karna, and other great warriors rushing into Krishna's dreadful mouths, some with their heads crushed between his teeth. The universal form is no longer a remote cosmic spectacle. It interprets the very battle about to unfold.

This is crucial for Arjuna's moral crisis. The people he loves, reveres, or fears are not outside the divine order. Their fate is already visible within Krishna's larger reality. The verse does not make their deaths trivial. On the contrary, it makes them more solemn. Even the noblest warriors are subject to a power before which prowess, lineage, and personal attachment cannot finally prevail.

The image of crushed heads is deliberately severe. It breaks every remaining illusion that history is controlled by human strategy alone. The battlefield outcome is not hanging in suspense at the level Arjuna has been using to judge it. Krishna is showing him that temporal conflict is already enclosed within divine judgment and time.

For readers, this verse is hard but necessary. Spiritual vision does not protect us from the fact of death. It reveals that death itself is not outside God. To see that truth is frightening, yet it also loosens the false belief that our private grasp can preserve what is destined to pass.

original_script:
यथा नदीनां बहवोऽम्बुवेगाः
समुद्रमेवाभिमुखा द्रवन्ति।
तथा तवामी नरलोकवीरा
विशन्ति वक्त्राण्यभिविज्वलन्ति।।11.28।।
transliteration:
yathā nadīnāṁ bahavo ’mbu-vegāḥ
samudram evābhimukhā dravanti
tathā tavāmī nara-loka-vīrā
viśhanti vaktrāṇy abhivijvalanti
word_meanings: yathā—as; nadīnām—of the rivers; bahavaḥ—the many; ambu-vegāḥ—waves of the waters; samudram—the ocean; eva—certainly; abhimukhāḥ—towards; dravanti—glide; tathā—similarly; tava—Your; amī—all these; nara-loka-vīrāḥ—kings of human society; viśhanti—are entering; vaktrāṇi—the mouths; abhivijvalanti—blazing
translation_english: As the many waves of the rivers flow into the ocean, so do all these great warriors enter blazing into Your mouths.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna compares the warriors entering Krishna's mouths to rivers rushing toward the sea. The image conveys inevitability. Rivers do not debate their destination. They move according to a current larger than any single wave. Likewise, the warriors are being carried toward an end already contained within the larger order revealed by Krishna.

This metaphor adds something distinct to the previous verse. The earlier image of crushed heads emphasized violence. The river image emphasizes vast process. Human conflict, however heated at ground level, is still caught up in a movement toward dissolution that no individual hero can finally arrest. The battlefield is part of a larger flow.

There is also theological depth here. The sea receives rivers without strain, just as the Lord receives the beings moving through time into their appointed end. Krishna is not surprised by the outcome. What feels sudden and catastrophic to human beings appears within him as part of a total pattern already known.

For the reader, the verse speaks against the illusion of absolute self-direction. We act, choose, and struggle, yet our lives move within currents we did not create. Wisdom begins when we stop mistaking our local momentum for ultimate control and learn to see the larger destination toward which all worldly forms are drawn.

original_script:
यथा प्रदीप्तं ज्वलनं पतङ्गा
विशन्ति नाशाय समृद्धवेगाः।
तथैव नाशाय विशन्ति लोका-
स्तवापि वक्त्राणि समृद्धवेगाः।।11.29।।
transliteration:
yathā pradīptaṁ jvalanaṁ pataṅgā
viśhanti nāśhāya samṛiddha-vegāḥ
tathaiva nāśhāya viśhanti lokās
tavāpi vaktrāṇi samṛiddha-vegāḥ
word_meanings: yathā—as; pradīptam—blazing; jvalanam—a fire; pataṅgāḥ—moths; viśhanti—enter; nāśhāya—for destruction; samṛiddha-vegāḥ—with full speed; tathā eva—similarly; nāśhāya—for destruction; viśhanti—are entering; lokāḥ—all people; tava—Your; api—also; vaktrāṇi—mouths; samṛiddha-vegāḥ—with full speed
translation_english: I see all people rushing full speed into Your mouths, as moths dash to destruction in a blazing fire.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna adds a second comparison: people rush into Krishna's blazing mouths as moths rush into a fire to be destroyed. Unlike the river image, which emphasizes inevitability, this one highlights blindness. Moths are drawn toward what consumes them. The verse therefore introduces the tragic dimension of human behavior under ignorance.

In the context of war, the image is severe. Ambition, rage, pride, and honor can drive people toward destruction with the force of fascination. They do not merely drift toward death; they hurry into it, often under the illusion that they are moving toward triumph. Krishna's universal form exposes the fatal vanity hidden inside worldly momentum.

This comparison also deepens Arjuna's education. He must learn that many human pursuits carry self-destructive energy when severed from true wisdom. The battlefield becomes a visible example of a wider spiritual law: what the ego pursues most feverishly may be the very thing that consumes it.

For readers, the verse remains painfully relevant. It asks us to examine the fires toward which we rush without discernment. Not every strong attraction is life-giving. Some desires shine only because they are already burning. The Lord's vision reveals both the attraction and the ruin together.

original_script:
लेलिह्यसे ग्रसमानः समन्ताल्-
लोकान्समग्रान्वदनैर्ज्वलद्भिः।
तेजोभिरापूर्य जगत्समग्रं
भासस्तवोग्राः प्रतपन्ति विष्णो।।11.30।।
transliteration:
lelihyase grasamānaḥ samantāl
lokān samagrān vadanair jvaladbhiḥ
tejobhir āpūrya jagat samagraṁ
bhāsas tavogrāḥ pratapanti viṣhṇo
word_meanings: lelihyase—You are licking; grasamānaḥ—devouring; samantāt—from all directions; lokān—people; samagrān—all; vadanaiḥ—by the mouths; jvaladbhiḥ—blazing; tejobhiḥ—by effulgence; āpūrya—covering; jagat—the universe; samagram—all; bhāsaḥ—illuminating rays; tava—Your; ugrāḥ—terrible; pratapanti—are scorching; viṣhṇo—O all-pervading Lord
translation_english: O Vishnu, I see You devouring all people from all sides with Your flaming mouths. Covering all the universe with Your immeasurable rays, You are manifest with terrible, scorching rays.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now sees Krishna devouring all people from every side with flaming mouths while scorching the entire universe with immeasurable radiance. This is one of the chapter's starkest moments. The sustaining Lord appears in the aspect of total consumption. Nothing stands outside the range of his power.

The verse corrects a sentimental theology that admits God as creator and preserver but resists God as the one before whom all created forms finally perish. Krishna does not become cruel here. Rather, Arjuna is being shown that divine sovereignty includes the dissolving of worlds as well as their upholding. The same absolute reality embraces both.

The radiance is important. Krishna's devouring power is not dark chaos. It is bound up with blazing splendor. Judgment is not outside glory. The universe is scorched not by randomness but by the presence of the One whose reality exceeds all temporary arrangements.

For readers, the verse can be sobering in the best sense. We often seek a God who will confirm the permanence of our preferred structures. Krishna shows instead that all structures are contingent. To know this is painful, but it can free the soul from clinging to what was never ultimate.

original_script:
आख्याहि मे को भवानुग्ररूपो
नमोऽस्तु ते देववर प्रसीद।
विज्ञातुमिच्छामि भवन्तमाद्यं
न हि प्रजानामि तव प्रवृत्तिम्।।11.31।।
transliteration:
ākhyāhi me ko bhavān ugra-rūpo
namo ’stu te deva-vara prasīda
vijñātum ichchhāmi bhavantam ādyaṁ
na hi prajānāmi tava pravṛittim
word_meanings: ākhyāhi—please explain; me—unto me; kaḥ—who; bhavān—You; ugra-rūpaḥ—in a fierce form; namaḥ astu—obeisances; te—unto You; deva-vara—O great one amongst the demigods; prasīda—be gracious; vijñātum—to know; ichchhāmi—I wish; bhavantam—You; ādyam—the original; na—not; hi—certainly; prajānāmi—do I know; tava—Your; pravṛittim—mission
translation_english: O Lord of lords, so fierce of form, please tell me who You are. I offer my obeisances unto You; please be gracious to me. You are the primal Lord. I want to know about You, for I do not know what Your mission is.
deep_dive_text:
Unable to bear the fierceness of what he sees, Arjuna asks, "Who are You in this terrible form?" and begs the Lord to be gracious. At first this may sound strange, since Arjuna clearly knows he is speaking to Krishna. But the question is not about basic identity. It arises because Krishna's revealed aspect has exceeded the categories by which Arjuna previously knew him.

When a familiar reality discloses an unfamiliar depth, we often have to ask again who stands before us. Arjuna has known Krishna as friend, guide, and teacher. Now he confronts Krishna as devouring cosmic power. The question expresses disorientation, but also genuine theological need. Arjuna wants to understand the purpose of this fearsome manifestation.

The verse is therefore a model of reverent inquiry under pressure. Arjuna does not turn away in offended confusion, nor does he pretend to understand. He bows and asks. That combination matters. There are moments in spiritual life when only a prayerful question can carry us forward.

For readers, the lesson is valuable. When God unsettles our familiar images, the right response is not immediate rejection. It is humble questioning joined to a request for grace. Arjuna seeks not escape from truth, but understanding of the truth that now overwhelms him.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
कालोऽस्मि लोकक्षयकृत्प्रवृद्धो
लोकान्समाहर्तुमिह प्रवृत्तः।
ऋतेऽपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
येऽवस्थिताः प्रत्यनीकेषु योधाः।।11.32।।
transliteration:
śhrī-bhagavān uvācha
kālo ’smi loka-kṣhaya-kṛit pravṛiddho
lokān samāhartum iha pravṛittaḥ
ṛite ’pi tvāṁ na bhaviṣhyanti sarve
ye ’vasthitāḥ pratyanīkeṣhu yodhāḥ
word_meanings: kālaḥ—Time; asmi—I am; loka-kṣhaya-kṛit—the destroyer of worlds; pravṛiddhaḥ—mighty; lokān—the worlds; samāhartum—to annihilate; iha—here; pravṛittaḥ—engaged; ṛite—without; api—even; tvām—you; na bhaviṣhyanti—will cease to be; sarve—all; ye—who; avasthitāḥ—situated; pratyanīkeṣhu—in the opposing armies; yodhāḥ—the warriors
translation_english: I am Kāla (Time), the great destroyer of worlds, currently manifest to engage in the dissolution of these people.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna answers with one of the most arresting declarations in the Gita: "I am Time, the great destroyer of worlds." Here the universal form receives an interpretive key. What Arjuna sees is not arbitrary violence. It is the divine presence operating as time, bringing beings to the dissolution already woven into created existence.

Time in this verse is not merely a clock or sequence. It is the power by which all compounded things move toward fulfillment and ending. Kingdoms, bodies, reputations, lineages, and civilizations all lie under its law. By identifying himself with Time, Krishna reveals that this law is not independent of him. The process that undoes worlds is still within divine sovereignty.

This statement should not be reduced to fatalism. Krishna is not saying that human action is meaningless. Rather, he is showing Arjuna the scale on which action must be understood. The warriors are already subject to a divine determination that no human hesitation can reverse. Arjuna's role will be real, but not ultimate.

For readers, the verse is both humbling and clarifying. Much anxiety arises from acting as though everything rests on us. Krishna reveals a harder truth and a liberating one: time is not ours to command, and the endings we fear are already held within a larger divine order.

original_script:
तस्मात्त्वमुत्तिष्ठ यशो लभस्व
जित्वा शत्रून्भुङ्क्ष्व राज्यं समृद्धम्।
मयैवैते निहताः पूर्वमेव
निमित्तमात्रं भव सव्यसाचिन्।।11.33।।
transliteration:
tasmāt tvam uttiṣhṭha yaśho labhasva
jitvā śhatrūn bhuṅkṣhva rājyaṁ samṛiddham
mayaivaite nihatāḥ pūrvam eva
nimitta-mātraṁ bhava savya-sāchin
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; tvam—you; uttiṣhṭha—get up; yaśhaḥ—glory; labhasva—gain; jitvā—conquering; śhatrūn—enemies; bhuṅkṣhva—enjoy; rājyam—kingdom; samṛiddham—flourishing; mayā—by Me; eva—certainly; ete—all these; nihatāḥ—killed; pūrvam eva—by previous arrangement; nimitta-mātram—just the instrument; bhava—become; savya-sāchin—O Savyasachi (ambidextrous archer)
translation_english: Therefore, get up. Prepare to fight and win glory. Conquer your enemies and enjoy a flourishing kingdom. They are already put to death by My arrangement, and you, O Savyasachi, can be but an instrument in the fight.
deep_dive_text:
Having revealed himself as Time, Krishna tells Arjuna to rise, fight, win glory, and enjoy the kingdom, for the enemies have already been slain by divine arrangement. Arjuna is to be only an instrument. This verse resolves a major tension in the Gita. Divine sovereignty does not cancel human action; it relocates its meaning.

Arjuna must still fight. The outcome being known to Krishna does not make duty unnecessary. Instead, it removes the burden of false authorship. Arjuna is not asked to create history from nothing or to carry the whole moral universe on his own shoulders. He is asked to align his action with what God has already ordained.

This is a demanding teaching. To become an instrument is not to become passive. Instruments must be available, disciplined, and responsive. Arjuna's courage is still required. But the ego's craving to be the independent doer is denied. That denial is not a diminishment. It is a release into clearer action.

For the reader, the verse offers a practical spiritual principle. Our task is not to control everything, but to act rightly within what God has set before us. When action is offered in that spirit, it becomes steadier, freer, and less poisoned by self-importance.

original_script:
द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च
कर्णं तथान्यानपि योधवीरान्।
मया हतांस्त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठा
युध्यस्व जेतासि रणे सपत्नान्।।11.34।।
transliteration:
droṇaṁ cha bhīṣhmaṁ cha jayadrathaṁ cha
karṇaṁ tathānyān api yodha-vīrān
mayā hatāns tvaṁ jahi mā vyathiṣhṭhā
yudhyasva jetāsi raṇe sapatnān
word_meanings: droṇam—Drona; cha—also; bhīṣhmam—Bhishma; cha—also; jayadratham—Jayadratha; cha—also; karṇam—Karna; tathā—also; anyān—others; api—certainly; yodha-vīrān—great warriors; mayā—by Me; hatān—already killed; tvam—you; jahi—destroy; mā—do not; vyathiṣhṭhāḥ—be disturbed; yudhyasva—just fight; jetāsi—you will conquer; raṇe—in the fight; sapatnān—enemies
translation_english: Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, Karna and the other great warriors have already been destroyed by Me. Therefore, kill them and do not be disturbed. Simply fight, and you will vanquish your enemies in battle.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna goes further by naming specific warriors already destroyed by his will: Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, Karna, and others. The teaching becomes personal and concrete. Arjuna cannot keep the matter at the level of abstraction. The men he reveres or fears most are explicitly included in the divine decree.

This specificity is important because Arjuna's attachments are specific. His hesitation has always concerned actual persons, not theoretical enemies. Krishna therefore addresses him at the same level. The verse forces Arjuna to see that even the most formidable and beloved figures do not stand outside divine judgment or beyond the reach of time.

Krishna's command not to be disturbed does not trivialize the grief involved. It means that Arjuna must not allow emotional upheaval to obscure duty once the larger truth has been shown. He is to fight, not as a self-appointed destroyer, but as one participating in a judgment already determined by the Lord.

For readers, the verse reminds us that spiritual surrender becomes most difficult exactly where attachment is most concrete. General trust is easy. Trust that includes particular losses, particular people, and particular sorrows is the sharper test. Arjuna is being led through that sharper test here.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
एतच्छ्रुत्वा वचनं केशवस्य
कृताञ्जलिर्वेपमानः किरीटी।
नमस्कृत्वा भूय एवाह कृष्णं
सगद्गदं भीतभीतः प्रणम्य।।11.35।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
etach chhrutvā vachanaṁ keśhavasya
kṛitāñjalir vepamānaḥ kirīṭī
namaskṛitvā bhūya evāha kṛiṣhṇaṁ
sa-gadgadaṁ bhīta-bhītaḥ praṇamya
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; etat—thus; śhrutvā—hearing; vachanam—the speech; keśhavasya—of Krishna; kṛita-añjaliḥ—with folded hands; vepamānaḥ—trembling; kirīṭī—Arjuna; namaskṛitvā—offering obeisances; bhūyaḥ—again; eva—also; āha—spoke; kṛiṣhṇam—to Krishna; sa-gadgadam—with a faltering voice; bhīta-bhītaḥ—fearful; praṇamya—bowing down
translation_english: Sanjaya said to Dhritarashtra: O King, after hearing these words from the Supreme Personality of Godhead, the trembling Arjuna offered obeisances with folded hands again and again. He fearfully spoke to Lord Krishna in a faltering voice, as follows.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya resumes the narrative, describing Arjuna as trembling, bowing again and again, and speaking in a faltering voice. The verse records the aftermath of Krishna's declaration. The vision and its interpretation have entered Arjuna's nerves, posture, and speech. Revelation has become bodily.

This is a useful reminder that spiritual experience is not merely an idea entertained by the mind. Arjuna's trembling shows that he has encountered something that cannot be domesticated at will. The repeated bowing shows that fear has not driven him away from Krishna, but more deeply toward reverence. Even his broken speech becomes a form of honesty before the Lord.

Sanjaya's narration also slows the pace after the terrifying declaration of verse 32. The hearer is allowed to see Arjuna's condition before hearing his next words of praise and repentance. This deepens the realism of the chapter. Great revelation does not produce polished serenity on command.

For readers, the verse can be comforting. One need not appear spiritually composed at every moment. Trembling, repeated surrender, and imperfect speech may all be part of a genuine response when one is being rearranged by truth that is too large for easy handling.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
स्थाने हृषीकेश तव प्रकीर्त्या
जगत्प्रहृष्यत्यनुरज्यते च।
रक्षांसि भीतानि दिशो द्रवन्ति
सर्वे नमस्यन्ति च सिद्धसङ्घाः।।11.36।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sthāne hṛiṣhīkeśha tava prakīrtyā
jagat prahṛiṣhyaty anurajyate cha
rakṣhānsi bhītāni diśho dravanti
sarve namasyanti cha siddha-saṅghāḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; sthāne—rightly; hṛiṣhīka-īśha—O master of all senses; tava—Your; prakīrtyā—by the glories; jagat—the entire world; prahṛiṣhyati—rejoices; anurajyate—is attached; cha—and; rakṣhānsi—the demons; bhītāni—out of fear; diśhaḥ—in all directions; dravanti—flee; sarve—all; namasyanti—are offering respects; cha—also; siddha-saṅghāḥ—the perfect human beings
translation_english: Arjuna said: O master of the senses, the world becomes joyful upon hearing Your name, and thus everyone becomes attached to You. Although the perfected beings offer You their respectful homage, the demons are afraid, and they flee here and there. All this is rightly done.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now says that the world rightly rejoices in Krishna's praise, while demons flee in fear and perfected beings offer homage. This is more than flattery. It is Arjuna's recognition that different responses to the Divine reveal the moral condition of those responding. Love of God is not arbitrary; it corresponds to alignment with truth.

The verse establishes a kind of spiritual order. Those attuned to what is good are drawn toward Krishna's name and presence. Those hostile to divine order recoil. The same reality that attracts the purified heart becomes unbearable to the rebellious will. In that sense, the verse explains not only the reactions within the universal form but the deeper logic behind them.

Arjuna's statement that all this is "rightly done" is also significant. He is regaining interpretive steadiness. The fearsome vision has not thrown him into nihilism. Instead, he begins to see that the universe is morally structured, and that responses to the Lord are not all equivalent.

For readers, this verse offers a searching question. What does the name and reality of God awaken in us: attraction, resistance, indifference, gratitude, discomfort? Arjuna suggests that our response is itself revelatory. It tells us something about the present shape of the heart.

original_script:
कस्माच्च ते न नमेरन्महात्मन्
गरीयसे ब्रह्मणोऽप्यादिकर्त्रे।
अनन्त देवेश जगन्निवास
त्वमक्षरं सदसत्तत्परं यत्।।11.37।।
transliteration:
kasmāch cha te na nameran mahātman
garīyase brahmaṇo ’py ādi-kartre
ananta deveśha jagan-nivāsa
tvam akṣharaṁ sad-asat tat paraṁ yat
word_meanings: kasmāt—why; cha—also; te—unto You; na—not; nameran—they should offer proper obeisances; mahā-ātman—O great one; garīyase—who are better; brahmaṇaḥ—than Brahma; api—although; ādi-kartre—to the supreme creator; ananta—O unlimited; deva-īśha—O God of the gods; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe; tvam—You; akṣharam—imperishable; sat-asat—cause and effect; tat param—transcendental; yat—because
translation_english: O great one, greater even than Brahma, You are the original creator. Why then should they not offer their respectful obeisances unto You? O limitless one, God of gods, refuge of the universe! You are the invincible source, the cause of all causes, transcendental to this material manifestation.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna asks why the great ones should not bow to Krishna, who is greater even than Brahma, the original creator, the cause of causes, and the refuge of the universe. The verse marks a deepening of Arjuna's theological clarity. He is no longer only reacting to the vision's power. He is articulating why worship is necessarily due to Krishna.

To say Krishna is greater than Brahma is to place him beyond the highest creaturely source within the cosmos. Creation itself points beyond itself to a more original ground. Krishna is not simply first in a sequence of beings. He is the source from which even the creator derives. Arjuna recognizes that if this is so, then reverence toward Krishna is not optional piety but the proper alignment of intelligence with reality.

The verse also calls Krishna "transcendental to this material manifestation." That matters because the universal form, however all-encompassing, must not be mistaken for mere material totality. Krishna includes and pervades the cosmos, yet is not exhausted by it. His lordship is both immanent and beyond.

For readers, the verse offers a firm measure for devotion. Worship is not sentiment projected upward. It is the fitting response to the One from whom all causes arise and in whom all orders of existence find their refuge. Arjuna's praise is grounded in metaphysical insight.

original_script:
त्वमादिदेवः पुरुषः पुराणस्-
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।
वेत्तासि वेद्यं च परं च धाम
त्वया ततं विश्वमनन्तरूप।।11.38।।
transliteration:
tvam ādi-devaḥ puruṣhaḥ purāṇas
tvam asya viśhvasya paraṁ nidhānam
vettāsi vedyaṁ cha paraṁ cha dhāma
tvayā tataṁ viśhvam ananta-rūpa
word_meanings: tvam—You; ādi-devaḥ—the original Supreme God; puruṣhaḥ—personality; purāṇaḥ—the oldest; tvam—You; asya—of this; viśhvasya—universe; param—transcendental; nidhānam—refuge; vettā—the knower; asi—You are; vedyam—the knowable; cha—and; param—transcendental; cha—and; dhāma—refuge; tvayā—by You; tatam—pervaded; viśhvam—the universe; ananta-rūpa—O unlimited form
translation_english: You are the original Personality of Godhead, the oldest, the ultimate sanctuary of this manifested cosmic world. You are the knower of everything, and You are all that is knowable. You are the supreme refuge, above the material modes. O limitless form! This whole cosmic manifestation is pervaded by You!
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna continues by calling Krishna the original Person, the oldest, the supreme sanctuary of the cosmos, the knower and the known, and the one beyond the material modes who pervades all. This verse gathers multiple strands of theology into one confession. Krishna is the source, the goal, the subject who knows, and the object worthy of being known.

That combination is profound. Human knowing is usually divided between knower and known, seeker and sought. But in Krishna the deepest truth is personal and unifying. He is not only the reality to be discovered; he is the intelligence by which discovery becomes possible. Thus devotion is not a departure from knowledge. Properly understood, devotion carries knowledge toward its fulfillment.

The verse also says Krishna is beyond the gunas, the changing qualities of material nature, even while pervading the whole manifestation. This protects against confusion. The Lord is present everywhere, yet not trapped in the fluctuations that govern embodied existence. He is immanent without becoming bound.

For readers, the verse invites a larger vision of spiritual life. We do not merely seek information about God. We seek the One who grounds knowing itself, shelters the cosmos, and remains free beyond all material change. Arjuna's praise is expansive because the reality it praises is total.

original_script:
वायुर्यमोऽग्निर्वरुणः शशाङ्कः
प्रजापतिस्त्वं प्रपितामहश्च।
नमो नमस्तेऽस्तु सहस्रकृत्वः
पुनश्च भूयोऽपि नमो नमस्ते।।11.39।।
transliteration:
vāyur yamo ’gnir varuṇaḥ śhaśhāṅkaḥ
prajāpatis tvaṁ prapitāmahaśh cha
namo namas te ’stu sahasra-kṛitvaḥ
punaśh cha bhūyo ’pi namo namas te
word_meanings: vāyuḥ—air; yamaḥ—the controller; agniḥ—fire; varuṇaḥ—water; śhaśha-aṅkaḥ—the moon; prajāpatiḥ—Brahma; tvam—You; prapitāmahaḥ—the great-grandfather; cha—also; namaḥ—My respects; namaḥ—again my respects; te—unto You; astu—let there be; sahasra-kṛitvaḥ—a thousand times; punaḥ cha—and again; bhūyaḥ—again; api—also; namaḥ—offering respects; namaḥ te—offering respects unto You
translation_english: You are air, and You are the supreme controller! You are fire, You are water, and You are the moon! You are Brahma, the first living creature, and You are the great-grandfather. I therefore offer my respectful obeisances unto You a thousand times, and again and yet again!
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now names Krishna as air, fire, water, moon, Brahma, and the great-grandfather, offering repeated obeisance. The point is not to collapse all distinctions crudely, but to confess that the powers by which the cosmos functions are rooted in and expressive of the one Lord. Krishna is present at the source of every element and governing principle.

This verse shows how the universal form transforms religious perception. Once Krishna has been seen as the all-containing reality, the world can no longer be approached as a disconnected collection of forces. Breath, flame, water, growth, cyclical order, and creative intelligence all become transparent to their divine ground. The many no longer conceal the One.

The repeated obeisance is also fitting. Arjuna has moved beyond isolated praise into a rhythm of surrender. When divine greatness is recognized across multiple aspects of reality, reverence multiplies with it. Worship becomes recurrent because the reasons for worship appear everywhere.

For readers, the verse suggests a contemplative discipline. One can begin to see the Lord's reflected greatness in the sustaining forces of life without mistaking those forces for ultimate realities in themselves. Creation becomes legible as sign, dependence, and gift.

original_script:
नमः पुरस्तादथ पृष्ठतस्ते
नमोऽस्तु ते सर्वत एव सर्व।
अनन्तवीर्यामितविक्रमस्त्वं
सर्वं समाप्नोषि ततोऽसि सर्वः।।11.40।।
transliteration:
namaḥ purastād atha pṛiṣhṭhatas te
namo ’stu te sarvata eva sarva
ananta-vīryāmita-vikramas tvaṁ
sarvaṁ samāpnoṣhi tato ’si sarvaḥ
word_meanings: namaḥ—offering obeisances; purastāt—from the front; atha—also; pṛiṣhṭhataḥ—from behind; te—unto You; namaḥ astu—I offer my respects; te—unto You; sarvataḥ—from all sides; eva—indeed; sarva—because You are everything; ananta-vīrya—unlimited potency; amita-vikramaḥ—and unlimited force; tvam—You; sarvam—everything; samāpnoṣhi—You cover; tataḥ—therefore; asi—You are; sarvaḥ—everything
translation_english: Obeisances to You from the front, from behind and from all sides! O unbounded power, You are the master of limitless might! You are all-pervading, and thus You are everything!
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna offers obeisance to Krishna from the front, from behind, and from every side, declaring him all-pervading and possessed of limitless power. The language of direction is important. In ordinary life, we orient ourselves in relation to objects outside us. But before the universal form, every direction leads back to the same divine presence.

This means that worship can no longer be restricted to a single facing. There is no safe backside from which one may stand outside the Lord's reality. Krishna surrounds, sustains, and penetrates all space. Arjuna's prayer adapts to that truth by becoming spatially total. Reverence is offered from every conceivable angle because every angle is already within God.

The verse also speaks of unbounded might. Krishna's power is not one force among competitors. It is the unlimited basis upon which all subordinate powers depend. This is why Arjuna can say, in effect, that Krishna is all in the sense that all things exist by pervasion, support, and relation to him.

For readers, the verse teaches that devotion must widen beyond compartmentalization. If God is truly all-pervading, then worship cannot be confined to a protected corner of life. Every direction, every setting, and every circumstance falls under the claim of reverence.

original_script:
सखेति मत्वा प्रसभं यदुक्तं
हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखेति।
अजानता महिमानं तवेदं
मया प्रमादात्प्रणयेन वापि।।11.41।।
यच्चावहासार्थमसत्कृतोऽसि
विहारशय्यासनभोजनेषु।
एकोऽथवाप्यच्युत तत्समक्षं
तत्क्षामये त्वामहमप्रमेयम्।।11.42।।
transliteration:
sakheti matvā prasabhaṁ yad uktaṁ
he kṛiṣhṇa he yādava he sakheti
ajānatā mahimānaṁ tavedaṁ
mayā pramādāt praṇayena vāpi
yach chāvahāsārtham asat-kṛito ’si
vihāra-śhayyāsana-bhojaneṣhu
eko ’thavāpy achyuta tat-samakṣhaṁ
tat kṣhāmaye tvām aham aprameyam
word_meanings: sakhā—friend; iti—thus; matvā—thinking; prasabham—presumptuously; yat—whatever; uktam—said; he kṛiṣhṇa—O Krishna; he yādava—O Yadava; he sakhe—O my dear friend; iti—thus; ajānatā—without knowing; mahimānam—glories; tava—Your; idam—this; mayā—by me; pramādāt—out of foolishness; praṇayena—out of love; vā api—either; yat—whatever; cha—also; avahāsa-artham—for joking; asat-kṛitaḥ—dishonored; asi—You have been; vihāra—in relaxation; śhayyā—in lying down; āsana—in sitting; bhojaneṣhu—or while eating together; ekaḥ—alone; athavā—or; api—also; achyuta—O infallible one; tat-samakṣham—in front of others; tat—all those; kṣhāmaye—ask forgiveness; tvām—from You; aham—I; aprameyam—immeasurable
translation_english: Thinking of You as my friend, I have rashly addressed You 'O Krishna,' 'O Yadava,' 'O my friend,' not knowing Your glories. Please forgive whatever I may have done in madness or in love. I have dishonored You many times, joking as we relaxed, lay on the same bed, or sat or ate together, sometimes alone and sometimes in front of many friends. O infallible one, please excuse me for all those offenses.
deep_dive_text:
Having praised Krishna's greatness, Arjuna turns to repentance. Remembering the many times he addressed Krishna casually as "friend," joked with him, rested with him, ate with him, and spoke without awareness of his full glory, he asks forgiveness. This is one of the most human moments in the chapter. Awe has led him not only to praise, but to self-revision.

The verse does not condemn friendship with God. Arjuna's closeness to Krishna is real and precious. But intimacy without reverence can become careless. Now that Krishna's majesty has been unveiled, Arjuna sees that even love may contain a kind of ignorance if it forgets whom it loves. His apology is therefore not a rejection of friendship, but a purification of it.

There is also tenderness here. Arjuna admits that some familiarity came from affection, not contempt. That distinction matters. Love can speak freely, laugh freely, and move without ceremony. Yet when divine greatness is recognized more fully, love itself desires to become more fitting. It wants to honor what it has discovered.

For readers, the verse offers a subtle lesson. Spiritual intimacy should never harden into irreverence, but reverence need not extinguish nearness. The deepest relationship to God is not cold distance or casual equality. It is loving closeness purified by wonder.

original_script:
पितासि लोकस्य चराचरस्य
त्वमस्य पूज्यश्च गुरुर्गरीयान्।
न त्वत्समोऽस्त्यभ्यधिकः कुतोऽन्यो
लोकत्रयेऽप्यप्रतिमप्रभाव।।11.43।।
transliteration:
pitāsi lokasya charācharasya
tvam asya pūjyaśh cha gurur garīyān
na tvat-samo ’sty abhyadhikaḥ kuto ’nyo
loka-traye ’py apratima-prabhāva
word_meanings: pitā—the father; asi—You are; lokasya—of the entire world; chara-acharasya—of the moving and nonmoving; tvam—You; asya—of this; pūjyaḥ—worshipable; cha—also; guruḥ—master; garīyān—glorious; na—never; tvat-samaḥ—equal to You; asti—there is; abhyadhikaḥ—greater; kutaḥ—how is it possible; anyaḥ—other; loka-traye—in the three planetary systems; api—also; apratima-prabhāva—O immeasurable power
translation_english: You are the father of this complete cosmic manifestation, of the moving and the nonmoving. You are its worshipable chief, the supreme spiritual master. No one is equal to You, nor can anyone be one with You. How then could there be anyone greater than You within the three worlds, O Lord of immeasurable power?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now declares Krishna to be the father of the whole moving and unmoving universe, its most worshipful lord, and the supreme teacher, with none equal or greater in the three worlds. The verse intensifies the theme of divine uniqueness. Krishna is not merely higher than others by degree. He stands in a relation to all beings that no one else shares.

Calling Krishna father of all that moves and does not move means that every level of existence, from conscious life to apparently inert matter, depends on him. Calling him the supreme guru means that all true wisdom ultimately originates in him. These two titles together present Krishna as both ontological source and spiritual authority.

The denial of any equal or superior is especially important in the chapter's context. After seeing the entire hierarchy of beings gathered within the universal form, Arjuna can no longer think in terms of rival ultimates. There is no second center. All greatness is derivative; Krishna alone is absolute.

For readers, this verse can clarify devotion by removing vagueness. If the Lord is truly the source, teacher, and unsurpassable sovereign of all, then spiritual life cannot remain half-committed. Arjuna's words call for a form of reverence shaped by the uniqueness of the One addressed.

original_script:
तस्मात्प्रणम्य प्रणिधाय कायं
प्रसादये त्वामहमीशमीड्यम्।
पितेव पुत्रस्य सखेव सख्युः
प्रियः प्रियायार्हसि देव सोढुम्।।11.44।।
transliteration:
tasmāt praṇamya praṇidhāya kāyaṁ
prasādaye tvām aham īśham īḍyam
piteva putrasya sakheva sakhyuḥ
priyaḥ priyāyārhasi deva soḍhum
word_meanings: tasmāt—therefore; praṇamya—offering obeisances; praṇidhāya—laying down; kāyam—the body; prasādaye—to beg mercy; tvām—unto You; aham—I; īśham—unto the Supreme Lord; īḍyam—worshipable; pitā iva—like a father; putrasya—with a son; sakhā iva—like a friend; sakhyuḥ—with a friend; priyaḥ—a lover; priyāyāḥ—with the dearmost; arhasi—You should; deva—O Lord; soḍhum—tolerate
translation_english: You are the Supreme Lord, to be worshiped by every living being. Thus I fall down to offer You my respectful obeisances and ask Your mercy. As a father tolerates the impudence of his son, or a friend tolerates the impertinence of a friend, or a wife tolerates the familiarity of her partner, please tolerate the wrongs I may have done You.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna therefore falls down and asks for mercy, pleading that Krishna tolerate his offenses as a father tolerates a son, a friend a friend, or a beloved partner the other's familiarity. This is a remarkable verse because it holds together majesty and intimacy without confusion. Krishna is infinitely exalted, yet Arjuna still appeals through bonds of love.

The analogies are carefully chosen. A father bears immaturity, a friend bears frankness, and a beloved bears nearness. Arjuna is not trying to excuse himself cheaply. He is trusting the generosity already present in relationship. Repentance is possible because he believes Krishna's greatness is not brittle. The Lord's superiority does not make him inaccessible to heartfelt appeal.

The verse also shows that humility is not self-hatred. Arjuna does not dissolve into despair over his past familiarity. He acknowledges it, bows, and asks for grace. That movement is spiritually healthy. Genuine remorse opens toward mercy rather than toward paralysis.

For readers, the verse gives language for repentance that is both tender and serious. One may acknowledge fault before God without losing confidence in divine compassion. In fact, it is often trust in compassion that makes confession possible at all.

original_script:
अदृष्टपूर्वं हृषितोऽस्मि दृष्ट्वा
भयेन च प्रव्यथितं मनो मे।
तदेव मे दर्शय देव रूपं
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.45।।
transliteration:
adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvaṁ hṛiṣhito ’smi dṛiṣhṭvā
bhayena cha pravyathitaṁ mano me
tad eva me darśhaya deva rūpaṁ
prasīda deveśha jagan-nivāsa
word_meanings: adṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam—never seen before; hṛiṣhitaḥ—gladdened; asmi—I am; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; bhayena—out of fear; cha—and; pravyathitam—perturbed; manaḥ—mind; me—my; tat—that; eva—certainly; me—unto me; darśhaya—show; deva—O Lord; rūpam—the form; prasīda—just be gracious; deva-īśha—O Lord of lords; jagat-nivāsa—O refuge of the universe
translation_english: After seeing this universal form, which I have never seen before, I am gladdened, but at the same time my mind is disturbed with fear. Therefore please bestow Your grace upon me and reveal again Your form as the Personality of Godhead, O Lord of lords, O abode of the universe.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna says that although he is gladdened by seeing the unprecedented universal form, his mind is also shaken with fear, and he asks Krishna to reveal again the familiar form of the Lord. This admission is important. The cosmic vision has been true and holy, but it is not the form in which Arjuna can remain at rest.

The verse teaches that awe alone is not the final mode of devotion. The soul may need to see divine immensity, but it cannot live continuously in that overwhelming intensity. Relationship requires a form in which love can endure without collapse. Arjuna's request is therefore not a retreat from truth, but a request for truth in a mode he can actually inhabit.

There is also no contradiction in being both delighted and afraid. Great revelation can attract and unsettle at once. Arjuna's honesty preserves both sides. He does not dismiss the experience because it frightened him, nor does he pretend that fear is irrelevant because the experience was exalted.

For readers, the verse offers permission to desire nearness after transcendence. God may reveal himself in ways that shatter our scale, but the heart still longs for the form in which reverence can become enduring love, trust, and stable communion.

original_script:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रहस्त-
मिच्छामि त्वां द्रष्टुमर्हं तथैव।
तेनैव रूपेण चतुर्भुजेन
सहस्रबाहो भव विश्वमूर्ते।।11.46।।
transliteration:
kirīṭinaṁ gadinaṁ chakra-hastam
ichchhāmi tvāṁ draṣhṭum ahaṁ tathaiva
tenaiva rūpeṇa chatur-bhujena
sahasra-bāho bhava viśhva-mūrte
word_meanings: kirīṭinam—with helmet; gadinam—with club; chakra-hastam—disc in hand; ichchhāmi—I wish; tvām—You; draṣhṭum—to see; aham—I; tathā eva—in that position; tena eva—in that; rūpeṇa—form; chatuḥ-bhujena—four-handed; sahasra-bāho—O thousand-handed Lord; bhava—just become; viśhva-mūrte—O universal form
translation_english: O universal form, O thousand-armed Lord, I wish to see You in Your four-armed form, with helmeted head and with club, wheel, conch and lotus in Your hands. I long to see You in that form.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna specifically asks to see Krishna in his four-armed form, bearing conch, discus, mace, and lotus. This request is significant because it seeks a form that is unmistakably divine yet relationally bearable. Arjuna is moving from the ungraspable totality of the universal form toward a clearer personal manifestation.

The four-armed form serves as a kind of sacred mediation. It preserves divine majesty through its symbols of sovereignty, power, protection, and grace, but it does so in a way the devotee can contemplate without being shattered. Unlike the universal form, it is not a revelation of everything at once. It is revelation shaped for devotion.

This shows that not every divine disclosure serves the same purpose. The universal form was necessary to correct Arjuna's perspective and reveal Krishna's cosmic lordship. The four-armed form answers a different need: the restoration of reverent composure and the continuation of personal relation.

For readers, the verse teaches that the highest truth is not always given in the most overwhelming mode. God also reveals himself in forms suited to worship, steadiness, and love. The request itself is not lesser spirituality. It is wisdom about what the human heart can faithfully hold.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मया प्रसन्नेन तवार्जुनेदं
रूपं परं दर्शितमात्मयोगात्।
तेजोमयं विश्वमनन्तमाद्यं
यन्मे त्वदन्येन न दृष्टपूर्वम्।।11.47।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
mayā prasannena tavārjunedaṁ
rūpaṁ paraṁ darśhitam ātma-yogāt
tejomayaṁ viśhvam anantam ādyaṁ
yan me tvad-anyena na dṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; mayā—by Me; prasannena—happily; tava—unto you; arjuna—O Arjuna; idam—this; rūpam—form; param—transcendental; darśhitam—shown; ātma-yogāt—by My internal mystic power; tejaḥ-mayam—full of effulgence; viśhvam—the entire universe; anantam—unlimited; ādyam—original; yat—which; me—My; tvat anyena—besides you; na dṛiṣhṭa-pūrvam—no one has previously seen
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, happily have I shown you, by My internal mystic power, this supreme universal form within the material world. No one before you has ever seen this primal form, unlimited and full of glaring effulgence.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna answers that he has shown this supreme universal form through his own internal power, and that no one before Arjuna has seen it. The emphasis falls squarely on divine initiative. The vision is not a reward produced by technique. It is a gift granted by the Lord for a specific purpose.

This matters because extraordinary spiritual experience can easily feed pride. Krishna cuts that off at the root. Arjuna did not attain this sight by personal greatness. He received it because Krishna chose to reveal it. Grace, not achievement, governs the deepest disclosures.

The uniqueness of the vision also highlights the seriousness of Arjuna's calling. He is not being entertained. He is being prepared. The universal form has been shown to reframe his duty, expose the divine scale of the coming battle, and establish Krishna's supreme status beyond all remaining doubt.

For readers, the verse is a warning against treating revelation as spiritual property. No one owns access to God by merit. What is given is given by divine freedom. The proper response is gratitude, humility, and readiness to receive the responsibility that often accompanies the gift.

original_script:
न वेदयज्ञाध्ययनैर्न दानैर्-
न च क्रियाभिर्न तपोभिरुग्रैः।
एवंरूपः शक्य अहं नृलोके
द्रष्टुं त्वदन्येन कुरुप्रवीर।।11.48।।
transliteration:
na veda-yajñādhyayanair na dānair
na cha kriyābhir na tapobhir ugraiḥ
evaṁ-rūpaḥ śhakya ahaṁ nṛi-loke
draṣhṭuṁ tvad-anyena kuru-pravīra
word_meanings: na—never; veda-yajña—by sacrifice; adhyayanaiḥ—or Vedic study; na—never; dānaiḥ—by charity; na—never; cha—also; kriyābhiḥ—by pious activities; na—never; tapobhiḥ—by serious penances; ugraiḥ—severe; evam-rūpaḥ—in this form; śhakyaḥ—can; aham—I; nṛi-loke—in this material world; draṣhṭum—be seen; tvat—than you; anyena—by another; kuru-pravīra—O best among the Kuru warriors
translation_english: O best of the Kuru warriors, no one before you has ever seen this universal form of Mine, for neither by studying the Vedas, nor by performing sacrifices, nor by charity, nor by pious activities, nor by severe penances can I be seen in this form in the material world.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna goes on to say that this universal form cannot be seen in the material world through Vedic study, sacrifice, charity, ritual worship, or severe austerity. This is a sharp and necessary correction. All of those practices have value, but none can compel the kind of vision Arjuna has been granted.

The verse does not reject disciplined religion. Rather, it puts discipline in its proper place. Sacred learning, sacrifice, generosity, and ascetic effort can purify, train, and prepare a person. But they do not place God under obligation. Revelation remains free. The Lord cannot be forced into visibility by accumulated merit.

This preserves the personal nature of the divine-human relation. Krishna is not an impersonal mechanism that yields identical results whenever the correct spiritual inputs are arranged. He is the supreme Person who gives himself as he wills. Arjuna's vision therefore teaches dependence as much as privilege.

For readers, the verse offers a needed safeguard. Religious effort can become subtle bargaining if one is not careful. Krishna reminds us that the greatest encounter is received, not purchased. Practices matter, but grace remains decisive.

original_script:
मा ते व्यथा मा च विमूढभावो
दृष्ट्वा रूपं घोरमीदृङ्ममेदम्।
व्यपेतभीः प्रीतमनाः पुनस्त्वं
तदेव मे रूपमिदं प्रपश्य।।11.49।।
transliteration:
mā te vyathā mā cha vimūḍha-bhāvo
dṛiṣhṭvā rūpaṁ ghoram īdṛiṅ mamedam
vyapeta-bhīḥ prīta-manāḥ punas tvaṁ
tad eva me rūpam idaṁ prapaśhya
word_meanings: mā—let it not be; te—unto you; vyathā—trouble; mā—let it not be; cha—also; vimūḍha-bhāvaḥ—bewilderment; dṛiṣhṭvā—by seeing; rūpam—this form; ghoram—horrible; īdṛik—as it is; mama—My; idam—this; vyapeta-bhīḥ—free from all fear; prīta-manāḥ—pleased in mind; punaḥ—again; tvam—you; tat—that; eva—thus; me—My; rūpam—form; idam—this; prapaśhya—just see
translation_english: You have been perturbed and bewildered by seeing this horrible feature of Mine. Now let it be finished. My devotee, be free again from all disturbances. With a peaceful mind you can now see the form you desire.
deep_dive_text:
Seeing Arjuna's distress, Krishna tells him not to be troubled or bewildered by the terrible form, and invites him to behold the desired form with a peaceful mind. This verse reveals the compassion within divine revelation. Krishna does not leave Arjuna stranded in fear. The same Lord who overwhelms also consoles and restores.

That movement is spiritually significant. Revelation is not meant to destroy the devotee, but to free him from illusion. Once the necessary truth has been shown, Krishna himself provides the transition back to steadiness. The terrible aspect had its purpose. It is not the final dwelling place of the relationship.

The instruction to regain peace of mind also shows that inner composure is not achieved by denial. Arjuna is not told to pretend the vision was harmless. He is told to look again, now at the form suited to his restored devotion. Peace comes through God's gracious self-adjustment to the need of the devotee.

For readers, the verse is deeply consoling. God may shake what is false in us, but he does not delight in leaving the soul in ruin. Divine severity serves truth, and divine tenderness leads the soul back into a peace rooted in that truth.

original_script:
सञ्जय उवाच
इत्यर्जुनं वासुदेवस्तथोक्त्वा
स्वकं रूपं दर्शयामास भूयः।
आश्वासयामास च भीतमेनं
भूत्वा पुनः सौम्यवपुर्महात्मा।।11.50।।
transliteration:
sañjaya uvācha
ity arjunaṁ vāsudevas tathoktvā
svakaṁ rūpaṁ darśhayām āsa bhūyaḥ
āśhvāsayām āsa cha bhītam enaṁ
bhūtvā punaḥ saumya-vapur mahātmā
word_meanings: sañjayaḥ uvācha—Sanjaya said; iti—thus; arjunam—unto Arjuna; vāsudevaḥ—Krishna; tathā—in that way; uktvā—speaking; svakam—His own; rūpam—form; darśhayām āsa—showed; bhūyaḥ—again; āśhvāsayām āsa—encouraged; cha—also; bhītam—fearful; enam—him; bhūtvā—becoming; punaḥ—again; saumya-vapuḥ—the beautiful form; mahā-ātmā—the great one
translation_english: Sanjaya said to Dhritarashtra: The Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna, having spoken thus to Arjuna, displayed His real four-armed form and at last showed His two-armed form, thus encouraging the fearful Arjuna.
deep_dive_text:
Sanjaya narrates that Krishna then showed Arjuna his four-armed form and finally his two-armed human form, thereby encouraging him. The sequence matters. Arjuna is led from cosmic immensity to divine icon and then to the familiar human presence of Krishna. Each manifestation has a purpose, and the movement itself is pastoral.

The four-armed form bridges the transition. It retains unmistakable divine symbolism while removing the unbearable totality of the universal vision. The final return to the two-armed form reveals something profound: the approachable Krishna who drives the chariot is not less divine than the cosmic Lord. He is that very Lord in intimate nearness.

This is one of the chapter's great theological gifts. The personal form is not a concession hiding the real God. It is a higher mercy in which the real God becomes lovingly accessible. Arjuna is encouraged not merely because the terrifying vision ends, but because the supreme reality stands before him again in a form he can trust and love directly.

For readers, the verse invites deep appreciation for divine accessibility. The Lord who contains all worlds does not remain locked in overwhelming grandeur. He comes near in forms through which the heart can be steadied, instructed, and drawn into devotion.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
दृष्ट्वेदं मानुषं रूपं तव सौम्यं जनार्दन।
इदानीमस्मि संवृत्तः सचेताः प्रकृतिं गतः।।11.51।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
dṛiṣhṭvedaṁ mānuṣhaṁ rūpaṁ tava saumyaṁ janārdana
idānīm asmi saṁvṛittaḥ sa-chetāḥ prakṛitiṁ gataḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; dṛiṣhṭvā—seeing; idam—this; mānuṣham—human; rūpam—form; tava—Your; saumyam—very beautiful; janārdana—O chastiser of the enemies; idānīm—now; asmi—I am; saṁvṛittaḥ—settled; sa-chetāḥ—in my consciousness; prakṛitim—to my own nature; gataḥ—returned
translation_english: When Arjuna thus saw Krishna in His original form, he said: O Janardana, seeing this humanlike form, so very beautiful, I am now composed in mind, and I am restored to my original nature.
deep_dive_text:
Seeing Krishna again in his beautiful humanlike form, Arjuna says he is restored to composure and returned to his natural state. This response shows how important the personal form is within the chapter. After beholding cosmic vastness, Arjuna finds peace not in abstraction, but in the gracious nearness of Krishna as beloved Lord.

The verse does not mean that the earlier vision was false or unnecessary. Rather, it means that human devotion requires more than metaphysical shock. The universal form corrected Arjuna's understanding. Krishna's personal form heals and settles him. Truth and tenderness meet here.

There is also a new depth in Arjuna's return to normalcy. He is not simply back where he started. The "humanlike" Krishna he now sees is no longer merely familiar. Arjuna has learned that this approachable form belongs to the One who pervades and governs the cosmos. Familiarity has been transfigured by revelation.

For readers, the verse teaches that spiritual maturity does not end in permanent overwhelm. God may enlarge us through awe, but he also restores us through beauty and personal presence. Peace becomes deeper when it is peace after vision, not peace before it.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
सुदुर्दर्शमिदं रूपं दृष्टवानसि यन्मम।
देवा अप्यस्य रूपस्य नित्यं दर्शनकाङ्क्षिणः।।11.52।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
su-durdarśham idaṁ rūpaṁ dṛiṣhṭavān asi yan mama
devā apy asya rūpasya nityaṁ darśhana-kāṅkṣhiṇaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; su-durdarśham—very difficult to see; idam—this; rūpam—form; dṛiṣhṭavān asi—as you have seen; yat—which; mama—of Mine; devāḥ—the demigods; api—also; asya—this; rūpasya—form; nityam—eternally; darśhana-kāṅkṣhiṇaḥ—aspiring to see
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, this form of Mine which you are now seeing is very difficult to behold. Even the demigods are ever seeking the opportunity to see this form, which is so dear.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna tells Arjuna that the form now before him is very difficult to behold, and that even the gods are ever eager to see it. This is a striking reversal of expectation. One might assume that the universal form would be the highest rarity, yet Krishna now stresses the preciousness of his personal form.

The verse suggests that intimacy with the Divine is not lower than cosmic spectacle. In some sense it is dearer. The gods may witness grandeur, but the personal form in which the Lord becomes directly lovable and relationally present is a special grace. Arjuna is being taught to value what had become ordinary to him.

This also prepares the transition from vision to devotion. The goal of the chapter is not to leave Arjuna attached to extraordinary displays. It is to deepen his recognition of who Krishna truly is. The most accessible form can be the most spiritually precious precisely because it unites majesty with nearness.
