For readers, the verse offers an important correction. We often chase the dramatic and overlook the holy form already given for relationship. Krishna teaches that what is quietly available in love may be rarer and more desirable than what merely overwhelms the senses.

original_script:
नाहं वेदैर्न तपसा न दानेन न चेज्यया।
शक्य एवंविधो द्रष्टुं दृष्टवानसि मां यथा।।11.53।।
transliteration:
nāhaṁ vedair na tapasā na dānena na chejyayā
śhakya evaṁ-vidho draṣhṭuṁ dṛiṣhṭavān asi māṁ yathā
word_meanings: na—never; aham—I; vedaiḥ—by study of the Vedas; na—never; tapasā—by serious penances; na—never; dānena—by charity; na—never; cha—also; ijyayā—by worship; śhakyaḥ—it is possible; evam-vidhaḥ—like this; draṣhṭum—to see; dṛiṣhṭavān—seeing; asi—you are; mām—Me; yathā—as
translation_english: The form you are seeing with your transcendental eyes cannot be understood simply by studying the Vedas, nor by undergoing serious penances, nor by charity, nor by worship. It is not by these means that one can see Me as I am.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna repeats that he cannot be known in this true form merely through Vedic study, austerity, charity, or ritual worship. The repetition matters. The lesson is central enough to be pressed again from a slightly different angle. Religious means, however noble, do not by themselves penetrate to the Lord as he is.

This is not an attack on scripture or practice. It is a warning against mistaking means for fulfillment. Study can inform the mind. Austerity can strengthen resolve. Charity can cleanse possessiveness. Worship can discipline attention. Yet without the right inner relation to Krishna, these remain incomplete routes. They can circle the threshold without entering the heart of the matter.

The verse also exposes spiritual self-reliance. We prefer methods that allow us to measure progress and claim accomplishment. Krishna insists that seeing him truly exceeds that calculus. The decisive factor is not human accumulation, but divine self-disclosure received in the right spirit.

For readers, this verse can bring both humility and relief. Humility, because effort is not sovereign. Relief, because the way to God is not reserved for spiritual performers. Something deeper than achievement is being asked.

original_script:
भक्त्या त्वनन्यया शक्य अहमेवंविधोऽर्जुन।
ज्ञातुं द्रष्टुं च तत्त्वेन प्रवेष्टुं च परन्तप।।11.54।।
transliteration:
bhaktyā tv ananyayā śhakya aham evaṁ-vidho ’rjuna
jñātuṁ draṣhṭuṁ cha tattvena praveṣhṭuṁ cha parantapa
word_meanings: bhaktyā—by devotional service; tu—but; ananyayā—without being mixed with fruitive activities or empirical speculation; śhakyaḥ—possible; aham—I; evam-vidhaḥ—like this; arjuna—O Arjuna; jñātum—to be known; draṣhṭum—to be seen; cha—and; tattvena—in fact; praveṣhṭum—to enter into; cha—also; parantapa—O mighty-armed one
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, only by undivided devotional service can I be understood as I am, standing before you, and can thus be seen directly. Only in this way can you enter into the mysteries of My understanding.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now states the positive key: only by undivided devotion can he be known as he truly is, seen directly, and entered into. This verse is the chapter's theological center of gravity. After awe, terror, repentance, and restored peace, the final explanation is devotion.

Undivided devotion means more than emotion. It means a heart no longer split among rival absolutes. Krishna can be known truly when he is not treated as one value among many, one aid among many, or one symbol among many. Exclusive devotion gathers the person into coherence before the One who is himself the center of all coherence.

The verse is also remarkable for joining knowing, seeing, and entering. Bhakti is not presented as anti-intellectual. It is the path by which knowledge becomes real, vision becomes possible, and participation begins. Devotion is therefore epistemic, existential, and relational at once.

For readers, the teaching is decisive. The chapter does not end by directing us to seek more overwhelming visions. It directs us to wholehearted devotion. The deepest access to God is not gained by spectacle, but by a heart wholly turned toward him.

original_script:
मत्कर्मकृन्मत्परमो मद्भक्तः सङ्गवर्जितः।
निर्वैरः सर्वभूतेषु यः स मामेति पाण्डव।।11.55।।
transliteration:
mat-karma-kṛin mat-paramo mad-bhaktaḥ saṅga-varjitaḥ
nirvairaḥ sarva-bhūteṣhu yaḥ sa mām eti pāṇḍava
word_meanings: mat-karma-kṛit—engaged in doing My work; mat-paramaḥ—considering Me the supreme; mat-bhaktaḥ—engaged in My devotional service; saṅga-varjitaḥ—freed from the contamination of fruitive activities and mental speculation; nirvairaḥ—without an enemy; sarva-bhūteṣhu—among all living entities; yaḥ—one who; saḥ—he; mām—unto Me; eti—comes; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, he who engages in My pure devotional service, free from the contaminations of fruitive activities and mental speculation, he who works for Me, who makes Me the supreme goal of his life, and who is friendly to every living being—he certainly comes to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter with a practical summary: the one who works for him, makes him the highest goal, is devoted to him, free from self-centered attachment, and friendly toward all beings comes to him. After the extraordinary vision of this chapter, the conclusion is strikingly livable. The path back to the Supreme is not hidden in rare experiences, but in transformed orientation.

Each phrase matters. To work for Krishna is to let action become offering. To make him the supreme goal is to order life around a final center. To be free from selfish attachment is to loosen the ego's demand to possess outcomes. To be without hostility toward any being is to let devotion reshape ethics. The chapter ends by turning metaphysical revelation into daily discipleship.

This verse also shows why Chapter 11 belongs within the larger argument of the Gita. The universal form was not given to create fascination with the spectacular. It was given to confirm who Krishna is, so that Arjuna might live and act in faithful alignment with him. Devotion is validated by vision, but fulfilled in conduct.

For readers, the closing instruction is wonderfully direct. If we want to come to the Lord, we must let work, desire, attachment, and relationship be reordered around him. The chapter's grandeur finally presses toward a simple demand: belong wholly to Krishna, and let that belonging govern how you live.

original_script: विश्वरूपदर्शनयोगः
transliteration: Viśhvarūpa Darśhana Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form
deep_dive_text: Chapter 11 is the awe-inspiring climax of the Bhagavad Gita's visual theology. Arjuna receives 'divine eyes' and witnesses the staggering, terrifying Vishvarupa—the Universal Form where all of space, time, gods, demons, and the inevitable destruction of the cosmic order are contained within Krishna. Overwhelmed and terrified by this blazing, all-devouring form of Time, Arjuna begs Krishna to return to His familiar, gentle shape. Krishna obliges, concluding with the ultimate lesson: the Infinite cannot be purchased by intelligence or austerity; it can only be realized through pure, undivided love.

original_script: अथ द्वादशोऽध्यायः - भक्तियोगः
transliteration: Bhakti Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Bhakti—devotion/participation; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Devotion
deep_dive_text: This chapter contrasts devotion to the personal Divine with meditation on the unmanifest Absolute. Krishna explains why devotion with loving attention is especially accessible for embodied beings, and He concludes by describing the qualities of a devotee whose life is marked by humility, steadiness, compassion, and inner freedom.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
एवं सततयुक्ता ये भक्तास्त्वां पर्युपासते।
ये चाप्यक्षरमव्यक्तं तेषां के योगवित्तमाः।।12.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
evaṁ satata-yuktā ye bhaktās tvāṁ paryupāsate
ye chāpy akṣharam avyaktaṁ teṣhāṁ ke yoga-vittamāḥ
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; evam—thus; satata-yuktāḥ—always engaged; ye—who; bhaktāḥ—devotees; tvām—You; paryupāsate—worship; ye—who; cha—also; api—certainly; akṣharam—the imperishable; avyaktam—the unmanifested; teṣhām—of them; ke—who; yoga-vit-tamāḥ—the most perfect in knowledge of yoga
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: Which are considered to be more perfect, those who are always properly engaged in Your devotional service or those who worship the unmanifested, the imperishable?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna opens this chapter with a practical spiritual question. After hearing of Krishna's greatness in personal and universal terms, he asks which path is better: devotion to the personal Lord or contemplation of the unmanifest, imperishable absolute. This is not a random theoretical question. It grows naturally out of what the Gita has already revealed. Krishna has spoken of the supreme as both beyond ordinary perception and personally present. Arjuna now wants clarity about how a seeker should actually approach that reality.

The question matters because these two approaches can seem very different in lived experience. One turns toward a knowable Lord through love, worship, remembrance, and surrender. The other seeks what is formless, unseen, and beyond sensory grasp. Arjuna is asking not which description is valid in a narrow philosophical sense, but which discipline more perfectly leads a human being to the goal.

There is also pastoral urgency here. A battlefield is not a monastery, and Arjuna is not a detached metaphysician. He needs a path that can be lived. His question therefore represents the reader's question too: when the highest truth can be spoken in more than one way, how is one to proceed with steadiness?

For a new reader, the verse is reassuring. The Gita does not fear comparison or honest inquiry. Arjuna is allowed to ask which spiritual orientation is most suitable, and Krishna's answer will not stay abstract. It will move toward what the embodied human person can actually practice.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मय्यावेश्य मनो ये मां नित्ययुक्ता उपासते।
श्रद्धया परयोपेतास्ते मे युक्ततमा मताः।।12.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
mayy āveśhya mano ye māṁ nitya-yuktā upāsate
śhraddhayā parayopetās te me yukta-tamā matāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; mayi—upon Me; āveśhya—fixing; manaḥ—the mind; ye—who; mām—unto Me; nitya-yuktāḥ—always engaged; upāsate—worship; śhraddhayā—with faith; parayā—transcendental; upetāḥ—endowed; te—they; me—by Me; yukta-tamāḥ—most perfect in yoga; matāḥ—are considered
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Those who fix their minds on My personal form and are always engaged in worshiping Me with great and transcendental faith are considered by Me to be most perfect.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna answers directly: those who fix their minds on his personal form and worship him with steady devotion and deep faith are considered by him to be most perfect. The clarity of the answer matters. Krishna does not dismiss devotion as a lesser aid for simpler minds. He identifies it as the most complete path.

The reason becomes clearer when we consider what devotion involves. To fix the mind on the personal Lord is not merely to prefer an image over an abstraction. It is to enter into relationship with the very source of reality through love, trust, remembrance, surrender, and obedience. Faith here is not blind sentiment. It is the steady placing of oneself in relation to Krishna as the supreme center.

This verse also shows that the highest path is not the coldest or most conceptually stripped down. Personal devotion engages the whole person. Mind, emotion, will, memory, conduct, and aspiration are all drawn toward one living object of love. That integrative power is one reason bhakti is praised so highly in this chapter.

For readers, the verse offers an important correction. Spiritual depth does not require abandoning the personal dimension of God in favor of something more remote. Krishna teaches that loving attention to him in his personal reality is not a concession. It is a fuller perfection because it gathers the human being more completely into union with the Divine.

original_script:
ये त्वक्षरमनिर्देश्यमव्यक्तं पर्युपासते।
सर्वत्रगमचिन्त्यं च कूटस्थमचलं ध्रुवम्।।12.3।।
transliteration:
ye tv akṣharam anirdeśhyam avyaktaṁ paryupāsate
sarvatra-gam achintyaṁ cha kūṭa-stham achalaṁ dhruvam
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; akṣharam—the imperishable; anirdeśhyam—the indefinable; avyaktam—the unmanifested; paryupāsate—completely worship; sarvatra-gam—all-pervading; achintyam—inconceivable; cha—also; kūṭa-stham—unchanging; achalam—immovable; dhruvam—fixed
translation_english: But those who fully worship the unmanifested, that which lies beyond the perception of the senses, the all-pervading, inconceivable, unchanging, fixed and immovable—
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then acknowledges the other path: some worship the unmanifest, the imperishable, the all-pervading, inconceivable, unchanging, fixed, and immovable reality. He does not deny that this orientation is directed toward the same supreme truth. The terms he uses are serious and exalted. They name a reality beyond the shifting forms grasped by the senses.

This matters because the Gita is not simplistic. Krishna does not answer Arjuna by pretending there is no genuine metaphysical depth to the unmanifest approach. The absolute is indeed beyond ordinary sensory capture and beyond all changing conditions. There is therefore a real spiritual discipline in turning the mind toward that which cannot be pictured or handled.

At the same time, the verse's very language hints at the challenge. To worship what is inconceivable and unmanifest requires a high degree of interior abstraction and stability. The seeker must hold attention on that which gives no support to imagination, emotion, or ordinary relational instinct. That is not false, but it is severe.

For a reader, the verse teaches balance. Krishna's preference for devotion to his personal form does not come from denying the transcendence of the absolute. It comes from a sober judgment about how human beings most fruitfully approach that transcendence. The path of the unmanifest is real, but it asks the mind to dwell where ordinary faculties find very little to hold.

original_script:
सन्नियम्येन्द्रियग्रामं सर्वत्र समबुद्धयः।
ते प्राप्नुवन्ति मामेव सर्वभूतहिते रताः।।12.4।।
transliteration:
sanniyamyendriya-grāmaṁ sarvatra sama-buddhayaḥ
te prāpnuvanti mām eva sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ
word_meanings: sanniyamya—controlling; indriya-grāmam—all the senses; sarvatra—everywhere; sama-buddhayaḥ—equally disposed; te—they; prāpnuvanti—achieve; mām—Me; eva—certainly; sarva-bhūta-hite—for the welfare of all living entities; ratāḥ—engaged
translation_english: Such persons, by controlling the various senses and being equally disposed to everyone, and engaged in the welfare of all, at last achieve Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by saying that those who pursue the unmanifest through sense-control, equanimity, and concern for the welfare of all ultimately attain him as well. This is important because it prevents the chapter from becoming sectarian in a crude way. The goal is not different. Krishna is not speaking of two unrelated ultimates. He is distinguishing ways of approach.

The disciplines named here are demanding. Control of the senses, equal regard toward all, and dedication to universal welfare are not marginal virtues. They show that contemplation of the unmanifest cannot remain a merely intellectual exercise. If one seeks what is beyond all limited identity, one must also be loosened from egoic preference, agitation, and selfishness.

There is a quiet generosity in Krishna's teaching. He can praise one path as superior for embodied beings without denying the sincerity or eventual attainment of those who follow another. This keeps the Gita from collapsing into narrow rivalry. Truth is one, even when modes of access differ in difficulty and suitability.

For readers, the verse gives a useful principle: genuine spiritual paths converge ethically. Whatever one claims to contemplate, if it does not produce self-restraint, fairness, and care for others, something is missing. Krishna ties real attainment to transformed character, not to metaphysical talk alone.

original_script:
क्लेशोऽधिकतरस्तेषामव्यक्तासक्तचेतसाम्।
अव्यक्ता हि गतिर्दुःखं देहवद्भिरवाप्यते।।12.5।।
transliteration:
kleśho ’dhikataras teṣhām avyaktāsakta-chetasām
avyaktā hi gatir duḥkhaṁ dehavadbhir avāpyate
word_meanings: kleśhaḥ—trouble; adhikataraḥ—very much; teṣhām—of them; avyakta—to the unmanifested; āsakta—attached; chetasām—of those whose minds; avyaktā—the unmanifested; hi—certainly; gatiḥ—progress; duḥkham—with trouble; deha-vadbhiḥ—by the embodied; avāpyate—is achieved
translation_english: For those whose minds are attached to the unmanifested, impersonal feature of the Supreme, advancement is very troublesome. To make progress in that discipline is always difficult for those who are embodied.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now states the difficulty plainly: for embodied beings, progress toward the unmanifest is very troublesome. This verse is one of the chapter's practical hinges. The issue is not that the unmanifest is unreal or unworthy, but that human beings live through bodies, senses, relationships, images, memory, and affection. To orient all of that toward what offers no personal or perceptible support is extremely hard.

The phrase "for those who are embodied" is crucial. Krishna is judging the path in relation to our present condition. We do not begin as pure abstractions. We begin as creatures whose minds are restless, senses active, emotions relational, and imaginations form-seeking. A path that asks such beings to remain steadily attached to the unmanifest places a severe strain on human nature.

This is why bhakti is not merely easier in a lazy sense. It is more fitting. The personal Lord receives the whole person where the person actually is and draws those faculties upward rather than asking them all to fall silent at once. Love, memory, worship, and offering become bridges instead of obstacles.

For readers, the verse is deeply realistic. Spiritual guidance must account for the structure of human life, not only for philosophical elegance. Krishna's preference for devotion is grounded in compassion for embodied seekers and in a lucid understanding of how transformation ordinarily happens.

original_script:
ये तु सर्वाणि कर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्पराः।
अनन्येनैव योगेन मां ध्यायन्त उपासते।।12.6।।
transliteration:
ye tu sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi sannyasya mat-parāḥ
ananyenaiva yogena māṁ dhyāyanta upāsate
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; sarvāṇi—all; karmāṇi—activities; mayi—unto Me; sannyasya—giving up; mat-parāḥ—being attached to Me; ananyena—without division; eva—certainly; yogena—by practice of yoga; mām—upon Me; dhyāyantaḥ—meditating; upāsate—worship
translation_english: But those who worship Me, giving up all their activities unto Me and being devoted to Me without deviation, engaged in devotional service and always meditating upon Me—
deep_dive_text:
Krishna turns back to the devotional path and describes those who offer all actions to him, remain devoted without deviation, and meditate on him steadily. This verse begins a beautiful movement in the chapter. Bhakti is not presented as vague religious feeling. It has structure. One offers action, centers devotion, and sustains remembrance.

To give all activities to Krishna means more than attaching a pious phrase to ordinary conduct. It means that the doer, the aim, and the meaning of work are reoriented. Action is no longer carried out as a private project of egoic acquisition. It becomes an offering performed in relationship to the Lord.

The phrase "without deviation" also matters. Devotion becomes transformative when it is not one loyalty among many competing absolutes. Krishna asks for a gathered heart. Meditation on him is therefore not occasional recollection alone, but a continuing inward return that shapes the direction of life.

For readers, this verse shows why devotion can be so powerful. It gives the seeker something concrete to do with ordinary life. Instead of escaping action, one consecrates it. Instead of suppressing thought by force, one gives the mind a worthy center. Bhakti becomes a path that can be lived in the middle of responsibility.

original_script:
तेषामहं समुद्धर्ता मृत्युसंसारसागरात्।
भवामि नचिरात्पार्थ मय्यावेशितचेतसाम्।।12.7।।
transliteration:
teṣhām ahaṁ samuddhartā mṛityu-saṁsāra-sāgarāt
bhavāmi na chirāt pārtha mayy āveśhita-chetasām
word_meanings: teṣhām—of them; aham—I; samuddhartā—the deliverer; mṛityu—of death; saṁsāra—in material existence; sāgarāt—from the ocean; bhavāmi—I become; na—not; chirāt—after a long time; pārtha—O son of Pritha; mayi—upon Me; āveśhita—fixed; chetasām—of those whose minds
translation_english: O son of Pritha, for those whose minds are fixed upon Me, I swiftly become the deliverer from the ocean of birth and death.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna promises that for those whose minds are fixed on him in this way, he himself becomes the swift deliverer from the ocean of birth and death. This verse reveals one of bhakti's deepest strengths: the burden of salvation is not placed on the seeker alone. Krishna does not merely point out the shore. He acts as rescuer.

The image of the ocean is important. Samsara is not a minor inconvenience but an engulfing expanse of repeated becoming, fear, attachment, loss, and death. Human effort within it is real, yet limited. One may struggle nobly and still feel overmatched. Krishna answers that condition not by demanding heroic self-sufficiency, but by offering divine intervention.

This promise also clarifies the meaning of devotion. To fix the mind on Krishna is not only to remember him; it is to entrust oneself to him. Bhakti contains surrender. The devotee does not deny the seriousness of bondage, but neither does he imagine that liberation will be manufactured by ego-driven effort alone.

For readers, the verse can be both humbling and encouraging. Humbling, because rescue is needed. Encouraging, because Krishna places himself on the side of the devotee's deliverance. The path of devotion is powerful partly because it rests not only on human striving, but on divine response.

original_script:
मय्येव मन आधत्स्व मयि बुद्धिं निवेशय।
निवसिष्यसि मय्येव अत ऊर्ध्वं न संशयः।।12.8।।
transliteration:
mayy eva mana ādhatsva mayi buddhiṁ niveśhaya
nivasiṣhyasi mayy eva ata ūrdhvaṁ na sanśhayaḥ
word_meanings: mayi—upon Me; eva—certainly; manaḥ—mind; ādhatsva—fix; mayi—upon Me; buddhim—intelligence; niveśhaya—apply; nivasiṣhyasi—you will live; mayi—in Me; eva—certainly; ataḥ ūrdhvam—thereafter; na—never; sanśhayaḥ—doubt
translation_english: Just fix your mind upon Me, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, and engage all your intelligence in Me. Thus you will live in Me always, without a doubt.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now gives a direct instruction: fix the mind on him and place the intelligence in him, and one will live in him without doubt. The pairing of mind and intelligence is significant. Devotion is not only emotional attraction. It includes thought, judgment, understanding, and conviction. The whole inner life is to be anchored in Krishna.

To place the mind in him means that memory, affection, and attention return to him again and again. To place the intelligence in him means that one's interpretation of life, duty, value, and truth is also shaped by him. Bhakti therefore unites love and understanding. It is not anti-intellectual, and it is not merely conceptual. It gathers both.

The promise that one will live in Krishna points to more than future destiny. It speaks of a present mode of existence in which consciousness becomes increasingly situated in relation to the Divine. Life is no longer experienced as spiritually homeless. The soul begins to dwell where its true center is.

For readers, this verse gives a clear test for devotion. Does remembrance of God remain isolated from judgment and reasoning, or does one's actual way of understanding the world come under his claim? Krishna calls for a deeper integration in which heart and mind are both entrusted to him.

original_script:
अथ चित्तं समाधातुं न शक्नोषि मयि स्थिरम्।
अभ्यासयोगेन ततो मामिच्छाप्तुं धनञ्जय।।12.9।।
transliteration:
atha chittaṁ samādhātuṁ na śhaknoṣhi mayi sthiram
abhyāsa-yogena tato mām ichchhāptuṁ dhanañjaya
word_meanings: atha—if, therefore; chittam—mind; samādhātum—to fix; na—not; śhaknoṣhi—you are able; mayi—upon Me; sthiram—steadily; abhyāsa-yogena—by the practice of yoga; tataḥ—then; mām—Me; ichchhā—desire; āptum—to get; dhanañjaya—O winner of wealth, Arjuna
translation_english: My dear Arjuna, O winner of wealth, if you cannot fix your mind upon Me without deviation, then follow the regulative principles of yoga. In this way develop a desire to attain Me.
deep_dive_text:
Knowing that not every seeker can instantly fix the mind on him without wavering, Krishna gives the next step: practice. This is one of the chapter's most compassionate features. Krishna does not speak only to the already accomplished. He provides a graded path. If spontaneous steadiness is not yet possible, disciplined repetition may cultivate the desire and capacity for it.

Practice here means returning again and again to God through intentional remembrance, regulation, worship, recitation, meditation, and habitual turning. The verse assumes that spiritual maturity is often formed gradually. Desire for Krishna may deepen through repeated acts before it becomes unbroken inclination.

This is important because many seekers become discouraged by instability. Krishna does not say that weakness disqualifies devotion. He simply adjusts the method. The ideal remains fixed, but the way toward it includes patient training. Grace does not eliminate discipline. It gives discipline its right direction and hope.

For readers, the verse is realistic and merciful. Do not wait for perfect love before beginning. Begin the practices that turn the mind toward Krishna, and let those practices educate desire. Spiritual constancy is often the fruit of faithful repetition long before it feels effortless.

original_script:
अभ्यासेऽप्यसमर्थोऽसि मत्कर्मपरमो भव।
मदर्थमपि कर्माणि कुर्वन्सिद्धिमवाप्स्यसि।।12.10।।
transliteration:
abhyāse ’py asamartho ’si mat-karma-paramo bhava
mad-artham api karmāṇi kurvan siddhim avāpsyasi
word_meanings: abhyāse—in practice; api—even if; asamarthaḥ—unable; asi—you are; mat-karma—My work; paramaḥ—dedicated to; bhava—become; mat-artham—for My sake; api—even; karmāṇi—actions; kurvan—performing; siddhim—perfection; avāpsyasi—you will achieve
translation_english: If you cannot practice the regulations of bhakti-yoga, then just try to work for Me, because by working for Me you will come to the perfect stage.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna descends one step further: if steady devotional practice is still difficult, then work for him. This is an extraordinarily practical instruction. It means that even when inward concentration is weak, outward action can become a means of union if it is undertaken for the sake of the Lord.

Working for Krishna includes duty performed in a spirit of offering, service rendered without self-display, and labor shaped by the desire to please God rather than merely to gratify the ego. It is not limited to overtly religious acts. Ordinary responsibilities can become part of bhakti when their intention is consecrated.

This verse protects the path from perfectionism. A seeker need not first become a contemplative master before beginning to belong to Krishna. One can start where one stands, in the realm of action, and allow action itself to become a school of devotion. Work becomes formative when it is inwardly redirected.

For readers, the lesson is liberating. If the mind is not yet steady enough for deep recollection, do not abandon the path. Offer what you do. Service often trains the heart for the devotion it cannot yet sustain in pure inward concentration. Krishna gives a doorway for the active person, not only the meditative one.

original_script:
अथैतदप्यशक्तोऽसि कर्तुं मद्योगमाश्रितः।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं ततः कुरु यतात्मवान्।।12.11।।
transliteration:
athaitad apy aśhakto ’si kartuṁ mad-yogam āśhritaḥ
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṁ tataḥ kuru yatātmavān
word_meanings: atha—if, therefore; etat—this; api—also; aśhaktaḥ—unable; asi—you are; kartum—to perform; mat-yogam—taking shelter of My yoga; āśhritaḥ—taking refuge; sarva-karma—of all activities; phala—of the results; tyāgam—renunciation; tataḥ—then; kuru—do; yata-ātma-vān—self-situated
translation_english: If, however, you are unable to work in this consciousness of Me, then try to act giving up all results of your work and try to be self-situated.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna offers yet another step: if one cannot even work consciously for him, then one should begin by renouncing the fruits of action and becoming self-controlled. This verse shows how patiently the Gita meets the seeker. The ladder is not abandoned even at a lower rung. If devotion is still dim, detachment can prepare the ground.

Renouncing the fruits does not mean refusing all results in a literal or careless way. It means loosening the possessive claim over outcomes, reputation, success, and reward. The ego ordinarily clings to action because it wants to own what action produces. Krishna here asks the seeker to weaken that grip.

This step matters because attachment to results hardens self-centeredness. Even before a person can act fully for God, he can begin to act less for himself. That negative purification has real value. It clears space where devotion may later take root more fully.

For readers, the verse is encouraging in its honesty. Spiritual ascent often begins with giving something up before one has learned completely what to love. Renunciation of fruits may seem modest compared to unbroken devotion, yet it is already a profound shift away from bondage. Krishna uses even that beginning as part of the path home.

original_script:
श्रेयो हि ज्ञानमभ्यासाज्ज्ञानाद्ध्यानं विशिष्यते।
ध्यानात्कर्मफलत्यागस्त्यागाच्छान्तिरनन्तरम्।।12.12।।
transliteration:
śhreyo hi jñānam abhyāsāj jñānād dhyānaṁ viśhiṣhyate
dhyānāt karma-phala-tyāgas tyāgāch chhāntir anantaram
word_meanings: śhreyaḥ—better; hi—certainly; jñānam—knowledge; abhyāsāt—than practice; jñānāt—than knowledge; dhyānam—meditation; viśhiṣhyate—is considered better; dhyānāt—than meditation; karma-phala-tyāgaḥ—renunciation of the results of action; tyāgāt—by such renunciation; śhāntiḥ—peace; anantaram—immediately
translation_english: If you cannot take to this practice, then engage yourself in the cultivation of knowledge. Better than knowledge, however, is meditation, and better than meditation is renunciation of the fruits of action, for by such renunciation one can attain peace of mind.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then summarizes a hierarchy: practice is good, knowledge is better than mere practice, meditation better than knowledge, and renunciation of the fruits of action leads to peace. This verse is compact and has been read in different ways, but its practical force is clear. The inner aim of the path is not spiritual complexity for its own sake. It is peace born of release from grasping.

The sequence should not be treated as a mechanical ranking detached from context. Krishna has just been giving Arjuna workable entry points. Here he shows how the path matures inwardly. Practice disciplines. Knowledge clarifies. Meditation deepens inward steadiness. Renunciation cuts the knot of possessiveness that keeps the heart agitated. Peace appears when grasping eases.

It is striking that peace is linked to renunciation of fruits. Much mental disturbance comes not from action itself but from clinging to what action might secure. The mind becomes divided by anxiety, hope, fear, and comparison. Letting go of those claims creates room for calm.

For readers, the verse offers a useful diagnostic. If spiritual effort is making one more tense, grasping, or self-absorbed, something has gone wrong in its direction. Krishna's path, whatever rung one currently occupies, is meant to move the soul toward simplicity, surrender, and interior peace.

original_script:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी।।12.13।।
सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः।।12.14।।
transliteration:
adveṣhṭā sarva-bhūtānāṁ maitraḥ karuṇa eva cha
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣhamī
santuṣhṭaḥ satataṁ yogī yatātmā dṛiḍha-niśhchayaḥ
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: adveṣhṭā—nonenvious; sarva-bhūtānām—toward all living entities; maitraḥ—friendly; karuṇaḥ—compassionate; eva—certainly; cha—and; nirmamaḥ—with no sense of proprietorship; nirahaṅkāraḥ—without false ego; sama—equal; duḥkha—in distress; sukhaḥ—and happiness; kṣhamī—forgiving; santuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; satatam—always; yogī—one engaged in devotion; yata-ātmā—self-controlled; dṛiḍha-niśhchayaḥ—with determination; mayi—upon Me; arpita—engaged; manaḥ—mind; buddhiḥ—and intelligence; yaḥ—one who; mat-bhaktaḥ—My devotee; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear
translation_english: One who is not envious but is a kind friend to all living entities, who does not think himself a proprietor and is free from false ego, who is equal in both happiness and distress, who is tolerant, always satisfied, self-controlled, and engaged in devotional service with determination, his mind and intelligence fixed on Me—such a devotee of Mine is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now describes the devotee who is dear to him: free from envy, friendly and compassionate toward all, without possessiveness or false ego, equal in pleasure and pain, forgiving, satisfied, self-controlled, determined, and fixed in mind and intellect on God. These verses are among the most beautiful ethical portraits in the Gita. They show what bhakti looks like when it shapes character rather than remaining a private feeling.

The first traits are telling. Absence of envy and presence of kindness indicate that devotion breaks the ego's habit of measuring itself against others. A heart centered in Krishna has less need to compete for significance. Compassion follows because the other is no longer primarily a rival or instrument.

The middle traits show inner stabilization. Freedom from possessiveness and false ego, endurance in pleasure and pain, contentment, and self-control all point to a person no longer ruled by constant reaction. Bhakti does not make one vague or sentimental. It makes one inwardly ordered.

Finally, determination and a mind fixed on Krishna reveal the source of this character. These virtues do not arise from bare moral effort alone. They are sustained by relation to the Lord. For readers, the passage gives a crucial standard: devotion is not proved only by worship practices, but by whether those practices are making us less envious, less self-centered, more steady, and more compassionate.

original_script:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो यः स च मे प्रियः।।12.15।।
transliteration:
yasmān nodvijate loko lokān nodvijate cha yaḥ
harṣhāmarṣha-bhayodvegair mukto yaḥ sa cha me priyaḥ
word_meanings: yasmāt—from whom; na—not; udvijate—are agitated; lokaḥ—people; lokāt—from people; na—not; udvijate—is agitated; cha—and; yaḥ—anyone who; harṣha—from gladness; amarṣha—anger; bhaya—fear; udvegaiḥ—and anxiety; muktaḥ—freed; yaḥ—who; saḥ—he; cha—also; me—to Me; priyaḥ—very dear
translation_english: He for whom no one is put into difficulty and who is not disturbed by anyone, who is equipoised in happiness and distress, fear and anxiety, is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the devotee dear to him is one by whom no one is disturbed and who is not disturbed by the world, remaining balanced amid joy, sorrow, fear, and anxiety. This verse extends the portrait of bhakti into the realm of social presence. A mature devotee does not become a source of agitation, injury, and unpredictability for others.

That does not mean such a person never speaks hard truths or never participates in conflict when duty requires it. The point is deeper. The devotee is not driven by inner turbulence into outward harm. He is not constantly throwing his instability into the lives of those around him. His presence tends toward steadiness rather than disturbance.

The second half of the verse is equally important. He is not easily shaken by others. Emotional life has not disappeared, but it is no longer tyrannized by every rise of pleasure, fear, or anxiety. Bhakti produces a heart that can remain inhabitable under pressure.

For readers, the verse offers a searching measure of spiritual progress. It is easy to think in terms of private experiences, but Krishna asks what kind of presence we are becoming in the lives of others. Do we spread agitation or peace? Are we ruled by volatility or shaped by inward trust? A dear devotee is one whose inner life no longer spills chaos into the world.

original_script:
अनपेक्षः शुचिर्दक्ष उदासीनो गतव्यथः।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः।।12.16।।
transliteration:
anapekṣhaḥ śhuchir dakṣha udāsīno gata-vyathaḥ
sarvārambha-parityāgī yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: anapekṣhaḥ—neutral/independent; śhuchiḥ—pure; dakṣhaḥ—expert; udāsīnaḥ—free from care/impartial; gata-vyathaḥ—freed from all distress; sarva-ārambha—of all endeavors; parityāgī—renouncer; yaḥ—anyone who; mat-bhaktaḥ—My devotee; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—very dear
translation_english: My devotee who is not dependent on the ordinary course of activities, who is pure, expert, without cares, free from all pains, and not striving for some result, is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next praises the devotee who is not dependent on ordinary cravings, who is pure, skillful, free from anxious entanglement, beyond inner pain, and not striving for selfish results. This description adds a disciplined and intelligent dimension to devotion. Bhakti is not incompetence decorated with piety. The dear devotee is expert.

Purity here includes motive as well as conduct. The heart is less cluttered by hidden agendas, manipulations, and self-serving calculations. Skill or expertise then becomes valuable because it is no longer chained to vanity. The devotee can act well precisely because action is not being used to inflate the ego.

Freedom from cares does not mean indifference or negligence. It means the absence of feverish preoccupation. One still fulfills responsibilities, but without being inwardly consumed by them. Likewise, not striving for result does not abolish effort. It purifies effort from anxious self-claim.

For readers, the verse is a needed correction to sentimental religion. Genuine devotion should make a person cleaner in motive, steadier in action, and less agitated by self-interest. Spiritual depth is not measured by emotional intensity alone. Krishna also honors purity, competence, composure, and freedom from restless ambition.

original_script:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः स मे प्रियः।।12.17।।
transliteration:
yo na hṛiṣhyati na dveṣhṭi na śhochati na kāṅkṣhati
śhubhāśhubha-parityāgī bhaktimān yaḥ sa me priyaḥ
word_meanings: yaḥ—one who; na—never; hṛiṣhyati—takes pleasure; na—never; dveṣhṭi—grieves; na—never; śhochati—laments; na—never; kāṅkṣhati—desires; śhubha—of the auspicious; aśhubha—and the inauspicious; parityāgī—renouncer; bhakti-mān—devotee; yaḥ—one who; saḥ—he; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear
translation_english: One who neither rejoices nor grieves, who neither laments nor desires, and who renounces both auspicious and inauspicious things—such a devotee is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues with the devotee who neither exults nor hates, neither grieves nor desires, and who renounces attachment to both favorable and unfavorable things. This verse describes emotional freedom, not emotional numbness. The point is not that the devotee becomes stone-like, but that he is no longer thrown into bondage by constant swings of attraction and aversion.

To rejoice in a disordered sense is to become inflated by gain. To grieve in the disordered sense is to be inwardly shattered by loss as though God had disappeared with the object lost. To desire obsessively is to live under compulsion. Krishna praises the person who has begun to stand deeper than these reflexes.

Renouncing both auspicious and inauspicious things means loosening attachment even to the categories through which the ego flatters or frightens itself. The devotee learns not to cling desperately to what seems favorable or recoil superstitiously from what seems unfavorable. Krishna becomes the reference point, not circumstance alone.

For readers, this verse suggests that devotion matures into freedom from emotional slavery. One still feels joy, pain, and longing, but they no longer dictate identity. The heart becomes more available to God because it is less imprisoned by the endless push and pull of preference.

original_script:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः।।12.18।।
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित्।
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः।।12.19।।
transliteration:
samaḥ śhatrau cha mitre cha tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
śhītoṣhṇa-sukha-duḥkheṣhu samaḥ saṅga-vivarjitaḥ
tulya-nindā-stutir maunī santuṣhṭo yena kenachit
aniketaḥ sthira-matir bhaktimān me priyo naraḥ
word_meanings: samaḥ—equal; śhatrau—to an enemy; cha—also; mitre—to a friend; cha—also; tathā—so; māna—in honor; apamānayoḥ—and dishonor; śhīta—in cold; uṣhṇa—heat; sukha—happiness; duḥkheṣhu—and distress; samaḥ—equipoised; saṅga-vivarjitaḥ—free from all association; tulya—equal; nindā—in defamation; stutiḥ—and repute; maunī—silent; santuṣhṭaḥ—satisfied; yena kenachit—with anything; aniketaḥ—having no residence; sthira—fixed; matiḥ—determination; bhakti-mān—engaged in devotion; me—to Me; priyaḥ—dear; naraḥ—a man
translation_english: One who is equal to friends and enemies, who is equipoised in honor and dishonor, heat and cold, happiness and distress, fame and infamy, who is always free from contaminating association, always silent and satisfied with anything, who doesn't care for any residence, who is fixed in knowledge and who is engaged in devotional service—such a person is very dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna broadens the portrait still further: the dear devotee is equal toward friend and enemy, honor and dishonor, heat and cold, pleasure and pain, fame and disgrace; free from binding company; measured in speech; content with what comes; unattached to residence; steady in understanding; and devoted. These verses gather outer and inner freedom together.

Equality toward friend and enemy does not mean moral blindness. It means the devotee's center is not determined by how others treat him. Praise does not define him, insult does not destroy him, and comfort does not become a master. He can remain rooted because his worth is not being borrowed from circumstance.

The later traits deepen this picture. To be content with little, not obsessed with place, and restrained in speech is to be less colonized by the need for control and display. Such a person carries inward home with him because that home is in God. Even social belonging is no longer an absolute necessity.

For readers, the passage is demanding and liberating. Krishna is describing a person who can move through changing conditions without surrendering his soul to them. Devotion here appears as radical inward simplification. The less we are owned by approval, possession, comfort, and status, the more fully the heart can belong to God.

original_script:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं यथोक्तं पर्युपासते।
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः।।12.20।।
transliteration:
ye tu dharmyāmṛitam idaṁ yathoktaṁ paryupāsate
śhraddhadhānāh mat-paramā bhaktās te ’tīva me priyāḥ
word_meanings: ye—those who; tu—but; dharmya-amṛitam—this nectar of dharma; idam—this; yathā-uktam—as declared; paryupāsate—completely engage in; śhraddhadhānāḥ—with full faith; mat-paramāḥ—taking Me as the supreme goal; bhaktāḥ—devotees; te—they; atīva—exceedingly; me—to Me; priyāḥ—dear
translation_english: Those who honor this immortal nectar of dharma as declared here, full of faith, holding Me as their supreme goal—such devotees are exceedingly dear to Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by praising those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, taking him as their supreme goal. The chapter ends not with an abstract conclusion, but with an invitation. Everything just described, from the preference for personal devotion to the long portrait of the dear devotee, is called an immortal nectar. It is nourishment for the soul.

The phrase "supreme goal" gathers the whole teaching. Bhakti is not one useful practice among several unrelated goals. It is the ordering of life around Krishna as final end. When that center is fixed, practices, virtues, renunciations, and disciplines all take their proper place. Without that center, even good habits can remain spiritually partial.

Faith is also essential here. Not vague optimism, but trust that Krishna is worthy of total orientation and that the way he has taught truly leads to life. The chapter's demands are high, yet Krishna presents them as life-giving rather than crushing. The nectar is immortal because it trains the soul for what does not perish.

For readers, this verse is a fitting seal on Chapter 12. The path of devotion is not merely emotional attachment to God. It is a complete mode of life marked by trust, reordered action, relinquished egoism, and transformed character. Those who embrace it with Krishna as their highest aim become exceedingly dear to him.

original_script: भक्तियोगः
transliteration: Bhakti Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of Devotion
deep_dive_text: Chapter 12 teaches that devotion to the personal Divine is especially accessible for embodied beings, while also acknowledging the difficulty of contemplation of the formless absolute. Krishna offers a compassionate sequence of practice: fix the mind on Him, practice steady remembrance, work for Him, or renounce the fruits of action. He concludes by describing the qualities of a true devotee, marked by humility, equanimity, kindness, and unwavering love.

original_script: अथ त्रयोदशोऽध्यायः - क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञविभागयोगः
transliteration: Kṣhetra Kṣhetrajña Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Kṣhetra—the Field; Kṣhetrajña—the Knower of the Field; Vibhāga—Division/Discrimination; Yogaḥ—the path of synthesis.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of the Field and the Knower of the Field
deep_dive_text: Chapter 13 distinguishes between the field of experience and the knower of the field. Krishna explains the difference between body, mind, and material nature on one side, and conscious awareness on the other. The chapter also describes the qualities that support true knowledge, linking insight with humility, discipline, and clarity of perception.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव क्षेत्रं क्षेत्रज्ञमेव च।
एतद्वेदितुमिच्छामि ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं च केशव।।13.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
prakṛitiṁ puruṣhaṁ chaiva kṣhetraṁ kṣhetrajñam eva cha
etad veditum ichchhāmi jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ cha keśhava
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; prakṛitim—nature; puruṣham—the enjoyer/soul; cha—also; eva—indeed; kṣhetram—the field; kṣhetrajñam—the knower of the field; eva—certainly; cha—and; etat—all this; veditum—to understand; ichchhāmi—I wish; jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—the object of knowledge; cha—also; keśhava—O Krishna
translation_english: Arjuna said: O Krishna, I wish to know about prakriti (nature), purusha (the enjoyer), and the field and the knower of the field, and of knowledge and the object of knowledge.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna begins this chapter by asking for clarity about a whole cluster of ideas: nature, the enjoyer, the field, the knower of the field, knowledge, and the object of knowledge. This is an important transition in the Gita. Earlier chapters have spoken about action, devotion, divine manifestations, and the universal form. Now Arjuna asks for a more exact account of the structure of embodied existence and the way true understanding works within it.

The question is not merely philosophical in a detached sense. Arjuna is still a man who must act, suffer, decide, and seek liberation while living in a body. To understand what the body is, who the self is, how matter operates, and what true knowledge aims at is therefore intensely practical. Confusion in these matters produces bondage. Clarity in them can loosen bondage at the root.

The arrangement of the terms is also significant. Arjuna does not ask only about the world "out there." He asks about the field and the knower, about knowledge and what is to be known. He is asking how reality, consciousness, and spiritual insight fit together. In that sense, the chapter moves inward. It wants to show not only what exists, but how a person must learn to see.

For a reader, the verse is a good reminder that spiritual life needs conceptual clarity as well as devotion. Love of God does not remove the need to understand body, self, nature, and liberation. Arjuna asks these questions because vague spirituality is not enough. One must learn what is acting, what is witnessing, and what is ultimately worth knowing.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते।
एतद्यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञ इति तद्विदः।।13.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
idaṁ śharīraṁ kaunteya kṣhetram ity abhidhīyate
etad yo vetti taṁ prāhuḥ kṣhetrajña iti tad-vidaḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Lord said; idam—this; śharīram—body; kaunteya—O son of Kunti; kṣhetram—the field; iti—thus; abhidhīyate—is called; etat—this; yaḥ—who; vetti—knows; tam—him; prāhuḥ—is called; kṣhetrajñaḥ—the knower of the field; iti—thus; tat-vidaḥ—by those who understand this
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: This body, O son of Kunti, is called the field, and one who knows this body is called the knower of the field.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins with a definition: this body is called the field, and the one who knows the body is called the knower of the field. The image of a field is rich. A field is a place where things are sown, grow, change, decay, and yield consequences. By describing the body this way, Krishna presents embodied life as a domain of processes, experiences, actions, and results rather than as the true self.

This is a foundational distinction. Most human confusion begins by identifying completely with the body and its changing conditions. Krishna does not deny the body's importance. The field matters. It is where karma unfolds, where pleasure and pain are experienced, and where the drama of earthly life takes place. But it is still a field, not the knower.

To call someone the knower of the body means there is an observing principle that is aware of bodily states, sensations, thoughts, and changes. Hunger is known. Aging is known. Emotion is known. Even mental agitation is known. That which knows cannot be identical in the simplest way with everything it observes. Krishna is beginning to separate the witnessing self from the domain it witnesses.

For readers, this verse offers a powerful practical shift. Much suffering becomes absolute because we do not distinguish between what happens in the field and who we are as knowers of it. Krishna does not ask us to neglect the body, but to stop mistaking the changing field for the whole truth of selfhood.

original_script:
क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि सर्वक्षेत्रेषु भारत।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोर्ज्ञानं यत्तज्ज्ञानं मतं मम।।13.3।।
transliteration:
kṣhetrajñaṁ chāpi māṁ viddhi sarva-kṣhetreṣhu bhārata
kṣhetra-kṣhetrajñayor jñānaṁ yat taj jñānaṁ mataṁ mama
word_meanings: kṣhetra-jñam—the knower of the field; cha—also; api—indeed; mām—Me; viddhi—know; sarva-kṣhetreṣhu—in all fields; bhārata—O scion of Bharata; kṣhetra—the field of activities; kṣhetrajñayoḥ—and the knower of the field; jñānam—knowledge of; yat—which; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; matam—opinion; mama—My
translation_english: O scion of Bharata, you should understand that I am also the knower in all bodies, and to understand this body and its knower is called knowledge. That is My opinion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna deepens the distinction by saying that he himself is the knower in all bodies, and that real knowledge consists in understanding both the field and its knower. This verse prevents a simplistic reading of the previous one. There is not only the individual embodied knower; there is also the Supreme who knows every field at once. The chapter's metaphysics is therefore relational from the start.

The individual self knows one body in a limited, conditioned way. Krishna knows all bodies universally and perfectly. This means that self-knowledge is incomplete if it stops at private inwardness. One must understand both the creaturely witness and the divine witness. The human knower is not the ultimate center. The Lord remains present as the all-pervading knower, overseer, and ground of awareness.

Krishna also gives a striking definition of knowledge. Knowledge is not mere accumulation of information about the world. It is discernment of reality's structure: body, self, and the divine relation between them. To know only the field is ignorance disguised as expertise. To know only some inward experience without seeing the Lord's presence is also incomplete.

For a reader, this verse is corrective in two directions. It protects against materialism, which treats the body as all, and against isolated spirituality, which forgets the Lord who knows every life from within. Real understanding begins when one sees that embodied existence is lived before God, not merely within private consciousness.

original_script:
तत्क्षेत्रं यच्च यादृक्च यद्विकारि यतश्च यत्।
स च यो यत्प्रभावश्च तत्समासेन मे शृणु।।13.4।।
transliteration:
tat kṣhetraṁ yach cha yādṛik cha yad-vikāri yataśh cha yat
sa cha yo yat-prabhāvaśh cha tat samāsena me śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: tat—that; kṣhetram—field of activities; yat—what; cha—also; yādṛik—what it is like; cha—and; yat-vikāri—what its changes are; yataḥ—from where; cha—and; yat—what; saḥ—he; cha—also; yaḥ—who; yat-prabhāvaḥ—what his influence is; cha—and; tat—that; samāsena—in brief; me—from Me; śhṛiṇu—understand
translation_english: Now please hear in brief from Me what this field of activity is, what its nature is, what its changes are, whence it is produced, who that knower of the field of activities is, and what his influences are.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now says he will explain what the field is, how it changes, where it comes from, who the knower is, and what powers or influences are involved. This verse functions like a roadmap. The chapter will not remain at the level of slogans. Krishna intends to unfold the anatomy of embodied existence with enough detail to support liberation.

That matters because vague distinctions are easily lost in daily life. Saying "I am not the body" can become an empty phrase unless one understands what the body-field includes, how it operates, and how the knower becomes entangled in it. Krishna therefore promises an account not only of definition but of process. How does the field arise? What are its transformations? How does the knower relate to it?

The verse also suggests that accurate knowledge requires patience. The human tendency is to reach for quick formulas, but Krishna invites Arjuna into careful discernment. Spiritual clarity is often built by sustained examination of what is happening in experience: sensation, desire, aversion, change, and the silent fact of knowing amid all of it.

For readers, the verse teaches an important discipline. Liberation is not served by confusion about the machinery of bondage. Krishna is preparing Arjuna to see the body-life clearly enough that he can cease being unconsciously ruled by it. The promise to explain "in brief" does not mean superficiality. It means a distilled wisdom that goes to the heart of the matter.

original_script:
ऋषिभिर्बहुधा गीतं छन्दोभिर्विविधैः पृथक्।
ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैश्चैव हेतुमद्भिर्विनिश्चितैः।।13.5।।
transliteration:
ṛiṣhibhir bahudhā gītaṁ chhandobhir vividhaiḥ pṛithak
brahma-sūtra-padaiśh chaiva hetumadbhir viniśhchitaiḥ
word_meanings: ṛiṣhibhiḥ—by the wise sages; bahudhā—in many ways; gītam—described; chhandobhiḥ—by Vedic hymns; vividhaiḥ—various; pṛithak—variously; brahma-sūtra—of the Vedanta; padaiḥ—by the aphorisms; cha—also; eva—certainly; hetu-madbhiḥ—with cause and effect; viniśhchitaiḥ—with conclusive logic
translation_english: That knowledge of the field of activities and of the knower of activities is described by various sages in various Vedic writings. It is especially presented in Vedanta-sutra with all logic as to cause and effect.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna adds that this teaching about the field and the knower has already been described by sages, by Vedic writings, and in reasoned form within traditions of inquiry about cause and effect. This verse is important because it shows continuity rather than novelty. Krishna is not inventing a private doctrine for Arjuna's convenience. He is speaking within a recognized stream of spiritual insight.

The appeal to sages and sacred texts gives authority, but the mention of reasoned analysis matters too. The truth about self and nature is not irrational. It can be examined with coherence. Cause, effect, change, dependence, and the distinction between witness and witnessed all admit serious reflection. The Gita therefore honors revelation and disciplined thought together.

This keeps the teaching balanced. On one side, Krishna's words come with divine authority. On the other, they are not offered as opaque decrees that bypass intelligence. The tradition has thought deeply about these matters, and Krishna expects Arjuna to understand, not merely to memorize.

For readers, the verse can remove a false opposition between faith and reasoning. Spiritual truth is received within tradition, but it also invites rational clarity. Krishna's teaching about body, self, and liberation belongs to a wisdom that has been contemplated, tested, and articulated across generations. That makes the chapter not less alive, but more trustworthy.

original_script:
महाभूतान्यहङ्कारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च।
इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः।।13.6।।
इच्छा द्वेषः सुखं दुःखं सङ्घातश्चेतना धृतिः।
एतत्क्षेत्रं समासेन सविकारमुदाहृतम्।।13.7।।
transliteration:
mahā-bhūtāny ahaṅkāro buddhir avyaktam eva cha
indriyāṇi daśhaikaṁ cha pañcha chendriya-gocharāḥ
ichchhā dveṣhaḥ sukhaṁ duḥkhaṁ saṅghātaśh chetanā dhṛitiḥ
etat kṣhetraṁ samāsena sa-vikāram udāhṛitam
word_meanings: mahā-bhūtāni—the great elements; ahaṅkāraḥ—false ego; buddhiḥ—intelligence; avyaktam—the unmanifested; eva—certainly; cha—also; indriyāṇi—senses; daśha ekam—eleven; cha—and; pañcha—five; cha—and; indriya-gocharāḥ—objects of the senses; ichchhā—desire; dveṣhaḥ—hatred; sukham—happiness; duḥkham—distress; saṅghātaḥ—the aggregate; chetanā—life symptoms; dhṛitiḥ—conviction; etat—all this; kṣhetram—the field of activities; samāsena—in summary; sa-vikāram—with interactions; udāhṛitam—exemplified
translation_english: The five great elements, false ego, intelligence, the unmanifested, the ten senses and the mind, the five sense objects, desire, hatred, happiness, distress, the aggregate, the life symptoms, and convictions—all these are considered, in summary, to be the field of activities and its interactions.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then lists what belongs to the field: the great elements, ego, intellect, the unmanifest basis of matter, the senses, the mind, their objects, desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, the bodily aggregate, vitality, and conviction or determination. The list is deliberately comprehensive. The field is not just flesh and bone. It includes the whole apparatus of embodied experience.

This is crucial for self-knowledge. Many people can say that they are "not just the body" while still remaining completely identified with mental reaction, desire, emotion, opinion, and preference. Krishna sweeps those too into the field. Wanting, disliking, enjoying, suffering, perceiving, organizing, and insisting are all part of the domain that is known. They are not the deepest knower itself.

The inclusion of intellect and ego is especially important. Even our sense of "I am this person" and our power to analyze belong, at one level, to the field. That does not make them worthless. It means they are still instruments and modifications within embodied nature. The witness is deeper than the narrative machinery through which ordinary personality operates.

For readers, this verse is both unsettling and liberating. It unsettles because it widens the range of what is not-self. Thoughts, reactions, and preferences cannot be treated as absolute identity. But it liberates because once these are seen as part of the field, they can be observed, disciplined, and offered to God rather than blindly obeyed.

original_script:
अमानित्वमदम्भित्वमहिंसा क्षान्तिरार्जवम्।
आचार्योपासनं शौचं स्थैर्यमात्मविनिग्रहः।।13.8।।
transliteration:
amānitvam adambhitvam ahinsā kṣhāntir ārjavam
āchāryopāsanaṁ śhauchaṁ sthairyam ātma-vinigrahaḥ
word_meanings: amānitvam—humility; adambhitvam—pridelessness; ahinsā—nonviolence; kṣhāntiḥ—tolerance; ārjavam—simplicity; āchārya-upāsanam—approaching a bona fide spiritual master; śhaucham—cleanliness; sthairyam—steadiness; ātma-vinigrahaḥ—self-control
translation_english: Humility, pridelessness, nonviolence, tolerance, simplicity, approaching a bona fide spiritual master, cleanliness, steadiness, self-control...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns from the field itself to what he astonishingly calls knowledge, beginning with humility, freedom from pretension, nonviolence, tolerance, simplicity, reverence toward a true teacher, cleanliness, steadiness, and self-control. This is a decisive move. Knowledge is not first presented as theory. It begins as the moral and spiritual formation required to see rightly.

Humility and lack of pride come first for good reason. A swollen ego cannot perceive truth clearly because it interprets everything in relation to self-importance. Nonviolence and tolerance follow because aggression and reactivity distort perception. Simplicity matters because the more divided and performative the person becomes, the less capable he is of seeing reality without self-deception.

Approaching a genuine teacher is also central. The self does not easily unravel its own confusions without guidance. Cleanliness, steadiness, and self-control then establish a life in which the mind is less scattered and the senses less tyrannical. Such qualities are not decorative virtues attached to knowledge after the fact. They make knowledge possible.

For readers, the verse gives a challenging correction. We often imagine that insight comes from intelligence alone. Krishna says that true knowing requires transformation of character. Pride, violence, restlessness, and impurity are not just moral problems. They are epistemic problems. They prevent us from seeing what is actually there.

original_script:
असक्तिरनभिष्वङ्गः पुत्रदारगृहादिषु।
नित्यं च समचित्तत्वमिष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु।।13.9।।
transliteration:
asaktir anabhiṣhvaṅgaḥ putra-dāra-gṛihādiṣhu
nityaṁ cha sama-chittatvam iṣhṭāniṣhṭopapattiṣhu
word_meanings: asaktiḥ—detachment; anabhiṣhvaṅgaḥ—freedom from entanglement; putra—children; dāra—wife; gṛiha-ādiṣhu—home, etc.; nityam—constant; cha—and; sama-chittatvam—equanimity; iṣhṭa—desirable; aniṣhṭa—undesirable; upapattiṣhu—upon obtaining
translation_english: Detachment, freedom from entanglement with children, wife, home and the rest, and constant equanimity amid pleasant and unpleasant events...
deep_dive_text:
The list continues with detachment, freedom from entanglement in family-centered possessiveness, and steady balance in pleasant and unpleasant events. Krishna is not rejecting human affection or responsibility. He is exposing the kind of entanglement in which relationships become extensions of ego, possession, and fear. Knowledge requires a more spacious heart.

Detachment here means the capacity to love without clinging as though one's being depended absolutely on people, roles, or domestic arrangements. Family life easily becomes the place where possessiveness hides under the language of care. Krishna asks for another mode of being: relational fidelity without bondage of identity.

Equanimity amid pleasant and unpleasant events is equally important. A mind dragged up and down by every favorable or unfavorable turn cannot discern the deeper truth of self and God. Knowledge is not indifference, but freedom from being ruled by the immediate emotional weather of circumstance.

For readers, the verse exposes how subtle bondage can be. Not all chains look dramatic. Some are woven from affection, expectation, pride in belonging, or panic at loss. Krishna does not ask us to abandon those entrusted to us. He asks us to stop making contingent conditions the foundation of who we are. Only then can love become steadier and wiser.

original_script:
मयि चानन्ययोगेन भक्तिरव्यभिचारिणी।
विविक्तदेशसेवित्वमरतिर्जनसंसदि।।13.10।।
transliteration:
mayi chānanya-yogena bhaktir avyabhichāriṇī
vivikta-deśha-sevitvam aratir jana-sansadi
word_meanings: mayi—unto Me; cha—and; ananya-yogena—by unalloyed devotion; bhaktiḥ—devotion; avyabhichāriṇī—constant; vivikta—solitary; deśha—places; sevitvam—resorting to; aratiḥ—detachment; jana-sansadi—from the masses of people
translation_english: Constant and unalloyed devotion to Me, resorting to solitary places, and detachment from the general masses of people...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next includes unwavering devotion to himself, preference for solitude, and disinterest in the restless crowd. This is vital because it shows that knowledge, as the Gita understands it, is not impersonal self-analysis alone. Devotion to Krishna belongs inside real knowledge. Without that center, detachment can turn dry and introspection can turn self-enclosed.

Solitude matters because the inner life cannot be heard clearly amid constant noise and social diffusion. Yet Krishna does not praise solitude as mere escape. He pairs it with devotion. The purpose is not to disappear from responsibility, but to become inwardly available to truth rather than endlessly absorbed in distraction and public performance.

Detachment from the general masses also means freedom from crowd-consciousness: the urge to think, desire, react, and speak merely because social currents carry us there. The knower of the field must learn to stand inwardly free enough to see what is real, not merely what is fashionable or collectively reinforced.

For readers, this verse is a practical warning about scattered living. Knowledge cannot ripen in a person who is constantly externally occupied and inwardly unfixed. Krishna makes devotion, inward recollection, and selective withdrawal from noise part of wisdom itself. To know truly, one must create room in which truth may actually be heard.

original_script:
अध्यात्मज्ञाननित्यत्वं तत्त्वज्ञानार्थदर्शनम्।
एतज्ज्ञानमिति प्रोक्तमज्ञानं यदतोऽन्यथा।।13.11।।
transliteration:
adhyātma-jñāna-nityatvaṁ tattva-jñānārtha-darśhanam
etaj jñānam iti proktam ajñānaṁ yad ato ’nyathā
word_meanings: adhyātma—spiritual; jñāna—knowledge; nityatvam—constancy; tattva-jñāna—knowledge of the truth; artha—for the object; darśhanam—philosophy; etat—all this; jñānam—knowledge; iti—thus; proktam—declared; ajñānam—ignorance; yat—which; ataḥ—from this; anyathā—otherwise
translation_english: Constancy in the knowledge of the Spirit, and insight into the goal of the knowledge of Truth—this is declared to be knowledge, and all that is contrary to it is ignorance.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna completes the list by naming constancy in spiritual insight and perception of the goal of truth, then declares that all else contrary to this is ignorance. This conclusion is striking because it draws a firm line. Knowledge is not whatever the world happens to reward as cleverness. It is a disposition and perception ordered toward the reality of the Spirit and its true end.

Constancy matters because occasional insight is not enough. The mind easily flashes with understanding and then sinks again into habit, ego, and forgetfulness. Krishna praises a sustained orientation toward the spiritual dimension of life. The goal of truth must remain in view, not merely as an interesting concept but as the destination governing how one lives.

By calling everything contrary to this ignorance, Krishna also strips away false prestige. One may be technically brilliant, socially influential, or philosophically subtle and still remain ignorant if life is not being ordered toward the true object of knowledge. Knowledge in the Gita is measured teleologically: where is consciousness being led?

For readers, the verse is clarifying. It asks not only what we know, but what our knowing serves. If intelligence is feeding vanity, distraction, and worldliness, it does not count as wisdom here. Real knowledge is whatever orients the soul toward truth, Spirit, God, and liberation from delusion.

original_script:
ज्ञेयं यत्तत्प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वामृतमश्नुते।
अनादिमत्परं ब्रह्म न सत्तन्नासदुच्यते।।13.12।।
transliteration:
jñeyaṁ yat tat pravakṣhyāmi yaj jñātvāmṛitam aśhnute
anādimat paraṁ brahma na sat tan nāsad uchyate
word_meanings: jñeyam—the knowable; yat—which; tat—that; pravakṣhyāmi—I shall now explain; yat—which; jñātvā—knowing; amṛitam—nectar of immortality; aśhnute—tastes; anādi-mat—without beginning; param—supreme; brahma—Brahman; na—not; sat—cause; tat—that; na—nor; asat—effect; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: I shall now explain the knowable, knowing which you will taste the eternal. Brahman, the spirit, beginningless and subordinate to Me, lies beyond the cause and effect of this material world.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns to the knowable itself, saying he will describe that which, once known, leads to immortality. He speaks of the supreme Brahman as beginningless, beyond ordinary categories of cause and effect, and not adequately captured by the common opposites through which the mind organizes the material world. The chapter now reaches its highest point of object: what all true knowledge is actually for.

This verse matters because it prevents the previous list of virtues from becoming a self-improvement program. Humility, nonviolence, steadiness, and devotion are not ends in themselves. They prepare the person to know the reality that transcends perishing conditions. Krishna's promise of immortality does not mean endless bodily continuation. It means participation in what is not subject to birth-and-death limitation.

The description of Brahman as beyond ordinary categories is also important. The material mind divides reality into familiar polarities: being and non-being, cause and effect, formed and unformed. Krishna indicates that the knowable supreme cannot be enclosed within those conceptual boxes. It grounds them without being reducible to them.

For readers, the verse teaches that spiritual knowledge is ultimately directed beyond the shifting world of appearances into the recognition of the supreme reality in relation to which mortality is relativized. One does not cultivate wisdom merely to become calmer within the field. One cultivates it to know what does not perish.

original_script:
सर्वतः पाणिपादं तत्सर्वतोऽक्षिशिरोमुखम्।
सर्वतः श्रुतिमल्लोके सर्वमावृत्य तिष्ठति।।13.13।।
transliteration:
sarvataḥ pāṇi-pādaṁ tat sarvato ’kṣhi-śhiro-mukham
sarvataḥ śhrutimal loke sarvam āvṛitya tiṣhṭhati
word_meanings: sarvataḥ—everywhere; pāṇi—hands; pādam—legs; tat—that; sarvataḥ—everywhere; akṣhi—eyes; śhiraḥ—heads; mukham—faces; sarvataḥ—everywhere; śhruti-mat—having ears; loke—in the world; sarvam—everything; āvṛitya—pervading; tiṣhṭhati—exists
translation_english: Everywhere are His hands and legs, His eyes, heads and faces, and He has ears everywhere. In this way the Supersoul exists, pervading everything.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes the knowable reality as having hands, feet, eyes, heads, faces, and ears everywhere, pervading all things. The language is symbolic and profound. It does not mean that God is a giant material organism spread through space. It means that the Supreme is present as the power of perception, action, awareness, and relation in all places at once.

The image corrects a common mistake in spiritual thinking: imagining transcendence as absence. Krishna's point is not that the supreme reality is remote from the world, but that it is intimately present within all modes of life while still exceeding them. Wherever there is seeing, hearing, reaching, moving, or living, the pervading reality of the Divine is already nearer than any isolated form.

This description also teaches reverence toward existence. If the supreme pervades all with such all-encompassing presence, then no place is spiritually empty in the final sense. The world remains finite and changing, but it is not abandoned ground. It is sustained within the reach and awareness of the One Krishna is revealing.

For readers, the verse invites a shift in perception. God is not to be sought only in rare moments or remote places. The very capacities and movements of life hint at a deeper divine presence. To learn this is to live less as though the world were spiritually inert and more as though everything unfolds before a pervasive sacred reality.

original_script:
सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासं सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम्।
असक्तं सर्वभृच्चैव निर्गुणं गुणभोक्तृ च।।13.14।।
transliteration:
sarvendriya-guṇābhāsaṁ sarvendriya-vivarjitam
asaktaṁ sarva-bhṛich chaiva nirguṇaṁ guṇa-bhoktṛi cha
word_meanings: sarva—all; indriya—senses; guṇa—qualities; ābhāsam—the original source; sarva—all; indriya—senses; vivarjitam—without; asaktam—without attachment; sarva-bhṛit—the maintainer of everyone; cha—and; eva—certainly; nirguṇam—without material qualities; guṇa-bhoktṛi—the master of the gunas; cha—also
translation_english: The Supersoul is the original source of all senses, yet He is without senses. He is unattached, although He is the maintainer of all living beings. He transcends the modes of nature, and at the same time He is the master of all modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues with a paradox: the Supreme is the source of all senses, yet himself without material senses; unattached, yet the sustainer of all beings; beyond the modes, yet master of the modes. These apparent contradictions are deliberate. They teach that the Divine cannot be understood by simple transfer of creaturely categories upward.

To say that the Supreme is the source of sense-capacity while not limited by senses means that God grounds all perception without depending on embodied organs. He sees without eyes in the creaturely sense, hears without ears in the creaturely sense, and knows without limitation. Likewise, he supports all beings while not being entangled in the dependencies that bind creatures to one another.

The statement about the modes is equally important. Material nature operates through changing qualities and forces, but Krishna is not trapped inside their fluctuations. He governs them. This preserves divine transcendence without sacrificing divine immanence. The Supreme is fully present to the world without being bound by the world's unstable conditions.

For readers, the verse is a lesson in theological discipline. God is not less than the world, but he is not merely a bigger version of worldly capacities either. Whenever spiritual understanding becomes too literal or too simplistic, paradox is needed to protect the truth from reduction.

original_script:
बहिरन्तश्च भूतानामचरं चरमेव च।
सूक्ष्मत्वात्तदविज्ञेयं दूरस्थं चान्तिके च तत्।।13.15।।
transliteration:
bahir antaśh cha bhūtānām acharaṁ charam eva cha
sūkṣhmatvāt tad avijñeyaṁ dūra-sthaṁ chāntike cha tat
word_meanings: bahiḥ—outside; antaḥ—inside; cha—also; bhūtānām—of all living entities; acharam—not moving; charam—moving; eva—also; cha—and; sūkṣhmatvāt—on account of being subtle; tat—that; avijñeyam—unknowable; dūra-stham—far away; cha—and; antike—near; cha—and; tat—that
translation_english: The Supreme Truth exists outside and inside of all living beings, the moving and the nonmoving. Because He is subtle, He is beyond the power of the material senses to see or to know. Although far, far away, He is also near to all.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the Supreme exists both outside and inside all beings, is moving and nonmoving, subtle beyond ordinary perception, far away and yet near. This is one of the great contemplative verses of the chapter because it teaches simultaneity where the mind prefers either-or. The Divine is not imprisoned by our categories of distance and location.

To say that God is inside and outside all beings means he is more inward than our inwardness and more expansive than the whole universe around us. He is not one item among other items, somewhere within the cosmic arrangement. He is present at the root of all presence. That is why he may be called near and far at once.

His subtlety also explains why ordinary perception misses him. The problem is not that the Supreme is absent, but that the senses are tuned to gross surfaces and changing forms. What is most foundational can be hardest to notice because it is not one more object standing over against the observer.

For readers, the verse offers both consolation and challenge. Consolation, because the divine reality is never finally absent. Challenge, because nearness does not guarantee recognition. The heart and mind must be refined to perceive what has been close all along while still transcending every local image we form of it.

original_script:
अविभक्तं च भूतेषु विभक्तमिव च स्थितम्।
भूतभर्तृ च तज्ज्ञेयं ग्रसिष्णु प्रभविष्णु च।।13.16।।
transliteration:
avibhaktaṁ cha bhūteṣhu vibhaktam iva cha sthitam
bhūta-bhartṛi cha taj jñeyaṁ grasiṣhṇu prabhaviṣhṇu cha
word_meanings: avibhaktam—without division; cha—also; bhūteṣhu—in all living beings; vibhaktam—divided; iva—as if; cha—also; sthitam—situated; bhūta-bhartṛi—the maintainer of all living entities; cha—also; tat—that; jñeyam—to be understood; grasiṣhṇu—devouring; prabhaviṣhṇu—developing; cha—also
translation_english: Although the Supersoul appears to be divided among all beings, He is never divided. He is situated as one. Although He is the maintainer of every living entity, it is to be understood that He devours and develops all.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna adds that although the Supreme appears divided among beings, he is never truly divided. He is one, sustaining all, devouring all, and bringing all to development. This verse addresses one of the central mysteries of divine presence. The one Lord seems to be present in countless individual lives, yet he does not fragment into pieces the way material things do.

The distinction matters greatly. We might imagine that if God is present in many beings, then the divine must be split into many little portions. Krishna refuses that model. The Supreme remains fully one while being fully present. His sustaining presence in each life does not diminish him or turn him into a collection of fragments.
