Krishna says that when the embodied being transcends the three modes associated with the body, he becomes free from birth, death, old age, and suffering, and tastes nectar even here. This is the fruit toward which the whole chapter has been moving. To go beyond the gunas is not to become blank. It is to recover a freedom deeper than the body's cycle.

Birth, aging, and death belong to embodied life under material nature. The soul suffers them as absolute when it remains identified with the field and governed by the modes. Transcendence does not mean the body vanishes immediately. It means the soul ceases to be existentially imprisoned by what belongs to bodily nature.

The promise of tasting nectar "in this life" is especially significant. Liberation is not postponed entirely to some remote future. Even now, while living in the body, one may begin to experience a freedom, peace, and inward immortality that are not reducible to shifting conditions. The taste comes before the final completion.

For readers, the verse is a powerful encouragement. Krishna is not calling Arjuna to a grim endurance of nature until death. He is pointing to a real transformation of consciousness available here and now, a foretaste of freedom that begins when the modes no longer define the soul's identity.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो।
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते।।14.21।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
kair liṅgais trīn guṇān etān atīto bhavati prabho
kim āchāraḥ kathaṁ chaitāns trīn guṇān ativartate
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; kaiḥ—by which; liṅgaiḥ—symptoms; trīn—three; guṇān—qualities; etān—all these; atītaḥ—having transcended; bhavati—is; prabho—O my Lord; kim—what; āchāraḥ—behavior; katham—how; cha—also; etān—these; trīn—three; guṇān—qualities; ativartate—transcends
translation_english: Arjuna inquired: O my dear Lord, by which symptoms is one known who is transcendental to these three modes? What is his behavior? And how does he transcend the modes of nature?
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna now asks the practical questions that naturally follow: how is the person beyond the three modes recognized, how does such a person behave, and by what means does he transcend them? This is characteristic of Arjuna at his best. He does not leave Krishna's teaching at the level of doctrine. He asks how liberation looks in lived human form.

These questions matter because "going beyond the gunas" could easily remain vague or romantic. Arjuna wants signs, conduct, and process. How would one know that a person is no longer inwardly governed by clarity, restlessness, and dullness in the ordinary binding way? What kind of life does such freedom produce?

There is also an implicit humility in the question. Arjuna is not assuming that transcendence is self-evident. He knows the soul can flatter itself, especially in spiritual matters. Asking for marks and means is a safeguard against illusion. The Gita often works this way: lofty truth is followed by criteria that expose counterfeit spirituality.

For readers, this verse is very useful. It reminds us not to be satisfied with abstract admiration of liberation. We should ask the same questions Arjuna asks: What does freedom actually look like? How does it reshape behavior? And what path leads there in reality, not just in theory?

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव।
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति।।14.22।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
prakāśhaṁ cha pravṛittiṁ cha moham eva cha pāṇḍava
na dveṣhṭi sampravṛittāni na nivṛittāni kāṅkṣhati
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; prakāśham—illumination; cha—and; pravṛittim—attachment; cha—and; moham—illusion; eva cha—also; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu; na dveṣhṭi—does not hate; sampravṛittāni—although developed; na nivṛittāni—nor disappearing; kāṅkṣhati—desires
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O son of Pandu, he who does not hate illumination, attachment and delusion when they are present or long for them when they disappear...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins his answer by saying that the transcendent person does not hate illumination, activity, or delusion when they arise, nor crave them when they subside. This is a subtle and profound opening. The liberated person is not compulsively reactive even toward the modes themselves. He does not cling to sattva, rage against rajas, or panic before tamas in the egoic manner.

This does not mean he cannot distinguish them. On the contrary, he sees them clearly. But seeing clearly is different from entering into emotional bondage toward them. The ordinary mind either grasps or resists. It says, "I must keep this bright state," or "I cannot bear this agitation," or "I am ruined by this dullness." Krishna describes a freedom deeper than those reflexes.

The verse is especially important because even spiritual people often become attached to favorable inner states. They love illumination and hate confusion in a way that still centers the ego. The person beyond the gunas remains steady because he no longer derives identity from the rise and fall of these modes.

For readers, the verse offers a hard but liberating discipline. Learn to observe states without immediate hatred or craving. This is not indifference to growth. It is refusal to let the modes themselves dictate the heart's center. Such freedom is one of the first signs that transcendence is becoming real.

original_script:
उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते।
गुणा वर्तन्त इत्येव योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते।।14.23।।
transliteration:
udāsīnavad āsīno guṇair yo na vichālyate
guṇā vartanta ity eva yo ’vatiṣhṭhati neṅgate
word_meanings: udāsīna-vat—like one neutral; āsīnaḥ—situated; guṇaiḥ—by the modes; yaḥ—who; na—never; vichālyate—is agitated; guṇāḥ—the modes; vartante—are acting; iti eva—knowing thus; yaḥ—who; avatiṣhṭhati—remains; na—never; iṅgate—flickers
translation_english: He who is situated like a neutral observer, who is not agitated by the modes of material nature, and who remains firm, knowing that only the modes are active...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by comparing the liberated person to a neutral witness who remains unmoved, knowing that the modes alone are active. This verse deepens the inner posture of transcendence. The one beyond the gunas does not collapse into the movements of nature. He stands in stable awareness, allowing their operations to be seen without being swallowed by them.

The image of the witness does not encourage apathy. A witness sees accurately. What it rejects is false fusion. When rajas surges or tamas descends, the ordinary self says, "I am this." The liberated one knows instead that a mode is acting in the field. This knowledge creates space between consciousness and compulsion.

To remain firm in that knowledge is no small matter. It requires practice, discernment, and inward rooting. Yet Krishna presents it as central to freedom. Without this witnessing steadiness, the soul remains dragged into every fluctuation of nature as though each one defined reality completely.

For readers, the verse points toward a practical skill of spiritual life: cultivate the observing stance that names what is happening without surrendering identity to it. Such witnessing is not the whole of liberation, but it is a necessary part of loosening the knot by which the modes command us.

original_script:
समदुःखसुखः स्वस्थः समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः।
तुल्यप्रियाप्रियो धीरस्तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः।।14.24।।
मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते।।14.25।।
transliteration:
sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ svasthaḥ sama-loṣhṭāśhma-kāñchanaḥ
tulya-priyāpriyo dhīras tulya-nindātma-sanstutiḥ
mānāpamānayos tulyas tulyo mitrāri-pakṣhayoḥ
sarvārambha-parityāgī guṇātītaḥ sa uchyate
word_meanings: sama—equal; duḥkha—in distress; sukhaḥ—and happiness; sva-sthaḥ—being situated in himself; sama—equally; loṣhṭa—a lump of earth; aśhma—stone; kāñchanaḥ—and gold; tulya—equally disposed; priya—to the dear; apriyaḥ—and the undesirable; dhīraḥ—steady; tulya—equal; nindā—in defamation; ātma-sanstutiḥ—and praise of himself; māna—in honor; apamānayoḥ—and dishonor; tulyaḥ—equal; tulyaḥ—equal; mitra—of friends; ari—and enemies; pakṣhayoḥ—to the parties; sarva—of all; ārambha—endeavors; parityāgī—renouncer; guṇa-atītaḥ—transcendental to the material modes of nature; saḥ—he; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: One who is situated in the self and behaves identically in happiness and distress; who looks upon a clod of dirt, a stone, and a piece of gold with an equal eye; who is equal toward the desirable and the undesirable; who is steady, situated equally well in praise and blame, honor and dishonor; who treats friend and enemy alike; and who has renounced all material endeavors—such a person is said to have transcended the modes of nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then gives a fuller portrait of the one who has transcended the gunas: steady in selfhood, equal in pleasure and pain, regarding earth, stone, and gold alike, even toward praise and blame, honor and dishonor, friend and enemy, and free from self-seeking undertakings. These verses show that transcendence is not hidden in vague inward feelings. It becomes visible in radical equanimity.

Each comparison exposes a domain in which the ordinary ego is easily captured. Pleasure and pain, wealth and worthlessness, reputation and humiliation, ally and opponent, success and loss of status all generate strong reactions. The person beyond the gunas is not numb to reality, but he is no longer inwardly owned by these distinctions. The center of self has shifted elsewhere.

Renouncing undertakings does not mean inactivity in every literal sense. It means freedom from ego-driven projects by which the self seeks to secure importance, identity, or mastery through the field. Action may still occur, but it is no longer feverishly self-referential. The modes have lost their usual handles.

For readers, these verses are demanding because they reveal how much of our lives are governed by valuation and reaction. We constantly sort experience into gain and loss, friend and threat, glitter and dirt. Krishna says transcendence becomes real when those oppositions stop ruling the soul. That is a high measure, but it is a truthful one.

original_script:
मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते।
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते।।14.26।।
transliteration:
māṁ cha yo ’vyabhichāreṇa bhakti-yogena sevate
sa guṇān samatītyaitān brahma-bhūyāya kalpate
word_meanings: mām—unto Me; cha—also; yaḥ—one who; avyabhichāreṇa—unfailing; bhakti-yogena—by devotional service; sevate—renders service; saḥ—he; guṇān—the modes of material nature; samatītya—transcending; etān—all these; brahma-bhūyāya—to be elevated to the Brahman platform; kalpate—becomes eligible
translation_english: One who engages in full devotional service, unfailing in all circumstances, at once transcends the modes of material nature and thus comes to the level of Brahman.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now gives the direct means of transcendence: unwavering devotion to him. The one who serves him through unfailing bhakti at once goes beyond the three modes and comes to the level of Brahman. This is one of the chapter's decisive statements. After all the analysis of material nature, the final release is not achieved by analysis alone. It is achieved through wholehearted relation to Krishna.

This makes deep sense in the logic of the Gita. The gunas belong to material nature. As long as the soul remains oriented within that field alone, even refined detachment may still be limited. Devotion joins the soul to the One who is already beyond the modes. Bhakti is therefore not merely emotional religion added after philosophy. It is the actual bridge beyond nature.

The phrase "unfailing in all circumstances" matters. Devotion transcends the modes when it does not depend entirely on favorable inner weather. If one only remembers God in sattvic clarity, but forgets him in rajas or tamas, the modes still rule. Krishna calls for a steadier belonging than that.

For readers, the verse offers great hope. The path beyond the gunas is not reserved for rare metaphysical specialists. The heart that clings to Krishna with steady devotion is drawn beyond the whole binding machinery of nature. Love becomes liberation because its object is the Lord beyond all modes.

original_script:
ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च।
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च।।14.27।।
transliteration:
brahmaṇo hi pratiṣhṭhāham amṛitasyāvyayasya cha
śhāśhvatasya cha dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya cha
word_meanings: brahmaṇaḥ—of the impersonal brahmajyoti; hi—certainly; pratiṣhṭhā—the rest; aham—I am; amṛitasya—of the immortal; avyayasya—of the imperishable; cha—also; śhāśhvatasya—of the eternal; cha—and; dharmasya—of the constitutional position; sukhasya—of happiness; aikāntikasya—ultimate; cha—also
translation_english: And I am the basis of the impersonal Brahman, which is immortal, imperishable and eternal and is the constitutional position of ultimate happiness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by saying that he is the foundation of the imperishable Brahman, of immortality, eternal dharma, and ultimate bliss. This final verse is enormous in implication. It places Krishna not beneath the impersonal absolute, but as its basis. The chapter ends by anchoring transcendence itself in the Lord.

This means that Brahman is not a reality superior to Krishna into which devotion must finally disappear. Rather, the impersonal aspect of the absolute rests in him. Immortality, imperishability, righteousness, and lasting happiness all have their ground in Krishna's own being. The personal Lord is therefore not a lower religious form. He is the deepest foundation.

The verse also clarifies why devotion transcends the gunas. Bhakti joins the soul to the very source of the Brahman-state. To cling to Krishna is already to cling to the root of freedom, permanence, and bliss. The chapter's movement is therefore complete: from analysis of material qualities to the Lord who stands as the ground beyond them.

For readers, this is a fitting conclusion. The gunas must be understood, observed, purified, and finally surpassed. But the soul does not surpass them by floating into abstraction. It rises by being established in Krishna, the basis of the imperishable and the home of ultimate happiness. That is the chapter's final assurance.

original_script: गुणत्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Guṇa Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of the Division of the Three Gunas
deep_dive_text: Chapter 14 provides the operational manual for the material world. It explains that our minds and actions are constantly manipulated by three invisible ropes (Gunas): Goodness (Sattva), Passion (Rajas), and Ignorance (Tamas). While Goodness is the most illuminating, even it binds the soul with attachment to comfort and knowledge. The ultimate goal is to become a 'Gunatita'—one who observes these modes with total neutrality and transcends them entirely through unalloyed devotion (Bhakti), reaching the supreme, eternal reality.

original_script: अथ पञ्चदशोऽध्यायः - पुरुषोत्तमयोगः
transliteration: Puruṣhottama Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Puruṣha—person; Uttama—supreme; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of the Supreme Person
deep_dive_text: Chapter 15 uses the image of the upside-down banyan tree to describe worldly entanglement and the need for detachment. It then turns to the nature of the individual self, the sustaining presence of the Divine in the world, and the revelation of the Supreme Person who transcends both the changing and the unchanging.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
ऊर्ध्वमूलमधःशाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम्।
छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित्।।15.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
ūrdhva-mūlam adhaḥ-śhākham aśhvatthaṁ prāhur avyayam
chhandānsi yasya parṇāni yas taṁ veda sa veda-vit
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; ūrdhva-mūlam—with roots upward; adhaḥ-śhākham—with branches downward; aśhvattham—a banyan tree; prāhuḥ—is said; avyayam—eternal; chhandānsi—the Vedic hymns; yasya—of which; parṇāni—the leaves; yaḥ—anyone who; tam—that; veda—knows; saḥ—he; veda-vit—the knower of the Vedas
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: It is said that there is an imperishable banyan tree that has its roots upward and its branches down and whose leaves are the Vedic hymns. One who knows this tree is the knower of the Vedas.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna opens the chapter with the striking image of an upside-down banyan tree whose roots are above and whose branches spread below, with the Vedic hymns as its leaves. This is one of the Gita's great symbolic pictures of worldly existence. The tree is "imperishable" not because individual forms within it last forever, but because the process of worldly becoming keeps reproducing itself generation after generation.

The inverted form matters. Its roots are above because the world does not ground itself. Its source lies beyond what it displays. What appears below in the field of experience is sustained from a higher principle. The branches spread downward into the visible order, but the life of the tree comes from a source transcending ordinary sight.

The leaves being the Vedic hymns suggests that the scriptures nourish understanding of this vast structure. Leaves protect and sustain a tree; sacred teaching helps preserve right knowledge of the world and one's place within it. To know this tree truly is therefore to understand the condition of embodied existence: branching multiplicity, hidden source, and ceaseless continuation.

For a reader, the verse teaches that worldly life is not a random mass of disconnected events. It has pattern, nourishment, hidden dependence, and an origin above itself. Yet the image is not meant merely to fascinate. Krishna introduces the tree so that he may soon teach how it is to be cut through. The first step toward freedom is to recognize the structure of what binds.

original_script:
अधश्चोर्ध्वं प्रसृतास्तस्य शाखा
गुणप्रवृद्धा विषयप्रवालाः।
अधश्च मूलान्यनुसन्ततानि
कर्मानुबन्धीनि मनुष्यलोके।।15.2।।
transliteration:
adhaśh chordhvaṁ prasṛitās tasya śhākhā
guṇa-pravṛiddhā viṣhaya-pravālāḥ
adhaśh cha mūlāny anusantatāni
karmānubandhīni manuṣhya-loke
word_meanings: adhaḥ—downward; cha—and; ūrdhvam—upward; prasṛitāḥ—extended; tasya—its; śhākhāḥ—branches; guṇa—by the modes of material nature; pravṛiddhāḥ—developed; viṣhaya—sense objects; pravālāḥ—twigs; adhaḥ—downward; cha—and; mūlāni—roots; anusantatāni—extended; karma—to work; anubandhīni—bound; manuṣhya-loke—in the world of human society
translation_english: The branches of this tree extend downward and upward, nourished by the three modes of material nature. The twigs are the objects of the senses. This tree also has roots going down, and these are bound to the fruitive actions of human society.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna expands the image by saying that the branches of the tree spread upward and downward, nourished by the three gunas, while the sense-objects are its twigs and its lower roots bind human beings through action. The tree now becomes unmistakably the whole network of conditioned life. It grows through the modes of nature and entangles through desire, perception, and karma.

The branches extending both upward and downward show that worldly existence includes higher and lower states, refined and coarse conditions, but all still belong to the same tree. A more elevated birth or clearer mental state does not by itself place one outside the structure. Even upward growth can still remain within bondage if the roots of attachment are untouched.

The sense-objects as twigs is a vivid detail. Twigs are where contact becomes more immediate and proliferating. The senses move constantly outward toward objects, and in that movement the tree renews its hold. The downward roots linked to human action show how karma sinks the structure deeper into life. What we repeatedly pursue becomes another anchoring root.

For readers, this verse is a map of entanglement. We are not bound only by gross evil. We are bound by the ongoing interaction of modes, senses, objects, and action. Krishna wants Arjuna to see that bondage is systemic. It is woven through perception and habit. Freedom will therefore require something more decisive than merely rearranging a few branches.

original_script:
न रूपमस्येह तथोपलभ्यते
नान्तो न चादिर्न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा।
अश्वत्थमेनं सुविरूढमूल-
मसङ्गशस्त्रेण दृढेन छित्त्वा।।15.3।।
ततः पदं तत्परिमार्गितव्यं
यस्मिन्गता न निवर्तन्ति भूयः।
तमेव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये
यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी।।15.4।।
transliteration:
na rūpam asyeha tathopalabhyate
nānto na chādir na cha sampratiṣhṭhā
aśhvattham enaṁ su-virūḍha-mūlam
asaṅga-śhastreṇa dṛiḍhena chhittvā
tataḥ padaṁ tat parimārgitavyaṁ
yasmin gatā na nivartanti bhūyaḥ
tam eva chādyaṁ puruṣhaṁ prapadye
yataḥ pravṛittiḥ prasṛitā purāṇī
word_meanings: na—not; rūpam—the form; asya—of this tree; iha—in this world; tathā—also; upalabhyate—can be perceived; na—never; antaḥ—end; na—never; cha—also; ādiḥ—beginning; na—never; cha—also; sampratiṣhṭhā—the foundation; aśhvattham—banyan tree; enam—this; su-virūḍha—strongly; mūlam—rooted; asaṅga-śhastreṇa—by the weapon of detachment; dṛiḍhena—strong; chhittvā—cutting; tataḥ—thereafter; padam—situation; tat—that; parimārgitavyam—has to be searched out; yasmin—where; gatāḥ—going; na—never; nivartanti—they come back; bhūyaḥ—again; tam—to Him; eva—certainly; cha—also; ādyam—original; puruṣham—the Personality of Godhead; prapadye—surrender; yataḥ—from whom; pravṛittiḥ—the beginning; prasṛitā—extended; purāṇī—very old
translation_english: The real form of this tree cannot be perceived in this world. No one can understand where it ends, where it begins, or where its foundation is. But with determination one must cut down this strongly rooted tree with the weapon of detachment. Thereafter, one must seek that place from which, having gone, one never returns, and there surrender to that Supreme Personality of Godhead from whom everything began and from whom the universal energy has been extending since time immemorial.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now says that the true form of this tree cannot be perceived here as a whole. Its beginning, end, and foundation are not easily grasped. Therefore it must be cut down with the strong weapon of detachment, and then one must seek that supreme state from which there is no return and surrender to the original Person from whom the ancient process of manifestation has flowed.

This is the chapter's turning point. The tree is not given to be admired endlessly. It is given to be severed. The inability to see its full shape means that worldly existence cannot be mastered by ordinary inspection alone. It is too ancient, too branching, too subtle in its roots. Mere analysis of samsara does not free the soul. A sword is needed, and that sword is detachment.

Detachment here is not hatred of the world, nor negligent withdrawal from duty. It is the cutting of possessive clinging, false identity, and fascination with the branching structure of becoming. Once that cut is made, the soul can truly seek the supreme abode rather than endlessly wandering within the tree's extensions.

Krishna also makes clear that liberation is not merely escape from a system. It is return by surrender to the Supreme Person. The goal is personal and ultimate. For readers, this passage is a demand for seriousness. It is not enough to understand bondage poetically. One must take up the inward work of severing attachment and then orient the heart toward the source beyond return.

original_script:
निर्मानमोहा जितसङ्गदोषा
अध्यात्मनित्या विनिवृत्तकामाः।
द्वन्द्वैर्विमुक्ताः सुखदुःखसंज्ञै-
र्गच्छन्त्यमूढाः पदमव्ययं तत्।।15.5।।
transliteration:
nirmāna-mohā jita-saṅga-doṣhā
adhyātma-nityā vinivṛitta-kāmāḥ
dvandvair vimuktāḥ sukha-duḥkha-sañjñair
gachchhanty amūḍhāḥ padam avyayaṁ tat
word_meanings: nirmāna—free from false prestige; mohāḥ—and illusion; jita—having conquered; saṅga—of association; doṣhāḥ—the faults; adhyātma—in spiritual knowledge; nityāḥ—in eternity; vinivṛitta—discarded; kāmāḥ—lusts; dvandvaiḥ—from the dualities; vimuktāḥ—liberated; sukha-duḥkha—happiness and distress; sañjñaiḥ—named; gachchhanti—attain; amūḍhāḥ—unbewildered; padam—situation; avyayam—eternal; tat—that
translation_english: Those who are free from false prestige, illusion and false association, who understand the eternal, who are done with material lust, who are freed from the dualities of happiness and distress, and who, unbewildered, know how to surrender unto the Supreme Person attain to that eternal kingdom.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes the qualities of those who reach that supreme state: freedom from pride, delusion, and false association; understanding of the eternal; release from lust; and steadiness beyond the dualities of pleasure and pain. This verse is important because it shows what detachment actually looks like in human character. Cutting the tree is not a dramatic external gesture. It is an inner purification.

False prestige and delusion stand first because the ego clings most fiercely through self-importance and misperception. As long as a person is invested in maintaining a certain image of self, worldly branching continues. False association means entanglement with influences that reinforce ignorance, vanity, and attachment rather than truth.

Understanding the eternal shifts the center of life. Lust loses its force when the heart is no longer convinced that fulfillment lies in transient grasping. Likewise, the dualities of happiness and distress lose their tyranny when the soul stops reading every changing condition as ultimate reality.

For readers, this verse shows that the path to the supreme abode is not mystical fantasy detached from ethics. It passes through humility, purification of company, freedom from compulsive desire, and steadiness amidst opposites. Krishna is teaching the kind of person who becomes capable of leaving the tree behind. Liberation is not only a destination; it is a transformation already underway in the seeker.

original_script:
न तद्भासयते सूर्यो न शशाङ्को न पावकः।
यद्गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम।।15.6।।
transliteration:
na tad bhāsayate sūryo na śhaśhāṅko na pāvakaḥ
yad gatvā na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṁ mama
word_meanings: na—not; tat—that; bhāsayate—illuminates; sūryaḥ—the sun; na—nor; śhaśha-aṅkaḥ—the moon; na—nor; pāvakaḥ—fire; yat—where; gatvā—going; na—never; nivartante—they come back; tat dhāma—that abode; paramam—supreme; mama—My
translation_english: That supreme abode of Mine is not illumined by the sun or moon, nor by fire or electricity. Those who reach it never return to this material world.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that his supreme abode is not illumined by sun, moon, fire, or any external light, and that those who reach it never return to the material world. This verse points beyond every familiar source of visibility and security. The highest reality is self-luminous. It does not depend on the borrowed lights by which material life is ordinarily known.

The absence of sun and moon should not be read as emptiness. It signifies a mode of existence beyond material mediation. In this world, everything is known through changing conditions and external means. Krishna's abode belongs to another order, one in which truth and presence are not lit from outside because the divine reality itself is the light.

The promise of no return matters deeply. Samsara is marked by recurrence: birth, striving, loss, death, rebirth. Krishna here offers not a temporary refuge within that cycle but a state beyond recurrence. This is why the cutting of the tree is so serious. The soul is not being invited to rearrange its place among the branches, but to seek final release from wandering.

For readers, the verse expands desire. We often settle for brighter branches within the same tree. Krishna speaks of an abode beyond all external dependence and beyond return itself. Such a promise relativizes worldly success. It asks whether we truly seek what cannot be taken away, or whether we remain content with lights that always fade.

original_script:
ममैवांशो जीवलोके जीवभूतः सनातनः।
मनःषष्ठानीन्द्रियाणि प्रकृतिस्थानि कर्षति।।15.7।।
transliteration:
mamaivānśho jīva-loke jīva-bhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ
manaḥ-ṣhaṣhṭhānīndriyāṇi prakṛiti-sthāni karṣhati
word_meanings: mama—My; eva—certainly; anśhaḥ—fragmental particle; jīva-loke—in the world of conditional life; jīva-bhūtaḥ—the conditioned living entity; sanātanaḥ—eternal; manaḥ—with the mind; ṣhaṣhṭhāni—the six; indriyāṇi—senses; prakṛiti—in material nature; sthāni—situated; karṣhati—is struggling hard
translation_english: The living entities in this conditioned world are My eternal fragmental parts. Due to conditioned life, they are struggling very hard with the six senses, which include the mind.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns to the individual soul and says that living beings in this world are his eternal fragmental parts, yet they struggle hard with the six senses, including the mind. This verse beautifully holds together dignity and suffering. The soul is not an accidental product of matter. It belongs to Krishna. Yet in conditioned life it undergoes severe struggle.

To be a fragmental part does not mean the soul is a broken piece cut off from God in a material way. It means that the living being reflects a dependent share in divine reality, conscious and real yet never independent of the Supreme. This gives immense worth to the soul. It also explains why no material satisfaction can completely fulfill it.

The struggle with the senses and mind shows how that dignity becomes obscured in embodiment. The soul is drawn outward, scattered through perception, desire, memory, and reaction. The mind itself becomes one more sense-like force pulling consciousness into the field rather than resting in its source.

For readers, this verse is both consoling and clarifying. The difficulty of human life does not mean the soul is low or meaningless. It means something noble has become entangled. Krishna's teaching preserves hope by reminding us that even under struggle, the soul's deepest identity is in relation to him. Spiritual life begins to heal when that forgotten belonging is remembered.

original_script:
शरीरं यदवाप्नोति यच्चाप्युत्क्रामतीश्वरः।
गृहीत्वैतानि संयाति वायुर्गन्धानिवाशयात्।।15.8।।
transliteration:
śharīraṁ yad avāpnoti yach chāpy utkrāmatīśhvaraḥ
gṛihītvaitāni sanyāti vāyur gandhān ivāśhayāt
word_meanings: śharīram—the body; yat—as; avāpnoti—gets; yat—as; cha api—also; utkrāmati—gives up; īśhvaraḥ—the lord of the body; gṛihītvā—taking; etāni—all these; sanyāti—goes away; vāyuḥ—the air; gandhān—smells; iva—like; āśhayāt—from their source
translation_english: The living entity in the material world carries his different conceptions of life from one body to another as the air carries aromas. Thus he takes one kind of body and again quits it to take another.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the embodied soul carries its impressions and tendencies from one body to another as the wind carries fragrances. This image makes transmigration vivid. The soul does not move through death as a blank abstraction. It carries subtle dispositions, orientations, and karmic patterns that shape future embodiment.

The comparison to fragrance is especially telling. A scent is invisible yet real, subtle yet influential. In the same way, the conceptions and tendencies of life are not gross objects, but they accompany the soul as it departs one body and takes another. The verse therefore explains continuity beneath outward discontinuity.

This teaching also shows why inner life matters so much. Thoughts, desires, attachments, and impressions are not trivial private events that die with the surface body. They participate in the soul's continuing history. What we cultivate becomes part of what we carry.

For readers, the verse can deepen responsibility without producing despair. Nothing is lost in the cheap sense, and nothing wholesome is wasted. The subtle shape of consciousness endures. Krishna wants Arjuna to see that spiritual work undertaken now reaches beyond one visible lifespan. Every act of remembrance, purification, and surrender changes what the soul carries into its next passage.

original_script:
श्रोत्रं चक्षुः स्पर्शनं च रसनं घ्राणमेव च।
अधिष्ठाय मनश्चायं विषयानुपसेवते।।15.9।।
transliteration:
śhrotraṁ chakṣhuḥ sparśhanaṁ cha rasanaṁ ghrāṇam eva cha
adhiṣhṭhāya manaśh chāyaṁ viṣhayān upasevate
word_meanings: śhrotram—ears; chakṣhuḥ—eyes; sparśhanam—touch; cha—and; rasanam—tongue; ghrāṇam—smelling power; eva—also; cha—and; adhiṣhṭhāya—being situated in; manaḥ—mind; cha—also; ayam—he; viṣhayān—sense objects; upasevate—enjoys
translation_english: The living entity, thus taking another gross body, obtains a certain type of ear, eye, tongue, nose and sense of touch, which are grouped about the mind. He thus enjoys a particular set of sense objects.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by saying that the soul, entering another body, takes up a new arrangement of senses around the mind and enjoys a corresponding set of sense objects. The verse shows how embodied experience is configured. The soul does not create the senses from nothing, but it comes into relation with a particular instrument-set through which experience unfolds.

The mind's inclusion is crucial. Perception is not just physical contact through eye, ear, nose, tongue, and skin. The mind organizes, interprets, remembers, compares, and projects. Thus the soul's worldly enjoyment is always mediated through a psycho-physical apparatus, not through pure awareness alone.

This helps explain the variation of embodied lives. Different bodies and mental structures yield different experiential worlds. Yet the soul behind them remains the continuing principle. The outward instrument changes; the witnessing traveler persists. Krishna is training Arjuna to distinguish between the user of the senses and the ever-changing equipment through which use occurs.

For readers, the verse invites disidentification without denial. The senses matter, and the mind profoundly shapes experience, but they are not the final self. They are instruments temporarily assembled around the traveler. Remembering this can soften obsession with bodily configuration and open a more stable perspective on who is actually passing through life's many forms.

original_script:
उत्क्रामन्तं स्थितं वापि भुञ्जानं वा गुणान्वितम्।
विमूढा नानुपश्यन्ति पश्यन्ति ज्ञानचक्षुषः।।15.10।।
transliteration:
utkrāmantaṁ sthitaṁ vāpi bhuñjānaṁ vā guṇānvitam
vimūḍhā nānupaśhyanti paśhyanti jñāna-chakṣhuṣhaḥ
word_meanings: utkrāmantam—quitting the body; sthitam—situated in the body; vā api—either; bhuñjānam—enjoying; vā—or; guṇa-anvitam—under the spell of the modes of material nature; vimūḍhāḥ—foolish persons; na—never; anupaśhyanti—can see; paśhyanti—can see; jñāna-chakṣhuṣhaḥ—those who have the eyes of knowledge
translation_english: The foolish cannot understand how a living entity can quit his body, nor can they understand what sort of body he enjoys under the spell of the modes of nature. But one whose eyes are trained in knowledge can see all this.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the foolish do not perceive the soul's departure, its staying, or its enjoyment under the modes, while those with the eye of knowledge do see. This verse returns to the theme of vision. The realities most decisive for spiritual life are not obvious to ordinary sight. One may watch bodies live and die and still miss what is actually occurring.

The foolishness Krishna names is not lack of technical information alone. It is the condition of consciousness that remains confined to surfaces. Such a person sees only external events and cannot perceive the deeper drama of the soul under nature's spell. Birth becomes only biology, death only dissolution, and experience only material contact.

The eye of knowledge, by contrast, is trained perception. It sees the soul's continuity, its entanglement with the gunas, and the subtle process of embodiment and departure. This is why wisdom in the Gita is never merely conceptual. It changes what one is able to notice.

For readers, the verse is a call to humility. Many things that seem obvious are superficial. If we cannot yet see what Krishna describes, the proper response is not dismissal but training. Knowledge must refine perception until reality becomes more transparent than appearances alone. Spiritual life is, in part, education of the eye.

original_script:
यतन्तो योगिनश्चैनं पश्यन्त्यात्मन्यवस्थितम्।
यतन्तोऽप्यकृतात्मानो नैनं पश्यन्त्यचेतसः।।15.11।।
transliteration:
yatanto yoginaśh chainaṁ paśhyanty ātmany avasthitam
yatanto ’py akṛitātmāno nainaṁ paśhyanty achetasaḥ
word_meanings: yatantaḥ—endeavoring; yoginaḥ—transcendentalists; cha—also; enam—this; paśhyanti—can see; ātmani—in the self; avasthitam—situated; yatantaḥ—endeavoring; api—although; akṛita-ātmānaḥ—those without self-realization; na—does not; enam—this; paśhyanti—see; achetasaḥ—having undeveloped minds
translation_english: The endeavoring transcendentalists, who are situated in self-realization, can see all this clearly. But those whose minds are not developed and who are not situated in self-realization cannot see what is taking place, though they may try to.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna adds that striving seekers established in self-realization can perceive this clearly, but those without disciplined understanding cannot, even if they try. This verse balances encouragement with realism. Vision is possible, but it is not granted to mere curiosity. It requires inner development.

The contrast is important. Both groups may make some kind of effort, but only those grounded in self-realization truly see. This suggests that spiritual perception depends not simply on desire to know, but on purification, steadiness, and actual inward transformation. The untrained mind remains too scattered or too opaque to register subtler realities clearly.

This is not meant to discourage sincere effort. Rather, it explains why progress in the Gita is cumulative. One cannot bypass discipline and expect transparent vision of soul, body, and God. The eye of knowledge becomes sharp through a way of life: restraint, devotion, discernment, and perseverance.

For readers, the verse provides a needed correction to impatience. Spiritual truths are not always hidden because they are far away. Often they are hidden because the instrument of seeing has not yet been prepared. Krishna encourages striving, but he also insists that striving must ripen into self-realization if it is to bear the fruit of actual sight.

original_script:
यदादित्यगतं तेजो जगद्भासयतेऽखिलम्।
यच्चन्द्रमसि यच्चाग्नौ तत्तेजो विद्धि मामकम्।।15.12।।
transliteration:
yad āditya-gataṁ tejo jagad bhāsayate ’khilam
yach chandramasi yach chāgnau tat tejo viddhi māmakam
word_meanings: yat—that which; āditya-gatam—in the sunshine; tejaḥ—splendor; jagat—the whole world; bhāsayate—illuminates; akhilam—entire; yat—that which; chandramasi—in the moon; yat—that which; cha—also; agnau—in fire; tat—that; tejaḥ—splendor; viddhi—understand; māmakam—from Me
translation_english: The splendor of the sun, which dissipates the darkness of this whole world, comes from Me. And the splendor of the moon and the splendor of fire are also from Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now shifts from the journey of the soul to the divine presence in the world, saying that the splendor of the sun, moon, and fire comes from him. This is not a digression. Having spoken of the hidden source above the world-tree, Krishna now teaches Arjuna how to see that source reflected within the world without being trapped by it.

Light is a fitting symbol. Sunlight reveals the world, moonlight governs rhythm and nourishment, fire gives warmth, transformation, and visibility. By claiming these splendors as his own, Krishna shows that whatever illumines, warms, and sustains derives its power from him. Created brilliance is real, but borrowed.

This verse also trains devotion in daily perception. The seeker need not wait for extraordinary visions to remember God. Every dawn, every flame, every calming lunar glow can become an occasion for recognition. Krishna is teaching a contemplative reading of the world in which visible splendor points back to invisible source.

For readers, the verse is especially practical. One often imagines the world and God as competitors for attention. Krishna instead teaches that the finest energies of the world can become reminders of him when seen rightly. The point is not to stop at the splendor, but to let the splendor awaken remembrance of the One from whom it shines.

original_script:
गामाविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्यहमोजसा।
पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः।।15.13।।
transliteration:
gām āviśhya cha bhūtāni dhārayāmy aham ojasā
puṣhṇāmi chauṣhadhīḥ sarvāḥ somo bhūtvā rasātmakaḥ
word_meanings: gām—the planets; āviśhya—entering; cha—also; bhūtāni—the living entities; dhārayāmi—sustain; aham—I; ojasā—by My energy; puṣhṇāmi—I nourish; cha—and; auṣhadhīḥ—vegetables; sarvāḥ—all; somaḥ—the moon; bhūtvā—becoming; rasa-ātmakaḥ—supplying the juice
translation_english: I enter into each planet, and by My energy they stay in orbit. I become the moon and thereby supply the juice of life to all vegetables.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that he enters the planets and sustains them, and that as the moon he nourishes vegetation with life-giving essence. The divine presence now appears not only as radiant glory but as sustaining order. Existence is upheld from within by God's energy; it does not hold itself together independently.

The phrase "I enter" is profound. Krishna is not only a remote creator who sets the system in motion from afar. He is intimately present in the ongoing maintenance of the world. The stability of planets, the rhythms of growth, and the nourishment of life all continue because divine power permeates them.

His association with the moon's nourishing influence also points to subtle providence. Growth is often quiet and gradual, not spectacular. Krishna's sustaining care is not limited to dazzling displays. It includes the gentle, repeated support by which life is fed and matured.

For readers, this verse invites gratitude for the world's ordinary continuities. We often notice dramatic interventions more easily than daily sustenance. Krishna teaches that the very regularities that make life possible are themselves signs of divine immanence. The world persists because it is being held, not because it stands by its own right.

original_script:
अहं वैश्वानरो भूत्वा प्राणिनां देहमाश्रितः।
प्राणापानसमायुक्तः पचाम्यन्नं चतुर्विधम्।।15.14।।
transliteration:
ahaṁ vaiśhvānaro bhūtvā prāṇināṁ deham āśhritaḥ
prāṇāpāna-samāyuktaḥ pachāmy annaṁ chatur-vidham
word_meanings: aham—I; vaiśhvānaraḥ—fire of digestion; bhūtvā—becoming; prāṇinām—of all living entities; deham—in the bodies; āśhritaḥ—situated; prāṇa—the outgoing air; apāna—the downgoing air; samāyuktaḥ—keeping in balance; pachāmi—I digest; annam—foodstuff; chatuḥ-vidham—the four kinds
translation_english: I am the fire of digestion in the bodies of all living entities, and I join with the air of life, outgoing and incoming, to digest the four kinds of foodstuff.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next says that he is the digestive fire in the bodies of living beings, working with the vital airs to process food. This verse brings the divine presence into the most intimate and ordinary bodily level. Not only cosmic order, but bodily sustenance itself unfolds through Krishna's power.

This matters because spiritual vision often fails by splitting sacred from ordinary life. Krishna refuses that split. The same Lord who is the source above the tree and the light of sun and moon is also present in digestion, metabolism, and embodied maintenance. Nothing is too humble to reveal dependence on him.

The verse also undermines pride. We often speak as though we sustain ourselves by our own effort, but even the body's basic power to receive nourishment is not self-grounded. The processes that keep life going are gifts operating beneath conscious control. Krishna's claim here reveals the depth of creaturely dependence.

For readers, this verse can transform daily habits into remembrance. Eating need not remain a purely mechanical act. It can become a moment of awareness that life is being sustained from within by divine power. Krishna teaches that reverence is not reserved for temple or text alone. It may begin at the level of breath, hunger, and the body's hidden fire.

original_script:
सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि सन्निविष्टो
मत्तः स्मृतिर्ज्ञानमपोहनं च।
वेदैश्च सर्वैरहमेव वेद्यो
वेदान्तकृद्वेदविदेव चाहम्।।15.15।।
transliteration:
sarvasya chāhaṁ hṛidi sanniviṣhṭo
mattaḥ smṛitir jñānam apohanaṁ cha
vedaiśh cha sarvair aham eva vedyo
vedānta-kṛid veda-vid eva chāham
word_meanings: sarvasya—of all living beings; cha—and; aham—I; hṛidi—in the heart; sanniviṣhṭaḥ—situated; mattaḥ—from Me; smṛitiḥ—remembrance; jñānam—knowledge; apohanam—forgetfulness; cha—and; vedaiḥ—by the Vedas; cha—also; sarvaiḥ—all; aham—I am; eva—certainly; vedyaḥ—knowable; vedānta-kṛit—the compiler of the Vedanta; veda-vit—the knower of the Vedas; eva—certainly; cha—and; aham—I
translation_english: I am seated in everyone's heart, and from Me come remembrance, knowledge and forgetfulness. By all the Vedas, I am to be known. Indeed, I am the compiler of Vedanta, and I am the knower of the Vedas.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that he is seated in everyone's heart, and that remembrance, knowledge, and forgetfulness arise from him. He adds that all the Vedas point toward him, that he is their true knower and the one who brought Vedanta forth. This is one of the chapter's strongest statements of divine inwardness and scriptural centrality.

To be seated in the heart means that Krishna is present at the center of consciousness, not merely at the edge of existence. Memory, insight, and even forgetfulness unfold under his sovereignty. This does not make the human person a puppet. It means that the very powers by which we know and recall are not self-originating. Conscious life is dependent all the way down.

The connection to the Vedas is equally important. Scripture is not an end in itself. Its true purpose is to lead toward Krishna. One may master words and systems, yet miss the one they are meant to reveal. Krishna therefore stands both as scriptural goal and as the ultimate knower behind scriptural truth.

For readers, the verse is corrective and devotional at once. It redirects learning toward encounter with the Lord and redirects inward life toward gratitude. The heart, the memory, the intellect, and the sacred text all converge in Krishna. Knowledge becomes complete only when it recognizes its own source and aim in him.

original_script:
द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च।
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते।।15.16।।
transliteration:
dvāv imau puruṣhau loke kṣharaśh chākṣhara eva cha
kṣharaḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni kūṭa-stho ’kṣhara uchyate
word_meanings: dvau—two; imau—these; puruṣhau—living entities; loke—in the world; kṣharaḥ—fallible; cha—and; akṣharaḥ—infallible; eva—certainly; cha—and; kṣharaḥ—fallible; sarvāṇi—all; bhūtāni—living entities; kūṭa-sthaḥ—in oneness; akṣharaḥ—infallible; uchyate—is said
translation_english: There are two classes of beings, the fallible and the infallible. In the material world every living entity is fallible, and in the spiritual world every living entity is called infallible.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now speaks of two classes of beings: the fallible and the infallible. In the material realm, beings are fallible because they are subject to change, decline, and conditioned limitation. In the spiritual realm, beings are called infallible because they stand outside that instability. The verse prepares for a major distinction that will culminate in Krishna's revelation as the Supreme Person.

This teaching is subtle. Krishna is not merely dividing good people from bad people. He is distinguishing orders of existence. The fallible are those participating in the mutable, conditioned sphere. The infallible belong to the unfallen spiritual order, beyond the vulnerability characteristic of material embodiment.

By drawing this distinction, Krishna expands Arjuna's map of reality. There is not only this perishable world and the struggling soul within it. There is also a spiritual domain whose mode of being is not subject to the same instability. Yet Krishna is not finished. He will soon say that even beyond these two stands the highest reality.

For readers, the verse teaches that worldly fragility is not the whole story of existence. Fallibility feels total when one lives only among changing things, but Krishna wants the mind to awaken to a wider structure. Material instability and spiritual stability are not equal levels. The soul must learn where it truly belongs.

original_script:
उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युदाहृतः।
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः।।15.17।।
transliteration:
uttamaḥ puruṣhas tv anyaḥ paramātmety udāhṛitaḥ
yo loka-trayam āviśhya bibharty avyaya īśhvaraḥ
word_meanings: uttamaḥ—the best; puruṣhaḥ—personality; tu—but; anyaḥ—another; parama-ātmā—the Supreme Soul; iti—thus; udāhṛitaḥ—is said; yaḥ—who; loka-trayam—the three planetary systems; āviśhya—entering; bibharti—maintains; avyayaḥ—inexhaustible; īśhvaraḥ—the Lord
translation_english: Besides these two, there is the greatest living personality, the Supreme Soul, the imperishable Lord Himself, who has entered the three worlds and is maintaining them.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then says that beyond the fallible and the infallible is the Supreme Person, the imperishable Lord, who enters the three worlds and sustains them. This is the chapter's decisive theological move. Krishna does not stop with the contrast between the mutable world and the stable spiritual order. He reveals the Lord who transcends and upholds both.

The title Supreme Person matters greatly. The ultimate reality is not a mere impersonal principle hovering above all distinctions. It is the highest personal divine reality, inexhaustible and sovereign. At the same time, this Supreme Person enters the worlds. Transcendence does not mean distance. Krishna remains both above all and present within all.

That he maintains the three worlds also connects this verse back to the earlier descriptions of light, nourishment, and bodily fire. The sustaining activity already attributed to Krishna is now grounded in his identity as the highest Person. The one behind all support is not an abstract force but the Lord himself.

For readers, this verse gathers many strands of the Gita together. God is beyond the perishable world, beyond even the unfallen order, yet intimately active within creation. The Supreme is therefore not less than personal, but more. Krishna's revelation here is meant to draw devotion upward into its fullest object.

original_script:
यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः।
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः।।15.18।।
transliteration:
yasmāt kṣharam atīto ’ham akṣharād api chottamaḥ
ato ’smi loke vede cha prathitaḥ puruṣhottamaḥ
word_meanings: yasmāt—because; kṣharam—to the fallible; atītaḥ—transcendental; aham—I am; akṣharāt—beyond the infallible; api—also; cha—and; uttamaḥ—the best; ataḥ—therefore; asmi—I am; loke—in the world; vede—in the Vedic literature; cha—and; prathitaḥ—celebrated; puruṣha-uttamaḥ—as the Supreme Personality
translation_english: Because I am transcendental, beyond both the fallible and the infallible, and because I am the greatest, I am celebrated both in the world and in the Vedas as that Supreme Person.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna explains that because he transcends both the fallible and the infallible and is the greatest, he is known in the world and in the Vedas as the Supreme Person. This verse makes explicit the conclusion toward which the chapter has been moving. The highest name belongs to Krishna because he surpasses every order of dependent being.

The world may know many forms of greatness, and sacred tradition may describe many levels of reality, but Krishna claims a supremacy that relativizes them all. The perishable does not define him. The imperishable created order does not exhaust him. He is celebrated as Purushottama precisely because he is the unsurpassed Person above all conditioned and derivative existence.

This title also resolves a tension that often appears in spiritual thought. Is the highest truth personal or beyond personality? Krishna's answer is not to reduce the Supreme to human limitation, but to reveal personhood at its most absolute and transcendent. The Supreme Person is not one being among others. He is the source and Lord of all.

For readers, the verse invites reverent clarity. Spiritual life need not remain suspended between vague transcendence and concrete devotion. Krishna identifies himself as the highest object of both scriptural recognition and existential surrender. To know him under this title is to understand why devotion is not sentimental excess, but alignment with the structure of reality itself.

original_script:
यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम्।
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत।।15.19।।
transliteration:
yo mām evam asammūḍho jānāti puruṣhottamam
sa sarva-vid bhajati māṁ sarva-bhāvena bhārata
word_meanings: yaḥ—anyone who; mām—Me; evam—thus; asammūḍhaḥ—without a doubt; jānāti—knows; puruṣha-uttamam—the Supreme Personality of Godhead; saḥ—he; sarva-vit—the knower of everything; bhajati—renders devotional service; mām—unto Me; sarva-bhāvena—in all respects; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: Whoever knows Me as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, without doubting, is the knower of everything. He therefore engages himself in full devotional service to Me, O son of Bharata.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the one who knows him as the Supreme Person without doubt is the knower of everything and therefore worships him with whole being. This is not a claim that the devotee suddenly possesses every factual detail in the universe. It means that the central truth by which all other truths find their place has been grasped.

To know Krishna rightly is to know the root of the world-tree, the source of the soul, the sustainer of the cosmos, the ground of scripture, and the Lord beyond all mutable and immutable orders. Once that center is known, reality is no longer interpreted through scattered fragments. Knowledge becomes rightly ordered because its highest reference point is no longer missing.

Wholehearted devotion follows naturally from such knowledge. If Krishna is truly the Supreme Person, then partial allegiance becomes intellectually and spiritually incoherent. Worship is not added after understanding as a separate emotional act. It is the fitting completion of understanding.

For readers, the verse shows why the Gita joins knowledge and bhakti so consistently. Right knowledge culminates in devotion because truth recognized at the highest level evokes surrender, love, and service. If knowing does not move the heart toward Krishna, then something essential in the knowing remains incomplete.

original_script:
इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ।
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत।।15.20।।
transliteration:
iti guhyatamaṁ śhāstram idam uktaṁ mayānagha
etad buddhvā buddhimān syāt kṛita-kṛityaśh cha bhārata
word_meanings: iti—thus; guhya-tamam—the most confidential; śhāstram—scripture; idam—this; uktam—disclosed; mayā—by Me; anagha—O sinless one; etat—this; buddhvā—understanding; buddhi-mān—intelligent; syāt—one becomes; kṛita-kṛityaḥ—the most perfect in his endeavors; cha—and; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: This is the most confidential part of the Vedic scriptures, O sinless one, and it is disclosed now by Me. Whoever understands this will become wise, and his endeavors will know perfection.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by calling this teaching the most confidential part of the scriptures and saying that one who understands it becomes truly wise and fulfills life's purpose. The confidentiality here does not mean secrecy in a merely hidden sense. It means depth. This teaching concerns the innermost truth of world, soul, bondage, transcendence, and the Supreme Person.

Chapter 15 has been compact, but not small. It has moved from the symbolic structure of worldly existence to the weapon of detachment, from the soul's wandering to Krishna's presence in light, nourishment, memory, and scripture, and finally to the revelation of Purushottama. To understand this is to possess a key that orders many other teachings.

The promise of fulfilled endeavor is important. Human life scatters energy into many pursuits, most of them partial and temporary. Krishna says that when this teaching is understood, effort reaches its right end. Wisdom is no longer a loose collection of ideas. It becomes participation in the truth that gives life its true aim.

For readers, the closing verse is both summary and summons. Chapter 15 is not merely to be admired as elegant metaphysics. It is to be understood deeply enough that detachment strengthens, remembrance awakens, and devotion to the Supreme Person becomes the governing center of life. That is the perfection Krishna places before Arjuna.

original_script: पुरुषोत्तमयोगः
transliteration: Puruṣhottama Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of the Supreme Person
deep_dive_text: Chapter 15 reveals the path of cutting the upside-down Banyan tree of material entanglement using the sharp weapon of detachment. It transitions from explaining the transmigration of the soul and the Divine presence sustaining the universe, to the ultimate philosophical revelation: the identity of the Purushottama. By understanding the Supreme Person who transcends both the fallible and infallible worlds, the seeker attains the highest perfection of wisdom and devotion.

original_script: अथ षोडशोऽध्यायः - दैवासुरसम्पद्विभागयोगः
transliteration: Daivāsura Sampad Vibhāga Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Daiva—divine; Asura—demoniac; Sampad—qualities/nature; Vibhāga—division; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of the Divine and Demoniac Natures
deep_dive_text: Chapter 16 draws a sharp, unmistakable line between two fundamentally opposite psychological and spiritual trajectories: the Divine (Daivi) nature and the Demoniac (Asuri) nature. Krishna outlines 26 qualities of a god-conscious person destined for liberation, followed by the toxic traits of the arrogant and materialistic, who bind themselves to endless suffering. This chapter is a stark behavioral mirror, urging the seeker to ruthlessly eliminate ego, anger, and lust, which are declared to be the three gateways to hell.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अभयं सत्त्वसंशुद्धिर्ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः।
दानं दमश्च यज्ञश्च स्वाध्यायस्तप आर्जवम्।।16.1।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
abhayaṁ sattva-sanśhuddhir jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ
dānaṁ damaśh cha yajñaśh cha svādhyāyas tapa ārjavam
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; abhayam—fearlessness; sattva-sanśhuddhiḥ—purification of one's existence; jñāna-yoga—in the yoga of knowledge; vyavasthitiḥ—the situation; dānam—charity; damaḥ—controlling the mind; cha—and; yajñaḥ—performance of sacrifice; cha—and; svādhyāyaḥ—study of Vedic literature; tapaḥ—austerity; ārjavam—simplicity
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Fearlessness; purification of one's existence; cultivation of spiritual knowledge; charity; self-control; performance of sacrifice; study of the Vedas; austerity; simplicity...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna opens the chapter not with a command but with a portrait of divine character. Fearlessness, purity of being, steadfastness in spiritual knowledge, charity, self-control, sacrifice, study, austerity, and simplicity are named as the beginning of the divine nature. This is important because the Gita does not define spirituality by emotion alone or by belief alone. It is seen in the shaping of a whole life.

Fearlessness comes first for good reason. A person ruled by fear clings, distorts, flatters, and compromises truth. Divine life begins when one rests deeply enough in God to stop being driven by insecurity. Purification of existence then follows, suggesting a life gradually made clean of confusion, vanity, and hidden corruption. Study, sacrifice, and austerity show that divine nature is disciplined, not accidental.

The list also balances inward and outward qualities. Charity and sacrifice concern relation to others and to God. Self-control and simplicity concern the ordering of the self. Cultivation of spiritual knowledge ensures that virtue does not become mere social respectability. Krishna is describing a way of being in which thought, worship, conduct, and inward motive are brought into alignment.

For readers, the verse is a corrective to shallow religion. Divine nature is not proved by intensity of language or public display. It is recognized by traits that reduce selfishness, purify motive, and make the person more available to truth. Krishna begins with these qualities because liberation does not grow out of chaos. It grows out of a character being steadily formed toward the good.

original_script:
अहिंसा सत्यमक्रोधस्त्यागः शान्तिरपैशुनम्।
दया भूतेष्वलोलुप्त्वं मार्दवं ह्रीरचापलम्।।16.2।।
transliteration:
ahinsā satyam akrodhas tyāgaḥ śhāntir apaiśhunam
dayā bhūteṣhv aloluptvaṁ mārdavaṁ hrīr achāpalam
word_meanings: ahinsā—nonviolence; satyam—truthfulness; akrodhaḥ—freedom from anger; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; śhāntiḥ—tranquility; apaiśhunam—aversion to fault-finding; dayā—mercy; bhūteṣhu—towards all living entities; aloluptvam—freedom from greed; mārdavam—gentleness; hrīḥ—modesty; achāpalam—steady determination
translation_english: Nonviolence; truthfulness; freedom from anger; renunciation; tranquility; aversion to faultfinding; compassion for all living entities; freedom from covetousness; gentleness; modesty; steady determination...
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues the list of divine qualities with nonviolence, truthfulness, freedom from anger, renunciation, tranquility, aversion to faultfinding, compassion, lack of covetousness, gentleness, modesty, and steadiness. The effect is cumulative. Divine nature is not a single heroic trait. It is a texture of character in which harm, falsehood, agitation, and grasping are progressively weakened.

Several of these qualities belong together. Nonviolence and compassion reveal how the divine disposition treats other beings. Truthfulness and freedom from faultfinding show how speech is purified. Renunciation, lack of covetousness, and tranquility show a heart less driven by hunger for possession and less disturbed by comparison. Gentleness and modesty protect spiritual life from becoming proud under the guise of righteousness.

The inclusion of freedom from anger is significant. Krishna does not deny that strong action may sometimes be required. But divine nature is not dominated by reactive fury. Anger easily claims moral justification while secretly serving wounded ego. The divine person acts from clarity and care, not from the intoxication of wrath.

For readers, this verse offers a searching measure. It is easy to admire large ideals while neglecting daily speech, social habits, and hidden cravings. Krishna brings spirituality down into these details because this is where the soul is actually formed. Divine character appears in how we speak, react, desire, and regard others when no dramatic moment is present.

original_script:
तेजः क्षमा धृतिः शौचमद्रोहो नातिमानिता।
भवन्ति सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातस्य भारत।।16.3।।
transliteration:
tejaḥ kṣhamā dhṛitiḥ śhaucham adroho nāti-mānitā
bhavanti sampadaṁ daivīm abhijātasya bhārata
word_meanings: tejaḥ—vigor; kṣhamā—forgiveness; dhṛitiḥ—fortitude; śhaucham—cleanliness; adrohaḥ—freedom from envy; na—not; ati-mānitā—expectation of honor; bhavanti—are; sampadam—the qualities; daivīm—the transcendental nature; abhijātasya—of one who is born of; bhārata—O son of Bharata
translation_english: Vigor, forgiveness, fortitude, cleanliness, and freedom from envy and from the passion for honor—these transcendental qualities, O son of Bharata, belong to godly men endowed with divine nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna completes the list with vigor, forgiveness, fortitude, cleanliness, freedom from envy, and absence of craving for honor, then says these qualities belong to those born for divine life. This final cluster is important because it prevents the earlier virtues from sounding passive or merely soft. Divine nature includes strength.

Vigor and fortitude show that spiritual goodness is not weakness. The godly person is not limp, indecisive, or morally exhausted. He has energy and endurance. Yet this strength is joined to forgiveness and freedom from envy, which means it does not harden into bitterness or rivalry. Cleanliness suggests both external order and inward transparency, a life less clouded by hidden contamination.

Freedom from craving honor is especially revealing. Many virtuous-seeming actions are secretly driven by the desire to be admired as virtuous. Krishna cuts beneath that. Divine nature does not feed on praise. It can do the good without needing constant recognition. This is one of the clearest signs that ego is losing its central place.

For readers, the verse completes a balanced image of spiritual excellence. The divine person is pure without being fragile, strong without being harsh, forgiving without being weak, and honorable without being hungry for honor. Krishna's portrait is meant both to reassure Arjuna and to show the shape of soul that leads toward freedom.

original_script:
दम्भो दर्पोऽभिमानश्च क्रोधः पारुष्यमेव च।
अज्ञानं चाभिजातस्य पार्थ सम्पदमासुरीम्।।16.4।।
transliteration:
dambho darpo ’bhimānaśh cha krodhaḥ pāruṣhyam eva cha
ajñānaṁ chābhijātasya pārtha sampadam āsurīm
word_meanings: dambhaḥ—pride; darpaḥ—arrogance; abhimānaḥ—conceit; cha—and; krodhaḥ—anger; pāruṣhyam—harshness; eva—certainly; cha—and; ajñānam—ignorance; cha—and; abhijātasya—of one who is born of; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sampadam—the qualities; āsurīm—the demoniac nature
translation_english: Pride, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness and ignorance—these qualities belong to those of demoniac nature, O son of Pritha.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna turns sharply to the opposite side and names the traits of demoniac nature: pride, arrogance, conceit, anger, harshness, and ignorance. The contrast is deliberate. Divine and demoniac are not superficial labels but opposed orientations of the soul. One opens toward truth and liberation; the other contracts around ego and moves toward bondage.

Pride, arrogance, and conceit form a destructive cluster. They all magnify the self, but in slightly different ways. Pride inflates one's importance, arrogance treats others with contempt, and conceit lives in a false estimate of oneself. Together they create an inner atmosphere in which truth becomes hard to receive because the self has become the chief object of devotion.

Anger and harshness then show how this interior disorder spills outward. The demoniac disposition does not merely think wrongly; it wounds. Speech becomes cutting, action becomes aggressive, and other beings are treated as obstacles or instruments. Ignorance is not an innocent lack of information here. It is the darkness in which such a life justifies itself.

For readers, this verse is not meant to create smug distance from "bad people." It is a warning about tendencies that can grow in anyone when ego is left unchecked. Krishna names them plainly because they are spiritually lethal precisely when they are admired as strength, confidence, or realism. The demoniac life begins in self-inflation and ripens in blindness.

original_script:
दैवी सम्पद्विमोक्षाय निबन्धायासुरी मता।
मा शुचः सम्पदं दैवीमभिजातोऽसि पाण्डव।।16.5।।
transliteration:
daivī sampad vimokṣhāya nibandhāyāsurī matā
mā śhuchaḥ sampadaṁ daivīm abhijāto ’si pāṇḍava
word_meanings: daivī—transcendental; sampat—nature; vimokṣhāya—meant for liberation; nibandhāya—for bondage; āsurī—demoniac qualities; matā—are considered; mā—do not; śhuchaḥ—worry; sampadam—nature; daivīm—transcendental; abhijātaḥ—born of; asi—you are; pāṇḍava—O son of Pandu
translation_english: The transcendental qualities are conducive to liberation, whereas the demoniac qualities make for bondage. Do not worry, O son of Pandu, for you are born with the divine qualities.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna states the central contrast of the chapter: divine qualities lead toward liberation, while demoniac qualities lead toward bondage. Then he reassures Arjuna that he is born with divine qualities. This verse gives both doctrine and pastoral care. Krishna is not describing character for curiosity. He is showing that these qualities have destiny attached to them.

Liberation and bondage are not arbitrary rewards assigned from outside. They grow organically from the kind of person one is becoming. A life shaped by humility, purity, truth, compassion, and self-restraint is already moving toward openness to God and freedom from self-caused entanglement. A life shaped by arrogance, cruelty, and delusion is weaving tighter chains around itself.

Krishna's reassurance to Arjuna is also important. The chapter could become crushing if heard only as accusation. But Krishna knows Arjuna's deeper nature and reminds him that the divine disposition is truly his inheritance. Correction is therefore given inside encouragement. Arjuna is not being abandoned to confusion; he is being called to live from what is highest in him.

For readers, the verse teaches that spiritual ethics are not secondary decoration on the path. They are directional. They carry the soul either toward freedom or deeper captivity. At the same time, Krishna's word to Arjuna suggests hope: divine qualities can be affirmed, strengthened, and lived from. The chapter diagnoses danger, but it does so in the service of liberation.

original_script:
द्वौ भूतसर्गौ लोकेऽस्मिन्दैव आसुर एव च।
दैवो विस्तरशः प्रोक्त आसुरं पार्थ मे शृणु।।16.6।।
transliteration:
dvau bhūta-sargau loke ’smin daiva āsura eva cha
daivo vistaraśhaḥ prokta āsuraṁ pārtha me śhṛiṇu
word_meanings: dvau—two; bhūta-sargau—created living beings; loke—in world; asmin—this; daivaḥ—godly; āsuraḥ—demoniac; eva—certainly; cha—and; daivaḥ—the divine; vistaraśhaḥ—at great length; proktaḥ—said; āsuram—the demoniac; pārtha—O son of Pritha; me—from Me; śhṛiṇu—hear
translation_english: O son of Pritha, in this world there are two kinds of created beings. One is called the divine and the other demoniac. I have already explained the divine at length. Now hear from Me of the demoniac.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that in this world there are two broad kinds of created beings, the divine and the demoniac, and that he has already described the divine while now turning to the demoniac. The statement is stark, but its purpose is clarity rather than simplification. Krishna is identifying two fundamental trajectories of human formation.

The chapter should not be read as teaching that people are sealed into unchangeable camps from birth in a fatalistic sense. Rather, Krishna is describing dominant dispositions. Some lives are oriented toward truth, restraint, reverence, and compassion. Others are oriented toward ego, appetite, domination, and denial of moral order. These orientations shape conduct, perception, and destiny.

By speaking so directly, Krishna forces Arjuna to see that spiritual life is not morally neutral. There really are ways of living that degrade the soul. The modern tendency is often to flatten all moral distinctions into preference or psychology. Krishna refuses that. He speaks in terms strong enough to wake discernment.

For readers, this verse is a summons to seriousness. One should ask not only whether one has noble moments, but what direction predominates. Is life bending toward divine order or toward self-exalting disorder? Krishna is not inviting shallow judgment of others. He is demanding clear recognition that the soul can truly grow in opposite directions.

original_script:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च जना न विदुरासुराः।
न शौचं नापि चाचारो न सत्यं तेषु विद्यते।।16.7।।
transliteration:
pravṛittiṁ cha nivṛittiṁ cha janā na vidur āsurāḥ
na śhauchaṁ nāpi chāchāro na satyaṁ teṣhu vidyate
word_meanings: pravṛittim—acting properly; cha—also; nivṛittim—not acting improperly; cha—and; janāḥ—persons; na—never; viduḥ—know; āsurāḥ—of demoniac quality; na—never; śhaucham—cleanliness; na—nor; api—also; cha—and; āchāraḥ—behavior; na—never; satyam—truth; teṣhu—in them; vidyate—there is
translation_english: Those who are demoniac do not know what is to be done and what is not to be done. Neither cleanliness nor proper behavior nor truth is found in them.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins describing the demoniac by saying they do not know what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, and that purity, proper conduct, and truth are absent in them. This is a crucial diagnosis. The demoniac life is not defined first by flamboyant wickedness, but by loss of moral discernment itself.

To not know what should and should not be done means more than occasional confusion. It means the inner compass has been damaged. Conscience is no longer reliable because desire, pride, and self-justification have taken over the work of judgment. Once that happens, impurity and misconduct do not appear shocking. They begin to seem normal or useful.

The absence of truth is equally serious. A person can endure correction only if he still loves truth more than self-protection. The demoniac disposition does not. It bends reality to appetite. This is why proper conduct and inner cleanliness disappear together. Outward disorder grows from inward unwillingness to live under what is real.

For readers, this verse shows that spiritual collapse begins before spectacular wrongdoing. It begins when the ability or willingness to distinguish right from wrong is corroded. Krishna therefore places discernment near the center of moral life. If truth and purity are neglected, destruction follows almost by necessity.

original_script:
असत्यमप्रतिष्ठं ते जगदाहुरनीश्वरम्।
अपरस्परसम्भूतं किमन्यत्कामहैतुकम्।।16.8।।
transliteration:
asatyam apratiṣhṭhaṁ te jagad āhur anīśhvaram
aparaspara-sambhūtaṁ kim anyat kāma-haitukam
word_meanings: asatyam—unreal; apratiṣhṭham—without foundation; te—they; jagat—the cosmic manifestation; āhuḥ—say; anīśhvaram—with no controller; aparaspara—without cause; sambhūtam—arisen; kim anyat—there is no other cause; kāma-haitukam—it is due to lust only
translation_english: They say that this world is unreal, with no foundation, no God in control. They say it is produced of sex desire and has no cause other than lust.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then reveals the worldview of the demoniac: they say the world is unreal in a moral sense, without foundation, without divine governance, and born merely of desire. This is more than bad behavior. It is metaphysical rebellion. The outer life is sustained by an inner creed, whether spoken or implicit.

If reality has no higher ground, no truth built into it, and no Lord above it, then appetite easily becomes the nearest thing to a god. Lust, power, pleasure, and self-interest no longer appear as forces to be disciplined, but as obvious guides. The demoniac view therefore dissolves accountability at its root by denying that there is anything objective to be accountable to.

Krishna is not rejecting inquiry here. He is exposing a worldview that takes fragmentation and desire as final. Such a vision cannot support reverence, self-restraint, or moral seriousness, because it has already stripped the world of sacred order. Once desire becomes the only cause recognized, desire will also become the only law obeyed.

For readers, the verse remains piercingly relevant. Many lives outwardly collapse because inwardly they have accepted some version of this creed: nothing higher, nothing binding, nothing true except wanting. Krishna warns that ideas about the world are never harmless. A false metaphysics becomes a false life.

original_script:
एतां दृष्टिमवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानोऽल्पबुद्धयः।
प्रभवन्त्युग्रकर्माणः क्षयाय जगतोऽहिताः।।16.9।।
transliteration:
etāṁ dṛiṣhṭim avaṣhṭabhya naṣhṭātmāno ’lpa-buddhayaḥ
prabhavanty ugra-karmāṇaḥ kṣhayāya jagato ’hitāḥ
word_meanings: etām—this; dṛiṣhṭim—vision; avaṣhṭabhya—accepting; naṣhṭa—having lost; ātmānaḥ—themselves; alpa-buddhayaḥ—the less intelligent; prabhavanti—flourish; ugra-karmāṇaḥ—engaged in painful activities; kṣhayāya—for destruction; jagataḥ—of the world; ahitāḥ—unbeneficial
translation_english: Following such conclusions, the demoniac, who are lost to themselves and who have no intelligence, engage in unbeneficial, horrible works meant to destroy the world.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that holding such views, the demoniac, lost to themselves and lacking sound understanding, engage in destructive actions that harm the world. This verse shows that false thought does not remain private. Worldview and conduct feed each other. When a person denies moral order and divine truth, action soon becomes predatory or ruinous.

To be "lost to themselves" is a strong phrase. It suggests that demoniac life is not self-possession but self-ruin. The person may appear assertive, powerful, or clever, yet inwardly he has lost contact with what the self is for. Intelligence cut loose from truth becomes destructive because it serves appetite without wisdom.

The damage is social as well as personal. Krishna is clear that such people perform works meant to destroy the world. This includes not only physical violence but all forms of action that corrode trust, truth, justice, and reverence. A soul in rebellion rarely harms only itself.

For readers, the verse is a warning against romanticizing cynical power. There is nothing mature or liberated about clever destruction. When a life is organized around denial of truth and service of appetite, it becomes dangerous. Krishna teaches this so that Arjuna may recognize that moral collapse, when scaled outward, becomes civilizational harm.

original_script:
काममाश्रित्य दुष्पूरं दम्भमानमदान्विताः।
मोहाद्गृहीत्वासद्ग्राहान्प्रवर्तन्तेऽशुचिव्रताः।।16.10।।
transliteration:
kāmam āśhritya duṣhpūraṁ dambha-māna-madānvitāḥ
mohād gṛihītvāsad-grāhān pravartante ’śhuchi-vratāḥ
word_meanings: kāmam—lust; āśhritya—taking shelter of; duṣhpūram—insatiable; dambha—of pride; māna—and false prestige; mada-anvitāḥ—absorbed in the conceit; mohāt—by illusion; gṛihītvā—taking; asat—nonpermanent; grāhān—things; pravartante—they flourish; aśhuchi—to the unclean; vratāḥ—avowed
translation_english: Taking shelter of insatiable lust and absorbed in the conceit of pride and false prestige, the demoniac, thus illusioned, are always sworn to unclean work, attracted by the impermanent.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by saying that the demoniac cling to insatiable lust, are swollen with pride and false prestige, and move toward unclean action under illusion, drawn by the impermanent. The verse identifies desire and vanity as twin engines of corruption. They are never satisfied, so they keep the person in constant outward movement and inward pollution.
