word_meanings: tat—that; iti—thus; anabhisandhāya—without desiring; phalam—the fruitive result; yajña—of sacrifice; tapaḥ—and penance; kriyāḥ—activities; dāna—of charity; kriyāḥ—activities; cha—also; vividhāḥ—various; kriyante—are done; mokṣha-kāṅkṣhibhiḥ—by those who actually desire liberation
translation_english: Without desiring fruitive results, one should perform various kinds of sacrifice, penance and charity with the word tat. The purpose of such transcendental activities is to get free from material entanglement.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next explains "Tat": acts of sacrifice, penance, and charity are performed with this word by those who seek freedom from material entanglement and do not desire fruitive reward. "Tat" means, in effect, "that" - pointing beyond the act, beyond the doer, beyond personal possession, toward the transcendent.

This word has a powerful spiritual effect. It loosens the instinct to say "mine." The act is no longer grasped as a personal project for self-enhancement. By dedicating it with "Tat," the practitioner acknowledges that the true reference point lies beyond egoic ownership. The deed is offered into transcendence rather than folded back into identity-building.

In the context of Chapter 17, this is crucial. So much rajasic and tamasic corruption comes from claiming action, result, and worth as the self's property. "Tat" interrupts that claim. It says that the act belongs to a greater truth than the doer and is not to be measured only by personal gain.

For readers, the verse gives a simple but profound principle: holy action must be released from possessiveness. Whether or not one literally uses the syllable, the inner movement matters. When we act as offering rather than acquisition, a major strand of bondage is weakened. "Tat" names that liberating release.

original_script:
सद्भावे साधुभावे च सदित्येतत्प्रयुज्यते।
प्रशस्ते कर्मणि तथा सच्छब्दः पार्थ युज्यते।।17.26।।
transliteration:
sad-bhāve sādhu-bhāve cha sad ity etat prayujyate
praśhaste karmaṇi tathā sach-chhabdaḥ pārtha yujyate
word_meanings: sat-bhāve—in the sense of the nature of the Supreme; sādhu-bhāve—in the sense of the nature of the devotee; cha—and; sat—the word sat; iti—thus; etat—this; prayujyate—is used; praśhaste—in bona fide; karmaṇi—activities; tathā—also; sat-śhabdaḥ—the sound sat; pārtha—O son of Pritha; yujyate—is used
translation_english: The Absolute Truth is the objective of devotional sacrifice, and it is indicated by the word sat. The performer of such sacrifice is also called sat, as are all works of sacrifice, penance and charity which, true to the absolute nature, are performed to please the Supreme Person, O son of Pritha.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then turns to "Sat," associating it with truth, goodness, reality, and acts performed for the sake of the Supreme. The term also applies to the one who performs such acts. This is an important expansion. "Sat" is not merely a label attached to ritual. It signifies ontological alignment with what is real and good.

Where "Om" consecrates and "Tat" releases ownership, "Sat" affirms authenticity. An act is "sat" when it participates in truth rather than illusion, in sincerity rather than show, in enduring good rather than passing vanity. The performer too can be called "sat" insofar as life is becoming aligned with that reality.

This is why the word has both moral and metaphysical depth. It points to being, truthfulness, and genuine worth all at once. Krishna is showing that sacred action is not magic. It must be real in the deepest sense. The word "Sat" becomes a reminder that what matters is not appearance of religion but actuality of orientation.

For readers, the verse offers a valuable standard. Many things can look respectable or intense. Krishna asks whether they are true. Do they participate in reality, goodness, and devotion, or only in image? "Sat" calls the soul away from pretense and toward substance. It is the chapter's answer to all forms of spiritually colored unreality.

original_script:
यज्ञे तपसि दाने च स्थितिः सदिति चोच्यते।
कर्म चैव तदर्थीयं सदित्येवाभिधीयते।।17.27।।
transliteration:
yajñe tapasi dāne cha sthitiḥ sad iti chochyate
karma chaiva tad-arthīyaṁ sad ity evābhidhīyate
word_meanings: yajñe—in sacrifice; tapasi—in penance; dāne—in charity; cha—also; sthitiḥ—the situation; sat—the Supreme; iti—thus; cha—and; uchyate—is pronounced; karma—work; cha—and; eva—certainly; tat—for that; arthīyam—meant; sat—the Supreme; iti—thus; eva—certainly; abhidhīyate—is indicated
translation_english: All works of sacrifice, penance and charity, performed to please the Supreme Person, are also called sat. And any action dedicated to the Supreme Lord is also called sat.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna continues by saying that steadfastness in sacrifice, penance, and charity is also called "Sat," and so is any action performed for the Supreme. This develops the previous verse by adding perseverance. Truth is not only a quality of isolated acts. It also appears as sustained commitment.

This matters because many actions begin well and then weaken when recognition fails, difficulty increases, or desire changes direction. Steadiness reveals whether the act is truly rooted in the Real. What is done for God endures differently than what is done for mood, vanity, or excitement. "Sat" therefore includes constancy as well as correctness.

Krishna also broadens the scope beyond the formal trio of sacrifice, penance, and charity. Any action dedicated to the Lord may participate in "Sat." The chapter thus returns from ritual classification to the larger vision of consecrated life. What counts is not only isolated sacred moments, but a pattern of action held steadily under divine orientation.

For readers, this verse is both demanding and encouraging. Small acts done steadily for the Supreme may be truer than dramatic acts done for self-display. "Sat" values endurance in the good. It reminds us that holy life is built not only by moments of intensity, but by sustained fidelity in what has been rightly offered.

original_script:
अश्रद्धया हुतं दत्तं तपस्तप्तं कृतं च यत्।
असदित्युच्यते पार्थ न च तत्प्रेत्य नो इह।।17.28।।
transliteration:
aśhraddhayā hutaṁ dattaṁ tapas taptaṁ kṛitaṁ cha yat
asad ity uchyate pārtha na cha tat pretya no iha
word_meanings: aśhraddhayā—without faith; hutam—offered in sacrifice; dattam—given; tapaḥ—penance; taptam—executed; kṛitam—performed; cha—also; yat—that which; asat—false; iti—thus; uchyate—is said to be; pārtha—O son of Pritha; na—never; cha—also; tat—that; pretya—after death; na u—nor; iha—in this life
translation_english: Anything done as sacrifice, charity or penance without faith in the Supreme, O son of Pritha, is impermanent. It is called asat and is useless both in this life and the next.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna closes the chapter by saying that whatever is done as sacrifice, charity, or penance without faith is "asat" - unreal, fruitless, and of no value here or hereafter. This is a severe but fitting ending. After all the distinctions of guna and all the sacred orientation of "Om Tat Sat," Krishna makes clear that faith remains indispensable. Outer action without living trust does not become spiritually real merely by imitation.

"Asat" means more than ineffective. It means lacking true substance. Something may be outwardly impressive, painful, generous, or solemn and yet be hollow before God because it is not animated by genuine faith. The chapter began with Arjuna's question about the nature of faith, and it ends by showing that even correct-looking acts collapse if faith is absent.

This is not a call to sentimentality. Faith in the Gita is not emotional intensity by itself. It is the soul's real orientation toward truth and the Supreme. When that orientation is missing, ritual and discipline lose contact with their source and end. They become religious surfaces without inner reality.

For readers, the closing verse is a final warning against spiritual formalism. Technique, generosity, effort, and suffering are not enough. The heart must truly stand in relation to God. Krishna closes Chapter 17 by insisting that faith is not one ingredient among others. It is what makes sacred action actually live.

original_script: श्रद्धात्रयविभागयोगः
transliteration: Śhraddhā Traya Vibhāga Yogaḥ
translation_english: Recap: The Yoga of the Threefold Division of Faith
deep_dive_text: Chapter 17 shows how faith, food, sacrifice, discipline, and charity are shaped by the three modes of nature. Krishna explains that actions performed in goodness uplift, those driven by passion agitate, and those rooted in ignorance lead downward. He concludes by showing how action is purified when it is offered with sincerity, selflessness, and dedication to the Supreme through 'Om Tat Sat'.

original_script: अथ अष्टादशोऽध्यायः - मोक्षसन्न्यासयोगः
transliteration: Mokṣha Sannyāsa Yogaḥ
word_meanings: Mokṣha—liberation; Sannyāsa—renunciation; Yogaḥ—the path of connection.
translation_english: Overview: The Yoga of Liberation and Renunciation
deep_dive_text: Chapter 18 gathers together the major teachings of the Gita and returns to the question of renunciation. Krishna explains the difference between giving up selfish attachment and abandoning rightful duty, and He reviews how the three modes influence knowledge, action, will, and happiness. The chapter culminates in a final call to surrender to the Divine with trust and devotion.

original_script:
अर्जुन उवाच
सन्न्यासस्य महाबाहो तत्त्वमिच्छामि वेदितुम्।
त्यागस्य च हृषीकेश पृथक्केशिनिषूदन।।18.1।।
transliteration:
arjuna uvācha
sannyāsasya mahā-bāho tattvam ichchhāmi veditum
tyāgasya cha hṛiṣhīkeśha pṛithak keśhi-niṣhūdana
word_meanings: arjunaḥ uvācha—Arjuna said; sannyāsasya—of renunciation; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; tattvam—the truth; ichchhāmi—I wish; veditum—to understand; tyāgasya—of renunciation; cha—also; hṛiṣhīka-īśha—O master of the senses; pṛithak—differently; keśhi-niṣhūdana—O killer of the Keshi demon
translation_english: Arjuna said: O mighty-armed one, I wish to understand the purpose of renunciation [sannyasa] and of the renounced order of life [tyaga], O killer of the Keshi demon, master of the senses.
deep_dive_text:
Arjuna opens the final chapter by asking Krishna to clarify the difference between sannyasa and tyaga, two forms of renunciation that sound similar but are not identical. This is a fitting beginning to the Gita's conclusion. After hearing so much about action, devotion, knowledge, duty, and surrender, Arjuna now wants the whole teaching gathered into a clear understanding of what must truly be relinquished.

The question matters because the path of the Gita has never been mere abandonment of life. Krishna has repeatedly told Arjuna to act. Yet he has also insisted on detachment, renunciation of fruits, and surrender of ego. Arjuna therefore asks what renunciation really means if one must still fight, serve, choose, and live in the world.

This is also a question for every serious seeker. Is freedom found in dropping work, in dropping desire, in dropping ownership, or in some deeper relinquishment of self-centeredness? Krishna is about to show that renunciation is subtler than simply stopping outward action.

For readers, the verse signals that the final teaching of the Gita will not discard earlier chapters. It will gather them. The issue is not whether life should cease, but how action, duty, and offering can be purified so that the soul is no longer inwardly bound by what it must outwardly do.

original_script:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासं सन्न्यासं कवयो विदुः।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं प्राहुस्त्यागं विचक्षणाः।।18.2।।
transliteration:
śhrī bhagavān uvācha
kāmyānāṁ karmaṇāṁ nyāsaṁ sannyāsaṁ kavayo viduḥ
sarva-karma-phala-tyāgaṁ prāhus tyāgaṁ vichakṣhaṇāḥ
word_meanings: śhrī-bhagavān uvācha—the Supreme Personality of Godhead said; kāmyānām—with desire; karmaṇām—of activities; nyāsam—renunciation; sannyāsam—the renounced order of life; kavayaḥ—the learned; viduḥ—know; sarva—of all; karma—activities; phala—of results; tyāgam—renunciation; prāhuḥ—call; tyāgam—renunciation; vichakṣhaṇāḥ—the experienced
translation_english: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: The giving up of activities that are based on material desire is what great learned men call the renounced order of life [sannyasa]. And giving up the results of all activities is what the wise call renunciation [tyaga].
deep_dive_text:
Krishna begins by distinguishing the two terms. Sannyasa, he says, is the giving up of desire-driven acts, while tyaga is the renunciation of the fruits of all action. This distinction is central to the whole chapter. It shows that not every renunciation is identical and that the Gita's teaching is more nuanced than mere withdrawal.

To give up actions rooted in material craving is one thing. To perform necessary action while relinquishing claim over its results is another. Krishna honors both ideas, but the second will prove especially important for Arjuna, because he cannot simply abandon all action. He must learn how to act without selfish possession.

This verse also clarifies a major Gita principle: bondage does not arise only from acting. It arises from desire, attachment, and the ego's claim over outcome. Therefore the deepest renunciation is not always of the deed itself, but of the craving and ownership entwined with it.

For readers, the teaching is practical. Many people imagine freedom lies in changing circumstances dramatically, leaving jobs, roles, or responsibilities. Krishna points deeper. Sometimes what most needs to be relinquished is not the field of action, but the possessive structure of desire around it. That is why tyaga can be so spiritually powerful.

original_script:
त्याज्यं दोषवदित्येके कर्म प्राहुर्मनीषिणः।
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यमिति चापरे।।18.3।।
transliteration:
tyājyaṁ doṣha-vad ity eke karma prāhur manīṣhiṇaḥ
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyam iti chāpare
word_meanings: tyājyam—must be given up; doṣha-vat—as an evil; iti—thus; eke—one group; karma—work; prāhuḥ—they say; manīṣhiṇaḥ—great thinkers; yajña—of sacrifice; dāna—charity; tapaḥ—and penance; karma—works; na—never; tyājyam—are to be given up; iti—thus; cha—and; apare—others
translation_english: Some learned men declare that all kinds of fruitive activities should be given up as faulty, yet other sages maintain that acts of sacrifice, charity and penance should never be abandoned.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna notes that some thinkers hold all fruitive action to be faulty and therefore to be abandoned, while others insist that sacrifice, charity, and austerity should never be given up. By beginning with disagreement, Krishna shows that renunciation has always been a matter requiring discernment. Serious people have reached different conclusions because the issue is not simple.

Those who wish to abandon all fruitive activity are reacting to a real problem: desire-driven action easily binds the soul. Those who insist on preserving sacrifice, charity, and penance also see something true: certain actions purify rather than degrade when rightly performed. The difficulty lies in distinguishing between activity that binds and activity that refines.

Krishna's mention of this debate prepares Arjuna to hear a more complete judgment. The answer will not be a flat yes or no to work. It will involve motive, mode, duty, and the interior relation one bears to action. The Gita refuses simplistic solutions.

For readers, the verse offers an intellectual virtue often missing in spiritual discussion: patience. Complex truths require more than slogans. Renunciation is not solved by saying "all action is bad" or "all action is necessary." Krishna is about to sort the matter at a deeper level where both activity and relinquishment can be seen rightly.

original_script:
निश्चयं शृणु मे तत्र त्यागे भरतसत्तम।
त्यागो हि पुरुषव्याघ्र त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः।।18.4।।
transliteration:
niśhchayaṁ śhṛiṇu me tatra tyāge bharata-sattama
tyāgo hi puruṣha-vyāghra tri-vidhaḥ samprakīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: niśhchayam—certainty; śhṛiṇu—hear; me—from Me; tatra—therein; tyāge—in the matter of renunciation; bharata-sat-tama—O best of the Bharatas; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; hi—certainly; puruṣha-vyāghra—O tiger among human beings; tri-vidhaḥ—of three kinds; samprakīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: O best of the Bharatas, now hear My judgment about renunciation. O tiger among men, renunciation is declared in the scriptures to be of three kinds.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now gives his own judgment: renunciation is of three kinds. This means that even giving up can be governed by goodness, passion, or ignorance. The act of renouncing something is therefore not automatically wise. One must ask from what state of consciousness the renunciation arises.

This is a crucial Gita move. Human beings often romanticize renunciation itself. Leaving, refusing, or abstaining can look spiritual from the outside. But Krishna insists that renunciation, like food, sacrifice, knowledge, and faith, must be interpreted through the gunas. It may purify, or it may merely express confusion, vanity, or fear.

By classifying renunciation threefold, Krishna prevents Arjuna from mistaking outer austerity for inner freedom. The same visible action may be sattvic in one person, rajasic in another, tamasic in another. The mode determines the spiritual meaning.

For readers, this verse offers a needed correction to dramatic spirituality. Not everything abandoned is wisely abandoned. Sometimes people renounce because they are clear and detached; sometimes because they are tired, frightened, angry, or confused. Krishna asks us to evaluate not only what is being left, but the quality of the consciousness doing the leaving.

original_script:
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यं कार्यमेव तत्।
यज्ञो दानं तपश्चैव पावनानि मनीषिणाम्।।18.5।।
transliteration:
yajña-dāna-tapaḥ-karma na tyājyaṁ kāryam eva tat
yajño dānaṁ tapaśh chaiva pāvanāni manīṣhiṇām
word_meanings: yajña—of sacrifice; dāna—charity; tapaḥ—and penance; karma—activity; na—never; tyājyam—to be given up; kāryam—must be done; eva—certainly; tat—that; yajñaḥ—sacrifice; dānam—charity; tapaḥ—penance; cha—also; eva—certainly; pāvanāni—purifying; manīṣhiṇām—even for the great souls
translation_english: Acts of sacrifice, charity and penance are not to be given up; they must be performed. Indeed, sacrifice, charity and penance purify even the great souls.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that sacrifice, charity, and austerity should not be abandoned, because they purify even the wise. This is a strong statement. It means that certain forms of disciplined, Godward action remain necessary even for those serious about liberation. Spiritual life is not completed by rejecting all formal practice.

These three acts matter because they strike at major forms of self-centeredness. Sacrifice trains offering. Charity loosens possessiveness. Austerity disciplines appetite and impulse. Together they help free the soul from ego's ordinary patterns. That is why Krishna says they purify even great souls rather than only beginners.

The verse also balances previous warnings. Renunciation is real, but it does not mean disdain for all practice. Some actions are vehicles of purification when done rightly. The point is not to become inactive, but to allow action to serve freedom instead of craving.

For readers, the verse is clarifying. Genuine spirituality rarely grows through mere negation. One must not only stop doing what degrades the soul; one must also keep doing what purifies it. Krishna preserves disciplined sacred practice because the heart usually needs repeated forms of offering, giving, and restraint if it is to be made transparent to truth.

original_script:
एतान्यपि तु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च।
कर्तव्यानीति मे पार्थ निश्चितं मतमुत्तमम्।।18.6।।
transliteration:
etāny api tu karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā phalāni cha
kartavyānīti me pārtha niśhchitaṁ matam uttamam
word_meanings: etāni—all these; api—even; tu—but; karmāṇi—activities; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; phalāni—the results; cha—also; kartavyāni—should be done as duty; iti—thus; me—My; pārtha—O son of Pritha; niśhchitam—definite; matam—opinion; uttamam—supreme
translation_english: All these activities should be performed without attachment or any expectation of result. They should be performed as a matter of duty, O son of Pritha. That is My final opinion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna immediately adds that these purifying actions must be performed without attachment and without desire for reward. That is his final judgment. Sacrifice, charity, and austerity are not to be abandoned, but their motive must be purified. This verse is one of the clearest summaries of karma-yoga in the whole Gita.

The act remains; the possessiveness is removed. One still gives, still worships, still disciplines oneself, but no longer in order to build ego, purchase merit, or secure worldly gain. Duty becomes offering rather than transaction. This is the form of renunciation Krishna consistently upholds for Arjuna.

Calling this his final opinion matters. Krishna is not leaving the issue open. He is clarifying that the heart of tyaga lies in detachment from result, not in reckless abandonment of what is spiritually or ethically necessary. The deed is purified by the surrender of its selfish claim.

For readers, this verse is enormously practical. Most people do not need less action so much as purer intention in action. Krishna teaches a way of living in which necessary deeds remain, but the chains attached to them are cut. Renunciation becomes inward freedom in the midst of obligation.

original_script:
नियतस्य तु सन्न्यासः कर्मणो नोपपद्यते।
मोहात्तस्य परित्यागस्तामसः परिकीर्तितः।।18.7।।
transliteration:
niyatasya tu sannyāsaḥ karmaṇo nopapadyate
mohāt tasya parityāgas tāmasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: niyatasya—prescribed; tu—but; sannyāsaḥ—renunciation; karmaṇaḥ—of activities; na—never; upapadyate—is proper; mohāt—by illusion; tasya—of them; parityāgaḥ—renunciation; tāmasaḥ—in the mode of ignorance; parikīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: Prescribed duties should never be renounced. If one gives up his prescribed duties because of illusion, such renunciation is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that giving up prescribed duty out of delusion is renunciation in the mode of ignorance. Here tamasic renunciation is named plainly. One may leave a duty not from clarity, but from confusion about what duty is or why it matters. Such abandonment is not freedom. It is spiritual darkness.

Prescribed duty matters because it locates a person within moral and relational order. To reject it blindly is to reject a field in which growth, service, and purification are meant to occur. The ignorant person often calls this freedom, but Krishna says it is illusion. He is not escaping bondage; he is refusing the very discipline that could mature him.

This verse is especially important for Arjuna. Walking away from the battlefield out of delusion would not have been lofty nonviolence. It would have been tamasic refusal of duty. Krishna is therefore exposing the danger of using renunciation language to excuse confusion.

For readers, the verse is a warning against spiritualized irresponsibility. Duties can be hard, complex, and painful, but abandoning them is not automatically wisdom. If the mind is clouded, retreat can become one more form of bondage. Krishna asks first for clarity, then for renunciation within duty, not flight from duty under the cover of vague spirituality.

original_script:
दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत्।
स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत्।।18.8।।
transliteration:
duḥkham ity eva yat karma kāya-kleśha-bhayāt tyajet
sa kṛitvā rājasaṁ tyāgaṁ naiva tyāga-phalaṁ labhet
word_meanings: duḥkham—unhappy; iti—thus; eva—certainly; yat—which; karma—work; kāya—for the body; kleśha—trouble; bhayāt—out of fear; tyajet—gives up; saḥ—he; kṛitvā—after doing; rājasam—in the mode of passion; tyāgam—renunciation; na—not; eva—certainly; tyāga-phalam—the results of renunciation; labhet—gains
translation_english: Anyone who gives up prescribed duties as troublesome or out of fear of bodily discomfort is said to have renounced in the mode of passion. Such action never leads to the elevation of renunciation.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next describes rajasic renunciation: giving up duty because it is troublesome or because one fears bodily discomfort. This kind of renunciation looks more active than tamasic confusion, yet it is still self-centered. The body and its convenience remain the hidden authority.

Rajas is always concerned with the self's felt condition. It wants what is pleasant, successful, and manageable. When duty becomes painful, strenuous, or costly, the rajasic person withdraws and calls the withdrawal reasonable. Yet Krishna says such renunciation never yields the true fruit of renunciation, because it was motivated by attachment all along.

This is subtle. The person has indeed given something up, but what he has protected is precisely the ego's comfort. Therefore no real relinquishment has occurred at the deepest level. He has not renounced attachment; he has obeyed it. The apparent sacrifice hides a stronger clinging beneath it.

For readers, this verse is a searching mirror. How often do we justify withdrawal because a task is draining, costly, or inconvenient, when in fact we are simply unwilling to bear discomfort? Krishna does not glorify needless suffering, but he insists that renunciation motivated by fear of pain is not yet spiritual freedom.

original_script:
कार्यमित्येव यत्कर्म नियतं क्रियतेऽर्जुन।
सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव स त्यागः सात्त्विको मतः।।18.9।।
transliteration:
kāryam ity eva yat karma niyataṁ kriyate ’rjuna
saṅgaṁ tyaktvā phalaṁ chaiva sa tyāgaḥ sāttviko mataḥ
word_meanings: kāryam—duty; iti—thus; eva—indeed; yat—which; karma—work; niyatam—prescribed; kriyate—is performed; arjuna—O Arjuna; saṅgam—attachment; tyaktvā—giving up; phalam—the result; cha—and; eva—certainly; saḥ—that; tyāgaḥ—renunciation; sāttvikaḥ—in the mode of goodness; mataḥ—is considered
translation_english: O Arjuna, when one performs his prescribed duty only because it ought to be done, and renounces all material association and all attachment to the fruit, his renunciation is said to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then defines sattvic renunciation: performing prescribed duty because it ought to be done, while relinquishing attachment and the fruit. This is the form of renunciation he wants Arjuna to embody. Duty remains, but craving and possessiveness are surrendered.

The phrase "because it ought to be done" is important. It locates action in objective order rather than in mood, comfort, or result. The sattvic renouncer does not need the act to flatter him or guarantee success. He acts because the act is right. This gives action steadiness and dignity.

At the same time, attachment and fruit are renounced. The doer does not cling to identity through the deed, nor does he demand a particular outcome as the condition of his peace. This combination of obligation and detachment is the mature middle path of the Gita.

For readers, the verse offers a concrete template for freedom in ordinary life. One need not wait to leave the world behind. One may begin now by doing what should be done and letting go of the anxious, possessive self that tries to own the result. This is renunciation at the level where most of us actually live.

original_script:
न द्वेष्ट्यकुशलं कर्म कुशले नानुषज्जते।
त्यागी सत्त्वसमाविष्टो मेधावी छिन्नसंशयः।।18.10।।
transliteration:
na dveṣhṭy akuśhalaṁ karma kuśhale nānuṣhajjate
tyāgī sattva-samāviṣhṭo medhāvī chhinna-sanśhayaḥ
word_meanings: na—never; dveṣhṭi—hates; akuśhalam—inauspicious; karma—work; kuśhale—in auspicious work; na—nor; anuṣhajjate—becomes attached; tyāgī—the renouncer; sattva—in goodness; samāviṣhṭaḥ—absorbed; medhāvī—intelligent; chhinna—cut; sanśhayaḥ—all doubts
translation_english: The intelligent renouncer situated in the mode of goodness, neither hateful of inauspicious work nor attached to auspicious work, has no doubts about work.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that the intelligent renouncer established in goodness neither hates unpleasant work nor clings to pleasant work and is free from doubt. This verse reveals the emotional stability of true renunciation. The sattvic person is no longer governed by preference in the old compulsive way.

Ordinarily we avoid what feels distasteful and cling to what feels favorable. Krishna says the mature renouncer has moved beyond that reflex as the governing principle of action. He can perform what is difficult without aversion and what is attractive without possessiveness. This freedom indicates that duty, not like and dislike, has become central.

Freedom from doubt also matters. The mind that is always bargaining with pleasure and pain cannot become steady. When attachment loosens, clarity strengthens. One sees more simply what is to be done without being endlessly clouded by private preference.

For readers, the verse shows that renunciation is not grim suppression but a reordering of love and aversion. The goal is not to feel nothing. It is to stop making attraction and resistance into final authorities. When that happens, the mind grows cleaner and action becomes more trustworthy.

original_script:
न हि देहभृता शक्यं त्यक्तुं कर्माण्यशेषतः।
यस्तु कर्मफलत्यागी स त्यागीत्यभिधीयते।।18.11।।
transliteration:
na hi deha-bhṛitā śhakyaṁ tyaktuṁ karmāṇy aśheṣhataḥ
yas tu karma-phala-tyāgī sa tyāgīty abhidhīyate
word_meanings: na—never; hi—certainly; deha-bhṛitā—by the embodied; śhakyam—is possible; tyaktum—to renounce; karmāṇi—activities; aśheṣhataḥ—entirely; yaḥ—anyone who; tu—but; karma-phala—of the result of work; tyāgī—the renouncer; saḥ—he; tyāgī—a renouncer; iti—thus; abhidhīyate—is said
translation_english: It is indeed impossible for an embodied being to give up all activities. But he who renounces the fruits of action is called one who has truly renounced.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then states that for embodied beings total cessation of action is impossible, but one who renounces the fruits of action is truly renounced. This is one of the Gita's most decisive corrections to fantasies of absolute withdrawal. As long as one lives in a body, action continues in some form.

Breathing, speaking, choosing, moving, maintaining, relating, and responding all belong to embodied life. The question is not whether one will act, but how. Krishna therefore relocates renunciation from impossible physical nonaction to the spiritual surrender of claim over result. This keeps the teaching realistic and profound at once.

The verse also protects against hypocrisy. A person may outwardly withdraw while inwardly teeming with desire, fantasy, and self-concern. Another may remain active but inwardly free of possessiveness. Krishna says the latter understands renunciation more deeply than the former.

For readers, the teaching is liberating. One need not wait for some imagined state of total inactivity in order to begin spiritual life. Renunciation is available wherever fruits are surrendered. If action is inevitable, then the path lies in purifying relation to action rather than dreaming of conditions under which action would disappear altogether.

original_script:
अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम्।
भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु सन्न्यासिनां क्वचित्।।18.12।।
transliteration:
aniṣhṭam iṣhṭaṁ miśhraṁ cha tri-vidhaṁ karmaṇaḥ phalam
bhavaty atyāgināṁ pretya na tu sannyāsināṁ kvachit
word_meanings: aniṣhṭam—undesirable; iṣhṭam—desirable; miśhram—mixed; cha—and; tri-vidham—of three kinds; karmaṇaḥ—of work; phalam—the result; bhavati—comes; atyāginām—for those who are not renounced; pretya—after death; na—not; tu—but; sannyāsinām—for the renounced order; kvachit—at any time
translation_english: For one who is not renounced, the threefold fruits of action—desirable, undesirable and mixed—accrue after death. But those who are in the renounced order of life have no such result to suffer or enjoy.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that those who have not renounced experience three kinds of fruit after death - pleasant, unpleasant, and mixed - whereas true renouncers are not bound to such results. This verse makes the karmic stakes explicit. Attachment to fruits does not merely trouble the mind in the present. It carries consequences forward.

The threefold fruit reflects the mixed moral texture of ordinary life. Some actions ripen sweetly, some painfully, many ambiguously. As long as the ego remains attached to ownership and result, it remains implicated in that entire structure of consequence. Renunciation cuts the bond by which the self keeps claiming what action produces.

This does not mean the renouncer becomes magically beyond all causal order in a crude sense. It means he is no longer inwardly appropriating action as "mine" in the same binding way. The karmic knot is loosened because the deepest claim has been released.

For readers, the verse reinforces why Krishna keeps returning to fruit-renunciation. Outcome is not a small matter. So long as the heart is organized around getting, fearing, and owning results, it remains vulnerable to the whole cycle of karma. Freedom grows as the demand to possess those fruits is surrendered to God.

original_script:
पञ्चैतानि महाबाहो कारणानि निबोध मे।
साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते प्रोक्तानि सिद्धये सर्वकर्मणाम्।।18.13।।
transliteration:
pañchaitāni mahā-bāho kāraṇāni nibodha me
sāṅkhye kṛitānte proktāni siddhaye sarva-karmaṇām
word_meanings: pañcha—five; etāni—these; mahā-bāho—O mighty-armed one; kāraṇāni—causes; nibodha—just understand; me—from Me; sāṅkhye—in the Vedanta; kṛita-ante—in the conclusion; proktāni—said; siddhaye—for the perfection; sarva-karmaṇām—of all activities
translation_english: O mighty-armed Arjuna, according to the Vedanta there are five causes for the accomplishment of all action. Now learn of these from Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now shifts the discussion from renunciation to doership, saying that according to Vedantic analysis there are five causes of any action. This move is crucial. If renunciation is to be real, the ego's illusion of being the sole doer must be corrected. Otherwise fruit may be verbally renounced while self-authorship remains intact.

The verse also shows that right action depends on right metaphysics. We act badly not only because we desire wrongly, but because we understand action wrongly. To imagine that the individual ego alone originates and completes action is to ignore the larger field in which action occurs.

By introducing five causes, Krishna prepares Arjuna to see the complexity of agency. The embodied self participates in action, but not alone and not in isolation from body, senses, circumstance, effort, and the divine presence. Renunciation deepens when this false simplicity is broken.

For readers, the verse is a reminder that humility often begins in accurate analysis. The ego likes short stories in which it is everything. Krishna interrupts that story. To understand action well is already to loosen the pride and anxiety that come from imagining oneself to be the solitary author of all outcomes.

original_script:
अधिष्ठानं तथा कर्ता करणं च पृथग्विधम्।
विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टा दैवं चैवात्र पञ्चमम्।।18.14।।
transliteration:
adhiṣhṭhānaṁ tathā kartā karaṇaṁ cha pṛithag-vidham
vividhāśh cha pṛithak cheṣhṭā daivaṁ chaivātra pañchamam
word_meanings: adhiṣhṭhānam—the place (the body); tathā—also; kartā—the doer (the soul); karaṇam—the senses; cha—and; pṛithak-vidham—of different kinds; vividhāḥ—various; cha—and; pṛithak—separate; cheṣhṭāḥ—endeavors; daivam—the Supreme Lord (destiny); cha—and; eva—certainly; atra—here; pañchamam—the fifth
translation_english: The place of action [the body], the performer, the various senses, the many different kinds of endeavor, and ultimately the Supersoul—these are the five factors of action.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna lists the five causes of action: the body as the field or seat of action, the individual doer, the senses and instruments, the various efforts involved, and finally the Supersoul. This is a remarkably balanced account of agency. It neither dissolves the person into impersonal process nor exaggerates the person into absolute authorship.

The body matters because action is embodied. The senses matter because action uses instruments. Effort matters because intention and exertion are real. The individual doer matters because personal participation is not denied. Yet the Supersoul also matters, because no action unfolds outside divine oversight and sustaining presence. Krishna inserts God into the analysis without erasing creaturely responsibility.

The verse therefore protects against two opposite errors: materialism, which forgets the divine dimension, and egoism, which forgets dependence and complexity. The human person acts, but only within a much larger structure. To ignore that structure is to misunderstand what action actually is.

For readers, this verse is deeply sobering and freeing. We are involved in what we do, but we are not isolated originators. Recognizing the layered nature of action can reduce pride when things go well and despair when they do not. Krishna is teaching a truer humility, one rooted in reality rather than self-denial.

original_script:
शरीरवाङ्मनोभिर्यत्कर्म प्रारभते नरः।
न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा पञ्चैते तस्य हेतवः।।18.15।।
transliteration:
śharīra-vāṅ-manobhir yat karma prārabhate naraḥ
nyāyyaṁ vā viparītaṁ vā pañchaite tasya hetavaḥ
word_meanings: śharīra—by the body; vāk—speech; manobhiḥ—and mind; yat—whatever; karma—work; prārabhate—begins; naraḥ—a person; nyāyyam—right; vā—or; viparītam—the opposite; vā—or; pañcha—five; ete—all these; tasya—its; hetavaḥ—causes
translation_english: Whatever right or wrong action a man performs by body, mind or speech is caused by these five factors.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna says that whatever action a person performs by body, speech, or mind - whether right or wrong - is accomplished through these five factors. This universalizes the previous analysis. There is no special category of action exempt from the structure Krishna has described. Even moral and immoral deeds alike arise within a layered field.

This matters because the ego prefers to simplify favorable action into "I did it" and blame unfavorable action entirely on something else. Krishna refuses both moves. All actions, noble or corrupt, occur through body, mind, instruments, effort, and the larger presence of the divine witness and permitter. The analysis holds regardless of moral quality.

At the same time, this does not flatten ethical difference. Right and wrong remain real. Krishna's point is not that all action is morally the same, but that no action is metaphysically simple. Understanding this is essential for mature responsibility. One can acknowledge layered causality without dissolving moral seriousness.

For readers, the verse teaches careful self-knowledge. We do not become wiser by pretending we are pure victims of circumstance, nor by imagining we are isolated sovereigns. Krishna trains a more exact sight: action is real, accountability is real, but both unfold within a broader structure than the ego usually admits.

original_script:
तत्रैवं सति कर्तारमात्मानं केवलं तु यः।
पश्यत्यकृतबुद्धित्वान्न स पश्यति दुर्मतिः।।18.16।।
transliteration:
tatraivaṁ sati kartāram ātmānaṁ kevalaṁ tu yaḥ
paśhyaty akṛita-buddhitvān na sa paśhyati durmatiḥ
word_meanings: tatra—there; evam—thus; sati—being; kartāram—the doer; ātmānam—himself; kevalam—only; tu—but; yaḥ—anyone who; paśhyati—sees; akṛita-buddhitvāt—due to unintelligence; na—never; saḥ—he; paśhyati—sees; durmatiḥ—foolish
translation_english: Therefore one who thinks himself the only doer, not considering the five factors, is certainly not very intelligent and cannot see things as they are.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna concludes that one who sees himself as the sole doer does not truly see. This is the direct strike against egoic authorship. The problem is not that the person participates in action, but that he absolutizes his participation and ignores the field, the instruments, the modes, and the divine presence.

To be the "only doer" in one's own imagination is spiritually dangerous for several reasons. It breeds pride when outcomes seem successful. It breeds crushing anxiety when outcomes are uncertain. It breeds resentment when other causes interrupt personal plans. Most of all, it obscures the true relation between the soul and God.

This blindness is especially relevant on the battlefield. Arjuna is tempted to imagine that everything will rest absolutely on his personal choice and force. Krishna is teaching that such a view is too narrow. One must act fully, but not under the illusion of solitary authorship.

For readers, the verse is a major corrective to modern individualism. Many forms of misery come from imagining that the self stands alone as absolute maker of life. Krishna does not remove responsibility. He removes delusion. The more clearly the ego sees its partial place, the more freedom it gains from self-inflation and self-torment.

original_script:
यस्य नाहङ्कृतो भावो बुद्धिर्यस्य न लिप्यते।
हत्वापि स इमाँल्लोकान्न हन्ति न निबध्यते।।18.17।।
transliteration:
yasya nāhaṅkṛito bhāvo buddhir yasya na lipyate
hatvāpi sa imānl lokān na hanti na nibadhyate
word_meanings: yasya—one whose; na—never; ahaṅkṛitaḥ—of false ego; bhāvaḥ—nature; buddhiḥ—intelligence; yasya—of whom; na—never; lipyate—is attached; hatvā—killing; api—even; saḥ—he; imān—this; lokān—world; na—never; hanti—kills; na—never; nibadhyate—becomes entangled
translation_english: One who is not motivated by false ego, whose intelligence is not entangled, though he kills men in this world, does not kill. Nor is he bound by his actions.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then says that one who is free from false ego and whose intelligence is not entangled is not truly bound even when involved in killing. This verse can only be heard correctly in context. It is not a blanket permission for violence. It is a statement about action performed without egoic authorship in a situation where duty truly requires force.

The key lies in freedom from false ego. The person is not acting from hatred, vanity, or private appetite. He is not inwardly claiming "I am the independent slayer." He acts in alignment with dharma within the larger field Krishna has been describing. Therefore the deepest bondage of action does not adhere in the same way.

This verse directly addresses Arjuna's crisis. War here is not being romanticized. Rather, Krishna is clarifying that morally serious action in a tragic world cannot be judged solely by surface appearance. The heart, the motive, the duty, and the metaphysical structure all matter.

For readers, the verse should evoke reverence and caution, not casualness. It teaches that bondage depends on ego and attachment more deeply than on outward motion alone. But it also teaches that such freedom is rare and demands great purity. One should never rush to claim the condition Krishna describes without the humility and discipline that alone could make it real.

original_script:
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना।
करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसङ्ग्रहः।।18.18।।
transliteration:
jñānaṁ jñeyaṁ parijñātā tri-vidhā karma-chodanā
karaṇaṁ karma karteti tri-vidhaḥ karma-saṅgrahaḥ
word_meanings: jñānam—knowledge; jñeyam—the objective of knowledge; parijñātā—the knower; tri-vidhā—of three kinds; karma-chodanā—the impetus for work; karaṇam—the senses; karma—the work; kartā—the doer; iti—thus; tri-vidhaḥ—of three kinds; karma-saṅgrahaḥ—the accumulation of work
translation_english: Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower are the three factors that motivate action; the senses, the work and the doer are the three constituents of action.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next analyzes what moves action: knowledge, the object known, and the knower are the three motives, while the senses, the action itself, and the doer are the three constituents. This verse shows that action does not arise out of nowhere. It is generated by a structure of knowing and engaging.

Knowledge shapes how a situation is interpreted. The object gives action something toward which it moves. The knower stands in relation to both. Then the senses and instruments carry action out, and the doer participates in the process. Krishna is mapping the anatomy of action at a fine-grained level so that Arjuna can see how complex human doing really is.

This analysis matters spiritually because it shows that purification of action requires more than correcting outward behavior. Knowledge must be purified. The object of pursuit must be examined. The doer must be cleansed of ego. Otherwise the same outer act may continue to bind because the inner machinery remains unchanged.

For readers, the verse suggests that ethical and spiritual growth demand attention at multiple levels. It is not enough to ask, "What did I do?" One must also ask, "What did I see? What did I seek? From what self did I act?" Krishna's careful breakdown helps expose the deeper roots from which action grows.

original_script:
ज्ञानं कर्म च कर्ता च त्रिधैव गुणभेदतः।
प्रोच्यते गुणसङ्ख्याने यथावच्छृणु तान्यपि।।18.19।।
transliteration:
jñānaṁ karma cha kartā cha tridhaiva guṇa-bhedataḥ
prochyate guṇa-saṅkhyāne yathā-vach chhṛiṇu tāny api
word_meanings: jñānam—knowledge; karma—action; cha—and; kartā—the worker; cha—and; tridhā—of three kinds; eva—certainly; guṇa-bhedataḥ—in terms of different modes of material nature; prochyate—is said; guṇa-saṅkhyāne—in the analytical study of the modes; yathā-vat—as they are; śhṛiṇu—hear; tāni—all of them; api—also
translation_english: According to the three different modes of material nature, there are three kinds of knowledge, action and performer of action. Now hear of them from Me.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now announces that knowledge, action, and the doer are each threefold according to the modes of nature. This is an important transition. The chapter moves from the structure of action in general to the quality of action under the gunas. Not every kind of knowing, doing, or agency is equal.

The threefold analysis matters because outward similarity can conceal inward difference. Two people may perform similar tasks while operating under very different knowledge and motive. One may act from clarity, another from ambition, another from delusion. The gunas are the hidden coloration of the whole process.

Krishna's method remains consistent across the Gita. He does not evaluate spiritual life by appearances alone. He repeatedly returns to inner quality, mode, intention, and relation to truth. This prevents superficial moralism and forces a deeper discernment.

For readers, the verse signals that what follows is diagnostic. We are being taught how to read the quality of consciousness expressed in knowledge, action, and agency. This can be uncomfortable, because it means self-examination must go past the visible deed into the mode governing it. But that discomfort is part of wisdom.

original_script:
सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते।
अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम्।।18.20।।
transliteration:
sarva-bhūteṣhu yenaikaṁ bhāvam avyayam īkṣhate
avibhaktaṁ vibhakteṣhu taj jñānaṁ viddhi sāttvikam
word_meanings: sarva-bhūteṣhu—in all living entities; yena—by which; ekam—one; bhāvam—spiritual nature; avyayam—imperishable; īkṣhate—one sees; avibhaktam—undivided; vibhakteṣhu—in the numberless divided; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; viddhi—know; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: That knowledge by which one undivided spiritual nature is seen in all living entities, though they are divided into innumerable forms, you should understand to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna first describes sattvic knowledge as that by which one sees a single undivided spiritual reality present in all beings despite their many forms. This is one of the most beautiful definitions of true knowledge in the Gita. It unites metaphysical depth with ethical consequence.

The many bodies and personalities are not denied. Difference remains real at the empirical level. But sattvic knowledge does not stop at the level of division. It sees that the same underlying spiritual principle is present throughout life. This vision loosens arrogance, contempt, and possessive exclusiveness because the heart no longer treats outward form as ultimate.

Such knowledge is not vague sameness. It is an intelligent perception of unity that includes rather than erases multiplicity. Krishna calls it goodness because it clarifies reality without violence to either truth or difference. It is knowledge that produces reverence rather than domination.

For readers, the verse offers a test of real spiritual understanding. Does our knowledge make us more capable of seeing one sacred ground across many forms, or does it intensify separateness and pride? Sattvic knowledge expands the soul into a vision of unity rich enough to support compassion and restraint.

original_script:
पृथक्त्वेन तु यज्ज्ञानं नानाभावान्पृथग्विधान्।
वेत्ति सर्वेषु भूतेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि राजसम्।।18.21।।
transliteration:
pṛithaktvena tu yaj jñānaṁ nānā-bhāvān pṛithag-vidhān
vetti sarveṣhu bhūteṣhu taj jñānaṁ viddhi rājasam
word_meanings: pṛithaktvena—by division; tu—but; yat—which; jñānam—knowledge; nānā-bhāvān—various natures; pṛithak-vidhān—different kinds; vetti—knows; sarveṣhu—in all; bhūteṣhu—living entities; tat—that; jñānam—knowledge; viddhi—know; rājasam—in the mode of passion
translation_english: That knowledge by which one sees that in every different body there is a different type of living entity you should understand to be in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes rajasic knowledge as that which sees separate beings in separate bodies as though their distinctness were the deepest truth. This is not sheer falsehood, because multiplicity is certainly present. The error lies in absolutizing difference and losing sight of the deeper unity beneath it.

Rajas thrives on division because division feeds comparison, competition, desire, and fear. When others are seen only as separate units, relation becomes transactional. One life stands over against another as ally, rival, tool, or threat. The field of existence becomes fragmented into competing centers of interest.

This form of knowledge is rajasic because it fuels action driven by grasping and self-advancement. It does not have to be loudly immoral to be spiritually limited. It simply cannot see widely enough. It remains trapped in multiplicity as final.

For readers, the verse invites a subtle self-check. Much ordinary thought is rajasic not because it is obviously vicious, but because it cannot see beyond difference into shared spiritual reality. Krishna is teaching that partial vision can still bind. To know rightly, one must learn to perceive more than separateness.

original_script:
यत्तु कृत्स्नवदेकस्मिन्कार्ये सक्तमहैतुकम्।
अतत्त्वार्थवदल्पं च तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्।।18.22।।
transliteration:
yat tu kṛitsna-vad ekasmin kārye saktam ahaitukam
atattvārtha-vad alpaṁ cha tat tāmasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; tu—but; kṛitsna-vat—as all in all; ekasmin—in one; kārye—work; saktam—attached; ahaitukam—without cause; atattva-artha-vat—without knowledge of reality; alpam—very meager; cha—and; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of darkness; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: And that knowledge by which one is attached to one kind of work as the all in all, without knowledge of the truth, and which is very meager, is said to be in the mode of darkness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then describes tamasic knowledge as narrow, attached to one fragment as if it were the whole, lacking truth, and very small in scope. This is knowledge degraded into fixation. The mind locks onto one limited object or viewpoint and loses all proportion.

This is darker than rajasic multiplicity. Rajasic knowledge at least recognizes many things, even if it misreads their relation. Tamasic knowledge becomes cramped and stubborn. It mistakes a part for the whole and resists wider understanding. Such a mind can become fanatical, superstitious, or simply inert in its inability to see beyond its chosen fragment.

The term "meager" or "small" is important. Ignorance is not only about being wrong. It is about being shrunken. The world of the tamasic mind contracts until only one obsession, one crude task, one local attachment, or one distorted belief fills the horizon. Truth cannot enter because there is no room left.

For readers, the verse warns against mental narrowing as a spiritual danger. Whenever we absolutize one fragment of life and refuse all broader truth, tamas is near. Krishna's remedy is not mere accumulation of facts, but liberation from the cramped attachment that mistakes its own tiny corner for reality itself.

original_script:
नियतं सङ्गरहितमरागद्वेषतः कृतम्।
अफलप्रेप्सुना कर्म यत्तत्सात्त्विकमुच्यते।।18.23।।
transliteration:
niyataṁ saṅga-rahitam arāga-dveṣhataḥ kṛitam
aphala-prepsunā karma yat tat sāttvikam uchyate
word_meanings: niyatam—regulated; saṅga-rahitam—without attachment; arāga-dveṣhataḥ—without love or hatred; kṛitam—done; aphala-prepsunā—by one without desire for fruitive result; karma—action; yat—which; tat—that; sāttvikam—in the mode of goodness; uchyate—is called
translation_english: That action which is regulated and which is performed without attachment, without love or hatred, and without desire for fruitive results is said to be in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes sattvic action as regulated, performed without attachment, without love or hatred, and without craving for results. This is action disciplined by order and freed from emotional compulsion. It is not passive action, but purified action.

Regulation matters because the sattvic person does not act on whim alone. He acts within dharma, under guidance, and with due proportion. Freedom from attachment means that action is not used to secure selfhood. Freedom from love and hatred in this context means that preference no longer dictates the whole field. One can do what is required without being dragged by attraction or aversion.

The absence of result-craving seals the quality. Sattvic action is effective and serious, yet it is not inwardly chained to outcome. It remains clean enough to serve the good rather than ego. This is one reason the Gita repeatedly returns to detached action as spiritually liberating.

For readers, the verse offers a robust picture of holy work. It is not merely what one does, but how. If action is ordered, steady, and released from private grasping, it begins to carry the flavor of goodness. Such action can become a field of freedom rather than another layer of bondage.

original_script:
यत्तु कामेप्सुना कर्म साहङ्कारेण वा पुनः।
क्रियते बहुलायासं तद्राजसमुदाहृतम्।।18.24।।
transliteration:
yat tu kāmepsunā karma sāhaṅkāreṇa vā punaḥ
kriyate bahulāyāsaṁ tad rājasam udāhṛitam
word_meanings: yat—that which; tu—but; kāma-īpsunā—by one with desires for fruitive results; karma—work; sa-ahaṅkāreṇa—with false ego; vā—or; punaḥ—again; kriyate—is performed; bahula-āyāsam—with great labor; tat—that; rājasam—in the mode of passion; udāhṛitam—is said to be
translation_english: But action performed with great effort by one seeking to gratify his desires, and enacted from a sense of false ego, is called action in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes rajasic action as work undertaken with great strain for the sake of desire and carried out from ego. The action may be energetic and impressive, but its interior source is restless self-seeking. Passion does not lack force; it lacks purity.

The effort here is important. Rajasic people often work intensely. Krishna is not condemning intensity itself. He is revealing that intensity driven by craving and self-importance creates bondage. The doer pours himself into the act, but the act becomes an extension of the ego's hunger rather than an offering governed by truth.

False ego distorts the whole field. One wants success not simply because something good is at stake, but because identity feels bound up with achievement. Then action becomes feverish. The soul is no longer free to serve. It must prove, acquire, and secure itself through what it does.

For readers, the verse is a useful warning against glorifying overexertion. Not every hard-working life is sattvic. Sometimes strain itself is a symptom that desire and ego are driving the system. Krishna is calling not for lethargy, but for action purified of frantic self-assertion.

original_script:
अनुबन्धं क्षयं हिंसामनपेक्ष्य च पौरुषम्।
मोहादारभ्यते कर्म यत्तत्तामसमुच्यते।।18.25।।
transliteration:
anubandhaṁ kṣhayaṁ hinsām anapekṣhya cha pauruṣham
mohād ārabhyate karma yat tat tāmasam uchyate
word_meanings: anubandham—of future bondage; kṣhayam—destruction; hinsām—and distress to others; anapekṣhya—without considering the consequences; cha—and; pauruṣham—self-sanctioned ability; mohāt—by illusion; ārabhyate—is begun; karma—work; yat—which; tat—that; tāmasam—in the mode of ignorance; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: That action performed in illusion, in disregard of scriptural injunctions, and without concern for future bondage or for violence or distress caused to others is said to be in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna calls tamasic that action which is undertaken in delusion, ignoring consequence, bondage, harm, and one's own capacity. This is reckless action. It is not only morally wrong in result; it is spiritually dark in method, because it proceeds without real perception of what is being done.

The tamasic doer does not ask what the act will produce, whom it may injure, whether it accords with truth, or whether he is even fit to carry it out. This is not courage. It is blindness made active. Tamas can remain lazy, but it can also act in ways that are destructive precisely because it is so unconscious.

The disregard for violence is especially serious. Ignorance often numbs the mind to the cost imposed on others. A person acts for impulse, habit, or dull compulsion, and only later is ruin seen. By then the field has already been damaged.

For readers, the verse is a reminder that not all boldness is admirable. Some action is dark because it refuses foresight, responsibility, and compassion. Krishna teaches that wisdom asks not only whether we can act, but whether we see enough to act without becoming agents of avoidable harm.

original_script:
मुक्तसङ्गोऽनहंवादी धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योर्निर्विकारः कर्ता सात्त्विक उच्यते।।18.26।।
transliteration:
mukta-saṅgo ’nahaṁ-vādī dhṛity-utsāha-samanvitaḥ
siddhy-asiddhyor nirvikāraḥ kartā sāttvika uchyate
word_meanings: mukta-saṅgaḥ—liberated from all material association; anaham-vādī—without false ego; dhṛiti—with determination; utsāha—and great enthusiasm; samanvitaḥ—qualified; siddhi—in perfection; asiddhyoḥ—and failure; nirvikāraḥ—without change; kartā—the worker; sāttvikaḥ—in the mode of goodness; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: One who performs his duty without association with the material modes of nature, without false ego, with great determination and enthusiasm, and without wavering in success or failure is said to be a worker in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna next describes the sattvic worker: free from attachment and ego, steady with determination and enthusiasm, and unchanged by success or failure. This is a beautiful portrait of mature agency. The worker is not inert or indifferent. He is energetic, but inwardly free.

Determination and enthusiasm are important because goodness is not dull. The sattvic worker acts with life and strength. Yet because attachment and false ego have been weakened, that energy is not unstable. Success does not inflate him, and failure does not undo him. He can keep serving because identity is not riding on outcome.

This kind of worker is rare and valuable. He combines zeal with humility, perseverance with detachment. Krishna is showing that true spiritual maturity does not make a person useless in the world. It makes him unusually dependable because he is less distorted by the emotional weather of results.

For readers, the verse provides a strong practical ideal. We often think we must choose between passionate engagement and inner freedom. Krishna says that in goodness the two can coexist. One can work wholeheartedly while remaining unowned by success and uncrushed by failure.

original_script:
रागी कर्मफलप्रेप्सुर्लुब्धो हिंसात्मकोऽशुचिः।
हर्षशोकान्वितः कर्ता राजसः परिकीर्तितः।।18.27।।
transliteration:
rāgī karma-phala-prepsur lubdho hinsātmako ’śhuchiḥ
harṣha-śhokānvitaḥ kartā rājasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
word_meanings: rāgī—very much attached; karma-phala—the fruit of the work; prepsuḥ—desiring; lubdhaḥ—greedy; hinsā-ātmakaḥ—always envious; aśhuchiḥ—unclean; harṣha-śhoka-anvitaḥ—subject to joy and sorrow; kartā—such a worker; rājasaḥ—in the mode of passion; parikīrtitaḥ—is declared
translation_english: The worker who is attached to work and the fruits of work, desiring to enjoy those fruits, and who is greedy, always envious, impure, and moved by joy and sorrow, is said to be in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes the rajasic worker as attached, desirous of the fruits, greedy, envious, impure, and tossed between joy and sorrow. This is the worker as emotional captive. He does act, often vigorously, but the act constantly feeds and wounds the ego.

Attachment to fruit means that the outcome determines the doer's inward state. Greed makes enough impossible. Envy poisons relationship with others doing similar work. Impurity suggests that mixed motive and self-interest run through the whole pattern. Joy and sorrow then alternate according to visible success and failure, leaving the person unstable.

This is rajasic because the worker is still being driven by gain, comparison, and possession. His labor is not free. It is psychologically expensive because every act becomes a referendum on self-worth. The soul is drained even when externally productive.

For readers, the verse is highly recognizable. Many ambitious lives bear these marks. Krishna's analysis helps explain why outward achievement so often fails to produce peace. If the worker remains attached, greedy, and emotionally dependent on result, labor becomes another vehicle of suffering. Freedom requires more than success. It requires purification of the worker.

original_script:
अयुक्तः प्राकृतः स्तब्धः शठो नैष्कृतिकोऽलसः।
विषादी दीर्घसूत्री च कर्ता तामस उच्यते।।18.28।।
transliteration:
ayuktaḥ prākṛitaḥ stabdhaḥ śhaṭho naiṣhkṛitiko ’lasaḥ
viṣhādī dīrgha-sūtrī cha kartā tāmasa uchyate
word_meanings: ayuktaḥ—not referring to the scriptural injunctions; prākṛitaḥ—materialistic; stabdhaḥ—obstinate; śhaṭhaḥ—deceitful; naiṣhkṛitikaḥ—expert in insulting others; alasaḥ—lazy; viṣhādī—morose; dīrgha-sūtrī—procrastinating; cha—also; kartā—worker; tāmasaḥ—in the mode of ignorance; uchyate—is said to be
translation_english: The worker who is always engaged in work against the injunctions of the scripture, who is materialistic, obstinate, cheating and expert in insulting others, and who is lazy, always morose and procrastinating is said to be a worker in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna finally describes the tamasic worker as undisciplined, vulgar, obstinate, deceitful, malicious, lazy, despondent, and procrastinating. This is a devastating portrait because it shows ignorance as a whole style of agency. The person is not merely uninformed. He is inwardly disordered in a way that makes sound action nearly impossible.

Notice the combination: obstinacy without clarity, deceit without intelligence, laziness without peace, and despondency without repentance. Tamas does not simply slow action. It distorts the worker's whole stance toward reality. Delay becomes habit, gloom becomes atmosphere, and responsibility becomes something to evade or resent.

Krishna includes malice and insult because tamasic agency is not always passive. It may resist the good with bitterness even while lacking the energy to pursue anything noble. This gives the portrait its particular darkness: heavy, evasive, and quietly harmful.

For readers, the verse is a warning not to underestimate the spiritual seriousness of inertia and gloom when they harden into character. Procrastination, bitterness, and habitual disorder can become a mode of bondage, not just a personality quirk. Krishna names these traits plainly because clarity is the first mercy offered to the tamasic soul.

original_script:
बुद्धेर्भेदं धृतेश्चैव गुणतस्त्रिविधं शृणु।
प्रोच्यमानमशेषेण पृथक्त्वेन धनञ्जय।।18.29।।
transliteration:
buddher bhedaṁ dhṛiteśh chaiva guṇatas tri-vidhaṁ śhṛiṇu
prochyamānam aśheṣheṇa pṛithaktvena dhanañjaya
word_meanings: buddheḥ—of intelligence; bhedam—the differences; dhṛiteḥ—of determination; cha—and; eva—certainly; guṇataḥ—by the modes of material nature; tri-vidham—of three kinds; śhṛiṇu—just hear; prochyamānam—as described by Me; aśheṣheṇa—in detail; pṛithaktvena—differently; dhanañjaya—O winner of wealth
translation_english: O winner of wealth, now please hear in detail the different kinds of understanding and determination, according to the three modes of material nature.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna now turns to understanding and determination, saying that both are also threefold according to the modes. This is an important shift. Until now he has classified knowledge, action, and the worker. But even before action is taken, two inner powers shape it: buddhi, the faculty of discernment, and dhrti, the power of sustained resolve.

This matters because a person may know something abstractly and still fail to judge rightly in concrete situations. Likewise, one may judge rightly and still lack the firmness to remain aligned with that judgment. Understanding without resolve becomes fragile; resolve without true understanding becomes dangerous. Krishna therefore examines both.

The verse also keeps the Gita psychologically realistic. Human beings are not moved by doctrine alone. They are guided by how they interpret life and by what they can hold themselves to over time. The gunas color both these powers, which is why moral and spiritual life can vary so widely.

For readers, the verse offers a valuable framework for self-examination. When life goes astray, the problem may lie in distorted understanding, weak resolve, or both. Krishna's threefold analysis helps locate the deeper issue. That is why this section is so practical: it diagnoses not only what we do, but how we decide and persist.

original_script:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च कार्याकार्ये भयाभये।
बन्धं मोक्षं च या वेत्ति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी।।18.30।।
transliteration:
pravṛittiṁ cha nivṛittiṁ cha kāryākārye bhayābhaye
bandhaṁ mokṣhaṁ cha yā vetti buddhiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
word_meanings: pravṛittim—deserving action; cha—and; nivṛittim—deserving cessation; cha—and; kārya—what ought to be done; akārye—and what ought not to be done; bhaya—fear; abhaye—and fearlessness; bandham—bondage; mokṣham—liberation; cha—and; yā—that which; vetti—knows; buddhiḥ—understanding; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sāttvikī—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that understanding by which one knows what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, what is to be feared and what is not to be feared, what is binding and what is liberating, is in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes sattvic understanding as the capacity to discern what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, what is to be feared and not feared, and what leads to bondage or liberation. This is moral and spiritual intelligence at its best. It is not mere cleverness, but right discrimination in relation to ultimate consequence.

The range of distinctions matters. Sattvic understanding sees duty clearly, but it also sees danger clearly. It knows that some things appearing attractive are spiritually harmful, while some difficult things are necessary. It can distinguish short-term discomfort from real danger, and temporary pain from true bondage.

Most importantly, this understanding keeps liberation in view. It does not judge action only by social success or private preference. It asks what frees the soul and what tightens its chains. This gives it a clarity unavailable to the mind governed merely by appetite or fear.

For readers, the verse offers a high but concrete ideal. True wisdom is not vague spirituality. It is the ability to judge life under the light of truth. Where sattvic understanding grows, confusion decreases because one begins to see not only what is pleasant or unpleasant, but what is actually right, safe, binding, and liberating.

original_script:
यया धर्ममधर्मं च कार्यं चाकार्यमेव च।
अयथावत्प्रजानाति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ राजसी।।18.31।।
transliteration:
yayā dharmam adharmaṁ cha kāryaṁ chākāryam eva cha
ayathāvat prajānāti buddhiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; dharmam—the principles of religion; adharmam—irreligion; cha—and; kāryam—what ought to be done; cha—and; akāryam—what ought not to be done; eva—certainly; cha—also; ayathā-vat—imperfectly; prajānāti—knows; buddhiḥ—intelligence; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rājasī—in the mode of passion
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that understanding which cannot distinguish between religion and irreligion, between action that should be done and action that should not be done, is in the mode of passion.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes rajasic understanding as that which cannot properly distinguish dharma from adharma and what should or should not be done. This is not total blindness, but unstable and self-serving discernment. The rajasic mind does not lack all intelligence; it lacks clarity because desire interferes with judgment.

Passion distorts understanding by making the person see through the lens of gain, attachment, and urgency. Duties become negotiable if they threaten ambition. Wrongdoing can appear justified if it promises reward. The mind still reasons, but the reasoning is bent by preference. This is why rajasic confusion can be harder to detect than tamasic stupidity: it often sounds plausible.

Such understanding is spiritually dangerous because it gives the ego moral permission. One no longer sees clearly enough to be corrected by truth. Instead one keeps adjusting truth to fit appetite. The result is a life that may look active and capable, yet remains inwardly misdirected.

For readers, the verse warns against trusting every sharp mind. Intelligence under passion can become sophisticated self-deception. Krishna's standard is whether understanding can actually distinguish right from wrong and obligation from indulgence. If desire keeps blurring those lines, rajasic distortion is at work.

original_script:
अधर्मं धर्ममिति या मन्यते तमसावृता।
सर्वार्थान्विपरीतांश्च बुद्धिः सा पार्थ तामसी।।18.32।।
transliteration:
adharmaṁ dharmam iti yā manyate tamasāvṛitā
sarvārthān viparītānśh cha buddhiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
word_meanings: adharmam—irreligion; dharmam—religion; iti—thus; yā—which; manyate—thinks; tamasā—by illusion; āvṛitā—covered; sarva-arthān—all things; viparītān—in the wrong direction; cha—also; buddhiḥ—intelligence; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; tāmasī—in the mode of ignorance
translation_english: That understanding which considers irreligion to be religion and religion to be irreligion, under the spell of illusion and darkness, and strives always in the wrong direction, O Partha, is in the mode of ignorance.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then names tamasic understanding as that which takes irreligion for religion and religion for irreligion, moving under darkness in the wrong direction. This is more than confusion. It is inversion. The mind not only fails to see rightly; it repeatedly misidentifies opposites.

This is the darkest condition of judgment because the corrective mechanisms themselves are damaged. What should warn instead attracts. What should attract instead repels. Under tamas, the soul can come to call cruelty strength, restraint weakness, delusion insight, or indulgence freedom. The map has been turned upside down.

Krishna adds that such understanding moves constantly in the wrong direction. This means tamasic error is not static. It has momentum. Once the mind habitually misnames reality, it keeps reinforcing that distortion through further choices. Wrong direction becomes way of life.

For readers, the verse is a grave warning. Not every sincere conviction deserves trust. A darkened mind can sincerely affirm what degrades it. That is why humility, scripture, and correction matter so much. Without them, inversion can become stable, and the soul may mistake its decline for fidelity.

original_script:
धृत्या यया धारयते मनःप्राणेन्द्रियक्रियाः।
योगेनाव्यभिचारिण्या धृतिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी।।18.33।।
transliteration:
dhṛityā yayā dhārayate manaḥ-prāṇendriya-kriyāḥ
yogenāvyabhichāriṇyā dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
word_meanings: dhṛityā—by determination; yayā—by which; dhārayate—one sustains; manaḥ—of the mind; prāṇa—life airs; indriya—and senses; kriyāḥ—the activities; yogena—by yoga practice; avyabhichāriṇyā—without any break; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; sāttvikī—in the mode of goodness
translation_english: O son of Pritha, that resolve which is unbreakable, which is sustained with steadfastness by yoga practice, and which thus controls the activities of the mind, life and senses, is determination in the mode of goodness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna describes sattvic determination as unwavering resolve that, through yoga, holds mind, life-force, and senses steady. This is disciplined perseverance ordered by spiritual practice. It is not mere stubbornness. It is a stable power by which the person remains aligned with truth over time.

The objects of control matter: mind, breath or life-force, and senses. These are precisely the areas where ordinary life scatters. Sattvic determination gathers them. Through repeated practice, attention becomes steadier, impulses less tyrannical, and inner life more governable. This is one reason yoga is so central in the Gita: it trains endurance in right alignment.

Such determination is unbreakable not because it is rigid in egoic pride, but because it is anchored in something deeper than mood. Passion burns hot and collapses. Tamas resists and then sinks. Sattva sustains because it is lucid, balanced, and spiritually directed.

For readers, the verse shows that freedom requires more than insight. One must be able to remain with what is true when distraction, fatigue, or temptation arise. Sattvic determination is the quiet strength that keeps the soul from being constantly pulled apart by passing impulses.

original_script:
यया तु धर्मकामार्थान्धृत्या धारयतेऽर्जुन।
प्रसङ्गेन फलाकाङ्क्षी धृतिः सा पार्थ राजसी।।18.34।।
transliteration:
yayā tu dharma-kāmārthān dhṛityā dhārayate ’rjuna
prasaṅgena phalākāṅkṣhī dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; tu—but; dharma—religiosity; kāma—sense gratification; arthān—and economic development; dhṛityā—by determination; dhārayate—one sustains; arjuna—O Arjuna; prasaṅgena—because of attachment; phala-ākāṅkṣhī—desiring fruitive results; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; rājasī—in the mode of passion
translation_english: But that resolve by which one holds fast to fruitive results in religion, economic development and sense gratification is of the nature of passion, O Arjuna.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna contrasts this with rajasic determination, which clings to duty, wealth, and pleasure for the sake of their fruits. Here resolve exists, but it is harnessed to acquisition. The person can persevere intensely, yet the perseverance remains bound to result.

This verse is subtle because rajasic determination often looks admirable. It can be disciplined, focused, and impressive. But Krishna asks what the will is actually holding onto. If the mind clings to religion for status, work for wealth, and life for pleasure, determination becomes a servant of bondage rather than liberation.

Rajas does not lack staying power. It lacks purity of aim. The will is strong, but it is yoked to achievement, security, and gratification. That means it remains deeply vulnerable to frustration, comparison, and burnout. It presses forward, but not yet in freedom.

For readers, the verse offers a needed distinction between strength and spiritual strength. One may be extremely driven and still inwardly unfree. Krishna's question is not, "Can you persist?" but "What are you persisting toward?" Rajasic resolve can build empires while leaving the soul no closer to peace.

original_script:
यया स्वप्नं भयं शोकं विषादं मदमेव च।
न विमुञ्चति दुर्मेधा धृतिः सा पार्थ तामसी।।18.35।।
transliteration:
yayā svapnaṁ bhayaṁ śhokaṁ viṣhādaṁ madam eva cha
na vimuñchati durmedhā dhṛitiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
word_meanings: yayā—by which; svapnam—dreaming; bhayam—fearfulness; śhokam—lamentation; viṣhādam—moroseness; madam—illusion; eva—certainly; cha—also; na—never; vimuñchati—one gives up; durmedhā—unintelligent; dhṛitiḥ—determination; sā—that; pārtha—O son of Pritha; tāmasī—in the mode of ignorance
translation_english: And that resolve which cannot go beyond dreaming, fearfulness, lamentation, moroseness and illusion—such unintelligent resolve is in the mode of darkness.
deep_dive_text:
Krishna then describes tamasic determination as the stubborn inability to let go of sleep, fear, grief, depression, and delusion. This is a striking use of the word determination. Even darkness has its own form of tenacity. The person clings to what diminishes him.

This kind of resolve is not energetic like rajas, but it is still powerful. Human beings can become deeply attached to their own inertia, sorrow, self-pity, or confusion. They do not merely suffer these states; they begin to inhabit and defend them. Tamas holds them there by making release seem impossible or undesirable.

The verse is psychologically profound because it recognizes that bondage is not always dramatic craving. Sometimes it is an entrenched refusal to rise. Fear and grief become identities. Sleep and dullness become shelters. The will remains active, but only in preserving what obscures.

For readers, this verse can be illuminating and compassionate. It names a real form of spiritual captivity without romanticizing it. If one finds oneself fixed in sadness, fear, or lethargy, Krishna's teaching suggests not simply moral condemnation but the need to recognize a dark kind of attachment at work. The first liberation may be admitting that one has begun to cling to what is hurting the soul.

original_script:
सुखं त्विदानीं त्रिविधं शृणु मे भरतर्षभ।
अभ्यासाद्रमते यत्र दुःखान्तं च निगच्छति।।18.36।।
यत्तदग्रे विषमिव परिणामेऽमृतोपमम्।
तत्सुखं सात्त्विकं प्रोक्तमात्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम्।।18.37।।
transliteration:
sukhaṁ tv idānīṁ tri-vidhaṁ śhṛiṇu me bharatarṣhabha
abhyāsād ramate yatra duḥkhāntaṁ cha nigachchhati
yat tad agre viṣham iva pariṇāme ’mṛitopamam
tat sukhaṁ sāttvikaṁ proktam ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam
